questions.text: What is the PRIMARY requirement that a data mining and auditing software tool should meet? The software tool should:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: interface with various types of enterprise resource planning software and databases.
choices.justification: The product must interface with the types of systems used by the enterprise and provide meaningful data for analysis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: accurately capture data from the enterprise systems without causing excessive performance problems.
choices.justification: Although all the requirements that are listed as answer choices are desirable in a software tool evaluated for auditing and data mining purposes, the most critical requirement is that the tool works effectively on the systems of the enterprise being audited.

choices.label: C
choices.text: introduce audit hooks into the financial systems of the enterprise to support continuous auditing.
choices.justification: The tool should probably work on more than just financial systems and does not necessarily require implementation of audit hooks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: be customizable and support inclusion of custom programming to aid in investigative analysis.
choices.justification: The tool should be flexible but not necessarily customizable. It should have built-in analysis software tools.

questions.text: Which technique BEST tests for the existence of dual control when auditing the wire transfer systems of a bank?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Analysis of transaction logs
choices.justification: Analysis of transaction logs helps to show that dual control is in place but does not necessarily guarantee that this process is being followed consistently. Therefore, observation is the better test technique.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reperformance
choices.justification: Although reperformance can provide assurance that dual control was in effect, reperforming wire transfers at a bank is not be an option for an information systems (IS) auditor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Observation
choices.justification: Dual control requires that two people carry out an operation. The observation technique helps to ascertain whether two individuals do get involved in execution of the operation and an element of oversight exists. It is obvious if one individual is masquerading and filling in the role of the second person.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Interviewing personnel
choices.justification: Interviewing personnel is useful to determine the level of awareness and understanding of the personnel carrying out the operations. However, it does provide direct evidence confirming the existence of dual control, because the information provided may not accurately reflect the process being performed.

questions.text: The internal audit department wrote some scripts that are used for continuous auditing. The IT department asked for copies of the scripts so that they can be used for setting up a continuous monitoring process on key systems. Considering the ability of the information systems (IS) auditors to independently and objectively audit the IT function, should sharing these scripts be permitted?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sharing the scripts is not permitted because it gives IT the ability to pre-audit systems and avoid an accurate, comprehensive audit.
choices.justification: The ability of IT to continuously monitor and address any issues on IT systems does not affect the ability of information systems (IS) audit to perform a comprehensive audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sharing the scripts is required because IT must have the ability to review all programs and software that run on information systems regardless of audit independence.
choices.justification: Sharing the scripts may be required by policy for quality assurance and configuration management, but that does not impair the ability to audit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sharing the scripts is permissible if IT recognizes that audits may be conducted in areas not covered in the scripts.
choices.justification: IS audit may not be able to review the effectiveness of the scripts, but it can still audit all aspects of the systems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sharing the scripts is not permitted because the IS auditors who wrote the scripts would not be permitted to audit any information systems where the scripts are being used for monitoring.
choices.justification: An audit of an IS system encompasses more than just the controls covered in the scripts.

questions.text: The MAIN purpose of a transaction audit trail is to:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: reduce the use of storage media.
choices.justification: Enabling audit trails increases the use of disk space.

choices.label: B
choices.text: determine accountability and responsibility for processed transactions.
choices.justification: Enabling audit trails aids in establishing the accountability and responsibility for processed transactions by tracing them through the information system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: help an information systems (IS) auditor trace transactions.
choices.justification: A transaction log file is used to trace transactions, but the primary purpose of an audit trail is to support accountability, not to support the work of the information systems (IS) auditor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: provide useful information for capacity planning.
choices.justification: The objective of capacity planning is the efficient and effective use of IT resources and requires information, such as central processing unit utilization, bandwidth and the number of users.

questions.text: For a retail business with a large volume of transactions, which of the following audit techniques is the MOST appropriate for addressing emerging risk?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use of computer-assisted audit techniques (CAATs)
choices.justification: Using software tools such as computer-assisted audit techniques (CAATs) to analyze transaction data can provide detailed analysis of trends and potential risk, but it is not as effective as continuous auditing, because a time differential may exist between executing the software and analyzing the results.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Quarterly risk assessments
choices.justification: Quarterly risk assessment may be a good technique but not as responsive as continuous auditing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sampling of transaction logs
choices.justification: The sampling of transaction logs is a valid audit technique; however, risk may exist that is not captured in the transaction log, and the analysis may have a potential time lag.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Continuous auditing
choices.justification: The implementation of continuous auditing enables a real-time feed of information to management through automated reporting processes so that management may implement corrective actions more quickly.

questions.text: How would data analytics BEST help in information systems (IS) auditing?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensuring that the information systems (IS) auditing process is completed on time and accurate
choices.justification: Like any other project, information systems (IS) auditing should be completed as per the constraints of scope, time and cost, and should be accurate to be able to assess the objectives and effectiveness of IS controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Automating auditing process and examining a large quantity of data
choices.justification: Automating the auditing process can give time for auditors to focus on critical aspects of their audit objectives, like reducing risk. Examining appropriate and sufficient evidence helps to evaluate the objectives and effectiveness of IS controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Improving auditing quality and decreasing human intervention
choices.justification: Accuracy, collecting authentic information for evidence analysis and reducing human errors in auditing helps to judge whether IS controls are effective and function as expected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Detecting potential issues with the IS controls of an organization
choices.justification: IS auditing, being a risk-based process, should be able to detect the potential issues within IS controls and conclude whether those controls are well-designed and effective.

questions.text: When testing program change requests for a remote system, an information systems (IS) auditor finds that the number of changes available for sampling does not provide a reasonable level of assurance. What is the MOST appropriate action for the IS auditor to take?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Develop an alternate testing procedure.
choices.justification: If a sample-size objective cannot be met with the given data, the information systems (IS) auditor cannot provide assurance regarding the testing objective. In this instance, the IS auditor should develop (with audit management approval) an alternate testing procedure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Report the finding to management.
choices.justification: There is not enough evidence to report the finding as a deficiency.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform a walkthrough of the change management process.
choices.justification: A walkthrough should not be initiated until an analysis is performed to confirm that this can provide the required assurance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Create additional sample data to test additional changes.
choices.justification: It is not appropriate for an IS auditor to create sample data for the purpose of the audit.

questions.text: The final decision to include a material finding in an audit report should be made by the:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: audit committee.
choices.justification: The audit committee should not impair the independence, professionalism and objectivity of the information systems (IS) auditor by influencing what is included in the audit report.

choices.label: B
choices.text: auditee’s manager.
choices.justification: The IS auditor’s manager may recommend what should or should not be included in an audit report, but the auditee’s manager should not influence the content of the report.

choices.label: C
choices.text: information systems (IS) auditor.
choices.justification: The IS auditor should make the final decision about what to include or exclude from the audit report.

choices.label: D
choices.text: chief executive officer.
choices.justification: The chief executive officer must not provide influence over the content of an audit report because that would be a breach of the independence of the audit function.

questions.text: While performing the review of the sampled exception transactions report, which of the following is the MOST appropriate indicator to move from compliance testing to substantive testing for an information systems (IS) auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Control documentation
choices.justification: Controls can be well documented, but that does not automatically put them in place.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Log analysis
choices.justification: Log analysis of posting and approval of exceptional transactions can lead to missing or ineffective controls, which may further require substantive sampling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: System requirements specifications
choices.justification: System requirements specifications record functional and nonfunctional requirements, but also need validation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Data flow diagrams
choices.justification: Data flow diagrams are not as effective as log analysis, because they do not necessarily identify and assess all control issues.

questions.text: Which of the following should be the FIRST action of an information systems (IS) auditor during a dispute with a department manager over audit findings?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Retest the control to validate the finding.
choices.justification: Retesting the control normally occurs after the evidence has been revalidated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Engage a third party to validate the finding.
choices.justification: Although there are cases where a third party may be needed to perform specialized audit procedures, an information systems (IS) auditor should first revalidate the supporting evidence to determine whether there is a need to engage a third party.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Include the finding in the report with the department manager’s comments.
choices.justification: Before putting a disputed finding or management response in the audit report, the IS auditor should take care to review the evidence that is used in the finding to ensure audit accuracy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Revalidate the supporting evidence for the finding.
choices.justification: Conclusions drawn by an IS auditor should be adequately supported by evidence, and any compensating controls or corrections that are pointed out by a department manager should be taken into consideration. Therefore, the first step is to revalidate the evidence for the finding. If, after revalidating and retesting, there are unsettled disagreements, those issues should be included in the report.

questions.text: Which of the following is the PRIMARY purpose of a risk-based audit?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: High-impact areas are addressed first.
choices.justification: High impact does not necessarily indicate high risk. Risk also takes into consideration probability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Audit resources are allocated efficiently.
choices.justification: Although a risk-based audit approach does address allocation of resources, that is not the primary function of a risk-based audit approach.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Material areas are addressed first.
choices.justification: Material risk is audited according to the risk ranking, thus enabling the audit team to concentrate on high-risk areas first.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Management concerns are prioritized.
choices.justification: Management concerns may not be aligned with high-risk areas.

questions.text: Which of the following represents an example of a preventive control with respect to IT personnel?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: A security guard stationed at the server room door
choices.justification: A security guard is a deterrent control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An intrusion detection system (IDS)
choices.justification: An intrusion detection system (IDS) is a detective control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A badge entry system for the IT facility
choices.justification: Preventive controls are used to reduce the probability of an adverse event. A badge entry system prevents unauthorized entry to the facility.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A fire-suppression system in the server room
choices.justification: A fire-suppression system is a corrective control.

questions.text: Which of the following audit techniques BEST helps an information systems (IS) auditor to determine whether unauthorized program changes have been made since the last authorized program update?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Test data run
choices.justification: Test data runs permit the auditor to verify the processing of preselected transactions but provide no evidence about unauthorized changes or unexercised portions of a program.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Code review
choices.justification: Code review is the process of reading program source code listings to determine whether the code follows coding standards or contains potential errors or inefficient statements. A code review can be used as a means of code comparison, but it is inefficient and unlikely to detect any changes in the code, especially in a large program.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Automated code comparison
choices.justification: An automated code comparison is the process of comparing two versions of the same program to determine whether the two correspond. It is an efficient technique because it is an automated procedure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: of code migration procedures
choices.justification: The review of code migration procedures does not detect unauthorized program changes.

questions.text: What is a KEY benefit of incorporating data analytics into the audit process?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Identification of patterns and trends in data for risk assessment
choices.justification: Incorporating data analytics into the audit process enables auditors to analyze large volumes of data efficiently and effectively. One of the key benefits of using data analytics is the ability to identify patterns and trends in data, which can provide valuable insights for risk assessment. By analyzing data, auditors can detect anomalies, assess the effectiveness of controls and identify potential areas of risk or noncompliance. Incorporating data analytics into the audit process brings several benefits, but a key benefit is enhanced decision-making.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elimination of the need for audit testing and sampling
choices.justification: Although data analytics can enhance the audit process, it does not eliminate the need for audit testing and sampling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduction of audit scope and reliance on internal controls
choices.justification: Data analytics does not directly reduce the audit scope but, instead, helps auditors focus their efforts on areas of higher risk and significance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Addition of work for auditors to learn data analysis tools
choices.justification: Although data analytics can enhance the audit process, it does not replace auditors with the use of automated tools. Using a data analytics tool may require some learning by auditors; however, it may reduce the workload in the long term.

category: IS Audit Standards, Guidelines, Functions, and Codes of Ethics

questions.text: Which of the following BEST represents an ethical responsibility of project auditors?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Auditors are permitted to divulge propriety information to which they might have access to support the auditee’s goals.
choices.justification: Auditors are not permitted to divulge propriety information. Such disclosure jeopardizes trust and eventually damages the working relationship between the auditor and auditee.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Auditors should discuss the outcomes of previous audit exercises conducted elsewhere during the audit to ensure completeness.
choices.justification: Auditors must avoid discussing the outcomes of previous audit exercises conducted elsewhere except in cases where such internal audit was to assess the efficiency of an internal system that is applicable to more than one facility (e.g., two or more projects owned by the same parent enterprise).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Audit team leaders must demonstrate fairness when settling disputes among members.
choices.justification: If an issue causes a dispute among audit team members, it should be investigated without prejudice to keep the project on course. Objectivity and fairness must be maintained.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unproven facts should be reported by the auditors to ensure further investigation.
choices.justification: Unproven facts should not be reported by the auditors. Their findings should be disclosed to responsible personnel to test the validity of the audit findings before formal exposure of the audit report.

questions.text: An IT auditor reviewed the transactions log of an audit engagement partner and discovered some suspicious activity, which may be interpreted as potential fraud. However, the auditor was not able to determine the circumstances around the incidents or obtain further evidence. The auditor decided to disclose this information in case there are questions in the audit quality assurance review. In taking this action, the auditor has:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Violated auditing standards because the auditor should inform the appropriate authorities/management of the suspected fraud.
choices.justification: In situations where auditors are not able to obtain sufficient or appropriate audit evidence necessary to achieve the audit objectives, they should disclose the situation to audit management and, if necessary, to those charged with audit governance, as per established procedures. Restrictions or limitations on the scope of the audit and achievement of the audit objectives should also be disclosed in the communication of the audit results. The auditor should also inform the appropriate authorities in the enterprise if the indicators of the fraud are sufficient to recommend an investigation. Thus, the auditor has a duty to act even though the available facts do not prove that an irregularity has occurred. Auditors should disclose all material facts and findings known to them that, if not disclosed, may distort the reporting of activities under review.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Violated laws because unlawful activities should have been reported to the appropriate regulatory agency.
choices.justification: Unlawful activities should not be reported to the outside entities until there is sufficient evidence; however, the suspicion of these activities should be disclosed to appropriate executives within the enterprise.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Not violated auditing standards because the auditor has committed to disclose the facts, when required.
choices.justification: The auditor has a duty to report the results of audit work in the audit communication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Not violated auditing standards because there is a lack of evidence as to whether a fraud has been committed or not.
choices.justification: Limitations on the achievement of audit objectives should be disclosed in the audit results and reporting, and a possible irregularity should be disclosed to the appropriate enterprise authorities.

questions.text: Which of the following situations can impair the independence of an information systems (IS) auditor? The IS auditor:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Implemented specific functionality during the development of an application.
choices.justification: Independence may be impaired if an information systems (IS) auditor is, or was, actively involved in the development, acquisition and implementation of the application system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Designed an embedded audit module for auditing an application.
choices.justification: Designing an embedded audit module does not impair an IS auditor’s independence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Participated as a member of an application project team and did not have operational responsibilities.
choices.justification: IS auditors should not audit work that they have done, but just participating as a member of the application system project team does not impair an IS auditor’s independence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provided consulting advice concerning application good practices.
choices.justification: An IS auditor’s independence is not impaired by providing advice on known good practices.

questions.text: What is the PRIMARY reason for an information systems (IS) auditor to exercise due professional care?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: To get reasonable assurance that IS controls are well-designed and effective
choices.justification: Exercising due professional care helps the information systems (IS) auditor to get reasonable, but not absolute, assurance that audit risk is reduced and evidence that is collected about the design and effectiveness of IS controls is appropriate and sufficient.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To eliminate inherent, control and detection risk associated with IS audit
choices.justification: Risk cannot be eliminated but reduced to an acceptable level.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To detect errors, misstatements or fraudulent transactions in IS and report them
choices.justification: There is detection risk associated with IS audit that cannot be avoided.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To make sure that evidence collected during the IS audit is appropriate and sufficient
choices.justification: Evidence collected should be appropriate and sufficient to help provide reasonable assurance about the design and effectiveness of IS controls.

questions.text: The internal information systems (IS) audit team is auditing controls over sales returns and is concerned about fraud. Which of the following sampling methods will BEST assist the IS auditors?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stop-or-go
choices.justification: Stop-or-go is a sampling method that helps limit the size of a sample and allows the test to be stopped at the earliest possible moment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Classical variable
choices.justification: Classical variable sampling is associated with dollar amounts and has a sample based on a representative sample of the population but is not focused on fraud.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discovery
choices.justification: Discovery sampling is used when an information systems (IS) auditor is trying to determine whether a type of event has occurred. Therefore, it is suited to assess the risk of fraud and to identify whether a single occurrence has taken place.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Probability-proportional-to-size
choices.justification: Probability-proportional-to-size sampling is typically associated with cluster sampling when there are groups within a sample. The question does not indicate that an IS auditor is searching for a threshold of fraud.

questions.text: Which of the following responsibilities is MOST likely to compromise the independence of an information systems (IS) auditor when reviewing the risk management process?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Participating in the design of the risk management framework
choices.justification: Participating in the design of the risk management framework involves designing controls, which compromises the independence of the information systems (IS) auditor to audit the risk management process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Advising on different implementation techniques
choices.justification: Advising on different implementation techniques does not compromise the IS auditor’s independence because the IS auditor will not be involved in the decision-making process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Facilitating risk awareness training
choices.justification: Facilitating awareness training does not hamper the IS auditor’s independence because the auditor will not be involved in the decision-making process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Performing a due diligence review of the risk management processes
choices.justification: Due diligence reviews are a type of audit generally related to mergers and acquisitions.

questions.text: The effect of which of the following should have priority when planning the scope and objectives of an information systems (IS) audit?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Applicable statutory requirements
choices.justification: The effect of applicable statutory requirements must be factored in while planning an information systems (IS) audit—the IS auditor has no options regarding statutory requirements because there can be no limitation of scope relating to statutory requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Applicable corporate standards
choices.justification: Statutory requirements always take priority over corporate standards.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Applicable industry good practices
choices.justification: Industry good practices help plan an audit; however, good practices are not mandatory and can be deviated from, to meet organization objectives.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Organizational policies and procedures
choices.justification: Organizational policies and procedures are important, but statutory requirements always take priority. Organizational policies must be in alignment with statutory requirements.

questions.text: During an exit interview, in cases where there is disagreement regarding the impact of a finding, an information systems (IS) auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the auditee to sign a release form accepting full legal responsibility.
choices.justification: Management is always responsible and liable for risk. The role of the information systems (IS) auditor is to inform management of the findings and associated risk discovered in an audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elaborate on the significance of the finding and the risk of not correcting it.
choices.justification: If the auditee disagrees with the impact of a finding, it is important for an IS auditor to elaborate and clarify the risk and exposures because the auditee may not fully appreciate the magnitude of the exposure. The goal should be to enlighten the auditee or uncover new information of which an IS auditor may not have been aware. Anything that appears to threaten the auditee lessens effective communications and sets up an adversarial relationship, but an IS auditor should not automatically agree just because the auditee expresses an alternate point of view.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Report the disagreement to the audit committee for resolution.
choices.justification: The audit report contains the finding from the IS auditor and the response from management. It is the responsibility of management to accept risk or mitigate it appropriately. The role of the auditor is to inform management clearly and thoroughly so that the best decision can be made.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Accept the auditee position because they are the process owners.
choices.justification: The IS auditor must be professional, competent and independent. They must not just accept an explanation or argument from management, unless the process used to generate the finding was flawed.

questions.text: Which audit technique provides the BEST evidence of the separation of duties in an IT department?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discussion with management
choices.justification: Management may not be aware of the detailed functions of each employee in the IT department and whether the controls are being followed. Therefore, discussion with management provides only limited information regarding separation of duties.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Review of the organization chart
choices.justification: An organization chart does not provide details of the functions of the employees or whether the controls are working correctly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Observation and interviews
choices.justification: Based on the observations and interviews, the information systems (IS) auditor can evaluate the separation of duties. By observing the IT staff performing their tasks, an IS auditor can identify whether they are performing any incompatible operations. By interviewing the IT staff, the auditor can get an overview of the tasks performed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Testing of user access rights
choices.justification: Testing of user rights provides information about the rights users have within the information systems but does not provide complete information about the functions they perform. Observation is a better option because user rights can be changed between audits.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing a software application that is built on the principles of service-oriented architecture. What is the INITIAL step?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Understanding services and their allocation to business processes by reviewing the service repository documentation
choices.justification: A service-oriented architecture relies on the principles of a distributed environment in which services encapsulate business logic as a black box and might be deliberately combined to depict real-world business processes. Before reviewing services in detail, it is essential for the information systems (IS) auditor to comprehend the mapping of business processes to services.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sampling the use of service security standards as represented by the Security Assertions Markup Language (SAML)
choices.justification: Sampling the use of service security standards as represented by Security Assertions Markup Language (SAML) is an essential follow-up step to understanding services and their allocation to business but is not the initial step.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reviewing the service level agreements established for all system providers
choices.justification: Reviewing the service level agreements is an essential follow-up step to understanding services and their allocation to business but is not the initial step.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Auditing the core service and its dependencies on other systems
choices.justification: Auditing the core service and its dependencies with others is most likely a part of the audit, but the IS auditor must first gain an understanding of the business processes and how the systems support those processes.

questions.text: A system developer transfers to the audit department to serve as an IT auditor. When production systems are to be reviewed by this employee, which of the following will become the MOST significant concern?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: The work may be construed as a self-audit.
choices.justification: Because the employee had been a developer, it is recommended that the audit coverage should exclude the systems developed by this employee to avoid any conflicts of interests.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Audit points may largely shift to technical aspects.
choices.justification: Because the employee has a technical background, it is possible that the audit findings tend to focus on technical matters. However, this is normally corrected in the review process before it is carried out in production.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The employee may not have sufficient control assessment skills.
choices.justification: Because auditing is a new role for this employee, they may not have adequate control assessment skills. However, this can be addressed by on-the-job training and is not as big of a concern as a potential conflict of interest.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The employee’s knowledge of business risk may be limited.
choices.justification: Because this employee was previously employed in the organization’s IT department, it is possible to build upon the employee’s current understanding of the business to address any gaps in knowledge.

questions.text: After reviewing the disaster recovery planning process of an organization, an information systems (IS) auditor requests a meeting with organization management to discuss the findings. Which of the following BEST describes the main goal of this meeting?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain management approval of the corrective action plan.
choices.justification: Management approval of the corrective action plan is not required. Management can elect to implement another corrective action plan to address the risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Confirm factual accuracy of the findings.
choices.justification: The goal of the meeting is to confirm the factual accuracy of the audit findings and present an opportunity for management to agree on or respond to recommendations for corrective action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assist management in the implementation of corrective actions.
choices.justification: Implementation of corrective actions should be done after the factual accuracy of findings is established, but the work of implementing corrective action is not typically assigned to the information systems (IS) auditor, because this impairs the auditor’s independence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prioritize the resolution of the items.
choices.justification: Rating the audit findings provides guidance to management for allocating resources to the high-risk items first.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of using information systems (IS) auditing and assurance standards?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Providing instruction in applying information systems (IS) audit and assurance processes
choices.justification: Guidelines are used to provide instruction or guidance in applying information systems (IS) audit and assurance processes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identifying instances of processes IS auditors follow in an audit engagement
choices.justification: Tools and techniques are used to provide instances or examples of processes IS auditors follow in an audit engagement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Providing information on how to meet the requirements when completing IS auditing
choices.justification: Tools and techniques are used to provide information on how to meet the requirements when completing IS auditing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defining mandatory requirements for IS audit and assurance and reporting
choices.justification: Standards are used mainly to define mandatory requirements for IS audit and assurance and reporting.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing a project risk assessment and notices that the overall residual risk level is high due to confidentiality requirements. Which of the following types of risk is normally high due to the number of unauthorized users the project may affect?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Control risk
choices.justification: Control risk can be high, but it is not due to internal controls not being identified, evaluated or tested, and is not due to the number of users or business areas affected.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compliance risk
choices.justification: Compliance risk is the penalty applied to current and future earnings for nonconformance to laws and regulations and may not be impacted by the number of users and business areas affected.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inherent risk
choices.justification: Inherent risk is normally high due to the number of users and business areas that may be affected. Inherent risk is the risk level or exposure without considering the actions that management has taken or might take.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Residual risk
choices.justification: Residual risk is the remaining risk after management has implemented a risk response and is not based on the number of users or business areas affected.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor realizes that the skilled resources required to test the specific technical controls are only available for a limited period during an IS audit; therefore, comprehensive testing of all audit areas may not be possible. Which of the following is the BEST course of action for the auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Execute the audit focusing on critical areas where skilled resources are used to test controls.
choices.justification: In a situation where limited resources are available; an auditor may use the services of skilled resources for high-risk audit areas to ensure that the maximum number of controls in high-risk areas are tested.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Have audit management request skilled resources for the entire audit period.
choices.justification: Auditors may escalate the issue to audit management, but, in the case of unavailable resources, it is best to address high-risk areas first.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refuse to conduct the audit unless skilled resources are available for the entire audit period.
choices.justification: Refusing to conduct an audit may not be a reasonable solution and will cause control failures or weaknesses in high-risk areas to remain undetected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Extend the timelines of the audit project for training to enhance the skills of the audit team.
choices.justification: It is useful to have the audit team increase their knowledge and skills; however, this option may not be reasonable to auditee management.

questions.text: Which of the following sampling methods is the MOST appropriate for testing automated invoice authorization controls to ensure that exceptions are not made for specific users?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Variable sampling
choices.justification: Variable sampling is used for substantive testing to determine the monetary or volumetric impact of characteristics of a population. This is not the most appropriate method in this case.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Judgmental sampling
choices.justification: In judgmental sampling, professionals place a bias on the sample (e.g., all sampling units over a certain value, all for a specific type of exception or all negatives). A judgmental sample is not statistically based, and results should not be extrapolated over the population because the sample is unlikely to be representative of the population.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stratified random sampling
choices.justification: Stratification is the process of dividing a population into subpopulations with similar characteristics explicitly defined, so that each sampling unit can belong to only one stratum. This method of sampling ensures that all sampling units in each subgroup have a known, nonzero chance of selection. It is the most appropriate method in this case.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Systematic sampling
choices.justification: Systematic sampling involves selecting sampling units using a fixed interval between selections with the first interval having a random start. This is not the most appropriate method in this case.

questions.text: Which of the following sampling methods is MOST useful when testing for compliance?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Attribute sampling
choices.justification: Attribute sampling is the primary sampling method used for compliance testing. Attribute sampling is a sampling model that is used to estimate the rate of occurrence of a specific quality (attribute) in a population and is used in compliance testing to confirm whether the quality exists. For example, an attribute sample may check all transactions over a certain predefined dollar amount for proper approvals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Variable sampling
choices.justification: Variable sampling is based on the calculation of a mean from a sample extracted from the entire population and using that to estimate the characteristics of the entire population. For example, a sample of 10 items shows an average price of US $10 per item. For the entire population of 1,000 items, the total value is estimated to be US $10,000. This is not a good way to measure compliance with a process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stratified mean-per-unit sampling
choices.justification: Stratified mean-per-unit sampling attempts to ensure that the entire population is represented in the sample. This is not an effective way to measure compliance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Difference estimation sampling
choices.justification: Difference estimation sampling examines measure deviations and extraordinary items and is not a good way to measure compliance.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST important to ensure before communicating the audit findings to top management during the closing meeting?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Risk statement includes an explanation of a business impact
choices.justification: It is important to have a well-elaborated risk statement; however, it might not be relevant if the findings are not accurate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Findings are clearly tracked back to evidence
choices.justification: Without adequate evidence, the findings hold no ground; therefore, this must be verified before communicating the findings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recommendations address root causes of findings
choices.justification: It is important to address the root causes of the findings, and they may not be included in the report. However, they might not be relevant if the findings are not accurate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remediation plans are provided by responsible parties
choices.justification: In some cases, top management might expect to see remediation plans during debriefing of the findings; however, the accuracy of findings should be proved first.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor received an engagement to audit the enterprise resource planning (ERP) implementation from an audit committee. The auditor does not have competency in the specific ERP solution implemented by the auditee. Which of the following is the BEST course of action for the auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Attend a course to understand the specific enterprise resource planning (ERP) solution before conducting the audit.
choices.justification: Attending a course might be time-consuming, expensive and still may not provide the required competency.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hire an independent expert to support the audit with approval from the audit committee.
choices.justification: information systems (IS) audit standards permit the use of other experts to fill a competency gap.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refuse to conduct the audit on the grounds of a lack of required competencies.
choices.justification: Refusing to conduct the audit is not the best course of action as standards permit the auditor to use other experts to fill the competency gap.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conduct a black-box audit around the ERP without going into specific controls.
choices.justification: Conducting a black box audit may not meet the audit objectives.

questions.text: Which of the following is the GREATEST concern if audit objectives are not established during the initial phase of an audit program?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Key stakeholders are incorrectly identified.
choices.justification: In certain cases, it may be difficult to discuss findings when incorrect stakeholders are identified, thus delaying the communication of audit findings. However, this is not as concerning as important business risk not being included in audit scope.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Control costs will exceed the planned budget.
choices.justification: Many factors determine the cost of controls. Therefore, it is difficult to state that only audit objectives will determine the control cost. However, this is not as important if the key risk is not identified.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Important business risk may be overlooked.
choices.justification: Without an audit scope, the appropriate risk assessment has not been performed, and therefore, the auditor might not audit those areas of highest risk for the organization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Previously audited areas may be inadvertently included.
choices.justification: Auditing previously audited areas is not an efficient use of resources; however, this is not as big of a concern as key risk not being identified.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor of an enterprise is also the auditor for a cloud services provider (CSP). Enterprise management requested a copy of the CSP’s audit report from the auditor because they are also a client of the CSP. Which of the following is the BEST course of action for the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Share the report because the enterprise will receive it anyway per their contract with the cloud services provider (CSP).
choices.justification: Sharing the report directly is a violation of professional ethics.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Request the management get a copy of the report directly from the CSP.
choices.justification: The auditor cannot share any information received from one client with another because this is against professional ethics. Therefore, the best course of action is for the enterprise to request the report directly from the cloud services provider (CSP).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refuse to share the report because the auditor has signed a nondisclosure agreement with the CSP.
choices.justification: The auditor may politely refuse to share the report, but direct refusal may not be the best approach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discuss general findings informally to provide assurance on compliance by the CSP.
choices.justification: Professional ethics require that the auditor should not share anything from one client with another, even informally.

questions.text: Due to unexpected resource constraints of the information systems (IS) audit team, the audit plan, as originally approved, cannot be completed. Assuming that the situation is communicated in the audit report, which course of action is MOST acceptable?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Test the adequacy of the control design.
choices.justification: Testing the adequacy of control design is not the best course of action because this does not ensure that controls operate effectively as designed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Test the operational effectiveness of controls.
choices.justification: Testing control operating effectiveness does not ensure that the audit plan is focused on areas of greatest risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Focus on auditing high-risk areas.
choices.justification: Reducing the scope and focusing on auditing high-risk areas is the best course of action.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rely on management testing of controls.
choices.justification: The reliance on management testing of controls does not provide an objective verification of the control environment.

questions.text: A long-term IT employee with a strong technical background and broad managerial experience has applied for a vacant position in the information systems (IS) audit department. Determining whether to hire this individual for this position should be PRIMARILY based on the individual’s experience and:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Length of service because this will help ensure technical competence.
choices.justification: Length of service does not ensure technical competency.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Age because training in audit techniques may be impractical.
choices.justification: Evaluating an individual’s qualifications based on the age of the individual is not a good criterion and is illegal in many parts of the world.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IT knowledge because this will bring enhanced credibility to the audit function.
choices.justification: The fact that the employee has worked in IT for many years may not ensure credibility. The information systems (IS) audit department’s needs should be defined, and any candidate should be evaluated against those requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ability, as an IS auditor, to be independent of existing IT relationships.
choices.justification: Independence should be continually assessed by the auditor and management. This assessment should consider such factors as changes in personal relationships, financial interests, and prior job assignments and responsibilities.

questions.text: While conducting an information systems (IS) audit, the auditor observed fraudulent activity in an area that was outside the scope of the audit. Which of the following is the BEST course of action for the auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Conduct an audit of the suspected area and include it in the report.
choices.justification: Since the fraudulent activity is not in the scope of the audit, the auditor may not have authority to conduct an audit in the suspected area.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Communicate the observations to senior management.
choices.justification: Although the fraudulent activity is not in the scope of the audit, it is prudent for the auditor to communicate to senior management or the audit committee to enable them to take necessary steps.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ignore the activity and do nothing, since it is not within the scope of the audit.
choices.justification: Ignoring possible fraud is not ethical.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Report the fraudulent activity to a law enforcement agency.
choices.justification: Reporting to an external law enforcement agency is against professional ethics and out of scope of audit performance.

category: Types of Audits, Assessments, and Reviews

questions.text: After initial investigation, an information systems (IS) auditor has reasons to believe that fraud may be present. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expand activities to determine whether an investigation is warranted.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor’s responsibility for detecting fraud include evaluating fraud indicators and deciding whether any additional action is necessary or whether an investigation should be recommended.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Report the matter to the audit committee.
choices.justification: The IS auditor should notify the appropriate authorities within the organization only if it has determined that the indicators of fraud are sufficient to recommend an investigation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Report the possibility of fraud to management.
choices.justification: The IS auditor should report the possibility of fraud to top management only after there is sufficient evidence to launch an investigation. This may be affected by whether management may be involved in the fraud.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Consult with external legal counsel to determine the course of action to be taken.
choices.justification: Normally, the IS auditor does not have authority to consult with external legal counsel.

questions.text: Which of the following choices is the BEST source of information when developing a risk-based audit plan?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Process owners identify key controls.
choices.justification: Although process owners should be consulted to identify key controls, senior management is a better source to identify business processes, which are more important.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System custodians identify vulnerabilities.
choices.justification: System custodians are a good source to better understand the risk and controls as they apply to specific applications; however, senior management is a better source to identify business processes, which are more important.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Peer auditors understand previous audit results.
choices.justification: The review of previous audit results is one input into the audit planning process; however, if previous audits focused on a limited or a restricted scope, or if the key business processes have changed and/or new business processes have been introduced, then this does contribute to the development of a risk-based audit plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Senior management identifies key business processes.
choices.justification: Developing a risk-based audit plan must start with the identification of key business processes, which determine and identify the risk that needs to be addressed.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST ensures the effectiveness of controls related to interest calculation for an accounting system?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reperformance
choices.justification: To ensure the effectiveness of controls, it is most effective to conduct reperformance. When the same result is obtained after the performance by an independent person, this provides the strongest assurance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Process walk-through
choices.justification: Process walk-through may help the auditor understand the controls better; however, it may not be as useful as conducting reperformance for a sample of transactions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Observation
choices.justification: Observation is a valid audit method to verify that operators are using the system appropriately; however, conducting reperformance is a better method.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Documentation review
choices.justification: Documentation review may be of some value for understanding the control environment; however, conducting reperformance is a better method.

questions.text: While auditing a third-party IT service provider, an information systems (IS) auditor discovers that access reviews are not being performed as required by the contract. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Report the issue to IT management.
choices.justification: During an audit, if there are material issues that are of concern, they need to be reported to management in the audit report.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discuss the issue with the service provider.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor may discuss the issue with the service provider; however, the appropriate response is to report the issue to IT management because they are ultimately responsible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform a risk assessment.
choices.justification: This issue can serve as an input for a future risk assessment, but the issue of noncompliance should be reported to management regardless of whether the IS auditor believes there is a significant risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform an access review.
choices.justification: The IS auditor can perform an access review as part of the audit to determine if there are errors, but not on behalf of the third-party IT service provider. It is more important to report the issue in the audit report to management.

questions.text: The PRIMARY purpose of the information systems (IS) audit charter is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Establish the organizational structure of the audit department.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) audit charter does not set forth the organizational structure of the IS audit department. The charter serves as a directive to create the IS audit function.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Illustrate the reporting responsibilities of the IS audit function.
choices.justification: The IS audit charter does not dictate the reporting requirements of the IS audit department. The charter sets forth the purpose, responsibility, authority and accountability of the information systems audit function.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Detail the resource requirements needed for the audit function.
choices.justification: Resources are determined by the audit and not the charter.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Outline the responsibility and authority of the IS audit function.
choices.justification: The primary purpose of the IS audit charter is to set forth the purpose, responsibility, authority and accountability of the IS audit function. The charter document grants authority to the audit function on behalf of the board of directors and organization stakeholders.

questions.text: The access control policy should be reviewed by the information systems (IS) auditor to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prevent unauthorized disclosure of organizational data.
choices.justification: The principles of confidentiality prevent the unauthorized disclosure of organizational data of an organization, not the access control policy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure that organizational objectives for separation of duties are met.
choices.justification: The organizational access control policy ensures that organizational objectives for separation of duties are met, preventing fraud or errors and meeting policy requirements for minimizing the risk of unauthorized access.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Minimize the impact of an incident.
choices.justification: The access control policy helps to minimize error, but it does not minimize the impact of an incident.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure the continuity of business after any disaster.
choices.justification: The business continuity policy is concerned with the continuity of business, not the access control policy.

questions.text: As part of audit planning, an information systems (IS) auditor is designing various data validation tests to effectively detect transposition and transcription errors. Which of the following will BEST help in detecting these errors?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Range check
choices.justification: Range checks can only ensure that data fall within a predetermined range but cannot detect transposition errors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Validity check
choices.justification: Validity checks are generally programmed checking of data validity in accordance with predetermined criteria.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Duplicate check
choices.justification: Duplicate check analysis is used to test defined or selected primary keys for duplicate primary key values.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check digit
choices.justification: A check digit is a numeric value that has been calculated mathematically and is added to data to ensure that original data have not been altered or that an incorrect, but valid, match has occurred. The check digit control is effective in detecting transposition and transcription errors.

questions.text: The PRIMARY purpose for meeting with auditees prior to formally closing a review is to:
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: Confirm that the auditors did not overlook any important issues.
choices.justification: The closing meeting identifies any misunderstandings or errors in the audit but does not identify any important issues overlooked in the audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gain agreement on the findings.
choices.justification: The primary purpose for meeting with auditees prior to formally closing a review is to gain agreement on the findings and responses from management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Receive feedback on the adequacy of the audit procedures.
choices.justification: The closing meeting may obtain comments from management on the conduct of the audit but is not intended to be a formal review of the adequacy of the audit procedures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Test the structure of the final presentation.
choices.justification: The structure of an audit report and the presentation follow accepted standards and practices. The closing meeting may indicate errors in the audit or presentation but is not intended to test the structure of the presentation.

questions.text: The PRIMARY objective of the audit initiation meeting with an information systems (IS) audit client is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discuss the scope of the audit.
choices.justification: The primary objective of the initiation meeting with an audit client is to help define the scope of the audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identify resource requirements of the audit.
choices.justification: Determining the resource requirements of the information systems (IS) audit is typically done by IS audit management during the early planning phase of the project rather than at the initiation meeting.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Select the methodology of the audit.
choices.justification: Selecting the methodology of the audit is not normally an objective of the initiation meeting.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Collect audit evidence.
choices.justification: For most audits, collecting audit evidence is performed during the course of the engagement and is not normally collected during the initiation meeting.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing risk and controls of the wire transfer system of a bank. To ensure that the bank financial risk is properly addressed, the IS auditor most likely reviews which of the following?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Privileged access to the wire transfer system
choices.justification: Privileged access, such as administrator access, is necessary to manage user account privileges and should not be granted to end users. The wire transfer procedures are a better control to review to ensure that there is separation of duties of the end users to help prevent fraud.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wire transfer procedures
choices.justification: Wire transfer procedures include separation of duties controls. This helps prevent internal fraud by not allowing one person to initiate, approve and send a wire. Therefore, the information systems (IS) auditor should review the procedures as they relate to the wire system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fraud monitoring controls
choices.justification: Fraud monitoring is a detective control and does not prevent financial loss. Separation of duties is a preventive control which is part of the wire transfer procedures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Employee background checks
choices.justification: Although controls related to background checks are important, the controls related to separation of duties as found in the wire transfer procedures are more critical.

questions.text: The data owners are responsible for:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Safeguarding the data by creating regular backups.
choices.justification: The data owners generally do not create the backups but define the requirements for data backup.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Security of data throughout their life cycle from origination to destruction.
choices.justification: The data owners are primarily responsible for safeguarding the data they own.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protecting the data by monitoring them in real time.
choices.justification: The data owners generally do not monitor the data communication but define the data monitoring requirements for the custodian or other responsible personnel.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Performing risk assessment and implementing appropriate controls.
choices.justification: The data owners generally do not perform the risk assessment but define the risk management requirements.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor who has discovered unauthorized transactions during a review of electronic data interchange (EDI) transactions is likely to recommend improving the:
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: EDI trading partner agreements.
choices.justification: Electronic data interchange trading partner agreements minimize exposure to legal issues but do not resolve the problem of unauthorized transactions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Physical controls for terminals.
choices.justification: Physical control is important and may provide protection from unauthorized people accessing the system but does not provide protection from unauthorized transactions by authorized users.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Authentication techniques for sending and receiving messages.
choices.justification: Authentication techniques for sending and receiving messages play a key role in minimizing exposure to unauthorized transactions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Program change control procedures.
choices.justification: Change control procedures do not resolve the issue of unauthorized transactions.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST critical step when planning an information systems (IS) audit?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Review findings from prior audits.
choices.justification: The findings of a previous audit are of interest to the auditor, but they are not the most critical step. The most critical step involves finding the current issues or high-risk areas, not reviewing the resolution of older issues. A review of historical audit findings can indicate that management is not resolving the items or the recommendation was ineffective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Obtain executive management approval of the audit plan
choices.justification: Executive management is not required to approve the audit plan. It is typically approved by the audit committee or board of directors. Management can recommend areas to audit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Review information security policies and procedures.
choices.justification: Reviewing information security policies and procedures is normally conducted during fieldwork, not planning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform a risk assessment.
choices.justification: Of all the steps listed, performing a risk assessment is the most critical. Risk assessment is required by ISACA IS Audit and Assurance Standard 1201 (Risk Assessment in Planning), statement 1201.2: “IT audit and assurance practitioners shall identify and assess risk relevant to the area under review when planning individual engagements.” In addition to the standards requirement, if a risk assessment is not performed, then high-risk areas of the auditee systems or operations may not be identified for evaluation.

questions.text: A financial institution with multiple branch offices has an automated control that requires the branch manager to approve transactions over a certain amount. What type of audit control is this?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Detective
choices.justification: Detective controls identify events after they have happened. In this case, the action of the branch manager prevents an event from occurring.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Preventive
choices.justification: Having a manager approve transactions over a certain amount is considered a preventive control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Corrective
choices.justification: A corrective control serves to remedy problems discovered by detective controls. In this case, the action of the branch manager is a preventive control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Directive
choices.justification: A directive control is a manual control that typically consists of a policy or procedure that specifies what actions are to be performed. In this case, an automated control prevents an event from occurring.

questions.text: The PRIMARY purpose of an IT forensic audit is:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: To participate in investigations related to corporate fraud.
choices.justification: Forensic audits are not limited to corporate fraud.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To enable the systematic collection and analysis of evidence after a system irregularity.
choices.justification: The systematic collection and analysis of evidence after a system irregularity best describes a forensic audit. The evidence collected can then be analyzed and used in judicial proceedings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To assess the correctness of an organization’s financial statements.
choices.justification: Assessing the correctness of an organization’s financial statements is not the primary purpose of most forensic audits.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To preserve evidence of criminal activity.
choices.justification: Forensics is the investigation of evidence related to a crime or misbehavior. Preserving evidence is the forensic process, but not the primary purpose.

questions.text: What BEST describes the risk that information collected may contain a material error that may go undetected during information systems (IS) auditing?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inherent risk
choices.justification: Inherent risk is the risk level or exposure of the process/entity to be audited without considering the controls that management has implemented.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Audit risk
choices.justification: Audit risk is the probability that information or financial reports may contain material errors and that the auditor may not detect an error that has occurred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Control risk
choices.justification: Control risk is the risk that a material error exists that would not be prevented or detected on a timely basis by the system of internal controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Detection risk
choices.justification: Detection risk is the risk that material errors or misstatements that have occurred will not be detected by an information systems (IS) auditor.

questions.text: While performing an audit of an accounting application’s internal data integrity controls, an information systems (IS) auditor identifies a major control deficiency in the change management software supporting the accounting application. The MOST appropriate action for the IS auditor to take is to:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue to test the accounting application controls and inform the IT manager about the control deficiency and recommend possible solutions.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor should not assume that the IT manager will follow through on a verbal notification to resolve the change management control deficiency, and it is inappropriate to offer consulting services on issues discovered during an audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Complete the audit and not report the control deficiency because it is not part of the audit scope.
choices.justification: Although not technically within the audit scope, it is the responsibility of the IS auditor to report findings discovered during an audit that can have a material impact on the effectiveness of controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continue to test the accounting application controls and include the deficiency in the final report.
choices.justification: It is the responsibility of the IS auditor to report on findings that can have a material impact on the effectiveness of controls—whether they are within the scope of the audit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cease all audit activity until the control deficiency is resolved.
choices.justification: It is not the role of the IS auditor to demand that IT work be completed before performing or completing an audit.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST source of information that the information systems (IS) auditor can use in assessing the process being audited?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interviews with a sample of employees from the IT department
