choices.justification: Interviews with some employees will help the information systems (IS) auditor to better understand processes and capture any missing items from the control self-assessment (CSA). However, this is not the best source of information.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Current audit plan
choices.justification: The audit plan is created after understanding the business risk within the audit scope. Therefore, it will not help to identify higher-risk processes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Past audit reports
choices.justification: Past audit reports can be another source of information. However, the enterprise could have had changes since that last audit. Therefore, a recently completed control self-assessment will be the best source of information that an IS auditor reviews.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recent control self-assessment
choices.justification: CSA is made by the staff and management of the unit involved. This assessment can be used by the IS auditor to identify higher-risk processes and then link them to the applications being used.

questions.text: The vice president of human resources has requested an information systems (IS) audit to identify payroll overpayments for the previous year. Which is the BEST audit technique to use in this situation?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Generate sample test data
choices.justification: Test data tests for the existence of controls that might prevent overpayments, but it does not detect specific, previous miscalculations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Generalized audit software
choices.justification: Generalized audit software features include mathematical computations, stratification, statistical analysis, sequence checking, duplicate checking and recomputations. An information systems (IS) auditor, using generalized audit software, can design appropriate tests to recompute the payroll, thereby determining whether overpayments were made and to whom.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Integrated test facility
choices.justification: An integrated test facility helps to identify a problem as it occurs but does not detect errors for a previous period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Embedded audit module
choices.justification: An embedded audit module can enable the IS auditor to evaluate a process and gather audit evidence, but it does not detect errors for a previous period.

questions.text: Which of the following is a PRIMARY objective of embedding an audit module while developing online application systems?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: To collect evidence while transactions are processed
choices.justification: Embedding a module for continuous auditing within an application processing a large number of transactions provides timely collection of audit evidence during processing and is the primary objective. The continuous auditing approach allows the information systems (IS) auditor to monitor system reliability on a continuous basis and to gather selective audit evidence through the computer.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To reduce requirements for periodic internal audits
choices.justification: An embedded audit module enhances the effectiveness of internal audit by ensuring timely availability of required evidence. It may not reduce the requirements for periodic internal audits, but it will increase their efficiency. Also, the question pertains to the development process for new application systems, and not to subsequent internal audits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To identify and report fraudulent transactions
choices.justification: An audit module collects data on transactions that may help identify fraudulent transactions, but it does not identify fraudulent transactions inherently.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To increase efficiency of the audit function
choices.justification: Although increased efficiency may be an added benefit of an embedded audit module, it is not the primary objective.

questions.text: When the information systems (IS) auditor encounters instances or indicators of fraud during regular assurance work, what should the IS auditor do FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Communicate with audit management.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor may communicate with audit management to get their recommendations after exercising due professional care.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Escalate the issue to the audit committee.
choices.justification: After doing further analysis and evaluation, audit management should communicate with the audit committee to consider the next step, especially if there is an indicator or instance of major fraud.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Report the fraud instance to appropriate authorities.
choices.justification: Reporting to appropriate authorities should be done after exercising due professional care and consulting audit management and the audit committee.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exercise careful analysis and evaluation .
choices.justification: The IS auditor must exercise due professional care and do further analysis and evaluation before considering the issue an instance of fraud.

questions.text: An enterprise has recently upgraded its purchase system to incorporate electronic data interchange (EDI) transmissions. Which of the following controls should be implemented in the EDI interface to provide efficient data mapping?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Key verification
choices.justification: Key verification is used for encryption and protection of data but not for data mapping.

choices.label: B
choices.text: One-for-one checking
choices.justification: One-for-one checking validates that transactions are accurate and complete but does not map data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Manual recalculations
choices.justification: Manual recalculations are used to verify that the processing is correct but do not map data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Functional acknowledgments
choices.justification: Acting as an audit trail for electronic data interchange transactions, functional acknowledgments are one of the main controls used in data mapping.

questions.text: The PRIMARY advantage of a continuous audit approach is that it:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Does not require an information systems (IS) auditor to collect evidence on system reliability while processing is taking place.
choices.justification: The continuous audit approach often requires an information systems (IS) auditor to collect evidence on system reliability while processing is taking place.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allows the IS auditor to review and follow up on audit issues in a timely manner.
choices.justification: Continuous audit allows audit and response to audit issues in a timely manner because audit findings are gathered in near real time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Places the responsibility for enforcement and monitoring of controls on the security department instead of audit.
choices.justification: Responsibility for enforcement and monitoring of controls is primarily the responsibility of management.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Simplifies the extraction and correlation of data from multiple and complex systems.
choices.justification: The use of continuous audit is not based on the complexity or number of systems being monitored.

questions.text: The decisions and actions of an information systems (IS) auditor are MOST likely to affect which of the following types of risk?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inherent
choices.justification: Inherent risk is the risk that a material error could occur, if there are no related internal controls to prevent or detect the error. Inherent risk is not usually affected by an information systems (IS) auditor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Detection
choices.justification: Detection risk is directly affected by the IS auditor’s selection of audit procedures and techniques. Detection risk is the risk that a review will not detect or notice a material issue.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Control
choices.justification: Control risk is the risk that a material error exists that would not be prevented or detected on a timely basis by the system of internal controls. Control risk can be mitigated by the actions of enterprise management.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Business
choices.justification: Business risk is a probable situation with uncertain frequency and magnitude of loss (or gain). Business risk is usually not directly affected by an IS auditor.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor discovers that devices connected to the network are not included in a network diagram that had been used to develop the scope of the audit. The chief information officer explains that the diagram is being updated and awaiting final approval. The IS auditor should FIRST:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expand the scope of the IS audit to include the devices that are not on the network diagram.
choices.justification: It is important that the information systems (IS) auditor does not immediately assume that everything on the network diagram provides information about the risk affecting a network/system. There is a process in place for documenting and updating the network diagram.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evaluate the impact of the undocumented devices on the audit scope.
choices.justification: In a risk-based approach to an IS audit, the scope is determined by the impact that the devices will have on the audit. If the undocumented devices do not impact the audit scope, then they may be excluded from the current audit engagement. The information provided on a network diagram can vary depending on what is being illustrated—for example, the network layer and cross connections.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Note a control deficiency because the network diagram has not been approved.
choices.justification: In this case, there is simply a mismatch in timing between the completion of the approval process and when the IS audit began. There is no control deficiency to be reported.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plan follow-up audits of the undocumented devices.
choices.justification: Planning for follow-up audits of the undocumented devices is contingent on the risk that the undocumented devices have on the ability of the entity to meet the audit scope.

questions.text: Which of the following should an information systems (IS) auditor use to detect duplicate invoice records within an invoice master file?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Attribute sampling
choices.justification: Attribute sampling aids in identifying records meeting specific conditions but does not compare one record to another to identify duplicates. To detect duplicate invoice records, the information systems (IS) auditor should check all items that meet the criteria and not just a sample of the items.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Computer-assisted audit techniques (CAATs)
choices.justification: Computer-assisted audit techniques (CAATs) enable the IS auditor to review the entire invoice file to look for those items that meet the selection criteria.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compliance testing
choices.justification: Compliance testing determines whether control procedures are adhered to. Using CAATs is the better option because it is most likely more efficient to search for duplicates.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Integrated test facility
choices.justification: An integrated test facility allows the IS auditor to test transactions through the production system but does not compare records to identify duplicates.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) audit department considers implementing continuous auditing techniques for a multinational retail enterprise that requires high availability of its key systems. A PRIMARY benefit of continuous auditing is that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Effective preventive controls are enforced.
choices.justification: Continuous monitoring is detective in nature and, therefore, does not necessarily assist the information systems (IS) auditor in monitoring for preventive controls. The approach will detect and monitor for errors that have already occurred. In addition, continuous monitoring will benefit the internal audit function in reducing the use of auditing resources and in the timely reporting of errors or inconsistencies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System integrity is ensured.
choices.justification: System integrity is typically associated with preventive controls, such as input controls and quality assurance reviews. These controls do not typically benefit an internal auditing function implementing continuous monitoring. Continuous monitoring benefits the internal audit function because it reduces the use of auditing resources.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Errors can be corrected in a timely fashion.
choices.justification: Continuous audit will detect errors but not correct them. Correcting errors is the function of the organization’s management and not the internal audit function. Continuous auditing benefits the internal audit function because it reduces the use of auditing resources to create a more efficient auditing function.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fraud can be detected more quickly.
choices.justification: Continuous auditing techniques assist the auditing function in reducing the use of auditing resources through continuous collection of evidence. This approach assists the IS auditors in identifying fraud in a timely fashion and allows the auditors to focus on relevant data.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is testing employee access to a large financial system and selects a sample from the current employee list provided by the auditee. Which of the following evidence is the MOST reliable to support the testing?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: A spreadsheet provided by the system administrator
choices.justification: A spreadsheet supplied by the system administrator may not be complete or may be inaccurate. Documentary evidence should be collected to support the auditee’s spreadsheet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Human resources access documents signed by employees’ managers
choices.justification: The human resources access documents signed by managers are good evidence; however, they are not as objective as the system-generated access list, because access may have changed, or the documents may have been incorrect when they were signed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A list of accounts with access levels generated by the system
choices.justification: The access list generated by the system is the most reliable because it is the most objective evidence to perform a comparison against the samples selected. The evidence is objective because it was generated by the system rather than by an individual.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Observations performed onsite in the presence of a system administrator
choices.justification: The observations are good evidence to understand the internal control structure; however, observations are not efficient for many users. Observations are not objective enough for substantive tests.

questions.text: The PRIMARY reason an information systems (IS) auditor performs a functional walk-through during the preliminary phase of an audit assignment is to:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Understand the business process.
choices.justification: Understanding the business process is the first step that an information systems (IS) auditor needs to perform.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Comply with auditing standards.
choices.justification: ISACA IS Audit and Assurance Standards encourage adoption of the audit procedures/processes required to assist the IS auditor in performing IS audits more effectively. However, standards do not require an IS auditor to perform a process walk-through at the commencement of an audit engagement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identify control weakness.
choices.justification: Identifying control weaknesses is not the primary reason for the walk-through and typically occurs at a later stage in the audit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Develop the risk assessment.
choices.justification: The main reason is to understand the business process. The risk assessment is developed after the business process is understood.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is developing an audit plan for an environment that includes new systems. Enterprise management wants the IS auditor to focus on recently implemented systems. How should the IS auditor respond?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Audit the new systems as requested by management.
choices.justification: Auditing the new systems does not reflect a risk-based approach. Although the systems can contain sensitive data and may present risk of data loss or disclosure to the organization, without a risk assessment, the decision to solely audit the newly implemented systems is not a risk-based decision.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Audit systems not included in last year’s scope.
choices.justification: Auditing systems not included in the previous year’s scope does not reflect a risk-based approach. In addition, management may know about problems with the new systems and may intentionally try to steer the audit away from that vulnerable area. Although, at first, the new system may seem to be the riskiest area, an assessment must be conducted rather than relying on the judgment of the information systems (IS) auditor or IT manager.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determine the highest-risk systems and plan accordingly.
choices.justification: The best action is to conduct a risk assessment and design the audit plan to cover the areas of highest risk. ISACA IS Audit and Assurance Standard 1201 (Risk Assessment in Planning), statement 1201.1: “The IT audit and assurance function shall use an appropriate risk assessment approach (i.e., data-driven with both quantitative and qualitative factors) and supporting methodology to develop the overall IT audit plan and to determine priorities for the effective allocation of IT audit resources.”

choices.label: D
choices.text: Audit systems not in last year’s scope and the new systems.
choices.justification: The creation of the audit plan should be performed in cooperation with management and based on risk. The IS auditor should not arbitrarily decide on what needs to be audited.

questions.text: The MOST effective audit practice to determine whether controls accurately support the operational effectiveness of transaction processing is:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Control design testing.
choices.justification: Testing of control design assesses whether the control is structured to meet a specific control objective. It does not help determine whether the control is operating effectively.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Substantive testing.
choices.justification: Among other methods, such as document review or walkthrough, tests of controls are the most effective procedures to assess whether controls accurately support operational effectiveness.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inspection of relevant documentation.
choices.justification: Control documents may not always describe the actual process in an accurate manner. Therefore, auditors relying on document review have limited assurance that the control is operating as intended.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform tests on risk prevention.
choices.justification: Performing tests on risk prevention is considered compliance testing. This type of testing is used to determine whether policies are adhered to.

questions.text: Which of the following is the FIRST step performed prior to creating a risk ranking for the annual internal information systems (IS) audit plan?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prioritize the identified risk.
choices.justification: After the audit universe is defined, the information systems (IS) auditor can prioritize risk based on its overall impact on different operational areas of the organization covered under the audit universe.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Define the audit universe.
choices.justification: In a risk-based audit approach, the IS auditor identifies risk to the organization based on the nature of the business. To plan an annual audit cycle, the types of risk must be ranked. To rank the types of risk, the auditor must first define the audit universe by considering the IT strategic plan, organizational structure and authorization matrix.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identify the critical controls.
choices.justification: The controls that help in mitigating high-risk areas are generally critical controls and their effectiveness provides assurance on mitigation of risk. However, this cannot be done unless the types of risk are ranked.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determine the testing approach.
choices.justification: The testing approach is based on the risk ranking.

questions.text: Why does an audit manager review the staff audit papers, even when the information systems (IS) auditors have many years of experience?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Internal quality requirements
choices.justification: Internal quality requirements may exist but are superseded by the requirement of supervision to comply with professional standards.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Audit guidelines
choices.justification: Audit guidelines exist to provide guidance on how to achieve compliance with professional standards. For example, they may provide insights on the purpose of supervision and examples of how supervisory duties are to be performed to achieve compliance with professional standards.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Audit methodology
choices.justification: An audit methodology is a well-configured process/procedure to achieve audit objectives. Although an audit methodology is a meaningful tool, supervision is generally driven by compliance with professional standards.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Professional standards
choices.justification: Professional standards from ISACA, The Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA) and the International Federation of Accountants (IFA) require supervision of audit staff to accomplish audit objectives and comply with competence, professional proficiency and documentation requirements, and more.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor notes daily reconciliation of visitor access card inventory is not aligned with the organization’s procedures. Which of the following is the auditor’s BEST course of action?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Do not report the lack of reconciliation.
choices.justification: Absence of discrepancy in physical count only confirms absence of any impact but cannot be a reason to overlook failure of operation of the control. The issue should be reported because the control was not followed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recommend regular physical inventory counts.
choices.justification: Although the information systems (IS) auditor may in some cases recommend a change in procedures, the primary goal is to observe and report when the current process is deficient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Report the lack of daily reconciliations.
choices.justification: The IS auditor should report the lack of daily reconciliation as an exception because a physical inventory count gives assurance only at a point in time and the practice is not in compliance with management-mandated activity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recommend the implementation of a more secure access system.
choices.justification: Although the IS auditor may in some cases recommend a more secure solution, the primary goal is to observe and report when the current process is deficient.

questions.text: What is the MAIN benefit of implementing a risk-based audit? A risk-based audit approach:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Links internal auditing to the enterprise’s overall risk management framework.
choices.justification: Linking internal audit to the enterprise’s overall risk management framework is one benefit derived from implementing risk-based audit, but it is not the main benefit. Risk-based audit refers to the enterprise’s risk management framework.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensures that risk, risk responses and actions are being properly classified and reported.
choices.justification: Classifying and reporting risk, responses and actions is not the main benefit; this is one of the outcomes of the risk-based audit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Helps to identify residual risk not in line with the risk appetite, so that appropriate action is being taken to treat the risk.
choices.justification: Identifying residual risk not in line with risk appetite is an outcome of the risk-based audit, but not the main benefit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Allows auditors to provide assurance to the board of directors that risk management processes are managing risk effectively in relation to the risk appetite.
choices.justification: The main benefit of implementing a risk-based audit is to assure senior management about the efficacy of risk management processes based on the outcome of the audit. Risk management processes, including the effectiveness of responses and the completion of actions, are being monitored by management to ensure that they continue to operate effectively.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST likely to be considered a conflict of interest for an information systems (IS) auditor who is reviewing a cybersecurity implementation?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delivering cybersecurity awareness training
choices.justification: Delivering cybersecurity awareness training is typically an operational responsibility, but it is not nearly as strong a conflict of interest as the auditor designing controls and then reviewing them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Designing the cybersecurity controls
choices.justification: If an auditor designs the controls, a conflict of interest arises in the neutrality of the auditor to address deficiencies during an audit. This is in violation of the ISACA Code of Ethics.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Advising on the cybersecurity framework
choices.justification: Part of the role of an information systems (IS) auditor can be to advise on a cybersecurity framework, provided that such advice does not rise to the level of designing specific controls that the auditor would later review.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conducting the vulnerability assessment
choices.justification: Conducting a vulnerability assessment can be the responsibility of the IS auditor and does not present a conflict of interest.

questions.text: Which of the following would MOST likely be used to establish the objectives and coverage of an audit?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prior audit reports
choices.justification: Although prior audit reports can give an idea of risk or deficiencies at a certain point in the past, they may not accurately represent the current state of the risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Business strategy
choices.justification: Understanding the business strategy can help the auditor to identify the type of risk that may impact the business but cannot be used to establish the audit objective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk assessment reports
choices.justification: Audit objectives and coverage should always be based on the risk. A risk-based approach for audit planning assists the auditor in determining the extent and nature of the type of testing. Risk assessment reports will best give the auditor a sense of the risk an enterprise faces.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Audit deliverables
choices.justification: Audit deliverables are the output of the audit and not something to be used in the initial planning.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST essential input required for a risk-based audit planning?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Internal controls and procedures description
choices.justification: Although all the answers are useful for risk-based audit planning, a description of internal controls and procedures is the most essential input required in planning control testing based on the risk being mitigated. The higher the risk, the more detailed testing required. This will help in determining the priority of the audit areas.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Action report on previous years’ audit findings
choices.justification: Repeat findings from previous audit reports may represent higher risk; however, that information is useful in reporting rather than planning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Information security policies and procedures
choices.justification: Security policies and procedures help in determining the security controls to be tested during the security audit planning and may not directly help in risk-based audit planning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Availability of resources and project timeline
choices.justification: The availability of resources and audit project timelines are required for risk-based audit planning. This information helps provide skilled resources for prioritized audit areas but is not the most essential input required.

questions.text: During a compliance audit of a small bank, the information systems (IS) auditor notes that the IT and accounting functions are being performed by the same user of the financial system. Which of the following reviews that are conducted by the user’s supervisor represents the BEST compensating control?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Audit trails that show the date and time of the transaction
choices.justification: An audit trail of only the date and time of the transaction is not sufficient to compensate for the risk of multiple functions being performed by the same individual.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A daily report with the total numbers and dollar amounts of each transaction
choices.justification: Review of the summary financial reports does not compensate for the separation of duties issue.

choices.label: C
choices.text: User account administration
choices.justification: Supervisor review of user account administration can be a good control; however, it may not detect inappropriate activities where a person fills multiple roles.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Computer log files that show individual transactions
choices.justification: Computer logs record the activities of individuals during their access to a computer system or data file and record any abnormal activities, such as the modification or deletion of financial data.

questions.text: Which of the following should be the FIRST step for the information systems (IS) auditor to take when planning for an audit?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Understand internal controls.
choices.justification: Understanding internal controls comes after information gathering.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform compliance tests.
choices.justification: Compliance and substantive tests are performed during the audit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gather information and plan.
choices.justification: The first step in planning an audit is gathering information to understand the business and industry (e.g., prior year audits, recent financial information, inherent risk assessments).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform substantive tests.
choices.justification: Compliance and substantive tests are performed during the audit.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST factor for determining the required extent of data collection during the planning phase of an information systems (IS) compliance audit?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Complexity of the organization’s operation
choices.justification: The complexity of the organization’s operation is a factor in the planning of an audit but does not directly affect the determination of how much data to collect. The extent of data collection is subject to the intensity, scope and purpose of the audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Findings and issues noted from the prior year
choices.justification: Prior findings and issues are factors in the planning of an audit but do not directly affect the determination of how much data to collect. Data must be collected outside of areas of previous findings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Purpose, objective and scope of the audit
choices.justification: The extent to which data will be collected during an information systems (IS) audit is related directly to the purpose, objective and scope of the audit. An audit with a narrow purpose and limited objective and scope is most likely to result in less data collection than an audit with a wider purpose and scope. Statistical analysis may also determine the extent of data collection, such as sample size or means of data collection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Auditor’s familiarity with the organization
choices.justification: An auditor’s familiarity with the organization is a factor in the planning of an audit but does not directly affect the determination of how much data to collect. The audit must be based on sufficient evidence of the monitoring of controls and not unduly influenced by the auditor’s familiarity with the organization.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST ensures the efficacy of risk-based auditing?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Risk analysis
choices.justification: Risk analysis is a subset of risk assessment and is used during audit planning to help identify risk and vulnerabilities so an information systems (IS) auditor can determine the controls needed to mitigate risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Risk mitigation
choices.justification: Risk mitigation applies appropriate controls to reduce the risk and should occur after conducting effective risk assessment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk assessment
choices.justification: Effective risk-based auditing uses risk assessments to identify, quantify and prioritize risk against criteria for risk acceptance and objectives relevant to the enterprise.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk treatment
choices.justification: Risk treatment is used to treat significant risk items not mitigated by existing controls and should happen after a successful risk assessment.

questions.text: When developing a risk-based audit strategy, an information systems (IS) auditor should conduct a risk assessment to ensure that:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Controls needed to mitigate risk are in place.
choices.justification: Determination of whether appropriate controls that are required to mitigate risk are in place is a result of an audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vulnerabilities and threats are identified.
choices.justification: While developing a risk-based audit strategy, it is critical that the risk and vulnerabilities are understood. They determine the areas to be audited and the extent of coverage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Audit risk is considered.
choices.justification: Audit risk is an inherent aspect of auditing, directly related to the audit process and not relevant to the risk analysis of the environment to be audited.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A gap analysis is appropriate.
choices.justification: A gap analysis is normally done to compare the actual state to an expected or desirable state.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor has been asked to audit the change management process in IT covering all operational systems. Which of the following documents will BEST aid the auditor in defining the scope for the audit project?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enterprise architecture
choices.justification: Because the objective covers the change management process for all IT systems, the auditor needs to understand the environment to define the audit scope. The enterprise architecture document is the best aid to use to accomplish this.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Control catalog
choices.justification: The control catalog is required for an auditor to plan the testing of controls, which is the next step after defining scope.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk register
choices.justification: The risk register is useful in planning the audit for determining systems to be audited on priority based on associated risk but not in defining the scope of the audit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: IT organizational chart
choices.justification: The IT organizational chart is useful for planning to understand the flow of process but is not the most helpful in determining the scope of the audit.

questions.text: When evaluating the collective effect of preventive, detective and corrective controls within a process, an information systems (IS) auditor should be aware of which of the following?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: The point at which controls are exercised as data flow through the system.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor should focus on when controls are exercised as data flow through a computer system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only preventive and detective controls are relevant.
choices.justification: Corrective controls may also be relevant because they allow an error or problem to be corrected.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Corrective controls are regarded as compensating.
choices.justification: Corrective controls remove or reduce the effects of errors or irregularities and are not exclusively regarded as compensating controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Classification allows an IS auditor to determine the controls that are missing.
choices.justification: The existence and function of controls are important but not the classification.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor wants to analyze audit trails on critical servers to discover potential anomalies in user or system behavior. Which of the following is the MOST suitable for performing that task?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Computer-aided software engineering tools
choices.justification: Computer-aided software engineering tools are used to assist in software development.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Embedded data collection tools
choices.justification: Embedded (audit) data collection software, such as systems control audit review file or systems audit review file, is used to provide sampling and production statistics, but not to conduct an audit log analysis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trend/variance detection tools
choices.justification: Trend/variance detection tools look for anomalies in user or system behavior, such as invoices with increasing invoice numbers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heuristic scanning tools
choices.justification: Heuristic scanning tools are a type of virus scanning used to indicate possible infected traffic.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviews one day of logs for a remotely managed server and finds one case where logging failed, and the backup restarts cannot be confirmed. What should the IS auditor do?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Issue an audit finding.
choices.justification: At this stage, it is too preliminary to issue an audit finding. Seeking an explanation from management is advisable, but it is better to gather additional evidence to properly evaluate the seriousness of the situation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Seek an explanation from IS management.
choices.justification: Without gathering more information on the incident and the frequency of the incident, it is difficult to obtain a meaningful explanation from management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Review the classifications of data held on the server.
choices.justification: A backup failure, which has not been established at this point, is serious if it involves critical data. However, the issue is not the importance of the data on the server, where a problem has been detected, but whether a systematic control failure that impacts other servers exists.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Expand the sample of logs reviewed.
choices.justification: IS Audit and Assurance Standards require that an IS auditor gather sufficient and appropriate audit evidence. The IS auditor found a potential problem and now needs to determine whether this is an isolated incident or a systematic control failure.

questions.text: An organization uses a bank to process its weekly payroll. Time sheets and payroll adjustment forms (e.g., hourly rate changes and terminations) are completed and delivered to the bank, which prepares the checks and reports for distribution. To BEST ensure payroll data accuracy:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Payroll reports should be compared to input forms.
choices.justification: The best way to confirm data accuracy, when input is provided by the organization and output is generated by the bank, is to verify the data input (input forms) with the results of the payroll reports.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gross payroll should be recalculated manually.
choices.justification: Recalculating gross payroll manually only verifies whether the processing is correct and not the data accuracy of inputs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Checks should be compared to input forms.
choices.justification: Comparing checks to input forms is not feasible because checks contain the processed information and input forms contain the input data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checks should be reconciled with output reports.
choices.justification: Reconciling checks with output reports only confirms that checks were issued as stated on output reports.

questions.text: During an audit, the information systems (IS) auditor notes that the application developer also performs quality assurance testing on another application. Which of the following is the MOST important course of action for the auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recommend compensating controls.
choices.justification: Although compensating controls may be a good idea, the primary response in this case should be to report the condition, because the risk associated with this should be reported to the users of the audit report.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Review the code created by the developer.
choices.justification: Evaluating the code created by the application developer is not the appropriate response in this case. The information systems (IS) auditor may evaluate a sample of changes to determine whether the developer tested his/her own code, but the primary response should be to report the condition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Analyze the quality assurance dashboards.
choices.justification: Analyzing the quality assurance dashboards can help evaluate the actual impact of the lack of separation of duties but does not address the underlying risk. The primary response should be to report the condition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Report the identified condition.
choices.justification: The software quality assurance role should be independent from development and development activities. The same person should not hold both roles, because this causes a separation of duties concern. The IS auditor should report this condition when identified.

questions.text: An internal information systems (IS) audit function is planning a general IS audit. Which of the following activities takes place during the FIRST step of the planning phase?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Developing an audit program
choices.justification: The results of the risk assessment are used for the input for the audit program.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defining the audit scope
choices.justification: The output of the risk assessment helps define the scope.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identifying key information owners
choices.justification: A risk assessment must be performed prior to identifying key information owners. Key information owners are generally not directly involved during the planning process of an audit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Developing a risk assessment
choices.justification: A risk assessment should be performed to determine how internal audit resources should be allocated to ensure that all material items will be addressed.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is conducting a compliance test to determine whether controls support management policies and procedures. The test will assist the IS auditor to determine:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: That the control is operating efficiently.
choices.justification: It is important that controls operate efficiently, but, in this case, the intent is to ensure that the controls support management policies and procedures. Therefore, the important issue is whether the controls are operating correctly and thereby meeting the control objective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: That the control is operating as designed.
choices.justification: Compliance tests can be used to test the existence and effectiveness of a defined process. Understanding the objective of a compliance test is important. Information systems (IS) auditors want reasonable assurance that the controls on which they are relying are effective. An effective control is one that meets management expectations and objectives.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The integrity of data controls.
choices.justification: Substantive tests, not compliance tests, are associated with data integrity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The reasonableness of financial reporting controls.
choices.justification: Determining the reasonableness of financial reporting controls is a very narrow answer because it is limited to financial reporting. It meets the objective of determining whether the controls are reasonable but does not ensure that the control is working correctly and thereby supporting management expectations and objectives.

questions.text: A PRIMARY benefit derived for an organization employing control self-assessment (CSA) techniques is that it:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Can identify high-risk areas that might need a detailed review later.
choices.justification: Control self-assessment (CSA) is predicated on the review of high-risk areas that either need immediate attention or may require a more thorough review later.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allows information systems (IS) auditors to independently assess risk.
choices.justification: CSA requires the involvement of information systems (IS) auditors and line management. The internal audit function shifts some of the control monitoring responsibilities to the functional areas.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Can be used as a replacement for traditional audits.
choices.justification: CSA is not a replacement for traditional audits. CSA is not intended to replace audit’s responsibilities, but to enhance them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Allows management to relinquish responsibility for control.
choices.justification: CSA does not allow management to relinquish its responsibility for control.

questions.text: During a security audit of IT processes, an information systems (IS) auditor finds that documented security procedures do not exist. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Create the procedures document based on the practices.
choices.justification: Information systems (IS) auditors should not prepare documentation, because the process may not be compliant with management objectives and doing so can jeopardize their independence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Issue an opinion of the current state and end the audit.
choices.justification: Ending the audit and issuing an opinion does not address identification of potential risk. The auditor should evaluate the practices in place. The recommendation may still be for the organization to develop written procedures. Terminating the audit may prevent achieving one of the basic audit objectives—identification of potential risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conduct compliance testing on available data.
choices.justification: Because there are no documented procedures, there is no basis against which to test compliance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identify and evaluate existing practices.
choices.justification: One of the main objectives of an audit is to identify potential risk; therefore, the most proactive approach is to identify and evaluate the existing security practices being followed by the organization and submit the findings and risk to management, with recommendations to document the current controls or enforce the documented procedures.

questions.text: When performing a risk analysis, the information systems (IS) auditor should FIRST:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Review the data classification program.
choices.justification: After the business objectives and the underlying systems are identified, the greatest degree of risk management effort should be focused on those assets containing data considered most sensitive to the organization. The data classification program assists the information systems (IS) auditor in identifying these assets.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identify the organization’s information assets.
choices.justification: The first step of the risk assessment process is to identify the systems and processes that support the business objectives because risk to those processes impacts the achievement of business goals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identify the inherent risk of the system.
choices.justification: Inherent risk is exposure without considering the actions that management has taken or might take. The purpose of a risk assessment is to identify vulnerabilities so that mitigating controls can be established. However, one must first understand the business and its supporting systems to best identify systems requiring the most risk assessment effort.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform a cost-benefit analysis for controls.
choices.justification: Designing and implementing controls to mitigate inherent risk of critical systems can only be performed after the above steps have been taken.

questions.text: The MOST appropriate action for an information systems (IS) auditor to take when shared user accounts are discovered is to:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inform the audit committee of the potential issue.
choices.justification: It is not appropriate for an information systems (IS) auditor to report findings to the audit committee before conducting a more detailed review and presenting them to management for a response.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Review audit logs for the IDs in question.
choices.justification: Review of audit logs would not be useful because shared IDs do not provide for individual accountability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the finding and explain the risk of using shared IDs.
choices.justification: An IS auditor’s role is to detect and document findings and control deficiencies. Part of the audit report is to explain the reasoning behind the findings. The use of shared IDs is not recommended because it does not allow for accountability of transactions. An IS auditor defers to management to decide how to respond to the findings presented.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Request that the IDs be removed from the system.
choices.justification: It is not the role of an IS auditor to request the removal of IDs from the system.

questions.text: Which of the following represents the GREATEST potential risk in an electronic data interchange (EDI) environment?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lack of transaction authorizations
choices.justification: Because the interaction between parties is electronic, there is no inherent authentication occurring; therefore, lack of transaction authorization is the greatest risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss or duplication of EDI transmissions
choices.justification: Loss or duplication of electronic data interchange transmissions is an example of risk, but because all transactions should be logged, the impact is not as great as that of unauthorized transactions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transmission delay
choices.justification: Transmission delays may terminate the process or hold the line until the normal time for processing has elapsed; however, there will be no loss of data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deletion or manipulation of transactions prior to, or after, establishment of application controls
choices.justification: Deletion or manipulation of transactions prior to, or after, establishment of application controls is an example of risk. Logging detects any alteration to the data, and the impact is not as great as that of unauthorized transactions.

questions.text: When developing a risk management program, what is the FIRST activity to be performed?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Threat assessment
choices.justification: The assets need to be identified first. A listing of the threats that can affect the assets is a later step in the process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Classification of data
choices.justification: Data classification is required for defining access controls and in criticality analysis, but the assets (including data) need be identified before doing classification.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inventory of assets
choices.justification: Identification of the assets to be protected is the first step in the development of a risk management program.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Criticality analysis
choices.justification: Criticality analysis is a later step in the process after the assets have been identified.

questions.text: Which of the following is in the BEST position to approve changes to the audit charter?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Board of directors
choices.justification: The board of directors does not need to approve the charter; it is best presented to the audit committee for approval.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Audit committee
choices.justification: The audit committee is a subgroup of the board of directors. The audit department should report to the audit committee and the audit charter should be approved by the committee.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Executive management
choices.justification: Executive management is not required to approve the audit charter and will not have the independence to approve the charter. The audit committee is in the best position to approve the charter because it is an independent and senior group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Director of internal audit
choices.justification: Although the director of internal audit may draft the charter and make changes, the audit committee should have the final approval of the charter.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds that the answers received during an interview with a payroll clerk do not support job descriptions and documented procedures. Under these circumstances, the IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Conclude that the controls are inadequate.
choices.justification: Based solely on the interview with the payroll clerk, the information systems (IS) auditor will not be able to collect evidence to determine the adequacy of existing controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Expand the scope to include substantive testing.
choices.justification: If the answers provided to an IS auditor’s questions are not confirmed by documented procedures or job descriptions, the IS auditor should expand the scope of testing the controls and include additional substantive tests.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place greater reliance on previous audits.
choices.justification: Placing greater reliance on previous audits is an inappropriate action because it provides no current knowledge of the adequacy of the existing controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Suspend the audit.
choices.justification: Suspending the audit is an inappropriate action because it provides no current knowledge of the adequacy of the existing controls.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor notes that failed login attempts to a core financial system are automatically logged and the logs are retained for a year by the organization. This logging is:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: An effective preventive control.
choices.justification: Generation of an activity log is not a preventive control because it cannot prevent inappropriate access.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A valid detective control.
choices.justification: Generation of an activity log is not a detective control because it does not help in detecting inappropriate access unless it is reviewed by appropriate personnel.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Not an adequate control.
choices.justification: Generation of an activity log is not a control by itself. It is the review of such a log that makes the activity a control (i.e., generation plus review equals control).

choices.label: D
choices.text: A corrective control.
choices.justification: Generation of an activity log is not a corrective control because it does not correct the effect of inappropriate access.

questions.text: When selecting audit procedures, an information systems (IS) auditor should use professional judgment to ensure that:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sufficient evidence will be collected.
choices.justification: Procedures are processes that an information systems (IS) auditor may follow in an audit engagement. In determining the appropriateness of any specific procedure, an IS auditor should use professional judgment that is appropriate to the specific circumstances. Professional judgment involves a subjective and often qualitative evaluation of conditions arising during an audit. Judgment addresses a grey area where binary (yes/no) decisions are not appropriate, and the IS auditor’s past experience plays a key role in making a judgment. The IS auditor should use judgment in assessing the sufficiency of evidence to be collected. ISACA guidelines provide information on how to meet the standards when performing IS audit work.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Significant deficiencies will be corrected within a reasonable period.
choices.justification: The correction of deficiencies is the responsibility of management and is not a part of the audit procedure selection process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: All material weaknesses will be identified.
choices.justification: Identifying material weaknesses is the result of appropriate competence, experience and thoroughness in planning and executing the audit, and not of professional judgment. Professional judgment is not a primary input to the financial aspects of the audit. Audit procedures and use of professional judgment cannot ensure that all deficiencies/weaknesses will be identified and corrected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Audit costs will be kept at a minimum level.
choices.justification: Professional judgment ensures that audit resources and costs are used wisely, but this is not the primary objective of the auditor when selecting audit procedures.

questions.text: Corrective action has been taken by an auditee immediately after the identification of a reportable finding. The information systems (IS) auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Include the finding in the final report because the IS auditor is responsible for an accurate report of all findings.
choices.justification: Including the finding in the final report is a generally accepted audit practice. If an action is taken after the audit started and before it ended, the audit report should identify the finding and describe the corrective action taken. An audit report should reflect the situation, as it existed at the start of the audit. All corrective actions taken by the auditee should be reported in writing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Not include the finding in the final report because management resolved the item.
choices.justification: The audit report should contain all relevant findings and the response from management even if the finding has been resolved. This means that subsequent audits may test for the continued resolution of the control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Not include the finding in the final report because corrective action can be verified by the IS auditor during the audit.
choices.justification: The audit report should contain the finding so that it is documented and the removal of the control subsequent to the audit is noticed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Include the finding in the closing meeting for discussion purposes only.
choices.justification: The audit report should contain the finding and resolution, and this can be mentioned in the final meeting. The audit report should list all relevant findings and the response from management.

questions.text: When preparing an audit report, the information systems (IS) auditor should ensure that the results are supported by:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Statements from IS management.
choices.justification: Statements from information systems (IS) management may be included in the audit analysis but these statements alone are not considered a sufficient basis for issuing a report.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Work papers of other auditors.
choices.justification: Work papers from other auditors may be used to substantiate and validate a finding but should not be used without the additional evidence of the work papers from the IS auditor who is preparing the report.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An organizational control self-assessment.
choices.justification: The results of a control self-assessment may assist the IS auditor in determining risk and compliance but on its own is not enough to support the audit report.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sufficient and appropriate audit evidence.
choices.justification: ISACA IS Audit and Assurance Reporting Standards require that the IS auditor has sufficient and appropriate audit evidence to support the reported results. Statements from IS management provide a basis for obtaining concurrence on matters that cannot be verified with empirical evidence. The report should be based on evidence that is collected during the review even though the IS auditor may have access to the work papers of other auditors. The results of an organizational control self-assessment can supplement the audit findings.

questions.text: In planning an information systems (IS) audit, the MOST critical step is the identification of the:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Areas of significant risk.
choices.justification: When designing a risk-based audit plan, it is important to identify the areas of highest risk to determine the areas to be audited.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Skill sets of the audit staff.
choices.justification: The skill sets of the audit staff should have been considered before deciding and selecting the audit. Where the skills are inadequate, the organization should consider using external resources.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Test steps in the audit.
choices.justification: Test steps for the audit are not as critical during the audit planning process as identifying the areas of risk that should be audited.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Time allotted for the audit.
choices.justification: The time allotted for an audit is determined during the planning process based on the areas to be audited and is primarily based on the requirement for conducting an appropriate audit.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST important skill that an information systems (IS) auditor should develop to understand the constraints of conducting an audit?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Managing audit staff
choices.justification: Managing audit staff is not the only aspect of conducting an audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allocating resources
choices.justification: Allocating resources, such as time and personnel, is needed for overall project management skills.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Project management
choices.justification: Audits often involve resource management, deliverables, scheduling and deadlines that are similar to project management good practices.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Attention to detail
choices.justification: Attention to detail is needed, but it is not a constraint of conducting audits.

questions.text: For which of the following controls would an information systems (IS) auditor look in an environment where duties cannot be appropriately segregated?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overlapping controls
choices.justification: Overlapping controls are two controls addressing the same control objective or exposure. Because primary controls cannot be achieved when duties cannot or are not appropriately segregated, it is difficult to install overlapping controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Boundary controls
choices.justification: Boundary controls establish the interface between the would-be user of a computer system and the computer system itself and are individual-based, not role-based, controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Access controls
choices.justification: Access controls for resources are based on individuals and not on roles. For a lack of separation of duties, the information systems (IS) auditor expects to find that a person has higher levels of access than are ideal. The IS auditor wants to find compensating controls to address this risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compensating controls
choices.justification: Compensating controls are internal controls that are intended to reduce the risk of an existing or potential control weakness that may arise when duties cannot be appropriately segregated.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes the objective of an information systems (IS) auditor discussing the audit findings with the auditee?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Communicate results to the auditee.
choices.justification: Based on this discussion, the information systems (IS) auditor will finalize the report and present the report to relevant levels of senior management after the findings are confirmed. This discussion should, however, also address a timetable for remediation of the audit findings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Develop timelines for the implementation of suggested recommendations.
choices.justification: This discussion informs management of the findings of the audit. Based on these discussions, management may agree to develop an implementation plan for the suggested recommendations, along with the timelines.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Confirm the findings and propose a course of corrective action.
choices.justification: Before communicating the results of an audit to senior management, the IS auditor should discuss the findings with the auditee. The goal of this discussion is to confirm the accuracy of the findings and to propose a course of corrective action.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identify compensating controls to the identified risk.
choices.justification: At the draft report stage, the IS auditor may recommend various controls to mitigate the risk, but the purpose of the meeting is to validate the findings of the audit with management.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST important for an information systems (IS) auditor to understand when auditing an ecommerce environment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: The technology architecture of the ecommerce environment
choices.justification: Understanding the technology architecture of the ecommerce environment is important; however, it is vital that the nature and criticality of the business process supported by the ecommerce application are well understood.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The policies, procedures and practices forming the control environment
choices.justification: Although the policies, procedure and practices that form the internal control environment need to be in alignment with the ecommerce environment, this is not the most important element that the information systems (IS) auditor needs to understand.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nature and criticality of the business processes supported by the application
choices.justification: The ecommerce application enables the execution of business transactions. Therefore, it is important to understand the nature and criticality of the business processes supported by the ecommerce application to identify specific controls to review.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Continuous monitoring of control measures for system availability and reliability
choices.justification: The availability of the ecommerce environment is important, but this is only one of the aspects to be considered with respect to business processes that are supported by the ecommerce application.

questions.text: Which of the following is evaluated as a preventive control by an information systems (IS) auditor performing an audit?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transaction logs
choices.justification: Transaction logs are a detective control and provide audit trails.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Before and after image reporting
choices.justification: Before and after image reporting makes it possible to trace the impact that transactions have on computer records. This is a detective control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Table lookups
choices.justification: Table lookups are preventive controls; input data are checked against predefined tables, which prevent any undefined data from being entered.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tracing and tagging
choices.justification: Tracing and tagging is used to test application systems and controls but is not a preventive control in itself.

questions.text: The MAIN purpose of the annual information systems (IS) audit plan is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Allocate resources for audits.
choices.justification: Because information systems (IS) audit assignments need to be accomplished with limited time and human resources, audits are scheduled and prioritized as determined by IS audit management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduce the impact of audit risk.
choices.justification: Audit risk is inherent to all audits, and the schedule has no bearing on the impact to audit risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Develop a training plan for auditors.
choices.justification: Developing a training plan for auditors is important, but it is not the main purpose of an IS audit plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Minimize the audit costs.
choices.justification: Minimizing the audit costs could be one of the objectives of an annual IS audit plan. However, this would be a result of ensuring audit resources are used effectively.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing the process of log monitoring wants to evaluate the organization’s manual review process. Which of the following audit techniques would the auditor MOST likely employ to fulfill this purpose?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inspection
choices.justification: Inspection is just one component of a walk-through and by itself does not supply enough information to provide a full understanding of the overall process and identify potential control weaknesses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inquiry
choices.justification: Inquiry provides only general information on how the control is executed. It does not necessarily enable the information systems (IS) auditor to determine whether the control performer has an in-depth understanding of the control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Walk-through
choices.justification: Walk-through procedures usually include a combination of inquiry, observation, inspection of relevant documentation and reperformance of controls. A walk-through of the manual log review process follows the manual log review process from start to finish to gain a thorough understanding of the overall process and identify potential control weaknesses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reperformance
choices.justification: Reperformance of the control is carried out by the IS auditor and does not provide assurance of the competency of the auditee.

questions.text: An organization’s information systems (IS) audit charter should specify the:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Plans for IS audit engagements.
choices.justification: Planning is the responsibility of audit management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Objectives and scope of IS audit engagements.
choices.justification: The objectives and scope of each information systems (IS) audit should be agreed on in an engagement letter. The charter specifies the objectives and scope of the audit function but not of individual engagements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Detailed training plan for the IS audit staff.
choices.justification: A training plan that is based on the audit plan should be developed by audit management.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Role of the IS audit function.
choices.justification: An IS audit charter establishes the role of the information systems audit function. The charter should describe the overall authority, scope and responsibilities of the audit function. The charter should be approved by the highest level of management and, if available, by the audit committee.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MAIN reason to perform a risk assessment in the planning phase of an information systems (IS) audit?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: To ensure that management concerns are addressed
