choices.justification: Management concerns have no bearing on the risk assessment process. If management has concerns and wants the auditor to focus on a certain area, the auditor should ensure adequate time is allocated to address the concerns.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To provide reasonable assurance that material items will be addressed
choices.justification: A risk assessment helps to focus the audit procedures on the highest risk areas included in the scope of the audit. The concept of reasonable assurance is also important.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To ensure that the audit team will perform audits within budget
choices.justification: A risk assessment is performed to determine where to place time and personnel resources, while budget constraints are limited to time resources.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To develop audit program and procedures needed to perform the audit
choices.justification: A risk assessment is not used in the development of the audit program and procedures. However, the risk assessment is used to allocate resources to audits.

questions.text: During a risk analysis, an information systems (IS) auditor identifies threats and potential impacts. Next, the IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure that the risk assessment is aligned to management’s risk assessment process.
choices.justification: An audit risk assessment is conducted for purposes that are different from management’s risk assessment process purposes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identify information assets and the underlying systems.
choices.justification: It is impossible to determine impact without first identifying the assets affected; therefore, this must already have been completed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Disclose the threats and impacts to management.
choices.justification: Upon completion of a risk assessment, an information systems (IS) auditor should describe and discuss with management the threats and potential impacts on the assets, and recommendations for addressing the risk. However, this action cannot be done until the controls are identified and the likelihood of the threat is calculated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identify and evaluate the existing controls.
choices.justification: It is important for an IS auditor to identify and evaluate the existence and effectiveness of existing and planned controls so that the risk level can be calculated after the potential threats and possible impacts are identified.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor discovers a potential material finding. The BEST course of action is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Report the potential finding to business management.
choices.justification: The item should be confirmed through additional testing before it is reported to management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discuss the potential finding with the audit committee.
choices.justification: The item should be confirmed through additional testing before it is discussed with the audit committee.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increase the scope of the audit.
choices.justification: Additional testing to confirm the potential finding should be within the scope of the engagement. Increasing the scope can demand more needed audit resources and can be subject to risk creep.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform additional testing.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor should perform additional testing to ensure that it is a finding. An auditor can quickly lose credibility if it is later discovered that the finding was not justified or accurate.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST effective for monitoring transactions exceeding predetermined thresholds?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Generalized audit software (GAS)
choices.justification: Generalized audit software (GAS) is a data analytics tool that can be used to filter large amounts of data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An integrated test facility
choices.justification: Integrated test facilities test the processing of the data and cannot be used to monitor real-time transactions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Regression tests
choices.justification: Regression tests are used to test new versions of software to ensure that previous changes and functionality are not inadvertently overwritten or disabled by the new changes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transaction snapshots
choices.justification: Gathering information through snapshots alone is not sufficient. GAS will assist with an analysis of the data.

questions.text: In a risk-based information systems (IS) audit, where both inherent and control risk have been assessed as high, an IS auditor would MOST likely compensate for this scenario by performing additional:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stop-or-go sampling.
choices.justification: Stop-or-go sampling is used when an information systems (IS) auditor believes few errors will be found in the population and, thus, is not the best type of testing to perform in this case.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Substantive testing.
choices.justification: Because both the inherent and control risk are high in this case, additional testing is required. Substantive testing obtains audit evidence on the completeness, accuracy, or existence of activities or transactions during the audit period.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compliance testing.
choices.justification: Compliance testing is evidence gathering for the purpose of testing an enterprise’s compliance with control procedures. Although performing compliance testing is important, performing additional substantive testing is more appropriate in this case.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discovery sampling.
choices.justification: Discovery sampling is a form of attribute sampling that is used to determine a specified probability of finding at least one example of an occurrence (attribute) in a population, typically used to test for fraud or other irregularities. In this case, additional substantive testing is the better option.

questions.text: Which of the following does a lack of adequate controls represent?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: An impact
choices.justification: Impact is the measure of the consequence (including financial loss, reputational damage and loss of customer confidence) that a threat event may have.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A vulnerability
choices.justification: The lack of adequate controls represents a vulnerability, exposing sensitive information and data to the risk of malicious damage, attack or unauthorized access by hackers, employee error, environmental threat, or equipment failure. This can result in a loss of sensitive information, financial loss, legal penalties or other losses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An asset
choices.justification: An asset is something of either tangible or intangible value worth protecting, including people, systems, infrastructure, finances and reputation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A threat
choices.justification: A threat is a potential cause of an unwanted incident.

questions.text: A centralized antivirus system determines whether each personal computer has the latest signature files and installs the latest signature files before allowing a PC to connect to the network. This is an example of a:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Directive control.
choices.justification: Directive controls, such as IT policies and procedures, do not apply in this case because this is an automated control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Corrective control.
choices.justification: Corrective controls are designed to correct errors, omissions and unauthorized uses and intrusions, when they are detected. This provides a mechanism to detect when malicious events happen and corrects the situation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compensating control.
choices.justification: A compensating control is used where other controls are not sufficient to protect the system. In this case, the corrective control in place effectively protects the system from access via an unpatched device.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Detective control.
choices.justification: Detective controls exist to detect and report when errors, omissions and unauthorized uses or entries occur.

questions.text: Which of the following would be MOST useful for an information systems (IS) auditor for accessing and analyzing digital data to collect relevant audit evidence from diverse software environments?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Structured Query Language (SQL)
choices.justification: Structured Query Language (SQL) provides options for auditors to query specific tables of a database according to audit objectives. However, skills are required to query specific databases, and a user must be able to understand the record structure to access the data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Application software reports
choices.justification: Reports from application software may be useful, but they are not as beneficial as computer-assisted auditing techniques (CAATs).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Data analytics controls
choices.justification: Data analytics controls might be a good technique to use for control testing, but they are not as comprehensive as CAATs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Computer-assisted auditing techniques (CAATs)
choices.justification: CAATs are tools used for accessing data in an electronic form from diverse software environments, record formats, etc. CAATs serve as useful tools for collecting and evaluating audit evidence according to audit objectives and can create efficiencies for collecting this evidence.

questions.text: Which of the following is the FIRST step in an IT risk assessment for a risk-based audit?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Identify all IT systems and controls that are relevant to audit objectives.
choices.justification: Understanding the business environment comes first; this is followed by understanding the IT environment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: List all controls from the audit program to select ones matching with audit objectives.
choices.justification: Listing controls and matching them to audit objectives is not the first step of risk assessment. This step follows understanding the business environment and the IT systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Review the results of a risk self-assessment.
choices.justification: A risk self-assessment is optional and applicable for some types of audit engagements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Understand the business, its operating model and key processes.
choices.justification: Risk-based auditing must be based on an understanding of the business, operating model and environment. This is the first step in an IT risk assessment for a risk-based audit.

questions.text: The MAIN advantage of an information systems (IS) auditor directly extracting data from a general ledger system is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduction of human resources needed to support the audit.
choices.justification: Although the burden on human resources to support the audit may decrease if the information systems (IS) auditor directly extracts the data, this advantage is not as significant as the increased data validity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduction in the time to have access to the information.
choices.justification: This will not necessarily reduce the time to have access to the information, because time will need to be scheduled for training and granting access.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Greater flexibility for the audit department.
choices.justification: There may be more flexibility for the IS auditor to adjust the data extracts to meet various audit requirements; however, this is not the main advantage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Greater assurance of data validity.
choices.justification: If the IS auditor executes the data extraction, there is greater assurance that the extraction criteria will not interfere with the required completeness, and, therefore, all required data will be collected. Asking IT to extract the data may expose the risk of filtering out exceptions that should be seen by the auditor. Also, if the IS auditor collects the data, all internal references correlating the various data tables/elements will be understood, and this knowledge may reveal vital elements to the completeness and correctness of the overall audit activity.

category: Quality Assurance and Improvement of Audit Process

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST important factor in ensuring the success of a new audit quality assurance (QA) program?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Using a systematic approach to develop the program
choices.justification: Using a systematic approach is one factor in developing the quality assurance (QA) program, but the most important factor is the commitment of the enterprise’s senior management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defining clear and measurable objectives and goals
choices.justification: Identifying the objective criteria is one factor in developing the quality assurance program, but the most important factor is the commitment of the enterprise’s senior management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensuring that continuous improvement efforts are embedded in the program
choices.justification: Continuous improvement is an important factor in developing the QA program, but the most important factor is the commitment of the enterprise’s senior management.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtaining commitment and support from senior management
choices.justification: The success of any QA program depends on the commitment of senior management. Without senior management support, the program is unlikely to be successful. Senior management must provide the resources necessary for the program to succeed and create a culture that supports continuous improvement. The most critical factor in guaranteeing the success of a new audit QA program is the commitment and support from the senior management.

questions.text: Which of the following is the PRIMARY objective of a quality assurance (QA) and improvement program for an audit process?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: To ensure that all audit findings are accepted, addressed and resolved on time
choices.justification: Although ensuring that audit findings are addressed and resolved on time is an important aspect of quality assurance (QA), it is not the primary objective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To provide a basis for evaluating the effectiveness and efficiency of the audit process
choices.justification: A QA and improvement program for an audit process is designed to evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of the audit process and identify areas for improvement. This can be achieved through various activities, such as conducting internal reviews and assessments, benchmarking against industry standards, and soliciting stakeholder feedback. The primary objective of a QA and improvement program for an audit process is to ensure that the audit activities are conducted consistently, accurately and efficiently. This involves confirming that the audit processes align with professional standards, regulatory requirements, and the enterprise’s policies and objectives.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To ensure that auditors are adequately trained, qualified and competent to perform audits
choices.justification: Ensuring that auditors are adequately trained and qualified is important but not the primary objective of a quality assurance and improvement program.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To establish standard procedures for conducting audits, analyzing evidence and reporting findings
choices.justification: Establishing standard procedures is important but not the primary objective of a quality assurance and improvement program.

questions.text: The success of a control self-assessment (CSA) depends highly on:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Line managers assuming a portion of the responsibility for control monitoring.
choices.justification: The primary objective of a control self-assessment (CSA) program is to leverage the internal audit function by shifting some of the control monitoring responsibilities to the functional area line managers. The success of a CSA program depends on the degree to which line managers assume responsibility for controls. This enables line managers to detect and respond to control errors promptly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assigning staff managers the responsibility for building controls.
choices.justification: CSA requires managers to participate in the monitoring of controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The implementation of a stringent control policy and rule-driven controls.
choices.justification: The implementation of stringent controls will not ensure controls are working correctly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The implementation of supervision and monitoring of controls of assigned duties.
choices.justification: Better supervision is a compensating and detective control and may assist in ensuring control effectiveness but would work best when used in a formal process such as CSA.

questions.text: Which of the following is a KEY benefit of a control self-assessment (CSA)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Management ownership of the internal controls supporting business objectives is reinforced.
choices.justification: The objective of control self-assessment (CSA) is to have business management become more aware of the importance of internal control and their responsibility in terms of corporate governance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Audit expenses are reduced when the assessment results are an input to external audit work.
choices.justification: Reducing audit expenses is not a key benefit of CSA.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fraud detection is improved because internal business staff are engaged in testing controls.
choices.justification: Improved fraud detection is important but not as important as control ownership. It is not a principal objective of CSA.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Internal auditors can shift to a consultative approach by using the results of the assessment.
choices.justification: CSA may give more insights to internal auditors, allowing them to take a more consultative role; however, this is an additional benefit, not the key benefit.

questions.text: An enterprise adopted a policy of periodic verification of IT assets through control self-assessment (CSA). Which of the following is the BEST approach for an information systems (IS) auditor of the enterprise?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: The IS auditor should actively participate in the CSA for asset verification.
choices.justification: The auditor should act as a facilitator, not an audit participant.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The IS auditor should suggest that the enterprise incentivize the CSA exercise.
choices.justification: Control self-assessment (CSA) should not be considered an extra activity and should not be linked with monetary benefits for motivation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The IS auditor should review the CSA outcome and look for other internal controls in place.
choices.justification: CSA alone may be mistaken as an audit function replacement; therefore, internal controls must exist and the IS auditor must look for them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The IS auditor should recuse from the CSA exercise, citing repetition/redundancy of work.
choices.justification: CSA cannot replace the audit function.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor uses computer-assisted audit techniques (CAATs) to collect and analyze data. Which of the following attributes of evidence is MOST affected by using CAATs?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Usefulness
choices.justification: Usefulness of audit evidence pulled by computer-assisted audit techniques (CAATs) is determined by the audit objective, and the use of CAATs does not have as direct of an impact on usefulness as reliability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reliability
choices.justification: Because the data are directly collected by the information systems (IS) auditor, the audit findings can be reported with an emphasis on the reliability of the records that are produced and maintained in the system. The reliability of the source of information used provides reassurance on the generated findings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relevance
choices.justification: Relevance of audit evidence pulled by CAATs is determined by the audit objective, and the use of CAATs does not have as direct of an impact on relevance as reliability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adequacy
choices.justification: Adequacy of audit evidence pulled by CAATs is determined by the processes and personnel who author the data, and the use of CAATs does not have any impact on competence.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST approach for an information systems (IS) auditor evaluating IT-related controls as a member of an integrated audit team?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Perform IT audits independently and submit findings to the audit manager to include in the final report.
choices.justification: The IT audit may be performed independently; however, describing the business impact will help the audit manager prepare meaningful and comprehensive audit reporting.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prioritize control testing based on the complexity of the technology implemented by the enterprise.
choices.justification: Prioritization of control testing based on the risk to the business is a better approach than just focusing on the complexity of implemented technology.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Include in the audit report the impact to the business due to weaknesses found in IT controls.
choices.justification: One of the objectives of an integrated audit is to provide added value to the auditee and improve the overall quality of the audit process. Describing the business impact due to weaknesses in IT-related controls best helps the auditee management in relating the deployment of IT with the business.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discuss the findings with the audit manager and afterwards submit the IT audit report to auditee’s management.
choices.justification: Discussing findings with the audit manager may be appropriate; however, it may not be effective unless the impact has been described in business terms.

questions.text: While reviewing a quality management system, the information systems (IS) auditor should PRIMARILY focus on collecting evidence to show that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Quality management systems comply with good practices.
choices.justification: Generally, good practices are adopted according to business requirements. Therefore, conforming to good practices may or may not be a requirement of the business.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Continuous improvement targets are being monitored.
choices.justification: Continuous and measurable improvement of quality is the primary requirement to achieve the business objective for the quality management system (QMS).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Standard operating procedures are updated annually.
choices.justification: Updating operating procedures is part of implementing the QMS; however, it must be part of change management and not an annual activity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Key performance indicators are defined.
choices.justification: Key performance indicators may be defined in a QMS, but they are of little value if they are not being monitored.

questions.text: Which of the following sampling methods is the MOST effective to determine whether purchase orders issued to vendors have been authorized as per the authorization matrix?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Variable sampling
choices.justification: Variable sampling is the method used for substantive testing, which involves testing transactions for quantitative aspects such as monetary values.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stratified mean per unit
choices.justification: Stratified mean per unit is used in variable sampling.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Attribute sampling
choices.justification: Attribute sampling is the method used for compliance testing. In this scenario, the operation of a control is being evaluated, and therefore, the attribute of whether each purchase order was correctly authorized would be used to determine compliance with the control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unstratified mean per unit
choices.justification: Unstratified mean per unit is used in variable sampling.

questions.text: As part of standardizing the audit functions of different subsidiaries, the chief internal auditor has asked each audit team to use the already developed comprehensive audit manual. This manual was written with large, decentralized audit teams in mind. One of the subsidiaries has a small internal audit function consisting of a supervisor and two junior auditors. What is the BEST approach in providing administrative guidance for this small audit team?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use informal supervisory directions for audit engagement management issues.
choices.justification: It is inappropriate to leave the small audit team with only informal supervisory directions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Select critical administrative processes from the audit manual and use verbal directions for other engagement management issues.
choices.justification: The small audit team should adopt key procedures from the audit manual as it starts to absorb the detailed guidance. The form and content of procedures are dependent upon the size and structure of the internal audit activity and the complexity of its work. A small internal audit function may be managed informally, for example, through daily close supervision and discussions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continue using the administrative procedures already being followed by the internal auditors of that subsidiary.
choices.justification: Standardization efforts require that all the administrative activities of audit functions be consistent for each subsidiary.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use processes from the already developed manual as written.
choices.justification: Being a small audit team, complete reliance on the existing manual would require more formal management and may be overkill in terms of efficiency of the audit work.

questions.text: While auditing database logs for a client, an information systems (IS) auditor needs to verify their redundant backup on the cloud. Which of the following is the BEST strategy?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inform the cloud service provider about the needed verification and obtain cloud logs.
choices.justification: The cloud service provider needs the client’s authentication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ignore the backup on the cloud because it is already a verbatim copy.
choices.justification: The cloud backup cannot be considered a verbatim copy until verification.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Consider the cloud backup in the next phase of the audit.
choices.justification: Local and remote logs should be compared simultaneously.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inform the client about the suggested modification in the original project plan.
choices.justification: The audit engagement project plan should be updated and changed as necessary (with appropriate approvals by IT audit and assurance management) during the audit engagement.

questions.text: The IS auditor’s report should recommend that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: The deputy CEO is censured for failure to approve the plan.
choices.justification: Censuring the deputy CEO will not improve the current situation and is generally not within the scope of an information systems (IS) auditor to recommend.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A group of senior managers is set up to review the existing plan.
choices.justification: Establishing a group to review the disaster recovery plan (DRP), which is two years out of date, may achieve an updated DRP but is not likely to be a speedy operation; issuing the existing DRP would be imprudent without first ensuring that it is workable.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The existing plan is approved and circulated to all key management and staff.
choices.justification: The current DRP may be unacceptable or ineffective and recommending the approval of the DRP may be unwise. The best way to develop a DRP in a short time is to make an experienced manager responsible for coordinating the knowledge of other managers into a single, formal document within a defined time limit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A manager coordinates the creation of a new or revised plan within a defined time limit.
choices.justification: The primary concern is to establish a workable DRP that reflects current processing volumes to protect the organization from any disruptive incident.

questions.text: Information systems (IS) audit objectives MAINLY focus on:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assuring compliance with business legal and regulatory requirements.
choices.justification: To ensure that compliance risk is reduced and the information assets are protected, relevant information systems (IS) controls should be reviewed in terms of their objectives and effectiveness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensuring that information systems (IS) controls exist to minimize business risk to an acceptable level and function as expected.
choices.justification: The main focus of IS audit objectives is to confirm whether IS controls are well-designed and effective to reduce business risk to an acceptable level.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reviewing the IS general controls’ objectives and whether those controls operate effectively.
choices.justification: IS audit objectives include reviewing different types of IS controls, not only general controls, and their objectives and effectiveness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determining if there is potential risk to the organization’s IS assets and finding ways to minimize that risk.
choices.justification: The main focus of the risk management process is determining potential risk. IS audit objectives focus on IS controls placed to reduce business risk to an acceptable level and whether those controls are effective and function as expected.

questions.text: The MOST important reason for an information systems (IS) auditor to obtain sufficient and appropriate audit evidence is to:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Comply with regulatory requirements.
choices.justification: Complying with regulatory requirements is relevant to an audit but is not the most important reason why sufficient and relevant evidence is required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide a basis for drawing reasonable conclusions.
choices.justification: The scope of an information systems (IS) audit is defined by its objectives. This involves identifying control weaknesses relevant to the scope of the audit. Obtaining sufficient and appropriate evidence assists the auditor in not only identifying control weaknesses but also documenting and validating them.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensure complete audit coverage.
choices.justification: Ensuring coverage is relevant to conducting an IS audit but is not the most important reason why sufficient and relevant evidence is required. The reason for obtaining evidence is to ensure that the audit conclusions are factual and accurate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform the audit according to the defined scope.
choices.justification: The execution of an audit to meet its defined scope is relevant to an audit but is not the reason why sufficient and relevant evidence is required.

questions.text: What is the MAJOR benefit of conducting a control self-assessment (CSA) over a traditional audit?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: It detects risk sooner.
choices.justification: Control self-assessments (CSAs) require employees to assess the control stature of their own function. CSAs help to increase the understanding of business risk and internal controls. Because they are conducted more frequently than audits, CSAs help to identify risk in a timelier manner.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It replaces the internal audit function.
choices.justification: CSAs do not replace the internal audit function; an audit must still be performed to ensure that controls are present.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It reduces the audit workload.
choices.justification: CSAs may not reduce the audit function’s workload and are not a major difference between the two approaches.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It reduces audit resource requirements.
choices.justification: CSAs do not affect the need for audit resources. Although the results of the CSA may serve as a reference point for the audit process, they do not affect the scope or depth of audit work that needs to be performed.

questions.text: During an information systems (IS) audit, which is the BEST method for an IS auditor to evaluate the implementation of separation of duties within an IT department?
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discuss with the IT managers.
choices.justification: Discussing the implementation of separation of duties with the IT managers is the best way to determine how responsibilities are assigned within the department.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Review the IT job descriptions.
choices.justification: Job descriptions may not be the best source of information because they can be outdated, or what is documented in the job descriptions may be different from what is performed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Research past IT audit reports.
choices.justification: Past information systems (IS) audit reports are not the best source of information because they may not accurately describe how IT responsibilities are assigned.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evaluate the organizational structure.
choices.justification: Evaluating the organizational structure may give a limited view on the allocation of IT responsibilities. The responsibilities also may have changed over time.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds that a disaster recovery plan (DRP) for critical business functions does not cover all systems. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate course of action for the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alert management and evaluate the impact of not covering all systems.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor should make management aware that some systems are omitted from the disaster recovery plan (DRP). An IS auditor should continue the audit and include an evaluation of the impact of not including all systems in the DRP.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cancel the audit.
choices.justification: Canceling the audit is an inappropriate action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Complete the audit of the systems covered by the existing DRP.
choices.justification: Ignoring the fact that some systems are not covered violates audit standards that require reporting all material findings and is an inappropriate action.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Postpone the audit until the systems are added to the DRP.
choices.justification: Postponing the audit is an inappropriate action. The audit should be completed according to the initial scope with identification to management of the risk of systems not being covered.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is assigned to review an enterprise’s network; however, the auditor is not comfortable with executing a vulnerability assessment. Which of the following would be the BEST approach for the auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Request a change of scope to exclude a vulnerability assessment.
choices.justification: Reducing scope by excluding the vulnerability assessment will impact the achievement of the audit objectives and is, therefore, not a viable option.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hire independent experts with consent from the authorities.
choices.justification: Practitioners who do not have the required competencies to perform the audit engagement, either in whole or in part, should seek assistance from independent experts with consent from the authorities who signed the audit charter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refuse to conduct the audit due to a competency issue.
choices.justification: Refusing to conduct an audit or requesting to pass it on to another auditor is an option but not as good as hiring an independent expert.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proceed to conduct vulnerability assessment with limited skills and lack of needed competencies.
choices.justification: Conducting a vulnerability assessment without needed skills and competencies is against professional ethics.

questions.text: Which of the following forms of evidence does an information systems (IS) auditor consider the MOST reliable?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: An oral statement from the auditee
choices.justification: An oral statement from the auditee is audit evidence but not as reliable as the results of a test that is performed by an external information systems (IS) auditor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The results of a test that is performed by an external information systems (IS) auditor
choices.justification: An independent test that is performed by an IS auditor should always be considered a more reliable source of evidence than a confirmation letter from a third party, because the letter is the result of an analysis of the process and may not be based on authoritative audit techniques. An audit should consist of a combination of inspection, observation and inquiry by an IS auditor as determined by risk. This provides a standard methodology and reasonable assurance that the controls and test results are accurate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An internally generated computer accounting report
choices.justification: An internally generated computer accounting report is audit evidence but is not as reliable as the results of a test performed by an external IS auditor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A confirmation letter that is received from an outside source
choices.justification: An independent test performed by an IS auditor should always be considered a more reliable source of evidence than a confirmation letter from a third party, because a letter is subjective and may not have been generated as a part of an authoritative audit or conform to audit standards.

questions.text: When evaluating the controls of an electronic data interchange (EDI) application, an information systems (IS) auditor should PRIMARILY be concerned with the risk of:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excessive transaction turnaround time.
choices.justification: An excessive turnaround time is an inconvenience, but not a serious risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Application interface failure.
choices.justification: The failure of the application interface is a risk, but not the most serious issue. Usually such a problem is temporary and easily fixed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Improper transaction authorization.
choices.justification: Foremost among the risk associated with electronic data interchange (EDI) is improper transaction authorization. Because the interaction with the parties is electronic, there is no inherent authentication. Improper authentication poses a serious risk of financial loss.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Non-validated batch totals.
choices.justification: The integrity of EDI transactions is important, but not as significant as the risk of unauthorized transactions.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing a monthly accounts payable transaction register using audit software. Why would the auditor be interested in using a check digit?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: To detect data transposition errors
choices.justification: A check digit is a numeric value added to data to ensure that original data are correct and have not been altered.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To ensure that transactions do not exceed predetermined amounts
choices.justification: Ensuring that data have not exceeded a predetermined amount is a limit check.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To ensure that data entered are within reasonable limits
choices.justification: Ensuring that data entered are within predetermined reasonable limits is a reasonableness check.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To ensure that data entered are within a predetermined range of values
choices.justification: Ensuring that data entered are within a predetermined range of values is a range check.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is planning to evaluate the control design effectiveness that is related to an automated billing process. Which of the following is the MOST effective approach for the auditor to adopt?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interview
choices.justification: An interview is not as strong evidence as an observation or walk-throughs. In addition, personnel might add some bias to interviews if they know they are being interviewed for an audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inquiry
choices.justification: Inquiry can be used to understand the controls in a process only if it is accompanied by verification of evidence. However, interviewees might be biased if they know they are being audited.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reperformance
choices.justification: Reperformance is used to evaluate the operating effectiveness of the control rather than the design of the control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Walk-through
choices.justification: Walk-throughs involve a combination of inquiry and inspection of evidence with respect to business process controls. This is the most effective basis for evaluation of the design of the control because it confirms that the control actually exists.

questions.text: After identifying the findings, the information systems (IS) auditor should FIRST:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gain agreement on the findings.
choices.justification: If findings are not agreed upon and confirmed by both parties, then there may be an issue during sign-off on the final audit report or while discussing findings with management. When an agreement is obtained with the auditee, it implies that the finding is understood and a clear plan of action can be determined.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Determine mitigation measures for the findings.
choices.justification: Although the auditor may recommend mitigation measures, the organization ultimately decides and implements the mitigation strategies as a function of risk management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inform senior management of the findings.
choices.justification: Before senior management is informed, it is imperative that the auditor informs the auditee and gains agreement on the audit findings to correctly communicate the risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain remediation deadlines to close the findings.
choices.justification: Obtaining remediation deadlines to close the findings is not the first step in communicating the audit findings.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor identifies a business process to be audited. The IS auditor should NEXT identify the:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most valuable information assets. B.IS audit resources to be deployed.
choices.justification: All assets need to be identified, not just information assets. To determine the key information assets to be audited, the information systems (IS) auditor should first determine which control objectives and key control activities should be validated. Only information assets that are related to the control objectives and key control activities are relevant for scoping the audit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Auditee personnel to be interviewed.
choices.justification: Only after determining the key control activities to be validated can the IS auditor identify the relevant process personnel who should be interviewed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Control objectives and activities.
choices.justification: After the business process is identified, the IS auditor should first identify the control objectives and activities associated with the business process that should be validated in the audit.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST important to ensure that effective application controls are maintained?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exception reporting
choices.justification: Exception reporting only looks at errors or problems but will not ensure that controls are still working.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Manager oversight
choices.justification: Manager oversight is important but may not be a consistent or well-defined process compared to control self-assessment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Control self-assessment (CSA)
choices.justification: Control self-assessment (CSA) is the review of business objectives and internal controls in a formal and documented collaborative process. It includes testing the design of automated application controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Peer reviews
choices.justification: Peer reviews lack the direct involvement of audit specialists and management.

questions.text: Which of the following is an attribute of the control self-assessment (CSA) approach?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Broad stakeholder involvement
choices.justification: The control self-assessment (CSA) approach emphasizes management of and accountability for developing and monitoring the controls of an organization’s business processes. The attributes of CSA include empowered employees, continuous improvement, extensive employee participation and training—all of which are representations of broad stakeholder involvement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Auditors are the primary control analysts
choices.justification: Information systems (IS) auditors are the primary control analysts in a traditional audit approach. CSA involves many stakeholders, not just auditors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Limited employee participation
choices.justification: Limited employee participation is an attribute of a traditional audit approach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Policy-driven
choices.justification: Policy-driven is an attribute of a traditional audit approach.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor wants to determine the effectiveness of managing user access to a server room. Which of the following is the BEST evidence of effectiveness?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Observation of a logged event
choices.justification: Observation of the process to reset an employee’s security access to the server room and the subsequent logging of this event provide the best evidence of the adequacy of the physical security control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Review of the procedure manual
choices.justification: Although reviewing the procedure manual can be helpful in gaining an overall understanding of a process, it is not evidence of the effectiveness of the execution of a control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Interview with management
choices.justification: Although interviewing management can be helpful in gaining an overall understanding of a process, it is not evidence of the effectiveness of the execution of a control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Interview with security personnel
choices.justification: Although interviewing security personnel can be helpful in gaining an overall understanding of a process, it is not evidence of the effectiveness of the execution of a control.

questions.text: An external information systems (IS) auditor discovers that systems in the scope of the audit were implemented by an associate. In such a circumstance, IS audit management should:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Remove the IS auditor from the engagement.
choices.justification: It is not necessary to withdraw the information systems (IS) auditor unless there is a statutory limitation, which exists in certain countries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cancel the engagement.
choices.justification: Canceling the engagement is not required if properly disclosed and accepted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Disclose the issue to the client.
choices.justification: In circumstances in which the IS auditor’s independence is impaired and the IS auditor continues to be associated with the audit, the facts surrounding the issue of the IS auditor’s independence should be disclosed to the appropriate management and in the report.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Take steps to restore the IS auditor’s independence.
choices.justification: This is not a feasible solution. The independence of the IS auditor cannot be restored while continuing to conduct the audit.

questions.text: Which of the following would MOST likely help an enterprise determine its scope related to the selection and implementation of information systems (IS) security standards?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Select a standard that most closely covers the regulatory requirements of the enterprise.
choices.justification: Complying with regulatory requirements is important, but this is not the only requirement for choosing a standard for the enterprise.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remove the clauses of the selected standard that are not relevant to the enterprise.
choices.justification: The enterprise should only choose the relevant portion of the standards that relate to implementation requirements that ensure the security posture of the enterprise.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Change the clauses of the selected standard that are relevant to the enterprise.
choices.justification: Changing clauses of a standard may cause the elimination of some mandatory requirements for ensuring the security environments of the enterprise.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determine the internal penalties of noncompliance with the selected standard.
choices.justification: Deciding on internal penalties for noncompliance may not be a good choice for selecting a standard for an enterprise.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds a small number of user access requests that were not authorized by managers through the normal predefined workflow steps and escalation rules. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Perform an additional analysis.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor needs to perform substantive testing and additional analysis to determine why the approval and workflow processes are not working as intended. Before making any recommendation, the IS auditor should gain a good understanding of the scope of the problem and the factors that caused this incident. The IS auditor should identify whether the issue was caused by managers not following procedures, a problem with the workflow of the automated system or a combination of the two.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Report the problem to the audit committee.
choices.justification: The IS auditor does not yet have enough information to report the problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conduct a security risk assessment.
choices.justification: Changing the scope of the IS audit or conducting a security risk assessment requires more detailed information about the processes and violations being reviewed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recommend that the owner of the identity management system fix the workflow issues.
choices.justification: The IS auditor must first determine the root cause and impact of the findings and does not have enough information to recommend fixing the workflow issues.

questions.text: In the process of evaluating program change controls, an information systems (IS) auditor uses source code comparison software to:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Examine source program changes without information from IS personnel.
choices.justification: When an information systems (IS) auditor uses a source code comparison to examine source program changes without information from IS personnel, the IS auditor has an objective, independent and relatively complete assurance of program changes because the source code comparison identifies the changes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Detect a source program change made between acquiring a copy of the source and the comparison run.
choices.justification: The changes detected by the source code comparison are between two versions of the software. This does not detect changes made since the acquisition of the copy of the software.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Confirm that the control copy is the current version of the production program.
choices.justification: This is a function of library management, not source code comparison. An IS auditor gains this assurance separately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure that all changes made in the current source copy are tested.
choices.justification: Source code comparison detects all changes between an original and a changed program; however, the comparison will not ensure that the changes have been adequately tested.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing access to an application to determine whether recently added accounts were appropriately authorized. This is an example of:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Variable sampling.
choices.justification: Variable sampling is used to estimate numerical values, such as dollar values.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Substantive testing.
choices.justification: Substantive testing substantiates the integrity of actual processing, such as balances on financial statements. The development of substantive tests is often dependent on the outcome of compliance tests. If compliance tests indicate that internal controls are adequate, then substantive tests can be minimized.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compliance testing.
choices.justification: Compliance testing determines whether controls are being applied in compliance with policy. This includes tests to determine whether new accounts were appropriately authorized.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stop-or-go sampling.
choices.justification: Stop-or-go sampling allows a test to be stopped as early as possible and is not appropriate for checking whether procedures have been followed.

questions.text: Which of the following impairs the independence of a quality assurance (QA) team?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensuring compliance with development methods
choices.justification: Ensuring compliance with development methods is a valid quality assurance (QA) function.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Checking the test assumptions
choices.justification: Checking the test assumptions is a valid QA function.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Correcting coding errors during the testing process
choices.justification: Correction of code should not be a responsibility of the QA team, because it does not ensure separation of duties and impairs the team’s independence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checking the code to ensure proper documentation
choices.justification: Checking the code to ensure proper documentation is a valid QA function.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor wants to determine the number of purchase orders that are not appropriately approved. Which of the following sampling techniques should an IS auditor use to make such a conclusion?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Attribute
choices.justification: Attribute sampling is used to test compliance of transactions to controls—in this instance, the existence of appropriate approval.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Variable
choices.justification: Variable sampling is used in substantive testing situations and deals with population characteristics that vary, such as monetary values and weights.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stop-or-go
choices.justification: Stop-or-go sampling is used when the expected occurrence rate is extremely low.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Judgment
choices.justification: Judgment sampling is not relevant here. It refers to a subjective approach of determining sample size and selection criteria of elements of the sample.

questions.text: While evaluating software development practices in an organization, an information systems (IS) auditor notes that the quality assurance (QA) function reports to project management. The MOST important concern for an IS auditor is the:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Effectiveness of the QA function because it should interact between project management and user management.
choices.justification: To be effective, the quality assurance (QA) function should be independent of project management. If it is not, project management may put pressure on the QA function to approve an inadequate product.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Efficiency of the QA function because it should interact with the project implementation team.
choices.justification: The efficiency of the QA function is not impacted by interacting with the project implementation team. The QA team does not release a product for implementation until it meets QA requirements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Effectiveness of the project manager because the project manager should interact with the QA function.
choices.justification: The project manager responds to the issues raised by the QA team. This does not impact the effectiveness of the project manager.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Efficiency of the project manager because the QA function needs to communicate with the project implementation team.
choices.justification: The QA function interaction with the project implementation team should not impact the efficiency of the project manager.

questions.text: To ensure that audit resources deliver the best value to the organization, the FIRST step in an audit project is to:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Schedule the audits and monitor the time spent on each audit.
choices.justification: Monitoring the audits and the time spent on audits is not effective if the wrong areas are being audited. It is most important to develop a risk-based audit plan to ensure effective use of audit resources.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Train the information systems (IS) audit staff on current technology used in the organization.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor may have specialties, or the audit team may rely on outside experts to conduct very specialized audits. It is not necessary for each IS auditor to be trained on all new technology.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Develop the audit plan based on a detailed risk assessment.
choices.justification: Although monitoring the time and audit programs, and adequate training improve the IS audit staff’s productivity (efficiency and performance), ensuring that the resources and efforts being dedicated to audit are focused on higher-risk areas delivers value to the organization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monitor progress of audits and initiate cost control measures.
choices.justification: Monitoring audits and initiating cost controls does not ensure the effective use of audit resources.

questions.text: While reviewing sensitive electronic work papers, the information systems (IS) auditor notices that they were not encrypted. This could compromise the:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Audit trail of the versioning of the work papers.
choices.justification: Audit trails do not, by themselves, affect confidentiality, but are part of the reason for requiring encryption.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Approval of the audit phases.
choices.justification: Audit phase approvals do not, by themselves, affect the confidentiality of the work papers, but are part of the reason for requiring encryption.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Access rights to the work papers.
choices.justification: Access to the work papers should be limited by need to know; however, a lack of encryption breaches the confidentiality of the work papers, not the access rights to the papers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Confidentiality of the work papers.
choices.justification: Encryption provides confidentiality for electronic work papers.

questions.text: An audit charter should:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Be dynamic and change to coincide with the changing nature of technology and the audit profession.
choices.justification: The audit charter should not be subject to changes in technology and should not significantly change over time. The charter should be approved at the highest level of management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clearly state the audit objectives and delegation of authority for the maintenance and review of internal controls.
choices.justification: An audit charter states the authority and reporting requirements for the audit but not the details of maintenance of internal controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Document the audit procedures designed to achieve the planned audit objectives.
choices.justification: An audit charter is not at a detailed level and, therefore, does not include specific audit objectives or procedures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Outline the overall authority, scope and responsibilities of the audit function.
choices.justification: An audit charter should state management objectives for, and delegation of authority to, information systems (IS) auditors.

questions.text: While planning an information systems (IS) audit, an assessment of risk should be made to provide:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reasonable assurance that the audit will cover material items.
choices.justification: ISACA Information Systems (IS) Audit and Assurance Performance Guideline 2201 (Risk Assessment in Planning) states that the applied risk assessment approach should help with the prioritization and scheduling process of the IS audit and assurance work. The risk assessment should support the selection process of areas and items of audit interest and the decision process to design and conduct particular IS audit engagements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Definite assurance that material items will be covered during the audit work.
choices.justification: Definite assurance that material items will be covered during the audit work is an impractical proposition.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reasonable assurance that all items will be covered by the audit.
choices.justification: Reasonable assurance that all items will be covered during the audit work is not the correct answer, because primarily material items need to be covered, not all items.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sufficient assurance that all items will be covered during the audit work.
choices.justification: Sufficient assurance that all items will be covered is not as important as ensuring that the audit will cover all material items.

questions.text: During an application software review, an information systems (IS) auditor identified minor weaknesses in a relevant database environment that is out of scope for the audit. The BEST option is to:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Include a review of the database controls in the scope.
choices.justification: Executing audits and reviews outside the scope is not advisable. In this case, the weaknesses identified are considered to be a minor issue, and it is sufficient to report the issue and address it at a later time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document for future review.
choices.justification: In this case, the weaknesses identified are considered to be a minor issue. The information systems (IS) auditor should formally report the weaknesses as an observation rather than documenting it to address during a future audit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Work with database administrators to correct the issue.
choices.justification: It is not appropriate for the IS auditor to work with database administrators to correct the issue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Report the weaknesses as observed.
choices.justification: Any weakness noticed should be reported, even if it is outside the scope of the current audit. Weaknesses identified during an application software review need to be reported to management.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is comparing equipment in production with inventory records. This type of testing is an example of:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Substantive testing.
choices.justification: Substantive testing obtains audit evidence on the completeness, accuracy or existence of activities or transactions during the audit period.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compliance testing.
choices.justification: Compliance testing is evidence gathering for the purpose of testing an enterprise’s compliance with control procedures. This differs from substantive testing in which evidence is gathered to evaluate the integrity of individual transactions, data or other information.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Analytical testing.
choices.justification: Analytical testing evaluates the relationship of two sets of data and discerns inconsistencies in the relationship.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Control testing.
choices.justification: Control testing is the same as compliance testing.

questions.text: An appropriate control for ensuring the authenticity of orders received in an electronic data interchange system application is to:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acknowledge receipt of electronic orders with a confirmation message.
choices.justification: Acknowledging the receipt of electronic orders with a confirming message is good practice but will not authenticate orders from customers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform reasonableness checks on quantities ordered before filling orders.
choices.justification: Performing reasonableness checks on quantities ordered before placing orders is a control for ensuring the correctness of the orders, not the authenticity of its customer orders.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Verify the identity of senders and determine if orders correspond to contract terms.
choices.justification: An electronic data interchange system is subject not only to the usual risk exposures of computer systems but also to those arising from the potential ineffectiveness of controls on the part of the trading partner and the third-party service provider, making authentication of users and messages a major security concern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encrypt electronic orders.
choices.justification: Encrypting sensitive messages is an appropriate step but does not prove authenticity of messages received.

questions.text: During the planning stage of an information systems (IS) audit, the PRIMARY goal of an IS auditor is to:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Address audit objectives.
choices.justification: ISACA Information Systems (IS) Audit and Assurance Standards require that an IS auditor plan the audit work to address the audit objectives. The activities described in the other options are all undertaken to address audit objectives and, thus, are secondary.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Collect sufficient evidence.
choices.justification: The IS auditor does not collect evidence in the planning stage of an audit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Specify appropriate tests.
choices.justification: Specifying appropriate tests is not the primary goal of audit planning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Minimize audit resources.
choices.justification: Effective use of audit resources is a goal of audit planning, not minimizing audit resources.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor who was involved in designing an organization’s business continuity plan (BCP) has been assigned to audit the plan. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-audit follow up to evaluate whether identified risk has been sufficiently addressed

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decline the assignment.
choices.justification: Declining the assignment can be acceptable only after obtaining management approval or it is appropriately disclosed to management, audit management and other stakeholders.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inform management of the possible conflict of interest after completing the audit assignment.
choices.justification: Approval should be obtained prior to commencement and not after the completion of the assignment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inform the BCP team of the possible conflict of interest prior to beginning the assignment.
choices.justification: Informing the business continuity plan (BCP) team of the possible conflict of interest prior to starting the assignment is not the correct answer because the BCP team does not have the authority to decide on this issue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Confirm the possibility of conflict of interest to audit management prior to starting the assignment.
choices.justification: A possible conflict of interest, likely to affect the information systems (IS) auditor’s independence, should be brought to the attention of management prior to starting the assignment.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is determining the appropriate sample size for testing the existence of program change approvals. Previous audits did not indicate any exceptions, and management has confirmed that no exceptions have been reported for the review period. In this context, the IS auditor can adopt a:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lower confidence coefficient, resulting in a smaller sample size.
choices.justification: When internal controls are strong, a lower confidence coefficient can be adopted, which will enable the use of a smaller sample size.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Higher confidence coefficient, resulting in a smaller sample size.
choices.justification: A higher confidence coefficient will result in the use of a larger sample size.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Higher confidence coefficient, resulting in a larger sample size.
choices.justification: A higher confidence coefficient need not be adopted in this situation because internal controls are strong.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lower confidence coefficient, resulting in a larger sample size.
choices.justification: A lower confidence coefficient will result in the use of a smaller sample size.

questions.text: An external information systems (IS) auditor issues an audit report pointing out the lack of firewall protection features at the perimeter network gateway and recommending a specific vendor product to address this vulnerability. The IS auditor has failed to exercise:
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Professional independence.
choices.justification: When an information systems (IS) auditor recommends a specific vendor, the auditor’s professional independence is compromised.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Organizational independence.
choices.justification: Organizational independence has no relevance to the content of an audit report and should be considered at the time of accepting the engagement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Technical competence.
choices.justification: Technical competence is not relevant to the requirement of independence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Professional competence.
choices.justification: Professional competence is not relevant to the requirement of independence.

questions.text: A substantive test to verify that tape library inventory records are accurate is:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Determining whether bar code readers are installed.
choices.justification: Determining whether bar code readers are installed is a compliance test.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Determining whether the movement of tapes is authorized.
choices.justification: Determining whether the movement of tapes is authorized is a compliance test.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conducting a physical count of the tape inventory.
choices.justification: A substantive test includes gathering evidence to evaluate the integrity (i.e., the completeness, accuracy and validity) of individual transactions, data or other information. Conducting a physical count of the tape inventory is a substantive test.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checking whether receipts and issues of tapes are accurately recorded.
choices.justification: Checking whether receipts and issues of tapes are accurately recorded is a compliance test.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor observes a potential zero-day exposure when performing a vulnerability assessment at a client site. Which of the following is the BEST approach that the IS auditor should take?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Annotate the findings and properly document the same in the audit report.
choices.justification: Prior to reporting, the information systems (IS) auditor should consult with the auditee and develop a remediation plan for the zero-day exploit.
