choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediately consult the client system administrator to alter the firewall rules.
choices.justification: The root cause needs to be identified, and the auditee’s management must be notified prior to any alternation to the network and systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediately consult the client developer to patch the system.
choices.justification: The root cause needs to be identified, and the auditee’s management must be notified prior to any alternation to systems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immediately discuss the findings with the auditee’s management along with evidence.
choices.justification: The IS auditor should discuss the findings with auditee’s management to gain agreement on the findings and develop an agreed-on course of corrective action.

questions.text: In a small organization, the function of release manager and application programmer are performed by the same employee. What is the BEST compensating control in this scenario?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize data analytics tools to enhance audit processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hiring additional staff to provide separation of duties
choices.justification: Establishing separation of duties is not a compensating control; it is a preventive control. In a small organization, it may not be feasible to hire new staff, which is why a compensating control may be necessary.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Preventing the release manager from making program modifications
choices.justification: Because the release manager is performing dual roles, preventing the release manager from making program modifications is not feasible, and, in a small organization, separation of duties may not be possible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Logging of changes to development libraries
choices.justification: Logging changes to development libraries does not detect changes to production libraries.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Verifying that only approved program changes are implemented
choices.justification: Compensating controls are used to mitigate risk when proper controls are not feasible or practical. In a small organization, it may not be feasible to hire new staff, which is why a compensating control may be necessary. Verifying program changes has roughly the same effect as intended by full separation of duties.

questions.text: An auditee disagrees with an audit finding. Which of the following is the BEST course of action for the information systems (IS) auditor to take?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discuss the finding with the IS auditor’s manager.
choices.justification: Discussing the disagreement with the auditor’s manager is the best course of action because other actions can weaken relationships with the auditee and auditor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Retest the control to confirm the finding.
choices.justification: This may unnecessarily expend human and time resources. The audit manager should determine if controls need to be retested.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevate the risk associated with the control.
choices.justification: Elevating the risk does not address the disagreement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discuss the finding with the auditee’s manager.
choices.justification: It is usually best to consult the audit manager prior to escalating the issue to the auditee’s manager. This could prove to be an adversarial action.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor performing a review of application controls evaluates the:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Efficiency of the application in meeting the business processes.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the effectiveness of the controls, not the suitability of the application to meet business needs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impact of any exposures discovered.
choices.justification: An application control review involves the evaluation of the application automated controls and an assessment of any exposures resulting from the control weaknesses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Business processes served by the application.
choices.justification: The other choices may be objectives of an application audit but are not part of an audit restricted to a review of the application controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Application optimization.
choices.justification: One area to be reviewed may be the efficiency and optimization of the application, but this is not the area being reviewed in this audit.

questions.text: Which of the following would normally be the MOST reliable evidence for an information systems (IS) auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: A confirmation letter received from a third party verifying an account balance
choices.justification: Evidence obtained from independent third parties is almost always considered to be more reliable than assurance provided by local management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assurance from line management that an application is working as designed
choices.justification: Because management is not objective and may not understand the risk and control environment, and it is only providing evidence that the application is working correctly (not the controls), management assurance is not an acceptable level of trust for audit evidence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trend data obtained from Internet sources
choices.justification: Data collected from the Internet is not always trustworthy or independently validated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ratio analysis developed by the IS auditor from reports supplied by line management
choices.justification: Ratio analysis can identify trends and deviations from a baseline but is not reliable evidence.

questions.text: While conducting an audit, an information systems (IS) auditor detects the presence of a virus. What should be the IS auditor’s NEXT step?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Observe the response mechanism.
choices.justification: Observing the response mechanism should be done after informing appropriate personnel. This enables an information systems (IS) auditor to examine the actual workability and effectiveness of the response system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clear the virus from the network.
choices.justification: The IS auditor is neither authorized nor capable in most cases of removing the virus from the network.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inform appropriate personnel immediately.
choices.justification: The first thing an IS auditor should do after detecting the virus is to alert the organization to its presence, then wait for their response.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure deletion of the virus.
choices.justification: An IS auditor should not make changes to the system being audited; ensuring the deletion of the virus is a management responsibility.

questions.text: Which of the following will MOST successfully identify overlapping key controls in business application systems?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reviewing system functionalities that are attached to complex business processes
choices.justification: In general, highly complex business processes may have more key controls than business areas with less complexity; however, finding, with certainty, unnecessary controls in complex areas is not always possible. If a well-thought-out key control structure was established from the beginning, finding any overlap in key controls will not be possible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Submitting test transactions through an integrated test facility
choices.justification: An integrated test facility is an audit technique to test the accuracy of the processes in the application system. This technique may find control flaws in the application system, but it would be difficult for it to find the overlap in key controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Replacing manual monitoring with an automated auditing solution
choices.justification: As part of the effort to realize continuous audit management, there are cases for introducing an automated monitoring and auditing solution. All key controls need to be clearly aligned for systematic implementation; thus, analysts can discover unnecessary or overlapping key controls in existing systems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Testing controls to validate that they are effective
choices.justification: By testing controls to validate whether they are effective, the information systems (IS) auditor can identify whether there are overlapping controls; however, the process of implementing an automated auditing solution better identifies overlapping controls.

questions.text: What is the BEST action for an information systems (IS) auditor to take when an outsourced monitoring process for remote access is inadequate and management disagrees because intrusion detection system (IDS) and firewall controls are in place?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: Revise the finding in the audit report per management’s feedback.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor may include the management response in the report, but that will not affect the requirement to report the finding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Retract the finding because the IDS controls are in place.
choices.justification: The finding remains valid and the management response is documented; however, the audit may indicate a need to review the validity of the management response.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Retract the finding because the firewall rules are monitored.
choices.justification: The finding remains valid and the management response is documented; however, the audit may indicate a need to review the validity of the management response.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the identified finding in the audit report.
choices.justification: IS auditor independence dictates that the additional information provided by the auditee is taken into consideration. Normally, an IS auditor does not automatically retract or revise the finding.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor should use statistical sampling when:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: The probability of error must be objectively quantified.
choices.justification: Given an expected error rate and confidence level, statistical sampling is an objective method of sampling that helps an information systems (IS) auditor determine the sample size and quantify the probability of error (confidence coefficient).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The auditor wants to avoid sampling risk.
choices.justification: Sampling risk is the risk of a sample not being representative of the population. This risk exists for judgment and statistical samples.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Generalized audit software is unavailable.
choices.justification: Statistical sampling can use generalized audit software, but it is not required.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The tolerable error rate cannot be determined.
choices.justification: The tolerable error rate must be predetermined for both judgment and statistical sampling.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor performing an audit of the risk assessment process should FIRST confirm that:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reasonable threats to information assets are identified.
choices.justification: The threats facing each of the organization’s assets should be analyzed according to their value to the organization. This occurs after identifying and ranking assets.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Technical and organizational vulnerabilities have been analyzed.
choices.justification: Analyzing how these weaknesses, in the absence of mitigating controls, will impact the organization’s information assets occurs after the assets and weaknesses have been identified.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assets have been identified and ranked.
choices.justification: Identification and ranking of information assets (e.g., data criticality, sensitivity, locations of assets) sets the tone or scope of how to assess risk in relation to the organizational value of the asset.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The effects of potential security breaches have been evaluated.
choices.justification: The effect of security breaches is dependent on the value of the assets and the threats, vulnerabilities and effectiveness of mitigating controls. The impact of an attack against a weakness should be identified so that controls can be evaluated to determine if they effectively mitigate the weaknesses.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is validating a control that involves a review of system-generated exception reports. Which of the following is the BEST evidence of the effectiveness of the control?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Walk-through with the reviewer of the operation of the control
choices.justification: A walk-through highlights how a control is designed to work, but it seldom highlights the effectiveness of the control, or exceptions or constraints in the process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System-generated exception reports for the review period with the reviewer’s sign-off
choices.justification: Reviewer sign-off does not demonstrate the effectiveness of the control if the reviewer does not note follow-up actions for the exceptions identified.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A sample system-generated exception report for the review period, with follow-up action items noted by the reviewer
choices.justification: A sample of a system-generated report with evidence that the reviewer followed up on the exceptions represents the best possible evidence of the effective operation of the control, because there is documented evidence that the reviewer reviewed the exception report and took actions based on the exception report.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Management’s confirmation of the effectiveness of the control for the review period
choices.justification: Management’s confirmation of effectiveness of the control suffers from lack of independence—management might be biased toward the effectiveness of the controls put in place.

questions.text: The extent to which data will be collected during an information systems (IS) audit should be determined based on the:
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Availability of critical and required information.
choices.justification: The extent to which data will be collected during an information systems (IS) audit should be based on the scope, purpose and requirements of the audit and not be constrained by the ease of obtaining the information or by the IS auditor’s familiarity with the area being audited.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Auditor’s familiarity with the circumstances.
choices.justification: An IS auditor must be objective and thorough and not subject to audit risk through preconceived expected results based on familiarity with the area being audited.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Auditee’s ability to find relevant evidence.
choices.justification: Collecting all the required evidence is a required element of an IS audit, and the scope of the audit should not be limited by the auditee’s ability to find relevant evidence. If evidence is not readily available, the auditor must ensure that other forms of audit are considered to ensure compliance in the area that is subject to audit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Purpose and scope of the audit being done.
choices.justification: The extent to which data will be collected during an IS audit should be related directly to the scope and purpose of the audit. An IS audit with a narrow purpose and scope, or just a high-level review, will most likely require less data collection than an audit with a wider purpose and scope.

category: Quality Assurance and Quality Management of IT

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing a contract management process to determine the financial viability of a software vendor for a critical business application. An IS auditor should determine whether the vendor being considered:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Can deliver on the immediate contract.
choices.justification: The capability of the organization to support the enterprise should extend beyond the time of execution of the immediate contract. The objective of a financial evaluation should not be confined to the immediate contract but should be to provide assurance of sustainability over a longer time frame.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Is of similar financial standing as the organization.
choices.justification: Whether the vendor is of similar financial standing as the purchaser is irrelevant to this review.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Has significant financial obligations that can impose liability to the organization.
choices.justification: The vendor should not have financial obligations that can impose a liability to the purchaser; the financial obligations are usually from the purchaser to the vendor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Can support the organization in the long term.
choices.justification: The long-term financial viability of a vendor is essential for deriving maximum value for the organization—it is more likely that a financially sound vendor will be in business for a long period of time and thereby more likely to be capable of providing long-term support for the purchased product.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes the function of control self-assessment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Quality control
choices.justification: Quality control can be defined as the part of quality management focused on fulfilling quality requirements. Quality control procedures monitor and evaluate the resulting service or product.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Quality assessment
choices.justification: Quality assessment is the data collection and analysis through which the degree of conformity to predetermined standards and criteria are exemplified.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Quality planning
choices.justification: The role of quality planning is to design a process or service that can meet established goals under operating conditions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Quality assurance (QA)
choices.justification: Quality assurance (QA) is often achieved by the information systems (IS) auditor serving as a consultant, advising and facilitating business areas to participate in process improvement and control. One such approach is through the process of control self-assessment.

questions.text: Errors in audit procedures PRIMARILY impact which type of risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: Detection risk
choices.justification: Detection risk is a component of audit risk and is the probability that the audit procedures may fail to detect the existence of a material error or fraud.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inherent risk
choices.justification: Inherent risk in auditing refers to the risk involved in the nature of business or transaction. It is not affected by human error and is a component of audit risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Control risk
choices.justification: Control risk is a component of audit risk and is the risk that a material error exists that would not be prevented or detected on a timely basis by the system of internal controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Business risk
choices.justification: Business risk is a broad category that includes strategic risk, compliance risk, operational risk and reputational risk. It applies to any event or circumstance that has the potential to prevent an enterprise from achieving its business goals or objectives and is not a component of audit risk.

questions.text: The PRIMARY goal of using maturity models in (information systems (IS)) is to:
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: Measure the current maturity levels of a certain aspect of an IS organization in a meaningful way.
choices.justification: Measuring and assessing the maturity levels enable stakeholders to clearly identify strengths and areas of improvement in information systems (IS).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prioritize what the organization should do to reach higher maturity levels.
choices.justification: Prioritizing the improvement initiatives comes after knowing what parts of IS need attention first.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Help gain senior management understanding, commitment and support for IS.
choices.justification: Gaining senior management support comes after measuring the maturity levels, identifying areas of improvement and prioritizing improvement initiatives.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enable stakeholders to clearly identify strengths and areas of improvement.
choices.justification: The main purpose of using maturity models is to enable stakeholders to clearly identify strengths and areas of improvement in IS after assessing the current maturity levels and to prioritize the improvement initiatives before gaining senior management support.

questions.text: The corporate IT policy for a call center requires that all users be assigned unique user accounts. On discovering that this is not the case for all current users, what is the MOST appropriate recommendation?
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: Have the current configuration approved by operations management.
choices.justification: Having the current configuration approved is a recommendation that is not in compliance with the enterprise’s own policy and violates good practice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure that there is an audit trail for all existing accounts.
choices.justification: Having an audit trail for existing shared accounts does not provide accountability or resolve the problem of noncompliance with policy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implement individual user accounts for all staff.
choices.justification: Individual user accounts allow for accountability of transactions and should be the most important recommendation, given the current scenario.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amend the IT policy to allow shared accounts.
choices.justification: Shared user accounts do not allow for accountability of transactions and do not reflect good practice.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the risk management process. Which of the following is the MOST important consideration during this review?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Controls are implemented based on cost-benefit analysis.
choices.justification: Controls to mitigate risk must be implemented based on cost-benefit analysis; however, the cost-benefit analysis is effective only if risk is presented in business terms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The risk management framework is based on global standards.
choices.justification: A risk management framework based on global standards helps in ensuring completeness; however, organizations must adapt it to suit specific business requirements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The approval process for risk response is in place.
choices.justification: Approvals for risk response come later in the process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: IT risk is presented in business terms.
choices.justification: For risk management to be effective, it is necessary to align IT risk with business objectives. This can be done by adopting acceptable terminology that is understood by all, and the best way to achieve this is to present IT risk in business terms.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor observes that an enterprise has outsourced software development to a third-party start-up organization. To ensure that the enterprise’s investment in software is protected, which of the following should be recommended by the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Due diligence should be performed on the software vendor.
choices.justification: Vendor due diligence is the way in which organizations vet their vendors to spot potential red flags, before entering a contractual relationship. Although due diligence is a good practice, it does not ensure availability of the source code in the event of vendor failure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A quarterly audit of the vendor facilities should be performed.
choices.justification: A quarterly audit of vendor facilities is a good practice to continuously evaluate the vendor, but it does not ensure availability of the source code in the event of failure of the start-up vendor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There should be a source code escrow agreement in place.
choices.justification: The objective of source code escrow is to ensure continued availability of software support and maintenance. Thus, it is primarily recommended to help protect the enterprise’s investment in software, because the source code will be available through a trusted third party and can be retrieved if the start-up vendor goes out of business.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A high penalty clause should be included in the contract.
choices.justification: A penalty clause imposes a fine on a guilty party for breaching contractual terms. Although it is good practice to include it in the contract, it does not provide protection or ensure availability of the source code in the event of vendor bankruptcy.

questions.text: The development, implementation and integration of capability and maturity modeling quality tools, techniques and processes will MAINLY facilitate and foster the quality of:
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enterprise IT strategies and policies.
choices.justification: When IT governance improves, all its constituent building blocks improve also, including strategies, policies and procedures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enterprise IT processes and procedures.
choices.justification: Effective and improved IT governance directs management and its processes, procedures, etc. to help the business achieve its goals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enterprise IT governance.
choices.justification: Quality management strategies and policies embedded into IT governance outline how the IT strategies, policies, processes, procedures and standards are maintained, used and improved over time as the enterprise changes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enterprise IT standards and frameworks.
choices.justification: When IT governance gets fostered, enterprise IT standards and frameworks are maintained and improved over time.

questions.text: Organizations requiring employees to take a mandatory vacation each year PRIMARILY want to ensure that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adequate cross-training exists between functions.
choices.justification: Cross-training is a good practice to follow but can be achieved without the requirement for mandatory vacation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An effective internal control environment is in place by increasing morale.
choices.justification: Good employee morale and high levels of employee satisfaction are worthwhile objectives, but they should not be considered a means to achieve an effective internal control system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potential irregularities in processing are identified by a temporary replacement.
choices.justification: Employees who perform critical and sensitive functions within an organization should be required to take some time off to help ensure that irregularities and fraud are detected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The risk of processing errors is reduced.
choices.justification: Although rotating employees can contribute to fewer processing errors, this is not typically a reason to require a mandatory vacation policy.

questions.text: A financial services enterprise has a small IT department, and individuals perform more than one role. Which of the following practices represents the GREATEST risk?
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: The developers promote code into the production environment.
choices.justification: Developers with access to the production environment may run unauthorized code or migrate untested code to production resulting in system unavailability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The business analyst writes the requirements and performs functional testing.
choices.justification: In situations in which there is no dedicated testing group, the business analyst is often the one to perform testing because the analyst has detailed knowledge of how the system must function because of writing the requirements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The IT manager also performs systems administration.
choices.justification: It is acceptable in a small team for the IT manager to perform system administration, if the manager does not also develop code.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The database administrator (DBA) also performs data backups.
choices.justification: The primary function of a database administrator (DBA) is to implement, maintain, optimize and manage database structures for the enterprise. Therefore, it may be part of the DBA’s duties to perform data backups.

questions.text: Which of the following reasons BEST describes the purpose of a mandatory vacation policy?
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: To ensure that employees are properly cross-trained in multiple functions.
choices.justification: Ensuring that employees are properly cross-trained in multiple functions improves the skills of employees and provides for succession planning but is not the primary purpose of mandatory vacations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To improve employee morale.
choices.justification: Improving employee morale helps in reducing employee burnout but is not the primary reason for mandatory vacations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To identify potential errors or inconsistencies in business processes.
choices.justification: Mandatory vacations help uncover potential fraud or inconsistencies. Ensuring that people who have access to sensitive internal controls or processes take a mandatory vacation annually is often a regulatory requirement and, most importantly, a good way to uncover fraud.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To be used as a cost-saving measure.
choices.justification: Mandatory vacations may or may not be a cost-saving measure, depending on the enterprise, but saving cost is not the primary purpose for the policy.

questions.text: Which of the following inputs adds the MOST value to the strategic IT initiative decision-making process?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's privacy program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maturity of the project management process
choices.justification: The maturity of the project management process is more important with respect to managing the day-to-day operations of IT versus performing strategic planning.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Regulatory environment
choices.justification: Regulatory requirements may drive investment in certain technologies and initiatives; however, having to meet regulatory requirements is not typically the main focus of the IT and business strategy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Past audit findings
choices.justification: Past audit findings may drive investment in certain technologies and initiatives; however, having to remediate past audit findings is not the main focus of the IT and business strategy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: IT project portfolio analysis
choices.justification: Portfolio analysis provides the best input into the decision-making process relating to planning strategic IT initiatives. An analysis of the IT portfolio provides comparable information of planned initiatives, projects and ongoing IT services, which allows the IT strategy to be aligned with the business strategy.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST important information systems (IS) audit consideration when an organization outsources a customer credit review system to a third-party service provider? The provider:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: claims to meet or exceed industry security standards.
choices.justification: Compliance with security standards is important, but there is no way to verify or prove that is the case without an independent review.

choices.label: B
choices.text: agrees to be subject to external security reviews.
choices.justification: An independent security review of an outsourcing vendor is critical because customer credit information will be kept with the vendor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: has a good market reputation for service and experience.
choices.justification: Although long experience in business and a good reputation is an important factor in assessing service quality, the business cannot outsource to a provider whose security control is weak.

choices.label: D
choices.text: complies with security policies of the organization.
choices.justification: Compliance with organizational security policies is important, but there is no way to verify or prove that that is the case without an independent review.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds that not all employees are aware of the enterprise’s information security policy. The IS auditor should conclude that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: this lack of knowledge may lead to unintentional disclosure of sensitive information.
choices.justification: All employees should be aware of the enterprise’s information security policy to prevent unintentional disclosure of sensitive information. Training is a preventive control. Security awareness programs for employees can prevent unintentional disclosure of sensitive information to outsiders.

choices.label: B
choices.text: information security is not critical to all functions.
choices.justification: Information security is everybody’s business, and all staff should be trained in how to handle information correctly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IS audit should provide security training to the employees.
choices.justification: Providing security awareness training is not an information systems (IS) audit function.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the audit finding will cause management to provide continuous training to staff.
choices.justification: Management may agree to or reject an audit finding. The IS auditor cannot be assured that management will act upon an audit finding unless they are aware of its impact; therefore, the auditor must report the risk associated with lack of security awareness.

questions.text: To ensure that an enterprise is complying with privacy requirements, an information systems (IS) auditor should FIRST review:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's privacy program

choices.label: A
choices.text: the IT infrastructure.
choices.justification: To comply with requirements, the information systems (IS) auditor must first know what the requirements are. They can vary from one jurisdiction to another. The IT infrastructure is related to the implementation of the requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: organizational policies, standards and procedures.
choices.justification: The policies of the enterprise are subject to the legal requirements and should be checked for compliance after the legal requirements are reviewed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: legal and regulatory requirements.
choices.justification: To ensure that the enterprise is complying with privacy issues, an IS auditor should address legal and regulatory requirements first. To comply with legal and regulatory requirements, enterprises need to adopt the appropriate infrastructure. After understanding the legal and regulatory requirements, an IS auditor should evaluate organizational policies, standards and procedures to determine whether they adequately address the privacy requirements, and then review the adherence to these specific policies, standards and procedures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: adherence to organizational policies, standards and procedures.
choices.justification: Checking for compliance is only done after the IS auditor is assured that the policies, standards and procedures are aligned with the legal requirements.

category: IT Policies, Standards, Procedures, and Guidelines

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is verifying IT policies and finds that some of the policies have not been approved by management (as required by policy), but the employees strictly follow the policies. What should the IS auditor do FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ignore the absence of management approval because employees follow the policies.
choices.justification: Absence of management approval is an important (material) finding and, although it is not currently an issue with relation to compliance because the employees are following the policy without approval, it may be a problem at a later time and should be resolved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recommend immediate management approval of the policies.
choices.justification: Although the information systems (IS) auditor would likely recommend that the policies should be approved as soon as possible and may also remind management of the critical nature of this issue, the first step is to report this issue to the relevant stakeholders.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Emphasize the importance of approval to management.
choices.justification: The first step is to report the finding and provide recommendations later.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Report the absence of documented approval.
choices.justification: The IS auditor must report the finding. Unapproved policies may present a potential risk to the organization, even if they are being followed, because this technicality may prevent management from enforcing the policies in some cases and may present legal issues. For example, if an employee was terminated as a result of violating an organizational policy, and it was discovered that the policies had not been approved, the organization may face a lawsuit.

questions.text: Which of the following should be included in an organization’s information security policy?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: A list of key IT resources to be secured
choices.justification: A list of key IT resources to be secured is more detailed than that which should be included in a policy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The basis for access control authorization
choices.justification: The security policy provides a broad framework of security as laid down and approved by senior management. It includes a definition of those authorized to grant access and the basis for granting the access.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identity of sensitive security assets
choices.justification: The identity of sensitive security assets is more detailed than that which should be included in a policy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relevant software security features
choices.justification: A list of the relevant software security features is more detailed than that which should be included in a policy.

questions.text: While conducting an audit of a service provider, an information systems (IS) auditor observes that the service provider has outsourced a part of the work to another provider. Because the work involves confidential information, the IS auditor’s PRIMARY concern should be that the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: requirement for securely protecting information can be compromised.
choices.justification: Many countries have enacted regulations to protect the confidentiality of information maintained in their countries and/or exchanged with other countries. When a service provider outsources part of its services to another service provider, there is a potential risk that the confidentiality of the information will be compromised.

choices.label: B
choices.text: contract may be terminated because prior permission from the outsourcer was not obtained.
choices.justification: Terminating the contract for a violation of the terms of the contract can be a concern but is not related to ensuring the security of information.

choices.label: C
choices.text: other service provider to whom work has been outsourced is not subject to audit.
choices.justification: The outsourcer not being subject to an audit can be a concern but is not related to ensuring the security of information.

choices.label: D
choices.text: outsourcer will approach the other service provider directly for further work.
choices.justification: There is no reason why an information systems (IS) auditor should be concerned with the outsourcer approaching the other service providers directly for further work.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor discovers several IT-based projects were implemented and not approved by the steering committee. What is the GREATEST concern for the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: The IT department’s projects will not be adequately funded.
choices.justification: Funding for the projects may be addressed through various budgets and may not require steering committee approval. The primary concern is to ensure that the project is working toward meeting the goals of the enterprise.

choices.label: B
choices.text: IT projects are not following the system development life cycle (SDLC) process.
choices.justification: Although requiring steering committee approval may be part of the system development life cycle (SDLC) process, the greater concern is whether the projects are working toward the corporate goals. Without steering committee approval, it is difficult to determine whether these projects are following the direction of the corporate goals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IT projects are not consistently formally approved.
choices.justification: Although having a formal approval process is important, the greatest concern is for the steering committee to provide corporate direction for the projects.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The IT department may not be working toward a common goal.
choices.justification: The steering committee provides direction and control over projects to ensure that the enterprise is making appropriate investments. Without approval, the project may or may not be working toward the enterprise goals.

questions.text: When auditing the IT governance framework and IT risk management practices existing within an enterprise, the information systems (IS) auditor identified some undefined responsibilities regarding IT management and governance roles. Which of the following recommendations is the MOST appropriate?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Review the strategic alignment of IT with the business.
choices.justification: Although the strategic alignment of IT with the business is important, it is not directly related to the gap identified in this scenario.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement accountability rules within the enterprise.
choices.justification: IT risk is managed by embedding accountability into the enterprise. The information systems (IS) auditor should recommend the implementation of accountability rules to ensure that all responsibilities are defined within the enterprise. Note that this question asks for the best recommendation—not about the finding itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensure that independent IS audits are conducted periodically.
choices.justification: Performing more frequent IS audits is not helpful if the accountability rules are not clearly defined and implemented.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Create a chief risk officer role in the enterprise.
choices.justification: Recommending the creation of a new role (e.g., chief risk officer) is not helpful if the accountability rules are not clearly defined and implemented.

questions.text: The output of the risk management process is an input for making:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: business plans.
choices.justification: Making a business plan is not the goal of the risk management process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: audit charters.
choices.justification: Risk management can help create the audit plan, but not the audit charter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: security policy decisions.
choices.justification: The risk management process is about making specific, security-related decisions, such as the level of acceptable risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: software design decisions.
choices.justification: Risk management drives the design of security controls in software but influencing security policy is more important.

questions.text: Effective IT governance requires organizational structures and processes to ensure that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: risk is maintained at a level acceptable for IT management.
choices.justification: Risk acceptance levels are set by senior management, not by IT management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the business strategy is derived from an IT strategy.
choices.justification: The business strategy drives the IT strategy, not the opposite direction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IT governance is separate and distinct from overall governance.
choices.justification: IT governance is not an isolated discipline; it must become an integral part of overall enterprise governance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the IT strategy extends the organization strategies and objectives.
choices.justification: Effective IT governance requires that board and executive management extend governance to IT and provide the leadership, organizational structures and processes that ensure that the organization IT sustains and extends the organization strategies and objectives, and that the strategy is aligned with business strategy.

questions.text: Which of the following should be of GREATEST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor when reviewing an information security policy? The policy:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: is driven by an IT department’s objectives.
choices.justification: Business objectives drive the information security policy, and the information security policy drives the selection of IT department objectives. A policy driven by IT objectives is at risk of not being aligned with business goals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is published, but users are not required to read the policy.
choices.justification: Policies should be written so that users can understand each policy, and employees should be able to easily access the policies. The fact that users have not read the policy is not the greatest concern because they still may be compliant with the policy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: does not include information security procedures.
choices.justification: Policies should not contain procedures. Procedures are established to assist with policy implementation and compliance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: has not been updated in over a year.
choices.justification: Policies should be reviewed annually, but they might not necessarily be updated annually unless there are significant changes in the environment, such as new laws, rules or regulations.

questions.text: When developing a formal enterprise security program, the MOST critical success factor (CSF) is the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: establishment of a review board.
choices.justification: Establishment of a review board is not effective without visible sponsorship of top management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: creation of a security unit.
choices.justification: The creation of a security unit is not effective without visible sponsorship of top management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: effective support of an executive sponsor.
choices.justification: The executive sponsor is in charge of supporting the organization’s strategic security program and aids in directing the organization’s overall security management activities. Therefore, support by the executive level of management is the most critical success factor (CSF).

choices.label: D
choices.text: selection of a security process owner.
choices.justification: The selection of a security process owner is not effective without visible sponsorship of top management.

questions.text: On which of the following factors should an information systems (IS) auditor PRIMARILY focus when determining the appropriate level of protection for an information asset?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Results of a risk assessment
choices.justification: The appropriate level of protection for an asset is determined based on the risk associated with the asset. The results of the risk assessment are, therefore, the primary information that the information systems (IS) auditor should review.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relative value to the business
choices.justification: The relative value of an asset to the business is one element considered in the risk assessment; this alone does not determine the level of protection required.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Results of a vulnerability assessment
choices.justification: The results of a vulnerability assessment are useful when creating the risk assessment; however, this is not the primary focus.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cost of security controls
choices.justification: The cost of security controls is not a primary factor to consider because the expenditures on these controls are determined by the value of the information assets being protected.

questions.text: When reviewing an organization’s approved software product list, which of the following is the MOST important thing to verify?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: The risk associated with the use of the products is periodically assessed.
choices.justification: Because the business conditions surrounding vendors may change, it is important for an organization to conduct periodic risk assessments of the vendor software list. This may be best incorporated into the IT risk management process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The latest version of software is listed for each product.
choices.justification: The organization may not be using the latest version of a product.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Due to licensing issues, the list does not contain open-source software.
choices.justification: The list may contain open-source software depending on the business requirements and associated risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: After-hours support is offered.
choices.justification: Support may be provided internally or externally, and technical support should be arranged depending on the criticality of the software.

questions.text: Which of the following is the GREATEST risk of an inadequate policy definition for ownership of data and systems?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: User management coordination does not exist.
choices.justification: The greatest risk is from unauthorized users being able to modify data. User management is important but not the greatest risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Specific user accountability cannot be established.
choices.justification: User accountability is important but not as great a risk as the actions of unauthorized users.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Unauthorized users may have access to modify data.
choices.justification: Without a policy defining who has the responsibility for granting access to specific systems, there is an increased risk that individuals can gain (be given) system access when they should not have authorization. The ability of unauthorized users to modify data is greater than the risk of authorized user accounts not being controlled properly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Audit recommendations may not be implemented.
choices.justification: The failure to implement audit recommendations is a management problem but not as serious as the ability of unauthorized users to make modifications.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST reason to implement a policy that places conditions on secondary employment for IT employees?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: To prevent the misuse of corporate resources
choices.justification: The misuse of corporate resources is an issue that must be addressed but is not necessarily related to secondary employment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To prevent conflicts of interest
choices.justification: The best reason to implement and enforce a policy governing secondary employment is to prevent conflicts of interest. Policies should be in place to control IT employees seeking secondary employment from releasing sensitive information or working for a competing organization. Conflicts of interest can result in serious risk, such as fraud, theft of intellectual property or other improprieties.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To prevent employee performance issues
choices.justification: Employee performance can certainly be an issue if an employee is overworked or has insufficient time off, but that should be dealt with as a management function and not the primary reason to have a policy on secondary employment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To prevent theft of IT assets
choices.justification: Theft of assets is a problem but not necessarily related to secondary employment.

questions.text: When reviewing the IT strategy, an information systems (IS) auditor can BEST assess whether the strategy supports the organizations’ business objectives by determining whether IT:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Has all the personnel and equipment it needs.
choices.justification: Having personnel and equipment is an important requirement to meet the IT strategy but does not ensure that the IT strategy supports business objectives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: plans are consistent with management strategy.
choices.justification: The only way to know if IT strategy meets business objectives is to determine if the IT plan is consistent with management strategy and that it relates IT planning to business plans.

choices.label: C
choices.text: uses its equipment and personnel efficiently and effectively.
choices.justification: Using equipment and personnel efficiently and effectively is an effective method for determining the proper management of the IT function but does not ensure that the IT strategy is aligned with business objectives.

choices.label: D
choices.text: has sufficient excess capacity to respond to changing directions.
choices.justification: Having sufficient excess capacity to respond to changing directions is important to show flexibility to meet organizational changes but is not in itself a way to ensure that IT is aligned with business goals.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) audit department is planning to minimize the risk of short-term employees. Activities contributing to this objective are documented procedures, knowledge sharing, cross-training and:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: succession planning.
choices.justification: Succession planning ensures that internal personnel with the potential to fill key positions in the organization are identified and developed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: staff job evaluation.
choices.justification: Job evaluation is the process of determining the worth of one job in relation to that of the other jobs in an organization so that a fair and equitable wage and salary system can be established.

choices.label: C
choices.text: responsibilities definitions.
choices.justification: Staff responsibilities definitions provide for well-defined roles and responsibilities; however, they do not minimize dependency on key individuals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: employee award programs.
choices.justification: Employee award programs provide motivation; however, they do not minimize dependency on key individuals.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is evaluating a newly developed IT policy for an organization. Which of the following factors does the IS auditor consider MOST important to facilitate compliance with the policy upon its implementation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Existing IT mechanisms enabling compliance
choices.justification: The organization should be able to comply with a policy when it is implemented. The most important consideration when evaluating the new policy should be the existing mechanisms in place that enable the organization and its employees to comply with the policy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alignment of the policy to the business strategy
choices.justification: Policies should be aligned with the business strategy, but this does not affect an organization’s ability to comply with the policy upon implementation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Current and future technology initiatives
choices.justification: Current and future technology initiatives should be driven by the needs of the business and does not affect an organization’s ability to comply with the policy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regulatory compliance objectives defined in the policy
choices.justification: Regulatory compliance objectives may be defined in the IT policy, but that does not facilitate compliance with the policy. Defining objectives only results in the organization knowing the desired state and does not aid in achieving compliance.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is assigned to review an organization’s information security policy. Which of the following issues represents the HIGHEST potential risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: The policy has not been updated in more than one year.
choices.justification: Although the information security policy should be updated on a regular basis, the specific time period may vary based on the organization. Although reviewing policies annually is a good practice, the policy may be updated less frequently and still be relevant and effective. An outdated policy is still enforceable, whereas a policy without proper approval is not enforceable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The policy includes no revision history.
choices.justification: The lack of a revision history with respect to the information security (IS) policy document is an issue but not as significant as not having it approved by management. A new policy, for example, may not have been subject to any revisions yet.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The policy is approved by the security administrator.
choices.justification: The information security policy should have an owner who has management responsibility for the development, review, approval and evaluation of the security policy. The position of security administrator is typically a staff-level position (not management), and therefore does not have the authority to approve the policy. In addition, an individual in a more independent position should also review the policy. Without proper management approval, enforcing the policy may be problematic, leading to compliance or security issues.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The organization does not have an information security policy committee.
choices.justification: Although a policy committee drawn from across the organization is a good practice and may help write better policies, a good policy can be written by a single person, and the lack of a committee is not a problem by itself.

questions.text: An organization has contracted with a vendor for a turnkey solution for its electronic toll collection system (ETCS). The vendor has provided its proprietary application software as part of the solution. The contract should require that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: a backup server is available to run ETCS operations with up-to-date data.
choices.justification: Having a backup server with current data is critical but not as critical as ensuring the availability of the source code.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a backup server is loaded with all relevant software and data.
choices.justification: Having a backup server with relevant software is critical but not as critical as ensuring the availability of the source code.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the systems staff of the organization is trained to handle any event.
choices.justification: Having staff training is critical but not as critical as ensuring the availability of the source code.

choices.label: D
choices.text: source code of the ETCS application is placed in escrow.
choices.justification: Whenever proprietary application software is purchased, the contract should provide for a source code escrow agreement. This agreement ensures that the purchasing organization can modify the software if the vendor ceases to be in business.

questions.text: While conducting an audit of a service provider for a government program involving confidential information, an information systems (IS) auditor noted that the service provider delegated a part of the IS work to another subcontractor. Which of the following provides the MOST assurance that the requirements for protecting confidentiality of information are met?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Monthly committee meetings include the subcontractor’s IS manager.
choices.justification: Regular committee meetings are a good monitoring tool for delegated operations; however, independent reviews provide better assurance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Management reviews weekly reports from the subcontractor.
choices.justification: Management should not rely only on self-reported information from the subcontractor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Permission is obtained from the government agent regarding the contract.
choices.justification: Obtaining permission from the government agent is not related to ensuring the confidentiality of information.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Periodic independent audits are performed of the work delegated to the subcontractor.
choices.justification: Periodic independent audits provide reasonable assurance that the requirements for protecting confidentiality of information are not compromised.

questions.text: While reviewing the IT governance processes of an organization, an information systems (IS) auditor discovers the firm has recently implemented an IT balanced scorecard (BSC). The implementation is complete; however, the IS auditor notices that performance indicators are not objectively measurable. What is the PRIMARY risk presented by this situation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Key performance indicators are not reported to management and management cannot determine the effectiveness of the BSC.
choices.justification: If the performance indicators are not objectively measurable, the most significant risk is the presentation of misleading performance results to management. This can create a false sense of assurance and, as a result, IT resources may be misallocated, or strategic decisions may be based on incorrect information. Whether or not the performance indicators are correctly defined, the results would be reported to management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: IT projects could suffer from cost overruns.
choices.justification: Although project management issues can arise from performance indicators that were not correctly defined, the presentation of misleading performance to management is a much more significant risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Misleading indications of IT performance may be presented to management.
choices.justification: The IT balanced scorecard is designed to measure IT performance. To measure performance, a sufficient number of performance drivers (key performance indicators [KPIs]) must be defined and measured over time. Failure to have objective KPIs may result in arbitrary, subjective measures that may be misleading and lead to unsound decisions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: IT service level agreements may not be accurate.
choices.justification: Although performance management issues related to service level agreements can arise from performance indicators that were not correctly defined, the presentation of misleading performance to management is a much more significant risk.

questions.text: Which of the following is the PRIMARY objective of an IT performance measurement process?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Minimize errors
choices.justification: Minimizing errors is an aspect of performance but not the primary objective of performance management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gather performance data
choices.justification: Gathering performance data is necessary to measure IT performance but is not the objective of the process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Establish performance baselines
choices.justification: The performance measurement process compares actual performance with baselines but is not the objective of the process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Optimize performance
choices.justification: An IT performance measurement process can be used to optimize performance, measure and manage products/services, assure accountability, and make budget decisions.

questions.text: An organization is considering making a major investment to upgrade technology. Which of the following choices is the MOST important to consider?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: A cost analysis
choices.justification: The information system solution should be cost-effective, but this is not the most important aspect.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The security risk of the current technology
choices.justification: The security risk of the current technology is one of the components of the risk analysis and alone is not the most important factor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compatibility with existing systems
choices.justification: Compatibility with existing systems is one consideration; however, the new system may be a major upgrade that is not compatible with existing systems, so this is not the most important consideration.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A risk analysis
choices.justification: Prior to implementing new technology, an organization should perform a risk assessment, which is then presented to business unit management for review and acceptance.

questions.text: Value delivery from IT to the business is MOST effectively achieved by:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: aligning the IT strategy with the enterprise strategy
choices.justification: IT’s value delivery to the business is driven by aligning IT with the enterprise’s strategy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: embedding accountability in the enterprise
choices.justification: Embedding accountability in the enterprise promotes risk management (another element of corporate governance).

choices.label: C
choices.text: providing a positive return on investment
choices.justification: While return on investment is important, it is not the only criterion by which the value of IT is assessed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: establishing an enterprise-wide risk management process
choices.justification: Enterprise-wide risk management is critical to IT governance; however, by itself, it will not guarantee that IT delivers value to the business unless the IT strategy is aligned with the enterprise strategy.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST important function to be performed by IT management when a service has been outsourced?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensuring that invoices are paid to the provider
choices.justification: Payment of invoices is a finance function, which would be completed per contractual requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Participating in systems design with the provider
choices.justification: Participating in systems design is a by-product of monitoring the outsourcing provider’s performance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renegotiating the provider’s fees
choices.justification: Renegotiating fees is usually a one-time activity and is not as important as monitoring the vendor’s performance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monitoring the outsourcing provider’s performance
choices.justification: In an outsourcing environment, the enterprise is dependent on the performance of the service provider. Therefore, it is critical that the outsourcing provider’s performance is monitored to ensure that services are delivered to the enterprise as required.

questions.text: Overall quantitative business risk for a particular threat can be expressed as:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: a product of the likelihood and magnitude of the impact if a threat successfully exploits a vulnerability.
choices.justification: Quantitative risk analysis methods use numeric values to describe the likelihood and impacts of risk. Thus, the overall business risk can be expressed as the likelihood of the occurrence and magnitude of the impact when a threat exploits a vulnerability and provides the best measure of the risk to an asset.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the magnitude of the impact if a threat source successfully exploits the vulnerability.
choices.justification: A threat source is the intent and method targeted at the intentional exploitation of a vulnerability or a situation and method that may accidentally trigger a vulnerability. A threat is an event or situation that has the potential for causing undesirable consequences or impact. The calculation of risk must consider impact and likelihood of a threat (not a threat source) exploiting a vulnerability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the likelihood of a given threat source exploiting a given vulnerability.
choices.justification: The likelihood and impact of a threat (not a threat source) needs to be considered when determining business risk, not just the likelihood.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the collective judgment of the risk assessment team.
choices.justification: The collective judgment of the risk assessment team is a part of qualitative risk assessment but must be combined with calculations of the impact on the business to determine overall risk.

questions.text: The ultimate purpose of IT governance is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: encourage optimal use of IT.
choices.justification: The purpose of IT governance is to direct the IT endeavors to ensure that IT performance meets the objectives of aligning IT with the enterprise’s objectives and the realization of promised benefits. Thus, generating business value and mitigating the risks associated with IT, results in the optimal use of IT.

choices.label: B
choices.text: reduce IT costs.
choices.justification: Reducing IT costs may not be the best IT governance outcome for an enterprise.

choices.label: C
choices.text: decentralize IT resources across the organization.
choices.justification: Decentralizing IT resources across the organization is not always desired, although it may be desired in a decentralized environment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: centralize control of IT.
choices.justification: Centralizing control of IT is not always desired. An example of where it might be desired is an enterprise wanting a single point of customer contact.

questions.text: In a review of the human resources policies and procedures within an organization, an information systems (IS) auditor is MOST concerned with the absence of a:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: requirement for periodic job rotations.
choices.justification: Job rotation is a valuable control to ensure continuity of operations, but not the most serious human resources policy risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: process for formalized exit interviews.
choices.justification: Holding an exit interview is desirable, when possible, to gain feedback but is not a serious risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: termination checklist.
choices.justification: A termination checklist is critical to ensure the logical and physical security of an enterprise. In addition to preventing the loss of enterprise property that was issued to the employee, there is the risk of unauthorized access, intellectual property theft and even sabotage by a disgruntled former employee.

choices.label: D
choices.text: requirement for new employees to sign a nondisclosure agreement.
choices.justification: Signing a nondisclosure agreement (NDA) is a recommended human resources practice, but a lack of an NDA is not the most serious risk listed.

questions.text: Which of the following should be of PRIMARY concern to an information systems (IS) auditor reviewing the management of external IT service providers?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Minimizing costs for the services provided
choices.justification: Minimizing costs, if applicable and achievable (depending on the customer’s need), is traditionally not part of an information systems (IS) auditor’s job. This work is normally done by a line management function within the IT department. Furthermore, during an audit, it is too late to minimize the costs for existing provider arrangements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prohibiting the provider from subcontracting services
choices.justification: Subcontracting providers can be a concern but is not the primary concern. This issue should be addressed in the contract.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evaluating the process for transferring knowledge to the IT department
choices.justification: Transferring knowledge to the internal IT department might be desirable under certain circumstances but should not be the primary concern of an IS auditor when auditing IT service providers and the management thereof.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determining if the services were provided as contracted
