choices.justification: From an IS auditor’s perspective, the primary objective of auditing the management of service providers should be to determine if the services that were requested were provided in a way that is acceptable, seamless and in line with contractual agreements.

questions.text: From a control perspective, the key element in job descriptions is that they:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: provide instructions on how to do the job and define authority.
choices.justification: Providing instructions on how to do the job and defining authority addresses the managerial and procedural aspects of the job and is a management responsibility. Job descriptions, which are a HR-related function, are primarily used to establish job requirements and accountability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: are current, documented and readily available to the employee.
choices.justification: It is important that job descriptions are current, documented and readily available to the employee, but this, in itself, is not the key element of the job description. Job descriptions, which are an HR-related function, are primarily used to establish job requirements and accountability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: communicate management’s specific job performance expectations.
choices.justification: Communication of management’s specific expectations for job performance would not necessarily be included in job descriptions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: establish responsibility and accountability for the employee’s actions.
choices.justification: From a control perspective, a job description should establish responsibility and accountability. This aids in ensuring that users are given system access in accordance with their defined job responsibilities and are accountable for how they use that access.

questions.text: Which of the following does an information systems (IS) auditor consider the MOST relevant to short-term planning for an IT department?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the role and/or impact of automatization and/or decision-making systems for an organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Allocating resources
choices.justification: The IT department should specifically consider the way resources are allocated in the short term. The information systems (IS) auditor ensures that the resources are being managed adequately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adapting to changing technologies
choices.justification: Investments in IT need to be aligned with top management strategies rather than relevant to short-term planning and focused on technology.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conducting control self-assessments (CSAs)
choices.justification: Conducting control self-assessments (CSAs) is not as critical as allocating resources during short-term planning for the IT department.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evaluating hardware needs
choices.justification: Evaluating hardware needs is not as critical as allocating resources during short-term planning for the IT department.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) audit group has been involved in the integration of an automated audit tool kit with an existing enterprise resource planning system. Due to enterprise resource planning (ERP) performance issues, the audit tool kit is not permitted to go live. What should the IS auditor’s BEST recommendation be?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: Review the implementation of selected integrated controls.
choices.justification: Reviewing the implementation of selected integrated controls validates the technical design and the control objective, but integrated controls over transactional tables consume large resources. They should be reviewed carefully to determine whether they are mandatory or can be implemented and integrated for only specific transactions over the enterprise resource planning application.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Request additional information systems (IS) audit resources.
choices.justification: The inability to implement the automated tool may necessitate additional audit resources because many audits will require more manual effort; however, the first step should be to try to resolve the performance issues.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Request vendor technical support to resolve performance issues.
choices.justification: Requesting vendor technical support to resolve performance issues is a good option, but not the first recommendation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Review the results of stress tests during user acceptance testing.
choices.justification: The appropriate recommendation is to review the results of stress tests conducted during user acceptance testing that demonstrated the performance issues.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor observes a cryptocurrency miner on a staff member’s computer linked to their account. It is discovered that this is against IT policy. Which of the following would BEST help the enterprise detect these issues sooner?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reviewing the computer resources usability and activity report
choices.justification: Cryptocurrency mining consumes a large amount of computing resources; therefore, regularly reviewing usability and activity reports of computing resources will help to detect unauthorized activity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enforcing the software accreditation policy to control software installation
choices.justification: Enforcing the software accreditation policy is a preventive control that will prevent similar incidents in the future but cannot detect the already-implemented tools.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Organizing awareness workshops on IT policy periodically for users
choices.justification: Awareness training will prevent future incidents but cannot help in detecting an incident.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monitoring application logs using an automated log monitoring solution
choices.justification: Although log monitoring helps in detecting, in this case, the tool is not authorized; therefore, the cryptocurrency mining may not be collected by a log monitoring tool.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST way to ensure that organizational policies comply with legal requirements?
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inclusion of a blanket legal statement in each policy
choices.justification: A blanket legal statement in each policy to adhere to all applicable laws and regulations is ineffective because the readers of the policy (internal personnel) will not know which statements are applicable or the specific nature of their requirements. As a result, personnel may lack the knowledge to perform the required activities for legal compliance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Periodic review by subject matter experts
choices.justification: Periodic review of policies by personnel with specific knowledge of regulatory and legal requirements best ensures that organizational policies are aligned with legal requirements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Annual sign-off by senior management on organizational policies
choices.justification: Annual sign-off by senior management on an organization’s policies helps set the tone at the top but does not ensure that the policies comply with regulatory and legal requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Policy alignment to the most restrictive regulations
choices.justification: Aligning policies to the most restrictive regulations may create an unacceptable financial burden for the organization. This can then lead to securing minimal risk systems to the same degree as those containing sensitive customer data and other information protected by legislation.

questions.text: An enterprise selected a vendor to develop and implement a new software system. To ensure that the enterprise’s investment in software is protected, which of the following security clauses is MOST important to include in the master services agreement?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Limitation of liability
choices.justification: A limitation of liability clause protects the financial exposure of the organization but not its software investment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Service level requirements
choices.justification: Service level requirements specify financial penalties for not meeting standards, but these do not address issues of vendor insolvency.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Software escrow
choices.justification: Software escrow clauses in a contract ensure that the software source code will still be available to the organization in the event of a vendor issue, such as insolvency and copyright issues.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Version control
choices.justification: Version control is related to the software development life cycle and not the software investment.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing the IT organization is MOST concerned if the IT steering committee:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: is responsible for project approval and prioritization.
choices.justification: The IT steering committee is responsible for project approval and prioritization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is responsible for developing the long-term IT plan.
choices.justification: The IT steering committee is responsible for oversight of the development of the long-term IT plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: reports the status of IT projects to the board of directors.
choices.justification: The IT steering committee advises the board of directors on the status of developments in IT.

choices.label: D
choices.text: is responsible for determining business goals.
choices.justification: Determining the business goals is the responsibility of senior management and not of the IT steering committee. IT should support business goals and be driven by the business—not the other way around.

questions.text: Which of the following should be considered FIRST when implementing a risk management program?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: An understanding of the organization’s threat, vulnerability and risk profile
choices.justification: Implementing risk management—one of the outcomes of effective information security governance—requires a collective understanding of the organization’s threat, vulnerability and risk profile as a first step.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An understanding of the risk exposures and the potential consequences of compromise
choices.justification: An understanding of risk exposure and potential consequences of compromise can be determined only after there is an understanding of the organization’s threat, vulnerability and risk profile.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A determination of risk management priorities that are based on potential consequences
choices.justification: Risk management priorities that are based on potential consequences (the magnitude of the impact) and the probability of occurrence can only be developed after the organization’s threat, vulnerability and risk profile is determined and evaluated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A risk mitigation strategy sufficient to keep risk consequences at an acceptable level
choices.justification: Risk mitigation priorities are based on the risk profile, risk acceptance levels and potential mitigating controls. These elements provide a basis for the formulation of strategies for risk mitigation sufficient to keep the consequences from risk at an acceptable level. This forms part of the last step in the risk assessment process.

questions.text: Which of the following does an information systems (IS) auditor consider to be MOST important when evaluating an organization’s IT strategy? That it:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: was approved by line management.
choices.justification: A strategic plan is a senior management responsibility and receives input from line managers but is not approved by them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: does not vary from the IT department’s preliminary budget.
choices.justification: The budget should not vary from the plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: complies with procurement procedures.
choices.justification: Procurement procedures are organizational controls, but not a part of strategic planning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: supports the business objectives of the organization.
choices.justification: Strategic planning sets corporate or department objectives into motion. Both long-term and short-term strategic plans should be consistent with the organization’s broader plans and its business objectives for attaining these goals.

questions.text: Who is ULTIMATELY responsible for data classification and protection?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Data steward
choices.justification: Data custodians are responsible for storing and safeguarding the data and include information security personnel, such as systems analysts and computer operators.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Data custodian
choices.justification: Data stewards are oversight or data governance roles within an organization and are responsible for ensuring the quality and fitness for the purpose of the organization's data assets, including the metadata for those data assets.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Data owner
choices.justification: Data owners are responsible for data classification and protection. Their security responsibilities include authorizing access, ensuring that access rules are updated when personnel changes occur and regularly reviewing access rules for the data for which they are responsible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Data controller
choices.justification: The data controllers determine the purposes for which and how data are processed.

questions.text: Effective IT governance ensures that the IT plan is consistent with the organization’s:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: business plan.
choices.justification: To govern IT effectively, IT and business should be moving in the same direction, requiring that the IT plans are aligned with an organization’s business plans.

choices.label: B
choices.text: audit plan.
choices.justification: The audit plan is not part of the IT plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: security plan.
choices.justification: The security plan is not a responsibility of IT and does not need to be consistent with the IT plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: investment plan.
choices.justification: The investment plan is not part of the IT plan.

questions.text: The PRIMARY purpose of an enterprise architecture is to:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: respond to the increasing complexity of IT and modern organizations.
choices.justification: The current focus of enterprise architecture (EA) is to respond to the increasing complexity of IT and modern organizations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: align IT with the business strategy and ensure that IT investments deliver real returns.
choices.justification: The main purpose of EA is to enable the enterprise to most effectively achieve its mission, business strategy and goals through successful alignment between IT and the strategic direction of the business.

choices.label: C
choices.text: facilitate understanding, management and planning for IT investments.
choices.justification: EA involves documenting an organization’s IT assets in a structured manner to facilitate understanding, management and planning for IT investments to facilitate the alignment of IT with the business.

choices.label: D
choices.text: develop roadmaps to enable safe and successful change from the current state to the desired state.
choices.justification: The development of IT strategies and roadmaps comes after establishing strategic alignment between the IT objectives and the business strategies.

questions.text: Which of the following would BEST facilitate the successful implementation of an IT-related framework?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Documenting IT-related policies and procedures
choices.justification: Documenting IT-related policies and procedures is part of the implementation of the framework; it does not guarantee a successful implementation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensuring the appropriate business representation within the framework
choices.justification: Ensuring the appropriate business representation in the IT framework is the best way to align IT framework with business.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Following the latest industry best practices to implement the framework
choices.justification: Regardless of the chosen framework, alignment with business processes is the key to successful implementation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Creating a committee to oversee the framework implementation
choices.justification: Creating a committee to oversee the framework implementation is a good effort, but if there is no representation from the business, the framework will not be successful.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes the goal of business process-driven enterprise architecture? It attempts to better understand an organization in terms of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: its strategies, policies and standards.
choices.justification: An organization’s strategies, policies and standards are used to better understand the core and supporting business processes used in value creation to be able to improve or replace them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: its laws, regulations and contractual commitments.
choices.justification: An organization’s laws, regulations and contractual commitments should be understood to know how stakeholders’ value should be created and preserved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: its organizational structure, roles and responsibilities.
choices.justification: Understanding the organization’s hierarchy, roles and responsibilities helps in knowing how different actors contribute to value creation and preservation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: its core value-adding and supporting processes.
choices.justification: Business process-driven enterprise architecture improves a business by understanding core business processes, their constituent parts and the technology that supports them to progressively redesign and replace them.

questions.text: Which of the following does an information systems (IS) auditor FIRST reference when performing an IS audit?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Implemented procedures
choices.justification: Procedures are implemented in accordance with policy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Approved policies
choices.justification: Policies are high-level documents that represent the corporate philosophy of an organization. Internal standards, procedures and practices are subordinate to policy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Internal standards
choices.justification: Standards are subordinate to policy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Documented practices
choices.justification: Practices are subordinate to policy.

questions.text: IT governance is PRIMARILY the responsibility of the:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: CEO.
choices.justification: The CEO is instrumental in implementing IT governance according to the directions of the board of directors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: board of directors.
choices.justification: IT governance is primarily the responsibility of the executives and shareholders (as represented by the board of directors).

choices.label: C
choices.text: IT steering committee.
choices.justification: The IT steering committee monitors and facilitates deployment of IT resources for specific projects in support of business plans. The IT steering committee enforces governance on behalf of the board of directors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: audit committee.
choices.justification: The audit committee reports to the board of directors and executes governance-related audits. The audit committee should monitor the implementation of audit recommendations.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is evaluating management’s risk assessment of information systems. The IS auditor should FIRST review the:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: controls in place.
choices.justification: The controls are irrelevant until the information systems (IS) auditor knows the threats and risk that the controls are intended to address.

choices.label: B
choices.text: effectiveness of the controls.
choices.justification: The effectiveness of the controls must be measured in relation to the risk (based on assets, threats and vulnerabilities) that the controls are intended to address.

choices.label: C
choices.text: mechanism for monitoring the risk.
choices.justification: The first step must be to determine the risk that is being managed before reviewing the mechanism of monitoring risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: threats/vulnerabilities affecting the assets.
choices.justification: One of the key factors to be considered while assessing the information systems risk is the value of the systems (the assets) and the threats and vulnerabilities affecting the assets. The risk related to the use of information assets should be evaluated in isolation from the installed controls.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes the focus of an information systems (IS) auditor when auditing the enterprise architecture (EA)? The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: follow the overall EA and use the EA as the main source of information.
choices.justification: Different types of evidence should be collected from the enterprise architecture (EA) to assess its conformity with the enterprise’s requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: use the EA organizational requirements as the audit criteria to assess the conformity of the EA against those requirements.
choices.justification: Audit criteria should be established based on and derived from the enterprise’s requirements to assess the conformity of the EA against them.

choices.label: C
choices.text: ensure that the information systems are in line with the EA and meet the enterprise’s objectives.
choices.justification: The aim of conducting an audit on the EA is to know whether information systems are in line with the EA and meet the enterprise’s objectives.

choices.label: D
choices.text: review the EA documentation to assess whether the EA conforms with the enterprise’s requirements.
choices.justification: EA documentation is one of many sources of information that the information systems (IS) auditor uses to collect appropriate and sufficient evidence of conformity.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is performing a review of the software quality management process in an organization. The FIRST step should be to:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verify how the organization complies with the standards.
choices.justification: The auditor needs to know what standards the organization has adopted and then measure compliance with those standards. Determining how the organization follows the standards is secondary to knowing what the standards are.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identify and report the existing controls.
choices.justification: The first step is to know the standards and what policies and procedures are mandated for the organization, then to document the controls and measure compliance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Review the metrics for quality evaluation.
choices.justification: The metrics cannot be reviewed until the auditor has a copy of the standards that describe or require the metrics.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Request all standards adopted by the organization.
choices.justification: Because an audit measures compliance with the standards of the organization, the first step of the review of the software quality management process should be to determine the evaluation criteria in the form of standards adopted by the organization. The evaluation of how well the organization follows its own standards cannot be performed until the information systems (IS) auditor has determined what standards exist.

questions.text: Which of the following is the PRIMARY objective of an enterprise architecture (EA) framework?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: To provide a business case for developing and implementing IT systems and applications
choices.justification: Providing a blueprint for developing and implementing IT systems is an important aspect of enterprise architecture (EA), but it is not the primary objectives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To manage IT-related risk and ensure compliance with legal and regulatory requirements
choices.justification: Although EA can also help manage IT-related risk and ensure compliance with legal and regulatory requirements, these are not the primary objectives of an EA framework.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To ensure the efficient and effective use of IT resources and capabilities
choices.justification: Ensuring the efficient and effective use of IT resources and capabilities is also an important aspect of EA, but it is not the primary objective.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To align IT with business objectives and strategy and facilitate decision making and innovation
choices.justification: EA provides a holistic and integrated view of the enterprise’s IT architecture and aligns IT with its business objectives and strategy. The primary objective of EA is to facilitate decision making, innovation and agility by providing a comprehensive and up-to-date understanding of the enterprise’s IT capabilities and resources and how they can be leveraged to support the enterprise’s goals.

questions.text: After an organization completed a threat and vulnerability analysis as part of a risk assessment, the final report suggested that an intrusion prevention system (IPS) should be installed at the main Internet gateways and that all business units should be separated via a proxy firewall. Which of the following is the BEST method to determine whether the controls should be implemented?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cost-benefit analysis
choices.justification: In a cost-benefit analysis, the total expected purchase and operational/support costs, and a qualitative value for all actions are weighed against the total expected benefits to choose the best technical, most profitable, least expensive or acceptable risk option.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Annual loss expectancy calculation
choices.justification: The annual loss expectancy is the expected monetary loss that is estimated for an asset over a one-year period. It is a useful calculation that should be included in determining the necessity of controls but is not sufficient alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Comparison of the cost of the IPS and firewall and the cost of the business systems
choices.justification: The cost of the hardware assets should be compared to the total value of the information that the asset protects, including the cost of the systems where the data reside and across which data are transmitted. This comparison is useful as part of the cost-benefit analysis, but not sufficient on its own to determine whether the controls should be implemented.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Business impact analysis (BIA)
choices.justification: A business impact analysis (BIA) predicts the consequences of disruption of a business function and process and gathers information needed to develop recovery strategies. The BIA is only one part of the cost-benefit analysis.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is performing a review of an organization’s governance model. Which of the following should be of MOST concern to the auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Determine whether the organization has defined ownership of IT risk, controls, and standards

choices.label: A
choices.text: The information security policy is not periodically reviewed by senior management.
choices.justification: Data security policies should be reviewed/refreshed once every year to reflect changes in the organization’s environment. Policies are fundamental to the organization’s governance structure, and, therefore, this is the greatest concern.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A policy ensuring systems are patched in a timely manner does not exist.
choices.justification: Although it is a concern that there is no policy related to system patching, the greater concern is that the information security policy is not reviewed periodically by senior management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The audit committee did not review the organization’s mission statement.
choices.justification: Mission statements tend to be long term because they are strategic in nature and are established by the board of directors and management. This is not the information systems (IS) auditor’s greatest concern because proper governance oversight can lead to meeting the objectives of the organization mission statement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An organizational policy related to information asset protection does not exist.
choices.justification: Although it is a concern that there is no policy related to the protection of information assets, the greater concern is that the security policy is not reviewed periodically by senior management because top-level support is fundamental to information security governance.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor found that the enterprise architecture (EA) recently adopted by an organization has an adequate current-state representation. However, the organization has started a separate project to develop a future-state representation. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recommend that this separate project be completed as soon as possible.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor does not ordinarily provide input on the timing of projects, but rather provides an assessment of the current environment. The most critical issue in this scenario is that the enterprise architecture (EA) is undergoing change, so the IS auditor should be most concerned with reporting this issue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Report this issue as a finding in the audit report.
choices.justification: It is critical for the EA to include the future state because the gap between the current state and the future state will determine IT strategic and tactical plans. If the EA does not include a future-state representation, it is not complete, and this issue should be reported as a finding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recommend the adoption of the Zachmann framework.
choices.justification: The organization is free to choose any EA framework, and the IS auditor should not recommend a specific framework.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rescope the audit to include the separate project as part of the current audit.
choices.justification: Changing the scope of an audit to include the secondary project is not required, although a follow-up audit may be desired.

questions.text: Measures of security risk should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: address all of the network risk.
choices.justification: Measures of security risk should not be limited to network risk, but rather focus on those areas with the highest criticality so that maximum risk reduction is achieved at the lowest possible cost.

choices.label: B
choices.text: be tracked over time against the IT strategic plan.
choices.justification: IT strategic plans are not granular enough to provide appropriate measures. Objective metrics must be tracked over time against measurable goals; thus, the management of risk is enhanced by comparing today’s results against results from last week, last month and last quarter. Risk measures will profile assets on a network to objectively measure vulnerability risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: consider the entire IT environment.
choices.justification: When assessing IT security risk, it is important to consider the entire IT environment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: result in the identification of vulnerability tolerances.
choices.justification: Measures of security risk do not identify tolerances.

questions.text: When reviewing an organization’s strategic IT plan, an information systems (IS) auditor should expect to find:
questions.taskStatement: Determine whether the organization has defined ownership of IT risk, controls, and standards

choices.label: A
choices.text: an assessment of the fit of the organization’s application portfolio with business objectives.
choices.justification: An assessment of how well an organization’s application portfolio supports the organization’s business objectives is a key component of the overall IT strategic planning process. This assessment drives the demand side of IT planning and should convert into a set of strategic IT intentions. Further assessment can then be made of how well the overall IT organization, encompassing applications, infrastructure, services, management processes, etc. can support the business objectives. The purpose of an IT strategic plan is to set out how IT will be used to achieve or support an organization’s business objectives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: actions to reduce hardware procurement cost.
choices.justification: Operational efficiency initiatives, including cost reduction of purchasing and maintenance activities of systems, belong to tactical planning, not strategic planning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a listing of approved suppliers of IT contract resources.
choices.justification: A list of approved suppliers of IT contract resources is a tactical, rather than a strategic, concern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a description of the technical architecture for the organization’s network perimeter security.
choices.justification: An IT strategic plan does not normally include detail of a specific technical architecture.

questions.text: When reviewing the IT strategic planning process, an information systems (IS) auditor should ensure that the plan:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: incorporates state-of-the-art technology.
choices.justification: The plan does not need to address state-of-the-art technology; the decision to implement new technology is dependent on the approach to risk and management strategy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: addresses the required operational controls.
choices.justification: The plan does not need to address operational controls because those are too granular for strategic planning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: articulates the IT mission and vision.
choices.justification: The IT strategic plan must include a clear articulation of the IT mission and vision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: specifies project management practices.
choices.justification: The plan should be implemented with proper project management, but the plan does not need to address project management practices.

questions.text: When reviewing the development of information security policies, the PRIMARY focus of an information systems (IS) auditor should be on assuring that these policies:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: are aligned with globally accepted industry good practices.
choices.justification: An organization is not required to base its IT policies on industry good practices. Policies must be based on the culture and business requirements of the organization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: are approved by the board of directors and senior management.
choices.justification: It is essential that policies be approved; however, that is not the primary focus during the development of the policies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: strike a balance between business and security requirements.
choices.justification: Because information security policies must be aligned with an organization’s business and security objectives, this is the primary focus of the information systems (IS) auditor when reviewing the development of information security policies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: provide direction for implementing security procedures.
choices.justification: Policies cannot provide direction if they are not aligned with business requirements.

questions.text: Which of the following should an information systems (IS) auditor recommend to BEST enforce alignment of an IT project portfolio with strategic organizational priorities?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Define a balanced scorecard (BSC) for measuring performance.
choices.justification: Measures such as a balanced scorecard (BSC) are helpful, but do not guarantee that the projects are aligned with business strategy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Consider user satisfaction in the key performance indicators (KPIs).
choices.justification: Key performance indicators (KPIs)are helpful to monitor and measure IT performance, but they do not guarantee that the projects are aligned with business strategy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Select projects according to business benefits and risk.
choices.justification: Prioritization of projects on the basis of their expected benefit(s) to business, and the related risk, is the best measure for achieving alignment of the project portfolio to an organization’s strategic priorities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Modify the yearly process of defining the project portfolio.
choices.justification: Modifying the yearly process of the project portfolio definition might improve the situation, but only if the portfolio definition process is closely tied to organizational strategies.

questions.text: When an organization’s disaster recovery plan (DRP) has a reciprocal agreement, which of the following risk treatment approaches is being applied?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transfer
choices.justification: Risk transfer is the transference of risk to a third party (e.g., buying insurance for activities that pose a risk).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mitigation
choices.justification: A reciprocal agreement in which two organizations agree to provide computing resources to each other in the event of a disaster is a form of risk mitigation. This usually works well if both organizations have similar information processing facilities. Because the intended effect of reciprocal agreements is to have a functional disaster recovery plan (DRP), it is a risk mitigation strategy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Avoidance
choices.justification: Risk avoidance is the decision to cease operations or activities that give rise to a risk. For example, an organization may stop accepting credit card payments to avoid the risk of credit card information disclosure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acceptance
choices.justification: Risk acceptance occurs when an organization decides to accept the risk as it is and to do nothing to mitigate or transfer it.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST enabler for strategic alignment between business and IT?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maturity model
choices.justification: Maturity models enable assessment of current process capability and can be used for process improvement and measuring the maturity of the alignment process, but they do not directly enable strategic alignment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Goals and metrics
choices.justification: Goals and metrics ensure that IT goals are set based on business goals, and they are the best enablers of strategic alignment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Control objectives
choices.justification: Control objectives are statements that address how risk is going to be effectively managed by an organization. Control objectives, therefore, facilitate the implementation of controls in the related processes according to business requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Responsible, accountable, consulted, informed (RACI) chart
choices.justification: Responsible, accountable, consulted, informed (RACI) charts enable the assignment of responsibility to key function areas but do not ensure strategic alignment.

questions.text: A financial enterprise has had difficulties establishing clear responsibilities between its IT strategy committee and its IT steering committee. Which of the following responsibilities would MOST likely be assigned to its IT steering committee?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Approving IT project plans and budgets
choices.justification: An IT steering committee typically has a variety of responsibilities, including approving IT project plans and budgets. Issues related to business objectives, risk and governance are responsibilities that are generally assigned to an IT strategy committee because it provides insight and advice to the board.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aligning IT to business objectives
choices.justification: Aligning IT to business objectives is a task usually assigned to an IT strategy committee. The steering committee is more involved in approval and monitoring of individual projects and budgets.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Advising on IT compliance risk
choices.justification: Issues related to IT compliance risk are tasks usually assigned to an IT strategy committee. The steering committee is more involved in approval and monitoring of individual projects and budgets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Promoting IT governance practices
choices.justification: IT governance is a task usually assigned to an IT strategy committee. The steering committee is more involved in approval and monitoring of individual projects and budgets.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is assigned to review IT structures and activities recently outsourced to various providers. Which of the following should the IS auditor determine FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: An audit clause is present in all contracts.
choices.justification: All choices are important, but the first step is to ensure that the contracts support the business—only then can an audit process be valuable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The service level agreement (SLA) of each contract is substantiated by appropriate key performance indicators (KPIs).
choices.justification: All service level agreements (SLAs) should be measurable and reinforced through key performance indicators (KPIs)—but the first step is to ensure that the SLAs are aligned with business requirements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The contractual warranties of the providers support the business needs of the organization.
choices.justification: The primary requirement is for the services provided by the outsource supplier to meet the needs of the business.

choices.label: D
choices.text: At contract termination, support is guaranteed by each outsourcer for new outsourcers.
choices.justification: Having appropriate controls in place for contract termination are important, but first the information systems (IS) auditor must be focused on the requirement of the supplier to meet business needs.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviews an organizational chart PRIMARILY for:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: understanding of the complexity of the organizational structure.
choices.justification: Understanding the complexity of the organizational structure is not the primary reason to review an organizational chart because the chart does not necessarily depict the complexity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: investigating various communication channels.
choices.justification: The organizational chart is a key tool for an auditor to understand roles and responsibilities and reporting lines but is not used for examining communications channels.

choices.label: C
choices.text: understanding the responsibilities and authority of individuals.
choices.justification: An organizational chart provides information about the responsibilities and authority of individuals in the organization. This helps an information systems (IS) auditor to know if there is a proper segregation of functions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: investigating the network connected to different employees.
choices.justification: A network diagram provides information about the usage of various communication channels and indicates the connection of users to the network.

questions.text: A driver of IT governance, transparency of IT cost, value and risk is primarily achieved through:
questions.taskStatement: Determine whether the organization has defined ownership of IT risk, controls, and standards

choices.label: A
choices.text: performance measurement.
choices.justification: Performance measurement includes setting and monitoring measurable objectives that the IT processes need to achieve to deliver (process outcome), and how they deliver it (process capability and performance). Transparency is primarily achieved through performance measurement because it provides information to the stakeholders on how well the enterprise is performing when compared to objectives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: strategic alignment.
choices.justification: Strategic alignment primarily focuses on ensuring linkage of business and IT plans, not on transparency.

choices.label: C
choices.text: value delivery.
choices.justification: Value delivery is about executing the value proposition throughout the delivery cycle. Value delivery ensures that IT investments deliver on promised values but does not ensure transparency of investment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: resource management.
choices.justification: Resource management is about the optimal investment in and proper management of critical IT resources but does not ensure transparency of IT investments.

questions.text: Sharing risk is a key factor in which of the following methods of managing risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transferring risk
choices.justification: Transferring risk (e.g., by taking an insurance policy) is a way to share risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tolerating risk
choices.justification: Tolerating risk means that the risk is accepted, but not shared.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Terminating risk
choices.justification: Terminating risk does not involve sharing the risk because the organization has chosen to terminate the process associated with the risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Treating risk
choices.justification: There are several ways of treating or controlling the risk, which may involve reducing or sharing the risk, but this is not as precise an answer as transferring the risk.

questions.text: An organization has a well-established risk management process. Which of the following risk management practices would MOST likely expose the organization to the greatest amount of compliance risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Risk reduction
choices.justification: Risk reduction is a term synonymous with risk mitigation. Risk reduction treats the risk, including compliance risk and lowers it to a level commensurate with the organization’s risk appetite.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Risk transfer
choices.justification: Risk transfer spreads the risk by either transferring some or all of it to a third party (e.g., through insurance or outsourcing) or applying the resources of multiple teams to hedge against possible losses. Risk transfer typically addresses financial risk and does not always address compliance risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk avoidance
choices.justification: Risk avoidance does not expose the organization to compliance risk because the business practice that caused the inherent risk to exist is no longer being pursued or is eliminated altogether.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk mitigation
choices.justification: mitigating risk will still expose the organization to a certain amount of risk, risk mitigation lowers risk, including compliance risk, to a level commensurate with the organization’s risk appetite.

questions.text: When developing a security architecture, which of the following steps should be executed FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Developing security procedures
choices.justification: Policy is used to provide direction for procedures, standards and baselines. Therefore, developing security procedures should be executed only after defining a security policy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defining a security policy
choices.justification: Defining a security policy for information and related technology is the first step toward building a security architecture. A security policy communicates a coherent security standard to users, management and technical staff. Security policies often set the stage in terms of the tools and procedures that are needed for an organization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Specifying an access control methodology
choices.justification: Specifying an access control methodology is an implementation concern and should be executed only after defining a security policy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defining roles and responsibilities
choices.justification: Defining roles and responsibilities should be executed only after defining a security policy.

questions.text: Which of the following is the PRIMARY critical success factor of a control self-assessment (CSA) program?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assigning a knowledgeable and experienced information systems (IS) auditor as a facilitator for the CSA program
choices.justification: The assignment of the suitable information systems (IS) auditor to facilitate the CSA program would come after identifying the primary objective of the business unit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Conducting a meeting with the business unit representatives, including relevant staff and management
choices.justification: The meeting with the business unit representatives at the beginning of the process is crucial to identify the business unit’s primary objective to determine the reliability of the internal control system afterward.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Developing the measures of success for each CSA phase (planning, implementation and monitoring)
choices.justification: The development of the measures of success for each CSA phase would be after identifying the primary objective of the business unit and while designing the program itself.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identifying the actions needed to increase the likelihood of achieving the business unit’s objective
choices.justification: The identification of the actions needed to increase the likelihood of achieving the primary objective of the business unit would take place through recommendations at the closing up of the CSA program.

questions.text: When implementing an IT governance framework in an organization, the MOST important objective is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: IT alignment with the business.
choices.justification: The goals of IT governance are to improve IT performance, deliver optimum business value and ensure regulatory compliance. The key practice in support of these goals is the strategic alignment of IT with the business. To achieve alignment, all other choices need to be tied to business practices and strategies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: accountability.
choices.justification: Accountability is important, but the most important objective of IT governance is to ensure that IT investment and oversight are aligned with business requirements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: value realization with IT.
choices.justification: IT must demonstrate value to the organization, but this value is dependent on the ability of IT to align with, and support, business requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: enhancing the return on IT investments.
choices.justification: Enhancing return is a requirement of the IT governance framework, but this requirement is only demonstrated through aligning IT with business requirements.

questions.text: Enterprise risk appetite is BEST established by:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: the chief legal officer (CLO).
choices.justification: The chief legal officer (CLO) is mandated to ensure that the legal risk of the enterprise is minimized. Although CLOs can give guidance regarding legal issues on the policy, they cannot determine the risk appetite.

choices.label: B
choices.text: security management.
choices.justification: Security management covers all aspects of protecting an organization's assets, including computers, people, buildings and other assets, against risk. The security management team is therefore concerned with managing the security posture but not with determining the posture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the audit committee.
choices.justification: The primary purpose of the audit committee is to provide oversight of the financial reporting process, the audit process, the company's system of internal controls and compliance with laws and regulations. The audit committee is not responsible for setting the risk tolerance or appetite of the enterprise.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the board of directors.
choices.justification: Risk appetite is set by the board of directors and should align with strategic objectives that the enterprise wants to achieve. Strategic objectives and/or highly ambitious targets that are more difficult to reach typically require a higher risk appetite.

questions.text: To aid management in achieving IT and business alignment, an information systems (IS) auditor should recommend the use of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: control self-assessments (CSAs).
choices.justification: Control self-assessments (CSAs) are used to improve monitoring of security controls but are not used to align IT with organizational objectives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a business impact analysis (BIA).
choices.justification: A business impact analysis (BIA) is used to calculate the impact on the business in the event of an incident that affects business operations, but it is not used to align IT with organizational objectives.

choices.label: C
choices.text: an IT balanced scorecard (BSC).
choices.justification: An IT balanced scorecard (BSC) provides the bridge between IT objectives and business objectives by supplementing the traditional financial evaluation with measures to evaluate customer satisfaction, internal processes and the ability to innovate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: business process reengineering.
choices.justification: Business process reengineering is an excellent tool to review and improve business processes but is not focused on aligning IT with organizational objectives.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor identifies that reports on product profitability produced by an organization’s finance and marketing departments give different results. Further investigation reveals that the product definition being used by the two departments is different. What should the IS auditor recommend?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: User acceptance testing occurs for all reports before release into production.
choices.justification: User acceptance testing supports the process of ensuring that the system is production ready and satisfies all documented requirements. Recommending that user acceptance testing occur for all reports before release into production does not address the root cause of the problem described.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Organizational data governance practices are put in place.
choices.justification: Data governance directly addresses the problem. An organizationwide approach is needed to achieve effective management of data assets and reporting standards. This includes enforcing standard definitions of data elements, which is part of a data governance initiative.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Standard software tools are used for report development.
choices.justification: The use of standard software tools is one way of achieving uniformity across the different reports and makes support of implemented reports easier. Therefore, recommending standard software tools be used for report development does not address the root cause of the problem described.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Management signs off on requirements for new reports.
choices.justification: The sign-off by management indicates that they believe all requirements, functions and features have been identified and documented. Recommending that management sign off on requirements for new reports does not address the root cause of the problem described.

questions.text: The MOST important element for the effective design of an information security policy is the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: threat landscape.
choices.justification: The threat landscape is dynamic. It should be considered when developing policy, but it is not the primary factor because policy is not meant to change as often as the threat landscape.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prior security incidents.
choices.justification: Prior security incidents may provide insight into the risk appetite statement; however, they are more likely to affect security standards and procedures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: emerging technologies.
choices.justification: Emerging technologies are continually evolving. They should be considered when developing policy, but they are not the primary factor because policy is not meant to change as often as technology.

choices.label: D
choices.text: enterprise risk appetite.
choices.justification: The risk appetite is the amount of risk on a broad level that an entity is willing to accept in pursuit of its mission to meet its strategic objectives. The purpose of the information security policy is to manage information risk to an acceptable level, so that the policy is principally aligned with the risk appetite.

questions.text: Who is MOST likely responsible for implementing a technical control to mitigate an identified risk?
questions.taskStatement: Determine whether the organization has defined ownership of IT risk, controls, and standards

choices.label: A
choices.text: Custodian
choices.justification: The custodian is a data administrator and manages the technical controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compliance officer
choices.justification: The compliance officer ensures compliance with various laws and regulations, which may or may not include technical controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk owner
choices.justification: The implementation of a technical control requires that the control is surrounded by proper procedures, the personnel who operate it are adequately trained, a person is assigned ownership of the control (often the person who owns the risk), and the control is monitored and tested to ensure its correct operation and effectiveness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Senior management
choices.justification: Senior management is ultimately legally responsible for the risk.

questions.text: After the merger of two organizations, multiple self-developed legacy applications from both organizations are to be replaced by a new common platform. Which of the following is the GREATEST risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Project management and progress reporting are combined in a project management office that is driven by external consultants.
choices.justification: In postmerger integration programs, it is common to form project management offices (often staffed with external experts) to ensure standardized and comparable information levels in the planning and reporting structures, and to centralize dependencies of project deliverables or resources.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The replacement effort consists of several independent projects without integrating the resource allocation in a portfolio management approach.
choices.justification: The efforts should be consolidated to ensure alignment with the overall strategy of the postmerger organization. If resource allocation is not centralized, the separate projects are at risk of overestimating the availability of key knowledge resources for the in-house-developed legacy applications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The resources of each of the organizations are inefficiently allocated while they are being familiarized with the other organization’s legacy systems.
choices.justification: The development of new integrated systems can require some knowledge of the legacy systems to gain an understanding of each business process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The new platform will force the business areas of both organizations to change their work processes, which will result in extensive training needs.
choices.justification: In most cases, mergers result in application changes and, thus, in training needs as organizations and processes change to leverage the intended synergy effects of the merger.

questions.text: Which of the following choices is the PRIMARY benefit of requiring a steering committee to oversee IT investment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: To conduct a feasibility study to demonstrate IT value
choices.justification: A steering committee may use a feasibility study in its reviews; however, it is not responsible for performing/conducting the study.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To ensure that investments are made according to business requirements
choices.justification: A steering committee consists of representatives from the business and IT and ensures that IT investment is based on business objectives rather than on IT priorities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To ensure that proper security controls are enforced
choices.justification: The steering committee is not responsible for enforcing security controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To ensure that a standard development methodology is implemented
choices.justification: The steering committee is not responsible for implementing development methodologies.

questions.text: Which of the following should be the MOST important consideration when deciding on areas of priority for IT governance implementations?
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: Process maturity
choices.justification: The level of process maturity evolves as the implementation of the IT governance program occurs and may feed into the decision-making process. Those areas that represent real risk to the business should be given priority.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Performance indicators
choices.justification: The level of process performance demonstrates the effectiveness of the program but is not the means to establish priorities for governance. Those areas that represent real risk to the business should be given priority.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Business risk
choices.justification: Priority should be given to those business areas of the enterprise operations that face real risk based on the probability of occurrence and the magnitude of the impact.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assurance reports
choices.justification: Assurance (audit) reports provide assurance of the effectiveness of the implementation of governance but do not determine the priorities for a program. Those areas that represent real risk to the business should be given priority.

questions.text: Establishing the level of acceptable risk is the responsibility of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: quality assurance management.
choices.justification: Quality assurance (QA) is concerned with reliability and consistency of processes. The QA team is not responsible for determining an acceptable risk level.

choices.label: B
choices.text: senior business management.
choices.justification: Senior management should establish the acceptable risk level because it has the ultimate or final responsibility for the effective and efficient operation of the organization as senior management of the business process. The person can be the QA manager, chief information officer (CIO) or chief security officer (CSO), if they are the business manager. Responsibility for establishing the level of acceptable risk rests with the business manager.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the chief information officer.
choices.justification: The establishment of acceptable risk levels is a senior business management responsibility. The CIO is the most senior official of the enterprise who is accountable for IT advocacy; aligning IT and business strategies; and planning, resourcing and managing the delivery of IT services, information and the deployment of associated human resources. The CIO is rarely the person who determines acceptable risk levels because this could be a conflict of interest unless the CIO is the senior business process owner.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the chief security officer.
choices.justification: The establishment of acceptable risk levels is a senior business management responsibility. The CSO is responsible for enforcing the decisions of the senior management team unless the CIO is the business process manager.

questions.text: Which of the following assures an enterprise of the existence and effectiveness of internal controls relative to the service provided by a third party?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Current service level agreement (SLA)
choices.justification: A service level agreement (SLA) defines the contracted level of service; however, it does not provide assurance related to internal controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recent independent third-party audit report
choices.justification: An independent third-party audit report, such as a Statements on Standards for Attestation Engagements 16, provides assurance of the existence and effectiveness of internal controls at the third party.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Current business continuity plan (BCP) procedures
choices.justification: Although a business continuity plan (BCP) is essential, it does not provide assurance related to internal controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recent disaster recovery plan (DRP) test report
choices.justification: Although a disaster recovery plan (DRP) is essential, it does not provide assurance related to internal controls.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is asked to review a contract for a vendor being considered to provide data center services. Which is the BEST way to determine whether the terms of the contract are adhered to after the contract is signed?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Require the vendor to provide monthly status reports.
choices.justification: Although providing monthly status reports may show that the vendor is meeting contract terms, without independent verification these data may not be reliable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Have periodic meetings with the client IT manager.
choices.justification: Having periodic meetings with the client IT manager will assist with understanding the current relationship with the vendor, but meetings may not include vendor audit reports, status reports and other information that a periodic audit review would take into consideration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conduct periodic audit reviews of the vendor.
choices.justification: Conducting periodic reviews of the vendor ensures that the agreements within the contract are completed in a satisfactory manner. Without future audit reviews after the contract is signed, service level agreements and the client’s requirements for security controls may become less of a focus for the vendor, and the results may slip. Periodic audit reviews allow the client to look at the vendor’s current state to ensure that the vendor is one with which they want to continue to work.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Require that performance parameters be stated within the contract.
choices.justification: Requiring that performance parameters be stated within the contract is important, but only if periodic reviews are performed to determine that performance parameters are met.

questions.text: During an audit, which of the following situations are MOST concerning for an organization that significantly outsources information systems (IS) processing to a private network?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: The contract does not contain a right-to-audit clause for the third party.
choices.justification: In the case of outsourcing to a private network, the organization should ensure that the third party has a minimum set of IT security controls in place and that they are operating effectively. The absence of a right-to-audit clause in the contract impacts the information systems (IS) auditor’s ability to perform the IS audit. Hence, the IS auditor is most concerned with such a situation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The contract was not reviewed by an information security subject matter expert prior to signing.
choices.justification: Having an information security subject matter expert review a contract is a good practice, but it is not a requirement in all industries.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The IS outsourcing guidelines are not approved by the board of directors.
choices.justification: Approval of the IS outsourcing guidelines by the board is a good practice of governance, and lack of approval is an audit issue. However, it does not impact the IS auditor’s ability to perform the IS audit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is a lack of well-defined IS performance evaluation procedures.
choices.justification: Lack of well-defined procedures does not enable objective evaluation of IS performance and is an audit issue. However, it does not result in major risk or repercussions and does not impact the IS auditor’s ability to perform an IS audit.

questions.text: To address the risk of operations staff’s failure to perform the daily backup, management requires that the systems administrator sign off on the daily backup. This is an example of risk:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: avoidance.
choices.justification: Risk avoidance is a strategy that provides for not implementing certain activities or processes that would incur risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: transfer.
choices.justification: Risk transfer is the strategy that provides for sharing risk with partners or purchasing insurance coverage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: mitigation.
choices.justification: Risk mitigation is the strategy that provides for the definition and implementation of controls to address the risk described. By requiring the system’s administrator to sign off on the completion of the backups, this is an administrative control that can be validated for compliance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: acceptance.
choices.justification: Risk acceptance is a strategy that provides for formal acknowledgment of the existence of a risk but not taking any action to reduce the risk, and the monitoring of that risk.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST supports the prioritization of new IT projects?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Internal control self-assessment (CSA)
choices.justification: Internal control self-assessment (CSA) may highlight noncompliance with the current policy but may not necessarily be the best source for driving the prioritization of IT projects.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Information systems (IS) audit
choices.justification: Like internal CSA, information systems (IS) audits are mostly a detective control and may provide only part of the picture for the prioritization of IT projects.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Investment portfolio analysis
choices.justification: It is most desirable to conduct an investment portfolio analysis, which not only presents a clear focus on investment strategy but also provides the rationale for terminating nonperforming IT projects.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Business risk assessment
choices.justification: Business risk analysis is part of the investment portfolio analysis but, by itself, is not the best method for prioritizing new IT projects.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is assessing services provided by an internet service provider (ISP) during an IS compliance audit of a nationwide corporation that operates a governmental program. Which of the following is MOST important?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Review the request for proposal.
choices.justification: Because the request for proposal is not the contracted agreement, it is more relevant to review the terms of the service level agreement (SLA).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Review monthly performance reports generated by the ISP.
choices.justification: The reports from the (internet service provider (ISP)) are indirect evidence that may require further review to ensure accuracy and completeness.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Review the service level agreement (SLA).
choices.justification: An SLA provides the basis for an adequate assessment of the degree to which the provider is meeting the level of agreed-on service.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Research other clients of the ISP.
choices.justification: The services provided to other clients of the ISP are irrelevant to the information systems (IS) auditor.

questions.text: In the context of effective information security governance, the PRIMARY objective of value delivery is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: optimize security investments in support of business objectives.
choices.justification: Information security governance provides strategic direction for security activities and ensures that business objectives are achieved. Therefore, in the context of effective information security governance, value delivery is implemented to ensure optimization of security investments in support of business objectives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: implement a standard set of security practices.
choices.justification: The tools and techniques for implementing value delivery include implementation of a standard set of security practices; however, implementation of standards is a means to achieve the objective of supporting value delivery, not the objective itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: institute a standards-based solution.
choices.justification: Value delivery may be supported with standards-based solutions, but the use of standards-based solutions is not the goal of value delivery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: implement a continuous improvement culture.
