choices.justification: Continuous improvement culture in relation to a security program is a process, not an objective.

questions.text: When separation of duties concerns exist between IT support staff and end users, what is a suitable compensating control?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Restricting physical access to computing equipment
choices.justification: IT support staff usually require physical access to computing equipment to perform their job functions. It would not be reasonable to remove this access.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reviewing transaction and application logs
choices.justification: Reviewing transaction and application logs directly addresses the threat posed by poor separation of duties. The review is a means of detecting inappropriate behavior and discourages abuse, because people who may otherwise be tempted to exploit the situation are aware of the likelihood of being caught.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Performing background checks prior to hiring IT staff
choices.justification: Performing background checks is a useful control to ensure that IT staff are trustworthy and competent but does not directly address the lack of an optimal separation of duties.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Locking user sessions after a specified period of inactivity
choices.justification: Locking user sessions after a specified period of inactivity acts to prevent unauthorized users from gaining system access, but the issue of a lack of separation of duties is more the misuse (deliberately or inadvertently) of access privileges that have officially been granted.

questions.text: During an audit, an information systems (IS) auditor notices that the IT department of a medium-sized organization has no separate risk management function, and the organization’s operational risk documentation only contains a few broadly described types of IT risk. What is the MOST appropriate recommendation in this situation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Create an IT risk management department and establish an IT risk framework with the aid of external risk management experts.
choices.justification: A medium-sized organization does not normally have a separate IT risk management department due to limited resources. Moreover, the IT risk can usually be managed without the assistance of external experts.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use common industry standard aids to divide the existing risk documentation into several individual types of risk, which will be easier to handle.
choices.justification: Although common risk may be covered by industry standards, they cannot address the specific situation of an organization. Individual types of risk will not be discovered without a detailed assessment from within the organization. Splitting the one risk position into several is not sufficient to manage IT risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No recommendation is necessary because the current approach is appropriate for a medium-sized organization.
choices.justification: The auditor should recommend a formal IT risk management effort because a few broadly described types of IT risk contained in the operational risk documentation are insufficient. The failure to demonstrate responsible IT risk management may be a liability for the organization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Establish regular IT risk management meetings to identify and assess risk and create a mitigation plan as input to the organization’s risk management.
choices.justification: Establishing regular IT risk management meetings is the best way to identify and assess IT-related risk in a medium-sized organization, to address responsibilities with the respective management and to keep the risk register and mitigation plans up to date.

questions.text: The PRIMARY benefit of an enterprise architecture initiative is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: enable the enterprise to invest in the most appropriate technology.
choices.justification: The primary focus of the enterprise architecture (EA) is to ensure that technology investments are consistent with the platform, data and development standards of the IT organization; therefore, the goal of the EA is to help the organization to implement the technology that is most effective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ensure security controls are implemented on critical platforms.
choices.justification: Ensuring that security controls are implemented on critical platforms is important, but this is not the function of the EA. The EA may be concerned with the design of security controls; however, the EA does not help to ensure that they were implemented. The primary focus of the EA is to ensure that technology investments are consistent with the platform, data and development standards of the IT organization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: allow development teams to be more responsive to business requirements.
choices.justification: Although the EA process may enable development teams to be more efficient, because they are creating solutions based on standard platforms using standard programming languages and methods, the more critical benefit of the EA is to provide guidance for IT investments of all types, which encompasses much more than software development.

choices.label: D
choices.text: provide business units with greater autonomy to select IT solutions that fit their needs.
choices.justification: A primary focus of the EA is to define standard platforms, databases and interfaces. Business units that invest in technology need to select IT solutions that meet their business needs and are compatible with the EA of the enterprise. There may be instances when a proposed solution works better for a business unit but is not at all consistent with the EA of the enterprise, so there would be a need to compromise to ensure that the application can be supported by IT. Overall, the EA would restrict the ability of business units in terms of the potential IT systems that they may wish to implement. The support requirements would not be affected in this case.

questions.text: Which of the following is the initial step in creating a firewall policy?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: A cost-benefit analysis of methods for securing the applications
choices.justification: Identifying methods to protect against identified vulnerabilities and their comparative cost-benefit analysis is the third step.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identification of network applications to be externally accessed
choices.justification: Identification of the applications required across the network should be the initial step. After identification, depending on the physical location of these applications in the network and the network model, the person in charge is able to understand the need for, and possible methods of, controlling access to these applications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identification of vulnerabilities associated with network applications to be externally accessed
choices.justification: Having identified the externally accessed applications, the second step is to identify vulnerabilities (weaknesses) associated with the network applications.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Creation of an application traffic matrix showing protection methods
choices.justification: The fourth step is to analyze the application traffic and create a matrix showing how each type of traffic will be protected.

questions.text: Which of the following is an implementation risk within the process of decision support systems (DSSs)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Management control
choices.justification: Management control is not a type of risk, but a characteristic of a decision support system (DSS).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Semi-structured dimensions
choices.justification: Semi-structured dimensions is not a type of risk, but a characteristic of a DSS.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inability to specify purpose and usage patterns
choices.justification: The inability to specify purpose and usage patterns is a risk that developers need to anticipate while implementing a DSS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Changes in decision processes
choices.justification: Changes in decision processes are not a type of risk, but a characteristic of a DSS.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST critical for the successful implementation and maintenance of a security policy?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assimilation of the framework and intent of a written security policy by all appropriate parties
choices.justification: Assimilation of the framework and intent of a written security policy by all levels of management and users of the system are critical to the successful implementation and maintenance of the security policy. If a policy is not assimilated into daily actions, it will not be effective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Management support and approval for the implementation and maintenance of a security policy
choices.justification: Management support and commitment is, no doubt, important, but for successful implementation and maintenance of a security policy, educating the users on the importance of security is paramount.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enforcement of security rules by providing punitive actions for any violation of security rules
choices.justification: Punitive actions are needed to enforce the policy but are not the key to successful implementation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stringent implementation, monitoring and enforcing of rules by the security officer through access control software
choices.justification: The stringent implementation, monitoring and enforcing of rules by the security officer through access control software, and provision for punitive actions for violation of security rules is important, but it is dependent on the support and education of management and users on the importance of security.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST important for an information systems (IS) auditor to consider when reviewing a service level agreement with an external IT service provider?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Payment terms
choices.justification: Payment terms are typically included in the master agreement rather than in the service level agreement (SLA).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Uptime guarantee
choices.justification: The most important element of an SLA is the measurable terms of performance, such as uptime agreements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Indemnification clause
choices.justification: The indemnification clause is typically included in the master agreement rather than in the SLA.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Default resolution
choices.justification: The default resolution only applies in case of a default of the SLA; therefore, it is more important to review the performance conditions of the SLA.

questions.text: The initial step in establishing an information security program is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate supply chains for IT risk factors and integrity issues

choices.label: A
choices.text: developing and implementing an information security standards manual.
choices.justification: The security program is driven by policy and the standards are driven by the program. The initial step is to have a policy and ensure that the program is based on the policy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: performing a comprehensive security control review by the information systems (IS) auditor.
choices.justification: Audit and monitoring of controls related to the program can only come after the program is set up.

choices.label: C
choices.text: adopting an enterprise information security policy statement.
choices.justification: A policy statement reflects the intent and support provided by executive management for proper security and establishes a starting point for developing the security program.

choices.label: D
choices.text: purchasing security access control software.
choices.justification: Access control software is an important security control but only after the policy and program are defined.

questions.text: Which of the following is normally a responsibility of the chief information security officer (CISO)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Periodically reviewing and evaluating the security policy
choices.justification: The role of the chief information security officer (CISO) is to ensure that the corporate security policy and controls are adequate to prevent unauthorized access to the enterprise assets, including data, programs and equipment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Executing user application and software testing and evaluation
choices.justification: User application and other software testing and evaluation normally are the responsibility of the staff assigned to development and maintenance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Granting and revoking user access to IT resources
choices.justification: Granting and revoking access to IT resources is usually a function of system, network or database administrators.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Approving access to data and applications
choices.justification: Approval of access to data and applications is the duty of the data or application owner.

questions.text: Responsibility for the governance of IT should rest with the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate supply chains for IT risk factors and integrity issues

choices.label: A
choices.text: IT strategy committee.
choices.justification: The IT strategy committee plays a significant role in the successful implementation of IT governance within an organization, but the ultimate responsibility resides with the board of directors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: chief information officer (CIO).
choices.justification: The chief information officer (CIO) plays a significant role in the successful implementation of IT governance within an organization, but the ultimate responsibility resides with the board of directors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: audit committee.
choices.justification: The audit committee plays a significant role in monitoring and overseeing the successful implementation of IT governance within an organization, but the ultimate responsibility resides with the board of directors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: board of directors.
choices.justification: Governance is the set of responsibilities and practices exercised by the board and executive management with the goal of providing strategic direction, ensuring that objectives are achieved, ascertaining that risk is managed appropriately and verifying that the enterprise’s resources are used responsibly.

questions.text: A comprehensive and effective email policy should address the issues of email structure, policy enforcement, monitoring and:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: recovery.
choices.justification: Email policy should address the business and legal requirements of email retention. Addressing the retention issue in the email policy would facilitate recovery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: retention.
choices.justification: In addition to being a good practice, laws and regulations may require an organization to keep information that has an impact on the financial statements. The prevalence of lawsuits in which email communication is held in the same regard as the official form of classic paper makes the retention policy of corporate email a necessity. All email generated on an organization’s hardware is the property of the organization, and an email policy should address the retention of messages, considering both known and unforeseen litigation. The policy should also address the destruction of emails after a specified time to protect the nature and confidentiality of the messages themselves.

choices.label: C
choices.text: rebuilding.
choices.justification: Email policy should address the business and legal requirements of email retention. Addressing the retention issue in the email policy facilitates rebuilding.

choices.label: D
choices.text: reuse.
choices.justification: Email policy should address the business and legal requirements of email retention. Reuse of email is not a policy matter.

questions.text: To support an organization’s goals, an IT department should have:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: a low-cost philosophy.
choices.justification: A low-cost philosophy is one objective, but more important is the cost-benefit and the relation of IT investment cost to business strategy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: long- and short-term plans.
choices.justification: To ensure its contribution to the realization of an organization’s overall goals, the IT department should have long- and short-range plans that are consistent with the organization’s broader and strategic plans for attaining its goals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: leading-edge technology.
choices.justification: Leading-edge technology is an objective, but IT plans are needed to ensure that those plans are aligned with organizational goals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: plans to acquire new hardware and software.
choices.justification: Plans to acquire new hardware and software can be a part of the overall plan but are required only if hardware or software is needed to achieve the organizational goals.

questions.text: From an IT governance perspective, what is the PRIMARY responsibility of the board of directors? To ensure that the IT strategy:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: is cost-effective.
choices.justification: The IT strategy should be cost-effective, but it must align with the business strategy for the strategy to be effective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is forward thinking and innovative.
choices.justification: The IT strategy should be forward-thinking and innovative, but it must align with the business strategy to be effective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: is aligned with the business strategy.
choices.justification: The board of directors is responsible for ensuring that the IT strategy is aligned with the business strategy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: has the appropriate priority level assigned.
choices.justification: The IT strategy should be appropriately prioritized; however, it must align with the business strategy first and then it will be prioritized.

questions.text: An employee who has access to highly confidential information resigned. Upon departure, which of the following should be done FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Conduct an exit interview with the employee.
choices.justification: It is important to have an exit interview with any employee; however, this is not the first step to take upon the employee’s departure to protect the confidentiality of information.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure succession plans are in place.
choices.justification: Succession plans are important to prevent disruption of operations. This addresses availability and not confidentiality of information.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Revoke the employee’s access to all systems.
choices.justification: If an employee has dealt with highly classified information, the first step is to revoke their access to all systems, to restrict access to the confidential information and thereby prevent exfiltration of data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Review the employee’s job history.
choices.justification: Reviewing the job history of an employee might be important to determine the functions the person performed, but its effectiveness in terms of confidentiality of information may be limited.

questions.text: When an employee is terminated from service, the MOST important action is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: hand over all of the employee’s files to another designated employee.
choices.justification: All the work of the terminated employee needs to be handed over to a designated employee; however, this is not as critical as removing terminated employee access.

choices.label: B
choices.text: complete a backup of the employee’s work.
choices.justification: All the work of the terminated employee needs to be backed up, but this is not as critical as removing terminated employee access.

choices.label: C
choices.text: notify other employees of the termination.
choices.justification: The employees need to be notified of the termination, but this is not as critical as removing terminated employee access.

choices.label: D
choices.text: disable the employee’s logical access.
choices.justification: There is a probability that a terminated employee may misuse access rights; therefore, disabling the terminated employee’s logical access is the most important and immediate action to take.

questions.text: The FIRST step in data classification is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate data classification practices for alignment with the organization's data governance program, privacy program, and applicable external requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: establish ownership.
choices.justification: Data classification is necessary to define access rules based on a need-to-do and need-to-know basis. The data owner is responsible for defining the access rules; therefore, establishing ownership is the first step in data classification.

choices.label: B
choices.text: perform a criticality analysis.
choices.justification: A criticality analysis is required to determine the appropriate levels of protection of data, according to the data classification.

choices.label: C
choices.text: define access rules.
choices.justification: Access rules are set up dependent of the data classification.

choices.label: D
choices.text: create a data dictionary.
choices.justification: Input for a data dictionary is prepared from the results of the data classification process.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor discovers that some hard drives disposed of by an enterprise were not sanitized in a manner that would reasonably ensure that the data cannot be recovered. In addition, the enterprise does not have a written policy on data disposal. The IS auditor should FIRST:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: draft an audit finding and discuss it with the auditor in charge.
choices.justification: Drafting a finding without a quantified risk is premature.

choices.label: B
choices.text: determine the sensitivity of the information on the hard drives.
choices.justification: Although a policy is not available, the information systems (IS) auditor should determine the nature of the information on the hard drives to quantify, as much as possible, the risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: discuss with the IT manager good practices in data disposal.
choices.justification: It would be premature to discuss good practices with the IT manager until the extent of the incident has been quantified.

choices.label: D
choices.text: develop an appropriate data disposal policy for the enterprise.
choices.justification: An IS auditor should not develop policies.

questions.text: The PRIMARY benefit of implementing a security program as part of a security governance framework is the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: alignment of the IT activities with information systems (IS) audit recommendations.
choices.justification: Recommendations of the information systems (IS) auditor are usually addressed as value addition within a security program, but that would not be the primary benefit of implementing a security program.

choices.label: B
choices.text: enforcement of the management of security risk.
choices.justification: The primary benefit of implementing a security program is management’s assessment of risk and its mitigation to an appropriate level, and monitoring of the residual risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: implementation of the chief information security officer’s recommendations.
choices.justification: Recommendations and input of the chief information security officer are usually included within a security program, but that would not be the primary benefit of implementing a security program as part of a security governance framework.

choices.label: D
choices.text: reduction of the cost for IT security.
choices.justification: The impact of the implementation of a security program on the cost of IT security is uncertain. It may or may not be reduced.

questions.text: Which of the following goals do you expect to find in an organization’s strategic plan?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the role and/or impact of automatization and/or decision-making systems for an organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Results of new software testing
choices.justification: Results of a new accounting package is a tactical or short-term goal and is included in a strategic plan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An evaluation of information technology needs
choices.justification: An evaluation of information technology needs is a way to measure performance, but not a goal to be found in a strategic plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Short-term project plans for a new planning system
choices.justification: Short-term project plans is project-oriented and is a method of implementing a goal but not the goal in itself. The goal is to have better project management—the new system is how to achieve that goal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Approved suppliers for products offered by the organization
choices.justification: Approved suppliers of choice for the product is a strategic business objective that is intended to focus the overall direction of the business and, thus, is a part of the organization’s strategic plan.

questions.text: While auditing an ecommerce architecture, an information systems (IS) auditor notes that customer master data are stored on the web server for six months after the transaction date and then purged due to inactivity. Which of the following is the PRIMARY concern for the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Availability of customer data
choices.justification: Availability of customer data may be affected during an Internet connection outage, but this is of a lower concern than confidentiality.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Integrity of customer data
choices.justification: Integrity of customer data is affected only if security controls are weak enough to permit unauthorized modifications to the data, and it may be tracked by logging of changes. Confidentiality of data is a larger concern.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Confidentiality of customer data
choices.justification: Due to its exposure to the Internet, storing customer data for six months raises concerns regarding confidentiality of customer data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: System storage performance
choices.justification: System storage performance may be a concern due to the volume of data. However, the bigger issue is that the information is protected.

questions.text: Which of the following is of MOST interest to an information systems (IS) auditor who is reviewing an organization’s risk strategy?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: All risk is mitigated effectively.
choices.justification: It is not possible to mitigate all existing risk. However, risk needs to be identified and ranked before effective mitigation can be implemented.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Residual risk is zero after control implementation.
choices.justification: It is highly unlikely that residual risk would be zero, but it can be reduced. There will always be some level of risk remaining. This is known as residual risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: All likely risk is identified and ranked.
choices.justification: Risk that is likely to impact the organization should be identified, ranked and documented as part of the risk strategy. Without knowing the risk, there is no risk strategy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The organization uses an established risk framework.
choices.justification: It is not as important to use an established risk framework as it is to identify and rank all likely risk so that it can be addressed.

questions.text: An IT steering committee should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: include a mix of members from different departments and staff levels.
choices.justification: Only senior management or high-level staff members should be on this committee because of its strategic mission. Each member should have the authority to make decisions within the group for his/her respective areas.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ensure that information security policies and procedures have been executed properly.
choices.justification: Ensuring that information security policies and procedures have been executed properly is not the responsibility of this committee, but the responsibility of IT management and the security administrator.

choices.label: C
choices.text: maintain minutes of its meetings and keep the board of directors informed.
choices.justification: It is important to keep detailed IT steering committee minutes to document the decisions and activities of the IT steering committee. The board of directors should be informed about those decisions on a timely basis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: be briefed about new trends and products at each meeting by a vendor.
choices.justification: A vendor should be invited to meetings only when appropriate.

questions.text: During an information systems (IS) risk assessment of a health care organization regarding protected health information (PHI), an IS auditor interviews IS management. Which of the following findings from the interviews would be of MOST concern to the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: The organization does not encrypt all of its outgoing email messages.
choices.justification: Encrypting all outgoing email is expensive and is not common business practice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Staff have to type “[PHI]” in the subject field of email messages to be encrypted.
choices.justification: There will always be human-error risk that staff members forget to type certain words in the subject field. The organization should have automated encryption set up for outgoing email for employees working with protected health information (PHI) to protect sensitive information.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An individual’s computer screen saver function is disabled.
choices.justification: Disabling the screen saver function increases the risk that sensitive data can be exposed to other employees; however, the risk is not as great as exposing the data to unauthorized individuals outside the organization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Server configuration requires the user to change the password annually.
choices.justification: Although changing the password annually is a concern, the risk is not as great as exposing the data to unauthorized individuals outside the organization.

questions.text: Which of the following is the responsibility of information asset owners?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Implementation of information security within applications
choices.justification: Implementation of information security within an application is the responsibility of the data custodians based on the requirements set by the data owner.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assignment of criticality levels to data
choices.justification: It is the responsibility of owners to define the criticality (and sensitivity) levels of information assets.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implementation of access rules to data and programs
choices.justification: Implementation of access rules is a responsibility of data custodians based on the requirements set by the data owner.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provision of physical and logical security for data
choices.justification: Provision of physical and logical security for data is the responsibility of the security administrator.

questions.text: The GREATEST benefit of having well-defined data classification policies and procedures is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: a more accurate inventory of information assets.
choices.justification: A more accurate inventory of information assets is a benefit but is not the greatest benefit of the choices listed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a decreased cost and improvement of controls.
choices.justification: An important benefit of a well-defined data classification process is to lower the cost of protecting data by ensuring that the appropriate controls are applied with respect to the sensitivity of the data. Without a proper classification framework, some security controls may be greater and, therefore, costlier than what is required based on the data classification.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a reduced risk of inappropriate system access.
choices.justification: Classifying the data may assist in reducing the risk of inappropriate system access, but that is not the greatest benefit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: an improved regulatory compliance.
choices.justification: Improved regulatory compliance is a benefit; however, achieving a cost reduction is a greater benefit.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST critical to the quality of data in a data warehouse?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Accuracy of the source data
choices.justification: Accuracy of source data is a prerequisite for the quality of the data in a data warehouse. Inaccurate source data will corrupt the integrity of the data in the data warehouse.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Credibility of the data source
choices.justification: Credibility of the data source is important but would not change inaccurate data into quality (accurate) data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Accuracy of the extraction process
choices.justification: Accurate extraction processes are important but would not change inaccurate data into quality (accurate) data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Accuracy of the data transformation
choices.justification: Accurate transformation routines are important but would not change inaccurate data into quality (accurate) data.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST provides assurance of the integrity of new staff?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Background screening
choices.justification: A background screening is the primary method for assuring the integrity of a prospective staff member. This may include criminal history checks, driver’s license abstracts, financial status checks, verification of education, etc.

choices.label: B
choices.text: References
choices.justification: References are important and need to be verified, but they are not as reliable as background screening, because the references themselves may not be validated as trustworthy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bonding
choices.justification: Bonding is directed at due-diligence compliance and does not ensure integrity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Qualifications listed on a resume
choices.justification: Qualifications listed on a resume may be used to demonstrate proficiency but will not indicate the integrity of the candidate employee.

questions.text: Which of the following choices BEST helps information owners to properly classify data?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate data classification practices for alignment with the organization's data governance program, privacy program, and applicable external requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Understanding of technical controls that protect data
choices.justification: Although understanding how the data are protected is important, these controls might not be applied properly if the data classification schema is not well understood.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Training on enterprise policies and standards
choices.justification: While implementing data classification, it is most essential that enterprise policies and standards, including the data classification schema, are understood by the owner or custodian of the data so they can be properly classified.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use of an automated data leak prevention tool
choices.justification: Although an automated data leak prevention (DLP) tool may enhance productivity, the users of the application still need to understand what classification schema was in place.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Understanding which people need to access the data
choices.justification: In terms of protecting the data, the data requirements of end users are critical, but if the data owner does not understand what data classification schema is in place, it is likely that inappropriate access to sensitive data might be granted by the data owner.

questions.text: The PRIMARY objective of implementing corporate governance is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: provide strategic direction.
choices.justification: Corporate governance is a set of management practices to provide strategic direction to the organization as a whole, thereby ensuring that goals are achievable, risk is properly addressed and organizational resources are properly used. Hence, the primary objective of corporate governance is to provide strategic direction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: control business operations.
choices.justification: Business operations are directed and controlled based on the strategic direction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: align IT with business.
choices.justification: Corporate governance applies strategic planning, monitoring and accountability to the entire organization, not just to IT.

choices.label: D
choices.text: implement good practices.
choices.justification: Governance is applied through the use of good practices, but this is not the objective of corporate governance.

questions.text: During a system development life cycle audit of a human resources and payroll application, the information systems (IS) auditor notes that the data used for user acceptance testing have been masked. The purpose of masking the data is to ensure the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: confidentiality of the data.
choices.justification: Masking is used to ensure the confidentiality of data, especially in a user acceptance testing exercise where testers have access to data that they would not have access to in normal production environments.

choices.label: B
choices.text: accuracy of the data.
choices.justification: Masking does not ensure accuracy of the data. If the underlying data are inaccurate, the masked data also are inaccurate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: completeness of the data.
choices.justification: Masking does not ensure the completeness of the data. If the underlying data are incomplete, the masked data also are incomplete.

choices.label: D
choices.text: reliability of the data.
choices.justification: Masking does not ensure the reliability of the data. If the underlying data are unreliable, the masked data also are unreliable.

questions.text: Assessing IT risk is BEST achieved by:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: evaluating threats and vulnerabilities that are associated with existing IT assets and IT projects.
choices.justification: To assess IT risk, threats and vulnerabilities need to be evaluated using qualitative or quantitative risk assessment approaches.

choices.label: B
choices.text: using the organization’s past actual loss experience to determine current exposure.
choices.justification: Basing an assessment on past losses does not adequately reflect new threats or inevitable changes to the organization’s IT assets, projects, controls and strategic environment. There are also likely to be problems with the scope and quality of the loss data available to be assessed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: reviewing published loss statistics from comparable organizations.
choices.justification: Comparable organizations have differences in their IT assets, control environment and strategic circumstances. Therefore, their loss experience cannot be used to directly assess organizational IT risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: reviewing IT control weaknesses identified in audit reports.
choices.justification: Control weaknesses identified during audits are relevant in assessing threat exposure, and further analysis may be needed to assess threat probability. Depending on the scope of the audit coverage, it is possible that not all of the critical IT assets and projects will have recently been audited, and there may not be a sufficient assessment of strategic IT risk.

questions.text: A top-down approach to the development of operational policies helps to ensure that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: they are consistent across the organization.
choices.justification: Deriving lower-level policies from corporate policies (a top-down approach) aids in ensuring consistency across the organization and consistency with other policies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: they are implemented as a part of risk assessment.
choices.justification: Policies should be influenced by risk assessment, but the primary reason for a top-down approach is to ensure that the policies are consistent across the organization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: there is compliance with all policies.
choices.justification: A top-down approach, alone, does not ensure compliance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: they are reviewed periodically.
choices.justification: A top-down approach, alone, does not ensure that policies are reviewed.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor of a large organization is reviewing the roles and responsibilities of the IT function and finds some individuals serving multiple roles. Which one of the following combinations of roles should be of GREATEST concern for the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Network administrators are responsible for quality assurance.
choices.justification: Ideally, network administrators should not be responsible for quality assurance because they can approve their own work. However, that is not as serious as the combination of system administrator and application programmer, which allows nearly unlimited abuse of privilege.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System administrators are application programmers.
choices.justification: When individuals serve multiple roles, this represents a separation-of-duties problem with associated risk. System administrators should not be application programmers, due to the associated rights of both functions. A person with both system and programming rights can do almost anything on a system, including creating a back door. The other combinations of roles are valid from a separation-of-duties perspective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: End users are security administrators for critical applications.
choices.justification: In some distributed environments, especially with small staffing levels, users may also manage security.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Systems analysts are database administrators (DBAs).
choices.justification: Although a database administrator (DBA) is a very privileged position it is not in conflict with the role of a systems analyst.

questions.text: An organization purchased a third-party application and made significant modifications. While auditing the development process for this critical customer-facing application, the information systems (IS) auditor noted that the vendor has been in business for only one year. Which of the following helps to mitigate the risk relating to continued application support?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Viability study on the vendor
choices.justification: Although a viability study on the vendor may provide some assurance on the long-term availability of the vendor’s services to the entity, in this case, it is more important that the organization has the rights to the source code.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Software escrow agreement
choices.justification: Considering that the vendor has been in the business for only one year, the biggest concern is financial stability or viability of the vendor and the risk of the vendor going out of business. The best way that this risk can be addressed is to have a software escrow agreement for the source code of the application, which provides the entity access to the source code if the vendor goes out of business.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Financial evaluation of the vendor
choices.justification: Considering that the vendor has been in business for only one year, financial evaluation of the vendor is not of much value and cannot provide assurance on the long-term availability of the vendor services to the entity. In this case, it is more important that the organization has rights to the source code.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Contractual agreement for future enhancements
choices.justification: A contractual agreement, while binding, is not enforceable or only has limited value in the event of bankruptcy.

questions.text: The effectiveness and efficiency of IT governance are dependent FIRST in quality management of: Options:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: strategies and policies.
choices.justification: Strategies and policies are the first building blocks of quality management embedded into IT governance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: processes and procedures.
choices.justification: Policies are defined first and then should be underpinned by suitable processes and procedures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: functions and services.
choices.justification: Functions, services and their underpinning processes should be managed and directed by relevant strategies and policies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: roles and responsibilities.
choices.justification: Roles and responsibilities are articulated based on strategies, policies, processes and procedures.

questions.text: A business unit has selected a new accounting application and did not consult with IT early in the selection process. The PRIMARY risk is that the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: security controls of the application may not meet requirements.
choices.justification: Although security controls should be a requirement for any application, the primary focus of the enterprise architecture (EA) is to ensure that new applications are consistent with enterprise standards. Although the use of standard supported technology may be more secure, this is not the primary benefit of the EA.

choices.label: B
choices.text: application may not meet the requirements of the business users.
choices.justification: When selecting an application, the business requirements and the suitability of the application for the IT environment must be considered. If the business unit selects its application without IT involvement, it is more likely to choose a solution that fits its business process the best with less emphasis on how compatible and supportable the solution will be in the enterprise, and this is not a concern.

choices.label: C
choices.text: application technology may be inconsistent with the enterprise architecture.
choices.justification: The primary focus of the EA is to ensure that technology investments are consistent with the platform, data and development standards of the IT organization. The EA defines both a current and future state in areas such as the use of standard platforms, databases or programming languages. If a business unit selected an application using a database or operating system that is not part of the EA for the business, this increases the cost and complexity of the solution and ultimately delivers less value to the business.

choices.label: D
choices.text: application may create unanticipated support issues for IT.
choices.justification: Although any new software implementation may create support issues, the primary benefit of the EA is ensuring that the IT solutions deliver value to the business. Decreased support costs may be a benefit of the EA, but the lack of IT involvement in this case does not affect the support requirements.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST important factor for IT management to consider in developing performance metrics?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Risk management and compliance with regulations
choices.justification: Risk and compliance with regulations is one of the dimensions of performance metrics.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Key IT processes, including solution and service delivery
choices.justification: Key IT processes, including solution and service delivery, are one of the dimensions of performance metrics.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Business contribution, including financials
choices.justification: Business contribution, including financials, is one of the dimensions of performance metrics.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Critical processes in place to meet customer requirements
choices.justification: Identifying critical processes to meet customer requirements is one of the most important aspects of developing performance metrics. Others are identifying specific, quantifiable outputs of work from the identified processes and establishing targets against which results can be scored.

category: Organizational Structures, IT Governance, and IT Strategy

questions.text: As an outcome of information security governance, strategic alignment provides:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: security requirements driven by enterprise requirements.
choices.justification: Information security governance, when properly implemented, should provide four basic outcomes: strategic alignment, value delivery, risk management and performance measurement. Strategic alignment provides input for security requirements driven by enterprise requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: baseline security following good practices.
choices.justification: Strategic alignment ensures that security aligns with business goals. Providing a standard set of security practices (i.e., baseline security following good practices or institutionalized and commoditized solutions) is a part of value delivery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: institutionalized and commoditized solutions.
choices.justification: Value delivery addresses the effectiveness and efficiency of solutions but is not a result of strategic alignment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: an understanding of risk exposure.
choices.justification: Risk management is a primary goal of IT governance, but strategic alignment is not focused on understanding risk exposure.

questions.text: The risk associated with electronic evidence gathering is MOST likely reduced by an email:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: destruction policy.
choices.justification: The email retention policy includes the destruction or deletion of emails. This must be compliant with legal requirements to retain emails.

choices.label: B
choices.text: security policy.
choices.justification: A security policy is too high-level and does not address the risk of inadequate retention of emails or the ability to provide access to emails when required.

choices.label: C
choices.text: archive policy.
choices.justification: With a policy requiring well-archived email records, access to or retrieval of specific email records to comply with legal requirements is possible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: audit policy.
choices.justification: An audit policy does not address the legal requirement to provide emails as electronic evidence.

questions.text: A team conducting a risk analysis is having difficulty projecting the financial losses that can result from a risk. To evaluate the potential impact, the team should:
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: compute the amortization of the related assets.
choices.justification: Amortization is used in a profit and loss statement, not in computing potential losses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: calculate a return on investment (ROI).
choices.justification: A return on investment (ROI) is computed when there is predictable savings or revenues that can be compared to the investment needed to realize the revenues.

choices.label: C
choices.text: apply a qualitative approach.
choices.justification: The common practice when it is difficult to calculate the financial losses is to take a qualitative approach, in which the manager affected by the risk defines the impact in terms of a weighted factor (e.g., one is a very low impact to the business and five is a very high impact).

choices.label: D
choices.text: spend the time needed to define the loss amount exactly.
choices.justification: Spending the time needed to define exactly the total amount is normally a wrong approach. If it has been difficult to estimate potential losses (e.g., losses derived from erosion of public image due to a hack attack), that situation is not likely to change, and the result will not be a well-supported evaluation.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST important element for the successful implementation of IT governance?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Implementing an IT scorecard
choices.justification: A scorecard is an excellent tool to implement a program based on good governance, but the most important factor in implementing governance is alignment with organizational strategies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identifying organizational strategies
choices.justification: The key objective of an IT governance program is to support the business; therefore, the identification of organizational strategies is necessary to ensure alignment between IT and corporate governance. Without identification of organizational strategies, the remaining choices—even if implemented—would be ineffective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Performing a risk assessment
choices.justification: A risk assessment is important to ensure that the security program is based on areas of highest risk, but risk assessment must be based on organizational strategies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Creating a formal security policy
choices.justification: A policy is a key part of security program implementation, but even the policy must be based on organizational strategies.

questions.text: A decision support system (DSS) is used to help high-level management:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: solve highly structured problems.
choices.justification: A decision support system (DSS) is aimed at solving less-structured problems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: combine the use of decision models with predetermined criteria.
choices.justification: A DSS combines the use of models and analytic techniques with traditional data access and retrieval functions but is not limited by predetermined criteria.

choices.label: C
choices.text: make decisions based on data analysis and interactive models.
choices.justification: A DSS emphasizes flexibility in the decision-making approach of management through data analysis and the use of interactive models, not fixed criteria.

choices.label: D
choices.text: support only structured decision-making tasks.
choices.justification: A DSS supports semi-structured decision-making tasks.

questions.text: Before implementing an IT balanced scorecard, an organization must:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: deliver effective and efficient services.
choices.justification: A balanced scorecard (BSC) is a method of specifying and measuring the attainment of strategic results. It measures the delivery of effective and efficient services, but an organization may not have those in place prior to using a BSC.

choices.label: B
choices.text: define key performance indicators.
choices.justification: Because a BSC is a way to measure performance, a definition of key performance indicators is required before implementing an IT BSC.

choices.label: C
choices.text: provide business value to IT projects.
choices.justification: A BSC measures the value of IT to business, not the other way around.

choices.label: D
choices.text: control IT expenses.
choices.justification: A BSC measures the performance of IT, but the control over IT expenses is not a key requirement for implementing a BSC.

questions.text: During a feasibility study regarding outsourcing IT processing, the relevance for the information systems (IS) auditor of reviewing the vendor’s business continuity plan is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: evaluate the adequacy of the service levels that the vendor can provide in a contingency.
choices.justification: A key factor in a successful outsourcing environment is the capability of the vendor to face a contingency and continue to support the organization’s processing requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: evaluate the financial stability of the service bureau and its ability to fulfill the contract.
choices.justification: Financial stability is not related to the vendor’s business continuity plan (BCP).

choices.label: C
choices.text: review the experience of the vendor’s staff.
choices.justification: Experience of the vendor’s staff is not related to the vendor’s BCP.

choices.label: D
choices.text: test the business continuity plan.
choices.justification: The review of the vendor’s BCP during a feasibility study is not a way to test the vendor’s BCP.

questions.text: Due to profitability pressure, senior management of an enterprise decided to keep investments in information security at an inadequate level. Which of the following is the BEST recommendation for an information systems (IS) auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use cloud providers for low-risk operations.
choices.justification: Cloud computing is cost-effective because costs that are usually directed at purchasing software and hardware are immediately eliminated. However, other costs need to be monitored closely, such as data transfer cost, cloud utilization cost, migration cost and business process reengineering cost. Therefore, the use of cloud providers may or may not provide cost savings or lower risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Revise compliance enforcement processes.
choices.justification: Compliance enforcement processes that identify high levels of residual risk are working as intended and should not be revised.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Request that senior management accept the risk.
choices.justification: Senior management determines resource allocations. Having established that the level of security is inadequate, it is imperative that senior management accept the risk resulting from their decisions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Postpone low-priority security procedures.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor should not recommend postponing any procedures. This is a management decision, and management should first accept the risk.

questions.text: An enterprise hosts its data center onsite and has outsourced the management of its key financial applications to a service provider. Which of the following controls BEST ensures that the service provider’s employees adhere to the security policies?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sign-off is required on the enterprise’s security policies for all users.
choices.justification: Having users sign off on policies is a good practice; however, this only puts the onus of compliance on the individual user, not on the organization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An indemnity clause is included in the contract with the service provider.
choices.justification: Having the service provider sign an indemnity clause ensures compliance with the enterprise’s security policies, because any violations discovered will lead to a financial liability for the service provider. This will also prompt the enterprise to monitor security violations closely.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mandatory security awareness training is implemented for all users.
choices.justification: Awareness training is an excellent control but does not ensure that the service provider’s employees adhere to policy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Security policies should be modified to address compliance by third-party users.
choices.justification: Modification of security policy does not ensure compliance by users unless the policies are appropriately communicated to users and enforced, and awareness training is provided.

questions.text: An organization has outsourced its help desk activities. An information systems (IS) auditor’s GREATEST concern when reviewing the contract and associated service level agreement between the organization and vendor should be the provisions for:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: documentation of staff background checks.
choices.justification: Although it is necessary to document the fact that background checks are performed, this is only one of the provisions that should be in place for audits.

choices.label: B
choices.text: independent audit reports or full audit access.
choices.justification: When the functions of an IT department are outsourced, an information systems (IS) auditor should ensure that a provision is made for independent audit reports that cover all essential areas, or that the outsourcer has full audit access.

choices.label: C
choices.text: reporting the year-to-year incremental cost reductions.
choices.justification: Financial measures, such as year-to-year incremental cost reductions, are desirable to have in a service level agreement (SLA); however, cost reductions are not as important as the availability of independent audit reports or full audit access.

choices.label: D
choices.text: reporting staff turnover, development or training.
choices.justification: An SLA might include human relationship measures, such as resource planning, staff turnover, development or training, but this is not as important as the requirements for independent reports or full audit access by the outsourcing organization.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing an organization that uses cross-training practices should assess the risk of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: dependency on a single person.
choices.justification: Cross-training helps decrease dependence on a single person.

choices.label: B
choices.text: inadequate succession planning.
choices.justification: Cross-training assists in succession planning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: one person knowing all parts of a system.
choices.justification: Cross-training is a process of training more than one individual to perform a specific job or procedure. However, before using this approach, it is prudent to assess the risk of any person knowing all parts of a system and the related potential exposures related to abuse of privilege.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a disruption of operations.
choices.justification: Cross-training provides for the backup of personnel in the event of an absence and, thereby, provides for the continuity of operations.

questions.text: Which of the following user profiles should be of MOST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor when performing an audit of an electronic funds transfer system?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Three users with the ability to capture and verify their own messages
choices.justification: The ability of one individual to capture and verify their own messages represents an inadequate segregation because messages can be taken as correct and appear like they had already been verified. The verification of messages should not be allowed by the person who sent the message.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Five users with the ability to capture and send their own messages
choices.justification: Users may have the ability to send messages but should not be able to verify their own messages.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Five users with the ability to verify other users and to send their own messages
choices.justification: This profile is an example of separation of duties. A person can send their own message but only verify the messages of other users.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Three users with the ability to capture and verify the messages of other users and to send their own messages
choices.justification: The ability to capture and verify the messages of others but only send their own messages is acceptable.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST important to consider when reviewing the classification levels of information assets?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Potential loss
choices.justification: The best basis for asset classification is an understanding of the total losses that a business may incur if the asset is compromised. Typically, estimating these losses requires a review of criticality and sensitivity beyond financial cost, such as operational and strategic.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Financial cost
choices.justification: The value of an asset can be greater than its monetary cost, such as impact on reputation and brand.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Potential threats
choices.justification: The classification of an asset does not change based on potential threats.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cost of insurance
choices.justification: Insurance is obtained based on asset classification.

questions.text: A benefit of open system architecture is that it:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: facilitates interoperability within different systems.
choices.justification: Open systems are those for which suppliers provide components whose interfaces are defined by public standards, thus facilitating interoperability between systems made by different vendors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: facilitates the integration of proprietary components.
choices.justification: Closed system components are built to proprietary standards so that other suppliers’ systems cannot interface with existing systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: is a basis for volume discounts from equipment vendors.
choices.justification: The ability to obtain volume discounts is achieved through bulk purchasing or a primary vendor, not through open system architecture.

choices.label: D
choices.text: allows for the achievement of more economies of scale for equipment.
choices.justification: Open systems may be less expensive than proprietary systems depending on the supplier, but the primary benefit of open system architecture is its interoperability between vendors.

questions.text: Which of the following situations is addressed by a software escrow agreement?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: The system administrator requires access to software to recover from a disaster.
choices.justification: Access to software should be managed by an internally managed software library. Escrow refers to the storage of software with a third party—not the internal libraries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A user requests to have software reloaded onto a replacement hard drive.
choices.justification: Providing the user with a backup copy of software is not escrow. Escrow requires that a copy be kept with a trusted third party.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The vendor of custom-written software goes out of business.
choices.justification: A software escrow is a legal agreement between a software vendor and a customer to guarantee access to source code. The application source code is held by a trusted third party, according to the contract. This agreement is necessary in the event that the software vendor goes out of business, there is a contractual dispute with the customer or the software vendor fails to maintain an update of the software as promised in the software license agreement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An information systems (IS) auditor requires access to software code written by the organization.
choices.justification: Software escrow is used to protect the intellectual property of software developed by one organization and sold to another organization. This is not used for software being reviewed by an auditor of the organization that wrote the software.

questions.text: Regarding the outsourcing of IT services, which of the following conditions should be of GREATEST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: Core activities that provide a differentiated advantage to the organization have been outsourced.
choices.justification: An organization’s core activities generally should not be outsourced because they are what the organization does best; an information systems (IS) auditor observing that condition should be concerned.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Periodic renegotiation is not specified in the outsourcing contract.
choices.justification: An IS auditor should not be concerned about periodic renegotiation in the outsourcing contract because that is dependent on the terms of the contract.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The outsourcing contract fails to cover every action required by the business.
choices.justification: Outsourcing contracts cannot be expected to cover every action and detail expected of the parties involved but should cover business requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Similar activities are outsourced to more than one vendor.
choices.justification: Multisourcing is an acceptable way to reduce risk associated with a single point of failure.

questions.text: Involvement of senior management is MOST important in the development of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: strategic plans.
choices.justification: Strategic plans provide the basis for ensuring that the enterprise meets its goals and objectives. Involvement of senior management is critical to ensuring that the plan adequately addresses the established goals and objectives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: IT policies.
choices.justification: IT policies are created and enforced by IT management and information security. They are structured to support the overall strategic plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IT procedures.
choices.justification: IT procedures are developed to support IT policies. Senior management is not involved in the development of procedures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: standards and guidelines.
choices.justification: Standards and guidelines are developed to support IT policies. Senior management is not involved in the development of standards, baselines and guidelines.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing an outsourcing contract of IT facilities expects it to define the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: hardware configuration.
choices.justification: The hardware configuration is generally irrelevant if the functionality, availability and security can be affected, which are specific contractual obligations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: access control software.
choices.justification: The access control software is generally irrelevant if the functionality, availability and security can be affected, which are specific contractual obligations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: ownership of intellectual property (IP).
choices.justification: The contract must specify who owns the intellectual property (IP) (i.e., information being processed and application programs). Ownership of IP is a significant cost and is a key aspect to be defined in an outsourcing contract.

choices.label: D
choices.text: application development methodology.
choices.justification: The development methodology should be of no real concern in an outsourcing contract.

questions.text: In reviewing the IT short-range (tactical) plan, an information systems (IS) auditor should determine whether:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: there is an integration of IT and business personnel within projects.
choices.justification: The integration of IT and business personnel in projects is an operational issue and should be considered while reviewing the short-range plan. A strategic plan provides a framework for the IT short-range plan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: there is a clear definition of the IT mission and vision.
choices.justification: A clear definition of the IT mission and vision is covered by a strategic plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a strategic information technology planning scorecard is in place.
choices.justification: A strategic information technology planning scorecard is covered by a strategic plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the plan correlates business objectives to IT goals and objectives.
choices.justification: Business objectives correlating to IT goals and objectives are covered by a strategic plan.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST reference for an information systems (IS) auditor to determine a vendor’s ability to meet service level agreement requirements for a critical IT security service?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compliance with the master contract
choices.justification: The master contract typically includes terms, conditions and costs but does not typically include service levels.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Agreed-on key performance indicators (KPIs)
choices.justification: Key performance indicators (KPIs) are metrics that allow for a means to measure performance. Service level agreements (SLAs) are statements related to expected service levels. For example, an Internet service provider (ISP) may guarantee that their service will be available 99.99 percent of the time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Results of business continuity tests
choices.justification: If applicable to the service, results of business continuity tests are typically included as part of the due diligence review.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Results of independent audit reports
choices.justification: Independent audits report on the financial condition of an organization or the control environment. Reviewing audit reports is typically part of the due diligence review. Even audits must be performed against a set of standards or metrics to validate compliance.

questions.text: Information systems (IS) control objectives are useful to IS auditors because they provide the basis for understanding the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: desired result or purpose of implementing specific control procedures.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) control objective is defined as the statement of the desired result or purpose to be achieved by implementing control procedures in a particular IS activity.

choices.label: B
