choices.text: best information systems (IS) security control practices relevant to a specific entity.
choices.justification: Control objectives provide the actual objectives for implementing controls and may or may not be based on good practices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: techniques for securing information.
choices.justification: Techniques are the means of achieving an objective, but it is more important to know the reason and objective for the control than to understand the technique itself.

choices.label: D
choices.text: security policy.
choices.justification: A security policy mandates the use of IS controls, but the controls are not used to understand policy.

questions.text: An enterprise is looking to obtain cloud hosting services from a cloud vendor with a high level of maturity. Which of the following is MOST important for the auditor to ensure continued alignment with the enterprise’s security requirements?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: The vendor provides the latest third-party audit report for verification.
choices.justification: Although the vendor is providing the most recent third-party audit report for review, there is no agreement contractually that requires the vendor to continue to provide annual reports for verification and review.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The vendor provides the latest internal audit report for verification.
choices.justification: Although the vendor is providing the most recent internal audit report for review, there is no agreement contractually that requires the vendor to continue to provide annual reports for verification and review.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The vendor agrees to implement controls in alignment with the enterprise.
choices.justification: Without a clause in the contract, an agreement to implement controls does not provide assurance that controls will continue to be implemented in alignment with the enterprise.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The vendor agrees to provide annual external audit reports in the contract.
choices.justification: The only way to ensure that any potential risk is mitigated today and in the future is to include a clause within the contract that the vendor will provide future external audit reports. Without the audit clause, the vendor can choose to forego future audits.

questions.text: A small organization has only one database administrator (DBA) and one system administrator. The DBA has root access to the UNIX server, which hosts the database application. How should separation of duties be enforced in this scenario?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hire a second DBA and split the duties between the two individuals.
choices.justification: Hiring additional staff is a costly way to ensure separation of duties.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remove the DBA’s root access on all UNIX servers.
choices.justification: The database administrator (DBA) needs root access to the database servers to install upgrades or patches.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensure that all actions of the DBA are logged and that all logs are backed up to removable media.
choices.justification: The administrator can modify or erase logs prior to the media backup event.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure that database logs are forwarded to a UNIX server where the DBA does not have root access.
choices.justification: By creating logs that the DBA cannot erase or modify, separation of duties is enforced.

questions.text: Which of the following IT governance good practices improves strategic alignment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Supplier and partner risk is managed.
choices.justification: Managing supplier and partner risk is a risk management good practice but not a strategic function.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A knowledge base of customers, products, markets and processes is in place.
choices.justification: A knowledge base of customers, products, markets and processes being in place is an IT value delivery good practice but does not ensure strategic alignment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A structure is provided that facilitates the creation and sharing of business information.
choices.justification: An infrastructure that facilitates the creation and sharing of business information is an IT value delivery and risk management good practice but is not as effective as top management involvement in business and technology alignment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Top management mediates between the imperatives of business and technology.
choices.justification: Top management mediating between the imperatives of business and technology is an IT strategic alignment good practice.

questions.text: Enterprise governance of IT frameworks has been developed MAINLY to help an organization’s leaders:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: use resources responsibly and manage information systems risk.
choices.justification: Using resources responsibly contributes to value creation, while managing information systems risk helps to preserve value.

choices.label: B
choices.text: realize benefits and manage the performance of practices and processes.
choices.justification: Benefits realization management measures how value is created, and performance management contributes to value preservation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: deliver value to stakeholders and preserve the value created.
choices.justification: Enterprise governance of IT frameworks help an organization’s leaders to deliver value to stakeholders by using resources responsibly, realizing value delivered, establishing roles and responsibilities, preserving value through information risk management, and maintaining the required levels of performance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: establish accountability and manage information security risk.
choices.justification: Establishing roles and responsibilities helps in value creation, while information assurance is about managing the risk associated with information assets and preserving the value of those .

questions.text: Which of the following factors is MOST critical when evaluating the effectiveness of an IT governance implementation?
questions.taskStatement: Communicate and collect feedback on audit progress, findings, results, and recommendations with stakeholders

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure that assurance objectives are defined.
choices.justification: Stakeholder needs and involvement form the basis for scoping the IT governance implementation and is used to define assurance objectives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Determine stakeholder requirements and involvement.
choices.justification: The most critical factor to be considered in auditing an IT governance implementation is to determine stakeholder requirements and involvement. This drives the success of the project. Based on this, the assurance scope and objectives are determined.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identify relevant risk and related opportunities.
choices.justification: The relevant risk and related opportunities are identified and driven by the assurance objectives.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determine relevant enablers and their applicability.
choices.justification: The relevant enablers and their applicability for the IT governance implementation are considered based on assurance objectives.

questions.text: The PRIMARY control purpose of required vacations or job rotations is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: allow cross-training for development.
choices.justification: Although cross-training is a good practice for business continuity, it is not achieved through mandatory vacations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: help preserve employee morale.
choices.justification: It is a good practice to maintain good employee morale, but this is not a primary reason to have a required vacation policy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: detect improper or illegal employee acts.
choices.justification: The practice of having another individual perform a job function is a control used to detect possible irregularities or fraud.

choices.label: D
choices.text: provide a competitive employee benefit.
choices.justification: Vacation time is a competitive benefit, but that is not a control.

questions.text: During an audit, the information systems (IS) auditor discovers that the HR department uses a cloud-based application to manage employee records. The HR department engaged in a contract outside of the normal vendor management process and manages the application on its own. Which of the following is of GREATEST concern?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maximum acceptable downtime metrics have not been defined in the contract.
choices.justification: Maximum acceptable downtime is a good metric to have in the contract to ensure application availability; however, HR applications are usually not mission-critical, and, therefore, maximum acceptable downtime is not the most significant concern in this scenario.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The IT department does not manage the relationship with the cloud vendor.
choices.justification: The responsibility for managing the relationship with a third party should be assigned to a designated individual or service management team; however, it is not essential that the individual or team belong to the IT department.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The help desk call center is in a different country, with different privacy requirements.
choices.justification: An organization-defined security policy ensures that help desk personnel do not have access to personnel data, and this is covered under the security policy. The more critical issue is whether the application complied with the security policy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Organization-defined security policies are not applied to the cloud application.
choices.justification: Cloud applications should adhere to the organization-defined security policies to ensure that the data in the cloud are protected in a manner consistent with internal applications. These include, but are not limited to, the password policy, user access management policy and data classification policy.

questions.text: Many organizations require an employee to take a mandatory vacation (holiday) of a week or more to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: ensure that the employee maintains a good quality of life, which will lead to greater productivity.
choices.justification: Maintaining a good quality of life is important, but the primary reason for a mandatory vacation is to catch fraud or errors.

choices.label: B
choices.text: reduce the opportunity for an employee to commit an improper or illegal act.
choices.justification: Required vacations/holidays of a week or more in duration in which someone other than the regular employee performs the job function of the employee on vacation is often mandatory for sensitive positions because this reduces the opportunity to commit improper or illegal acts. During this time off, it may be possible to discover any fraudulent activity that was taking place.

choices.label: C
choices.text: provide proper cross-training for another employee.
choices.justification: Providing cross-training is an important management function, but the primary reason for mandatory vacations is to detect fraud or errors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: eliminate the potential disruption caused when an employee takes vacation one day at a time.
choices.justification: Enforcing a rule that all vacations must be taken a week at a time is a management decision but not related to a mandatory vacation policy. The primary reason for mandatory vacations is to detect fraud or errors.

questions.text: During an audit, the information systems (IS) auditor observed that a user can log in to post a transaction and then change role to authorize the same. Which of the following is the BEST course of action for the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the system administrator to suspend the user’s access.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor cannot recommend suspending the user’s access.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check for suitable compensating controls.
choices.justification: If a violation of segregation/separation of duties (SoD) is observed, the IS auditor should first look for compensating controls before concluding the findings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the database administrator to merge the two roles into a single role.
choices.justification: An IS auditor would not recommend merging the two roles into a single role, because that is a violation of SoD.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Record the finding in the audit report.
choices.justification: The IS auditor identifies and evaluates all controls before recording findings.

questions.text: A local area network (LAN) administrator normally is restricted from:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: having end-user responsibilities.
choices.justification: Although not ideal, a local area network (LAN) administrator may have end-user responsibilities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: reporting to the end-user manager.
choices.justification: The LAN administrator may report to the director of the information processing facility (IPF) or, in a decentralized operation, to the end-user manager.

choices.label: C
choices.text: having programming responsibilities.
choices.justification: A LAN administrator should not have programming responsibilities because that can allow modification of production programs without proper separation of duties, but the LAN administrator may have end-user responsibilities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: being responsible for LAN security administration.
choices.justification: In small organizations, the LAN administrator may also be responsible for security administration over the LAN.

questions.text: What is the PRIMARY consideration for an information systems (IS) auditor reviewing the prioritization and coordination of IT projects and program management?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Projects are aligned with the organization’s strategy.
choices.justification: The primary goal of IT projects is to add value to the business, so they must be aligned with the business strategy to achieve the intended results. Therefore, the information systems (IS) auditor should first focus on ensuring this alignment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identified project risk is monitored and mitigated.
choices.justification: An adequate process for monitoring and mitigating identified project risk is important; however, strategic alignment helps in assessing identified risk in business terms.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Controls related to project planning and budgeting are appropriate.
choices.justification: Completion of projects within a predefined time and budget is important; however, the focus of project management should be on achieving the desired outcome of the project, which is aligned with the business strategy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: IT project metrics are reported accurately.
choices.justification: Adequate reporting of project status is important but may or may not help in providing the strategic perspective of project deliverables.

questions.text: Which of the following is a function of an IT steering committee?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: Monitoring vendor-controlled change control and testing
choices.justification: Vendor change control is a sourcing issue and should be monitored by IT management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensuring a separation of duties within the information’s processing environment
choices.justification: Ensuring a separation of duties within the information’s processing environment is an IT management responsibility.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Approving and monitoring the status of IT plans and budgets
choices.justification: The IT steering committee typically serves as a general review board for major IT projects and should not become involved in routine operations; therefore, one of its functions is to approve and monitor major projects, such as the status of IT plans and budgets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Liaising between the IT department and end users
choices.justification: Liaising between the IT department and end users is a function of the individual parties and not a committee responsibility.

questions.text: The MOST likely effect of the lack of senior management commitment to IT strategic planning is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: lack of investment in technology.
choices.justification: Lack of management commitment almost certainly affects investment, but the primary loss is the lack of alignment of IT strategy with the strategy of the business.

choices.label: B
choices.text: lack of a methodology for systems development.
choices.justification: Systems development methodology is a process-related function and not a key concern of management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Technology not aligning with organization objectives.
choices.justification: A steering committee should exist to ensure that the IT strategies support the organization’s goals. The absence of an information technology committee or a committee not composed of senior managers is an indication of a lack of top-level management commitment. This condition increases the risk that IT is not aligned with organization strategy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: absence of control over technology contracts.
choices.justification: Approval for contracts is a business process and is controlled through financial process controls. This is not applicable here.

questions.text: Which of the following is responsible for the approval of an information security policy?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: IT department
choices.justification: The IT department is responsible for the execution of the policy, having no authority in framing the policy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Security committee
choices.justification: The security committee also functions within the broad security policy framed by the board of directors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Security administrator
choices.justification: The security administrator is responsible for implementing, monitoring and enforcing the security rules that management established and authorized.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Board of directors
choices.justification: Normally, the approval of an information systems security policy is the responsibility of top management or the board of directors.

questions.text: A poor choice of passwords and unencrypted data transmissions over unprotected communications lines are examples of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: vulnerabilities.
choices.justification: Vulnerabilities represent weaknesses of information resources that may be exploited by a threat. Because these are weaknesses that can be addressed by the security specialist, they are examples of vulnerabilities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: threats.
choices.justification: Threats are circumstances or events with the potential to cause harm to information resources. Threats are usually outside the control of the security specialist.

choices.label: C
choices.text: probabilities.
choices.justification: Probabilities represent the likelihood of the occurrence of a threat.

choices.label: D
choices.text: impacts.
choices.justification: Impacts represent the outcome or result of a threat exploiting a vulnerability.

questions.text: Which of the following insurance types provide for a loss arising from fraudulent acts by employees?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the IT strategy for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Business interruption
choices.justification: Business interruption insurance covers the loss of profit due to the disruption in the operations of an organization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fidelity coverage
choices.justification: Fidelity insurance covers the loss arising from dishonest or fraudulent acts by employees.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Errors and omissions
choices.justification: Errors and omissions insurance provides legal liability protection if the professional practitioner commits an act that results in financial loss to a client.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Extra expense
choices.justification: Extra expense insurance is designed to cover the extra costs of continuing operations following a disaster/disruption within an organization.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is evaluating the IT governance framework of an organization. Which of the following is the GREATEST concern?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: Senior management has limited involvement.
choices.justification: To ensure that the IT governance framework is effectively in place, senior management must be involved and aware of their roles and responsibilities. Therefore, it is essential to ensure the involvement of senior management when evaluating the soundness of IT governance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Return on investment (ROI) is not measured.
choices.justification: Managing return on investment (ROI) and thereby creating business value is a part of the objectives in the IT governance framework. Therefore, it is not effective in verifying the soundness of IT governance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chargeback of IT cost is not consistent.
choices.justification: Introduction of a cost allocation system to facilitate the chargeback of IT cost to the applicable business units is part of the objectives in an IT governance framework. Therefore, it is not effective in verifying the soundness of IT governance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk appetite is not quantified.
choices.justification: Estimation of risk appetite is important; however, at the same time, management should ensure that controls are in place. Therefore, checking only on risk appetite does not verify soundness of IT governance.

questions.text: To gain an understanding of the effectiveness of an organization’s planning and management of investments in IT assets, an information systems (IS) auditor should review the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: enterprise data model.
choices.justification: An enterprise data model is a document defining the data structure of an organization and how data interrelate. The model is useful, but it does not provide information on investments in IT assets.

choices.label: B
choices.text: IT balanced scorecard (BSC).
choices.justification: The IT balanced scorecard (BSC) is a tool that provides the bridge between IT objectives and business objectives by supplementing the traditional financial evaluation with measures to evaluate customer satisfaction, internal processes and the ability to innovate. In this way, the auditor can measure the success of the IT investment and strategy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IT organizational structure.
choices.justification: The IT organizational structure provides an overview of the functional and reporting relationships in an IT entity but does not ensure effectiveness of IT investment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: historical financial statements.
choices.justification: Historical financial statements do not provide information about planning and lack sufficient detail to enable one to fully understand management’s activities regarding IT assets. Past costs do not necessarily reflect value, and assets such as data are not represented in the books of accounts.

questions.text: Which of the following is expected to approve the audit charter?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the effectiveness of IT governance structure and IT organizational structure

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chief financial officer
choices.justification: The chief financial officer (CFO) does not approve the audit charter but may be responsible for allocating funds in support of the audit charter. The CFO may also be a part of the audit committee or audit steering committee but would not approve the charter alone.

choices.label: B
choices.text: CEO
choices.justification: The CEO does not approve the audit charter. The CEO may be informed but is independent of the audit committee.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Audit steering committee
choices.justification: The steering committee is most likely composed of various members of senior management whose purpose is to work under the framework of the audit charter and does not approve the charter.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Audit committee
choices.justification: One of the primary functions of the audit committee is to create and approve the audit charter.

questions.text: Which of the following MOST likely indicates that a customer data warehouse should remain in-house rather than be outsourced to an offshore operation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Time-zone differences can impede communications between IT teams.
choices.justification: Time-zone differences are usually manageable issues for outsourcing solutions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Telecommunications cost can be much higher in the first year.
choices.justification: Higher telecommunications costs are a part of the cost-benefit analysis and not usually a reason to retain data in-house.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Privacy laws can prevent cross-border flow of information.
choices.justification: Privacy laws prohibiting the cross-border flow of personally identifiable information make it impossible to locate a data warehouse containing customer information in another country.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Software development may require more detailed specifications.
choices.justification: Software development typically requires more detailed specifications when dealing with offshore operations, but that is not a factor that should prohibit the outsourcing solution.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor was hired to review ebusiness security. The IS auditor’s first task was to examine each existing ebusiness application, looking for vulnerabilities. What is the IS auditor’s next task?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediately report the risk to the chief information officer and chief executive officer.
choices.justification: The risk can only be determined after the threats, likelihood and vulnerabilities are all documented.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Examine the ebusiness application in development.
choices.justification: The first step is to identify the risk levels to existing applications and then to apply those to applications in development. Risk can only be identified after the threats and likelihood have also been determined.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identify threats and the likelihood of occurrence.
choices.justification: To determine the risk associated with ebusiness, an information systems (IS) auditor must identify the assets, look for vulnerabilities, and then identify the threats and the likelihood of occurrence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Check the budget available for risk management.
choices.justification: The budget available for risk management is not relevant at this point, because the risk has not yet been determined.

domain: Information Systems Acquisition, Development, and Implementation

questions.text: An internal audit function is reviewing an internally developed common gateway interface script for a web application. The information systems (IS) auditor discovers that the script was not reviewed and tested by the quality control function. Which of the following types of risk is of GREATEST concern?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: System unavailability
choices.justification: Although untested common gateway interface (CGI) can cause the end-user web application to be compromised, this is not likely to make the system unavailable to other users.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exposure to malware
choices.justification: Untested CGI scripts do not inherently lead to malware exposures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Unauthorized access
choices.justification: Untested CGIs can have security weaknesses that allow unauthorized access to private systems because CGIs are typically executed on publicly available Internet servers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: System integrity
choices.justification: Although untested CGIs can cause the end-user web application to be compromised, this is not likely to significantly impact system integrity.

questions.text: An organization implemented a new client-server enterprise resource planning (ERP) system. Local branches transmit customer orders to a central manufacturing facility. Which of the following BEST ensures that the orders are processed accurately and the corresponding products are produced?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verifying production of customer orders
choices.justification: Verification of the products produced ensures that the produced products match the orders in the order system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Logging all customer orders in the ERP system
choices.justification: Logging can be used to detect inaccuracies but does not guarantee accurate processing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Using hash totals in the order transmitting process
choices.justification: Hash totals ensure accurate order transmission but not accurate processing centrally.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Approving (production supervisor) orders prior to production
choices.justification: Production supervisory approval is a time-consuming manual process that does not guarantee proper control.

questions.text: The information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing a recently completed conversion to a new enterprise resource planning system. In the final stage of the conversion process, the organization ran the old and new systems in parallel for 30 days before allowing the new system to run on its own. What is the MOST significant advantage to the organization by using this strategy?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Significant cost savings over other testing approaches
choices.justification: Parallel operation is generally expensive and does not provide a cost savings over most other testing approaches. In many cases, parallel operation is the most expensive form of system testing due to the need for dual data entry, dual sets of hardware, dual maintenance and dual backups. Parallel operation is twice the amount of work as running a production system and, therefore, costs more time and money.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assurance that new, faster hardware is compatible with the new system
choices.justification: Hardware compatibility should be determined and tested much earlier in the conversion project and is not an advantage of parallel operation. Compatibility is generally determined based on the application’s published specifications and on system testing in a lab environment. Parallel operation is designed to test the application’s effectiveness and integrity of application data, not hardware compatibility. In general, hardware compatibility relates more to the operating system level than to a particular application. Although new hardware in a system conversion must be tested under a real production load, this can be done without parallel systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assurance that the new system meets functional requirements
choices.justification: Parallel operation provides a high level of assurance that the new system functions properly compared to the old system, and therefore, the new system meets its functional requirements. This is the safest form of system conversion testing because, if the new system fails, the old system is still available for production use. In addition, this form of testing allows the application developers and administrators to simultaneously run operational tasks (e.g., batch jobs and backups) on both systems, to ensure that the new system is reliable before unplugging the old system.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased resiliency during the parallel processing time
choices.justification: Increased resiliency during parallel processing is a legitimate outcome from this scenario, but the advantage it provides is temporary and minor, so this is not the correct answer.

questions.text: Which testing environment is required to ensure complete code coverage to test every code path in software testing, including those that will only be used when an error occurs?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: White box
choices.justification: For testing the full code, a white box test is required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gray box
choices.justification: Without full visibility of the code, error conditions or other code can be missed; a gray box test is an inappropriate solution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Black box
choices.justification: Without full visibility of the code, error conditions or other code can be missed; a black box test is an inappropriate solution.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dynamic
choices.justification: Using dynamic testing that runs against live code can result in missed conditions due to sections of code not being exposed to typical usage.

questions.text: During which phase of software application testing should an organization perform the testing of architectural design?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acceptance testing
choices.justification: Acceptance testing determines whether the solution meets the requirements of the business and is performed after system staff has completed the initial system test. This testing includes quality assurance testing and user acceptance testing, although not combined.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System testing
choices.justification: System testing relates a series of tests by the test team or system maintenance staff to ensure that the modified program interacts correctly with other components. System testing references the functional requirements of the system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Integration testing
choices.justification: Integration testing evaluates the connection of two or more components that pass information from one area to another. The objective is to use unit-tested modules, thus building an integrated structure according to the design.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unit testing
choices.justification: Unit testing references the detailed design of the system and uses a set of cases that focus on the control structure of the procedural design to ensure that the internal operation of the program performs according to specification.

questions.text: The editing/validation of data entered at a remote site is performed MOST effectively at the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: central processing site after running the application system.
choices.justification: Due to the risk of errors being introduced during transmission to the central processing site, it is a good practice to re-validate the data after receiving them at the central processing site. However, this is not the most efficient method, because invalid data could have been transmitted and processed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: central processing site during the running of the application system.
choices.justification: Due to the risk of errors being introduced during transmission to the central processing site, it is a good practice to re-validate the data after receiving them at the central processing site. However, this is not the most efficient method, because invalid data could have been transmitted and processed at the central site.

choices.label: C
choices.text: remote processing site after transmission of the data to the central processing site.
choices.justification: To validate the data after they have been transmitted is not a valid control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: remote processing site prior to transmission of the data to the central processing site.
choices.justification: It is important that the data entered from a remote site are edited and validated prior to transmission to the central processing site.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds that a system under development has 12 linked modules and each item of data can carry up to 10 definable attribute fields. The system handles several million transactions a year. Which of the following techniques can an IS auditor use to estimate the size of the development effort?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Program evaluation review technique (PERT)
choices.justification: Program evaluation review technique (PERT) is a project management technique used in the planning and control of system projects.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Function point analysis
choices.justification: Function point analysis is a technique used to determine the size of a development task based on the number of function points. Function points are factors such as inputs, outputs, inquiries and logical internal sites.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Counting source lines of code
choices.justification: The number of source lines of code gives a direct measure of program size, but it does not allow for the complexity that may be caused by multiple, linked modules and a variety of inputs and outputs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: White box testing
choices.justification: White box testing involves a detailed review of the behavior of program code. It is a quality assurance technique suited to simpler applications during the design and building stage of development.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor’s PRIMARY concern when application developers wish to use a copy of yesterday’s production transaction file for volume tests is that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: users may prefer to use contrived data for testing.
choices.justification: Production data are easier for users to use for comparison purposes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: unauthorized access to sensitive data may result.
choices.justification: Unless the data are sanitized, there is a risk of disclosing sensitive data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: error handling and credibility checks may not be fully proven.
choices.justification: There is a risk that former production data may not test all error routines; however, this is not as serious as the risk of release of sensitive data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the full functionality of the new process may not necessarily be tested.
choices.justification: Using a copy of production data may not test all functionality, but this is not as serious as the risk of disclosure of sensitive data.

questions.text: When identifying an earlier project completion time, which is to be accomplished by paying a premium for early completion, the activities that should be selected are those:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: whose sum of activity time is the shortest.
choices.justification: Attention should focus on the tasks within the critical path that have no slack time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: that have zero slack time.
choices.justification: A critical path’s activity time is longer than that for any other path through the network. This path is important because if everything goes as scheduled, its length gives the shortest possible completion time for the overall project. Activities on the critical path become candidates for crashing (i.e., for reduction in their time by payment of a premium for early completion). Activities on the critical path have zero slack time and, conversely, activities with zero slack time are on a critical path. By successively relaxing activities on a critical path, a curve showing total project costs versus time can be obtained.

choices.label: C
choices.text: that give the longest possible completion time.
choices.justification: The critical path is the longest time length of the activities but is not based on the longest time of any individual activity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: whose sum of slack time is the shortest.
choices.justification: A task on the critical path has no slack time.

questions.text: Who should review and approve system deliverables as they are defined and accomplished, to ensure the successful completion and implementation of a new business system application?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: User management
choices.justification: User management assumes ownership of the project and resulting system, allocates qualified representatives to the team, and actively participates in system requirements definition, acceptance testing and user training. User management should review and approve system deliverables as they are defined and accomplished or implemented.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Project steering committee
choices.justification: A project steering committee provides overall direction, ensures appropriate representation of the major stakeholders in the project’s outcome, reviews project progress regularly and holds emergency meetings when required. A project steering committee is ultimately responsible for all deliverables, project costs and schedules.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Senior management
choices.justification: Senior management demonstrates commitment to the project and approves the necessary resources to complete the project. This commitment from senior management helps to ensure involvement by those who are needed to complete the project.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Quality assurance (QA) staff
choices.justification: Quality assurance (QA) staff review results and deliverables within each phase, and, at the end of each phase, confirm compliance with standards and requirements. The timing of reviews depends on the system development life cycle (SDLC), the impact of potential deviation methodology used, the structure and magnitude of the system, and the impact of potential deviation.

questions.text: Which of the following will BEST ensure the successful offshore development of business applications?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stringent contract management practices
choices.justification: Contract management practices, although important, do not ensure successful development if the specifications are incorrect.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Detailed and properly applied specifications
choices.justification: When dealing with offshore operations, it is essential to create detailed specifications. Language differences, a lack of interaction between developers and physically remote end users can create gaps in communication in which assumptions and modifications may not be adequately communicated. Inaccurate specifications cannot easily be corrected.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Awareness of cultural and political differences
choices.justification: Cultural and political differences, although important, should not affect the delivery of a good product.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Postimplementation review
choices.justification: Postimplementation review, although important, is too late in the process to ensure successful project delivery and is not as pivotal to the success of the project.

questions.text: Management observed that the initial phase of a multiphase implementation was behind schedule and over budget. Prior to commencing with the next phase, an information systems (IS) auditor’s PRIMARY suggestion for a postimplementation focus should be to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: assess whether the planned cost benefits are being measured, analyzed and reported.
choices.justification: While all choices are valid, the postimplementation focus and primary objective should be understanding the impact of the problems in the first phase on the remainder of the project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: review control balances and verify that the system is processing data accurately.
choices.justification: The review should assess whether the control is working correctly but should focus on the problems that led to project overruns in budget and time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: review the impact of program changes made during the first phase on the remainder of the project.
choices.justification: Because management is aware that the project had problems, reviewing the subsequent impact provides insight into the types and potential causes of the project issues. This insight helps to identify whether IT has adequately planned for those issues in subsequent projects.

choices.label: D
choices.text: determine whether the system’s objectives were achieved.
choices.justification: Ensuring that the system works is a primary objective for the information systems (IS) auditor, but, in this case, because the project planning was a failure, the IS auditor should focus on the reasons for, and impact of, the failure.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor performing a review of a major software development project finds that it is on schedule and under budget even though the software developers have worked considerable amounts of unplanned overtime. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: conclude that the project is progressing as planned because dates are being met.
choices.justification: Although the project is on time and budget, there may be problems with the project plan because considerable amounts of unplanned overtime have been required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: question the project manager further to identify whether overtime costs are being tracked accurately.
choices.justification: There is a possibility that the project manager has hidden some costs to make the project look better; however, the real problem may be with whether the project plan is realistic, not just the accounting.

choices.label: C
choices.text: conclude that the programmers are intentionally working slowly to earn extra overtime pay.
choices.justification: It is possible that the programmers are trying to take advantage of the time system, but if the overtime has been required to keep the project on track, it is more likely that the timelines and expectations of the project are unrealistic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: investigate further to determine whether the project plan may not be accurate.
choices.justification: Although the dates on which key projects are completed are important, there may be issues with the project plan if an extraordinary amount of unplanned overtime is required to meet those dates. In most cases, the project plan is based on a certain number of hours, and requiring programmers to work considerable overtime is not a good practice. Although overtime costs may be an indicator that something is wrong with the plan, in many organizations, the programming staff may be salaried, so overtime costs may not be directly recorded.

questions.text: An organization implemented a distributed accounting system, and the information systems (IS) auditor is conducting a post-implementation review to provide assurance of the data integrity controls. Which of the following choices should the auditor perform FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: user access.
choices.justification: The review of user access is important; however, in terms of data integrity, it is better to review the data flow diagram.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evaluate the change request process.
choices.justification: The lack of an adequate change control process can impact the integrity of the data; however, the system should be documented first to determine whether the transactions flow to other systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evaluate the reconciliation controls.
choices.justification: Evaluating the reconciliation controls helps to ensure data integrity; however, it is more important to understand the data flows of the application to ensure that the reconciliation controls are located in the correct place.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the data flow diagram.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor should review the application data flow diagram to understand the flow of data within the application and to other systems. This will enable the IS auditor to evaluate the design and effectiveness of the data integrity controls.

questions.text: Which of the following is an advantage of an integrated test facility?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: It uses actual master files or dummies, and the information systems (IS) auditor does not have to review the source of the transaction.
choices.justification: The integrated test facility (ITF) tests a test transaction as if it were a real transaction and validates that transaction processing is being done correctly. It is not related to reviewing the source of a transaction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Periodic testing does not require separate test processes.
choices.justification: An ITF creates a fictitious entity in the database to process test transactions simultaneously with live input. Its advantage is that periodic testing does not require separate test processes. Careful planning is necessary, and test data must be isolated from production data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It validates application systems and ensures the correct operation of the system.
choices.justification: An ITF does validate the correct operation of a transaction in an application, but it does not ensure that a system is being operated correctly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The need to prepare test data is eliminated.
choices.justification: The ITF is based on the integration of test data into the normal process flow, so test data is still required.

questions.text: Which of the following test techniques would the information systems (IS) auditor use to identify specific program logic that has not been tested?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Snapshot
choices.justification: A snapshot records the flow of designated transactions through logic paths within programs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tracing and tagging
choices.justification: Tracing and tagging show the trail of instructions executed during an application.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Logging
choices.justification: Logging is the activity of recording specific tasks for future review.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mapping
choices.justification: Mapping identifies specific program logic that has not been tested and analyzes programs during execution to indicate whether program statements have been executed.

questions.text: Which of the following is the GREATEST risk to the effectiveness of application system controls?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Removal of manual processing steps
choices.justification: Automation should remove manual processing steps wherever possible. The only risk is the removal of manual security controls without replacement with automated controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inadequate procedure manuals
choices.justification: The lack of documentation is a problem on many systems but not a serious risk in most cases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Collusion between employees
choices.justification: Collusion is an active attack where users collaborate to bypass controls, such as separation of duties. Such breaches may be difficult to identify because even well-thought-out application controls may be circumvented.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unresolved regulatory compliance issues
choices.justification: Unregulated compliance issues are a risk but do not measure the effectiveness of the controls.

questions.text: An enterprise is developing a strategy to upgrade to a newer version of its database software. Which of the following tasks can an information systems (IS) auditor perform without compromising the objectivity of the IS audit function?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Advise on the adoption of application controls to the new database software.
choices.justification: Independence can be compromised if the information systems (IS) auditor advises on the adoption of specific application controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provide future estimates of the licensing expenses to the project team.
choices.justification: Independence can be compromised if the IS auditor were to audit the estimate of future expenses used to support a business case for management approval of the project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recommend to the project manager how to improve the efficiency of the migration.
choices.justification: Advising the project manager on how to increase the efficiency of the migration may compromise the IS auditor’s independence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the acceptance test case documentation before the tests are carried out.
choices.justification: The review of the test cases will facilitate the objective of a successful migration and ensure that proper testing is conducted. An IS auditor can advise about the completeness of the test cases.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing a project for the implementation of a mission-critical system and notes that, instead of parallel implementation, the team opted for an immediate cutover to the new system. Which of the following is the GREATEST concern?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: The implementation phase of the project has no back-out plan.
choices.justification: One of the benefits of deploying a new system in parallel with an existing system is that the original system can always be used as a back-out plan. In an immediate cutover scenario, not having a back-out plan can create significant issues because it can take considerable time and cost to restore operations to the prior state if there is no viable plan to do so.

choices.label: B
choices.text: User acceptance testing was not properly documented.
choices.justification: The documentation of user acceptance testing is a much less important concern than not having a viable back-out plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Software functionality tests were completed, but stress testing was not performed.
choices.justification: The lack of stress testing is a much less important concern than not having a viable back-out plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The go-live date is over a holiday weekend when key IT staff are on vacation.
choices.justification: If there are support issues, having the go-live date happen over a holiday weekend may create some delays, but project managers should account for this to ensure that the required staff are available as needed. The greater risk is if there is no back-out plan.

questions.text: The MAJOR advantage of a component-based development approach is the:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: ability to manage an unrestricted variety of data types.
choices.justification: The data types must be defined within each component, and there is no certainty that any component will be able to handle multiple data types.

choices.label: B
choices.text: provision for modeling complex relationships.
choices.justification: Component-based development is no better than many other development methods at modeling complex relationships.

choices.label: C
choices.text: capacity to meet the demands of a changing environment.
choices.justification: Component-based development is one of the methodologies that can be effective at meeting changing requirements, but this is not its primary benefit or purpose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: support of multiple development environments.
choices.justification: Component-based development that relies on reusable modules can increase the speed of development. Software developers can then focus on business logic.

questions.text: The phases and deliverables of a system development life cycle project should be determined:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: during the initial planning stages of the project.
choices.justification: It is extremely important that the project is planned properly and that the specific phases and deliverables are identified during the early stages of the project. This enables project tracking and resource management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: after early planning has been completed but before work has begun.
choices.justification: Determining the deliverables and timelines of a project are a part of the early project planning work.

choices.label: C
choices.text: throughout the work stages, based on risk and exposures.
choices.justification: The requirements may change over the life of a project, but the initial deliverables should be documented from the beginning of the project.

choices.label: D
choices.text: only after all risk and exposures have been identified and the information systems (IS) auditor has recommended appropriate controls.
choices.justification: Risk management is a never-ending process, so project planning cannot wait until all risk has been identified.

questions.text: When planning to add personnel to tasks imposing time constraints on the duration of a project, which of the following should be revalidated FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Project budget
choices.justification: Given that there may be slack time available on some of the other tasks that are not on the critical path, the resource allocation should be based on the project segments that affect delivery dates.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Critical path for the project
choices.justification: Adding resources may change the route of the critical path. The critical path must be reevaluated to ensure that additional resources will shorten the project duration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Length of the remaining tasks
choices.justification: Given that there may be slack time available on some of the other tasks that are not on the critical path, a factor such as the length of other tasks may or may not be affected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Personnel assigned to other tasks
choices.justification: Depending on the skill level of the resources required or available, the addition of resources may not shorten the timeline. Therefore, the first step is to examine what resources are required to address the times on the critical path.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST helps organizations in optimizing audit resources and improving the quality of audits?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Independent review of audit work
choices.justification: An independent review of the audit helps in improving the quality of future audits but may not help in optimizing resources.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Integrated audit approach
choices.justification: An integrated audit approach focuses on combining different types of audits at the same time; the results of different audit scopes can be shared to provide a focused and quality audit report.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Global auditing standards
choices.justification: Global auditing standards help in improving the quality of an audit but may not help in optimizing resources.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk-based audit approach
choices.justification: A risk-based audit approach focuses on allocating resources to high-risk areas to make audits more effective but may not optimize resources.

questions.text: At the completion of a system development project, a postproject review should include which of the following?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assessing risk that may lead to downtime after the production release
choices.justification: An assessment of potential downtime should be made with the operations group and other specialists before implementing a system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identifying lessons learned that may be applicable to future projects
choices.justification: A project team has something to learn from every project. Because risk assessment is a key issue for project management, it is important for the organization to accumulate lessons learned and integrate them into future projects.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Verifying that the controls in the delivered system are working
choices.justification: Verifying that controls are working should be covered during the acceptance test phase and possibly again in the postimplementation review. The postproject review focuses on project-related issues.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensuring that test data are deleted
choices.justification: Test data should be retained for future regression testing.

questions.text: When preparing a business case to support the need of an electronic data warehouse solution, which of the following choices is the MOST important to assist management in the decision-making process?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discuss a single solution.
choices.justification: A business case should discuss all possible solutions to a given problem, which enables management to select the best option. This may include the option not to undertake the project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Consider security controls.
choices.justification: It may be important to include security considerations in the business case if security is important to the solution and will address the problem; however, the feasibility study is more important and is necessary regardless of the type of problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Demonstrate feasibility.
choices.justification: The business case should demonstrate feasibility for any potential project. By including a feasibility study in the business case along with a cost-benefit analysis, management can make an informed decision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Consult the audit department.
choices.justification: Although the person preparing the business case may consult with the organization audit department, this is situational and not necessary to include in the business case.

questions.text: A rapid application development methodology has been selected to implement a new enterprise resource planning system. All project activities have been assigned to the contracted consulting vendor because internal employees are unavailable. What is the information systems (IS) auditor’s FIRST step to compensate for the lack of resources?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: the project plan and approach.
choices.justification: Rapid methodologies require available resources with good expertise and a fast decision-making process because the plan duration is usually short. Reviewing the project plan and approach is the best recommendation to make the appropriate changes to compensate for the missing end users.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ask the vendor to provide additional external staff.
choices.justification: Adding external people to the project does not resolve the problem because they are not able to decide on behalf of the internal employees, who are usually end users from the business side.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recommend that the organization hire more people.
choices.justification: Hiring new people takes time and does not guarantee the readiness of new hires to make appropriate decisions in this project.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stop the project until all human resources are available.
choices.justification: Stopping the project can be a good option, but reviewing the project and considering all aspects should be done first.

questions.text: When implementing an application software package, which of the following presents the GREATEST risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Uncontrolled multiple software versions
choices.justification: Having multiple versions is a problem, but if the correct version is implemented, the most serious risk during implementation is to have the parameters for the program set incorrectly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Source programs that are not synchronized with object code
choices.justification: Lack of synchronization between source and object code is a serious risk for later maintenance of compiled programs but does not affect other types of programs and is not the most serious risk at the time of implementation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Incorrectly set parameters
choices.justification: Parameters that are not set correctly is the greatest concern when implementing an application software package. Incorrectly set parameters are an immediate problem that can lead to system breach, failure or noncompliance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Programming errors
choices.justification: Programming errors should be found during testing, not at the time of implementation.

questions.text: The specific advantage of white box testing is that it:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: verifies that a program can operate successfully with other parts of the system.
choices.justification: Verifying that the program can operate successfully with other parts of the system is sociability testing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ensures a program’s functional operating effectiveness without regard to the internal program structure.
choices.justification: Testing the program’s functionality without knowledge of internal structures is black box testing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: determines procedural accuracy or conditions of a program’s specific logic paths.
choices.justification: White box testing assesses the effectiveness of software program logic. Specifically, test data are used in determining procedural accuracy or conditions of a program’s logic paths.

choices.label: D
choices.text: examines a program’s functionality by executing it in a tightly controlled or virtual environment with restricted access to the host system.
choices.justification: Controlled testing of programs in a semidebugged environment, either heavily controlled step-by-step or via monitoring in virtual machines, is sandbox testing.

domain: Information Systems Acquisition, Development, and Implementation

questions.text: Which of the following BEST helps an information systems (IS) auditor assess and measure the value of a newly implemented system?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: of business requirements
choices.justification: Although reviewing the business requirements is important, only a postimplementation review provides evidence that the project met the business requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System certification
choices.justification: System certification involves performing a comprehensive assessment against a standard of management, operational and technical controls in an information system to examine the level of compliance in meeting certain requirements, such as standards, policies, processes, procedures, work instructions and guidelines.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Postimplementation review
choices.justification: One key objective of a postimplementation review is to evaluate the projected cost-benefits or the return-on-investment measurements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: System accreditation
choices.justification: System accreditation is an official management decision to authorize operation of an information system and to explicitly accept the risk to the organization’s operations, assets or individuals based on the implementation of an agreed-on set of requirements and security controls.

questions.text: An organization is implementing a new system to replace a legacy system. Which of the following conversion practices creates the GREATEST risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pilot
choices.justification: A pilot implementation is the implementation of the system at a single location or region and then a rollout of the system to the rest of the organization, after the application and implementation plan have been proven to work correctly at the pilot location.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Parallel
choices.justification: A parallel test requires running the old and new systems in parallel for a time period. This test highlights any problems or inconsistencies between the old and new systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct cutover
choices.justification: Direct cutover implies switching to the new system immediately, usually without the ability to revert to the old system in the event of problems. This approach has the highest risk and may have a significant impact on the organization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phased
choices.justification: A phased approach is used to implement the system in phases or sections—this minimizes the overall risk by only affecting one area at a time.

questions.text: Which of the following is an advantage of the top-down approach to software testing?
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interface errors are identified early.
choices.justification: The advantage of the top-down approach is that tests of major functions are conducted early, thus enabling the detection of interface errors sooner.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Testing can be started before all programs are complete.
choices.justification: The advantage that testing can be started before all programs are complete is characteristic of the bottom-up approach to system testing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is more effective than other testing approaches.
choices.justification: The most effective testing approach is dependent on the environment being tested.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Errors in critical modules are detected sooner.
choices.justification: Detecting errors in critical modules sooner is an advantage of the bottom-up approach to system testing.

questions.text: A project manager for a project that is scheduled to take 18 months to complete announces that the project is in a healthy financial position because, after six months, only one-sixth of the budget has been spent. The information systems (IS) auditor should FIRST determine:
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: the amount of progress achieved compared to the project schedule.
choices.justification: Cost performance of a project cannot be properly assessed in isolation of schedule performance. Cost cannot be assessed simply in terms of elapsed time on a project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: if the project budget can be reduced.
choices.justification: To properly assess the project budget position, it is necessary to know how much progress has actually been made and, given this, what level of expenditure would be expected. It is possible that project expenditure appears to be low because actual progress has been slow. Until the analysis of project against schedule has been completed, it is impossible to know whether there is any reason to reduce budget. If the project has slipped behind schedule, then it is possible that not only there is no spare budget, but also extra expenditure may be needed to retrieve the slippage. The low expenditure can be representative of a situation where the project is likely to miss deadlines rather than potentially come in ahead of time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: if the project can be completed ahead of schedule.
choices.justification: If the project is found to be ahead of budget after adjusting for actual progress, this is not necessarily a good outcome, because it points to flaws in the original budgeting process; and, until further analysis is undertaken, it cannot be determined whether any spare funds actually exist.

choices.label: D
choices.text: if the budget savings can be applied to increase the project scope.
choices.justification: If the project is behind schedule, adding scope may be the wrong thing to do.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor recommends that an initial validation control be programmed into a credit card transaction capture application. The initial validation process MOST likely:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: checks to ensure that the type of transaction is valid for the card type.
choices.justification: The initial validation is not used to check the transaction type—just the validity of the card number.

choices.label: B
choices.text: verifies the format of the number entered, then locates it on the database.
choices.justification: The initial validation should confirm whether the card is valid. This validity is established through the card number and personal identification number entered by the user.

choices.label: C
choices.text: ensures that the transaction entered is within the cardholder’s credit limit.
choices.justification: The initial validation is to prove the card number entered is valid—only then can the transaction amount be checked for approval from the bank.

choices.label: D
choices.text: confirms that the card is not shown as lost or stolen on the master file.
choices.justification: The verification that the card has not been reported as lost or stolen is only done after the card number has been validated as correctly entered.

questions.text: At the end of the testing phase of software development, an information systems (IS) auditor observes that an intermittent software error has not been corrected. No action has been taken to resolve the error. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: report the error as a finding and leave further exploration to the auditee’s discretion.
choices.justification: Recording it as a minor error and leaving it to the auditee’s discretion is inappropriate. Action should be taken before the application goes into production.

choices.label: B
choices.text: attempt to resolve the error.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor is not authorized to resolve the error.

choices.label: C
choices.text: recommend that problem resolution be escalated.
choices.justification: When an IS auditor observes such conditions, it is best to fully apprise the auditee and suggest that further problem resolutions be attempted, including escalation if necessary.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ignore the error because it is not possible to get objective evidence for the software error.
choices.justification: Neglecting the error indicates that the IS auditor has not taken steps to further probe the issue to its logical end.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing a project that is using an agile software development approach. Which of the following should the IS auditor expect to find?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
