choices.text: Use of a capability maturity model (CMM).
choices.justification: The capability maturity model (CMM) places heavy emphasis on predefined formal processes and formal project-management and software-development deliverables. Agile software development projects, by contrast, rely on refinement of process as dictated by the particular needs of the project and team dynamics.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Regular monitoring of task-level progress against schedule.
choices.justification: Task-level tracking is not used because daily meetings identify challenges and impediments to the project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Extensive use of software development tools to maximize team productivity.
choices.justification: Agile projects make use of suitable development tools; however, tools are not seen as the primary means of achieving productivity. Team harmony, effective communications and collective ability to solve challenges are of greater importance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Post-iteration reviews that identify lessons learned for future use in the project.
choices.justification: A key tenet of the agile approach to software project management is ongoing team learning to refine project management and software development processes as the project progresses. The team considers and documents what worked well and what could have worked better at the end of each iteration and identifies improvements to be implemented in subsequent iterations. Additionally, less importance is placed on formal paper-based deliverables, with the preference being effective informal communication within the team and with key outside contributors. Agile projects produce releasable software in short iterations, typically ranging from four-to-eight weeks, which instills considerable performance discipline within the team. These practices, combined with short daily meetings to agree on what the team is doing and the identification of any impediments, render task-level tracking against a schedule redundant.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST important factor in the design of a data warehouse?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Quality of the metadata
choices.justification: Quality of the metadata is the most important element in the design of a data warehouse. A data warehouse is a copy of transaction data specifically structured for query and analysis. Metadata describes the data in the warehouse and aims to provide a table of contents to the stored information. Organizations that have built warehouses believe that metadata are the most important component of the warehouse.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Speed of the transactions
choices.justification: A data warehouse is used for analysis and research, not for production operations, so the speed of transactions is not relevant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Volatility of the data
choices.justification: Data in a data warehouse are frequently received from many sources and vast amounts of information may be received on an hourly or daily basis. Except to ensure adequate storage capability, this is not a primary concern of the designer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vulnerability of the system
choices.justification: Data warehouses may contain sensitive information or can be used to research sensitive information, so the security of the data warehouse is important. However, this is not the primary concern of the designer.

questions.text: Which of the following types of risk is MOST likely encountered in a software as a service (SaaS) environment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Noncompliance with software license agreements
choices.justification: Software as a service (SaaS) is provisioned on a usage basis and the number of users is monitored by the SaaS provider; therefore, there should be no risk of noncompliance with software license agreements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Performance issues due to Internet delivery method
choices.justification: The risk that can be most likely encountered in a SaaS environment is speed and availability issues because SaaS relies on the Internet for connectivity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Higher cost due to software licensing requirements
choices.justification: The costs for a SaaS solution should be fixed as a part of the services contract and considered in the business case presented to management for approval of the solution.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Higher cost due to the need to update to compatible hardware
choices.justification: The open design and Internet connectivity allow most SaaS to run on any type of hardware.

questions.text: An organization is migrating from a legacy system to an enterprise resource planning system. While reviewing the data migration activity, the MOST important concern for the information systems (IS) auditor is to determine that there is a:
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: correlation of semantic characteristics of the data migrated between the two systems.
choices.justification: Because the two systems can have a different data representation, including the database schema, the information systems (IS) auditor’s main concern should be to verify that the interpretation of the data (structure) is the same in the new system as it was in the old system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: correlation of arithmetic characteristics of the data migrated between the two systems.
choices.justification: Arithmetic characteristics represent aspects of data structure and internal definition in the database and, therefore, are less important than the semantic characteristics.

choices.label: C
choices.text: correlation of functional characteristics of the processes between the two systems.
choices.justification: A review of the correlation of the functional characteristics between the two systems is not relevant to a data migration review.

choices.label: D
choices.text: relative efficiency of the processes between the two systems.
choices.justification: A review of the relative efficiencies of the processes between the two systems is not relevant to a data migration review.

questions.text: Which of the following is of GREATEST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor when performing an audit of a client relationship management system migration project?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: The technical migration is planned for a Friday preceding a long weekend, and the time window is too short for completing all tasks.
choices.justification: A weekend can be used as a time buffer so that the new system will have a better chance of being up and running after the weekend.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Employees pilot testing the system are concerned that the data representation in the new system is completely different from the old system.
choices.justification: A different data representation does not mean different data presentation at the front end. Even when this is the case, this issue can be solved by adequate training and user support.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A single implementation is planned, immediately decommissioning the legacy system.
choices.justification: Major system migrations should include a phase of parallel operation or a phased cutover to reduce implementation risk. Decommissioning or disposing of the old hardware would complicate any fallback strategy if the new system does not operate correctly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Five weeks prior to the target date, there are still numerous defects in the printing functionality of the new system’s software.
choices.justification: The printing functionality is commonly one of the last functions to be tested in a new system because it is usually the last step performed in any business event. Thus, meaningful testing and the respective error fixing are only possible after all other parts of the software have been successfully tested.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST helps to prioritize project activities and determine the timeline for a project?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gantt chart
choices.justification: A Gantt chart is a simple project management tool which typically shows the timeline, status, and responsible person for each task in the project. It helps with the prioritization requirement, but it is not as effective as program evaluation review technique (PERT).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Earned value analysis
choices.justification: Earned value analysis is a technique to track project cost versus project deliverables but does not assist in prioritizing tasks. It provides better clarity and control of the activities involved and enables project managers to respond to issues early.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Program evaluation review technique (PERT)
choices.justification: The PERT method works on the principle of obtaining project timelines based on project events for three likely scenarios—worst, best and normal. The timeline is calculated by a predefined formula and identifies the critical path, which identifies the key activities that must be prioritized.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Function point analysis
choices.justification: Function point analysis is a standardized method used commonly as an estimation technique in software engineering. It measures the complexity of input and output and does not help to prioritize project activities.

questions.text: Change control for business application systems being developed using prototyping can be complicated by the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: iterative nature of prototyping.
choices.justification: A characteristic of prototyping is its iterative nature, but it does not have an adverse effect on change control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: rapid pace of modifications in requirements and design.
choices.justification: Changes in requirements and design happen so quickly that they are seldom documented or approved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: emphasis on reports and screens.
choices.justification: A characteristic of prototyping is its emphasis on reports and screens, but it does not have an adverse effect on change control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: lack of integrated tools.
choices.justification: Lack of integrated tools is a characteristic of prototyping, but it does not have an adverse effect on change control.

questions.text: An organization sells books and music online on its secure website. Transactions are transferred to the accounting and delivery systems every hour to be processed. Which of the following controls BEST ensures that sales processed on the secure website are transferred to both systems?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transaction totals are recorded daily in the sales systems. Daily sales system totals are aggregated and totaled.
choices.justification: Totaling transactions on the sales system does not address the transfer of data from the online systems to the accounting system, but rather considers only the sales system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transactions are automatically numerically sequenced. Sequences are checked and gaps in continuity are accounted for.
choices.justification: Automatic numerical sequencing is the only option that accounts for completeness of transactions because any missing transactions are identified by a gap.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Processing systems check for duplicated transaction numbers. If a transaction number is duplicated (already present), it is rejected.
choices.justification: Checking for duplicates is a valid control; however, it does not address whether the sales transactions processed are complete (ensuring that all transactions are recorded).

choices.label: D
choices.text: System time is synchronized hourly using a centralized time server. All transactions have a date/time stamp.
choices.justification: A date/time stamp does not help account for transactions that are missing or incomplete in the accounting and delivery systems.

questions.text: Which of the following should an information systems (IS) auditor review to gain an understanding of the effectiveness of controls over the management of multiple projects?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Project information
choices.justification: Project information may contain the information about control effectiveness for one specific project and updates to various parameters pertaining to the current status of that single project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Policy documents
choices.justification: Policy documents on project management set direction for the design, development, implementation and monitoring of the project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Project portfolio information
choices.justification: Project portfolio information is the basis for project portfolio management. It includes project data, such as owner, schedules, objectives, project type, status and cost. Project portfolio management requires specific project portfolio reports.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Program organization
choices.justification: Program organization is the team required (steering committee, quality assurance, systems personnel, analyst, programmer, hardware support, etc.) to meet the delivery objectives of the projects.

questions.text: Ideally, stress testing should be carried out in a:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: test environment using test data.
choices.justification: A test environment should always be used to avoid damaging the production environment, but testing only with test data may not test all aspects of the system adequately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: production environment using live workloads.
choices.justification: Testing should never take place in a production environment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: test environment using live workloads.
choices.justification: Stress testing is carried out to ensure that a system can cope with production workloads. Testing with production-level workloads is important to ensure that the system will operate effectively when moved into production.

choices.label: D
choices.text: production environment using test data.
choices.justification: It is not advisable to do stress testing in a production environment. Additionally, if only test data are used, there is no certainty that the system was stress tested adequately.

questions.text: In an online transaction processing system, data integrity is maintained by ensuring that a transaction is either completed in its entirety or not at all. This principle of data integrity is known as:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: isolation.
choices.justification: Isolation ensures that each transaction is isolated from other transactions; hence, each transaction can only access data if they are not being simultaneously accessed or modified by another process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: consistency.
choices.justification: Consistency ensures that all integrity conditions in the database are maintained with each transaction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: atomicity.
choices.justification: The principle of atomicity requires that a transaction be completed in its entirety or not at all. If an error or interruption occurs, all changes made up to that point are backed out.

choices.label: D
choices.text: durability.
choices.justification: Durability ensures that, when a transaction has been reported back to a user as complete, the resultant changes to the database will survive subsequent hardware or software failures.

questions.text: Which of the following is a characteristic of timebox management?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Not suitable for prototyping or rapid application development
choices.justification: Timebox management is very suitable for prototyping and rapid application development.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eliminates the need for a quality process
choices.justification: Timebox management does not eliminate the need for a quality process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prevents cost overruns and delivery delays
choices.justification: Timebox management, by its nature, sets specific time and cost boundaries. It is effective in controlling costs and delivery timelines by ensuring that each segment of the project is divided into small controllable time frames.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Separates system and user acceptance testing (UAT)
choices.justification: Timebox management integrates system and user acceptance testing (UAT).

questions.text: Which of the following types of risk can result from inadequate software project baselining?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sign-off delays
choices.justification: Sign-off delays may occur due to inadequate software baselining; however, these are most likely caused by scope creep.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Software integrity violations
choices.justification: Software integrity violations can be caused by hardware or software failures, malicious intrusions, or user errors. Software baselining does not help prevent software integrity violations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Scope creep
choices.justification: A software baseline is the cutoff point in the design and development of a system. Beyond this point, additional requirements or modifications to the scope must go through formal, strict procedures for approval based on a business cost-benefit analysis. Failure to adequately manage a system through baselining can result in uncontrolled changes in a project’s scope and may incur time and budget overruns.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inadequate controls
choices.justification: Inadequate controls are most likely present in situations in which information security is not duly considered from the beginning of system development; they are not a risk that can be adequately addressed by software baselining.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST critical when creating data for testing the logic in a new or modified application system?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enough data for each test case
choices.justification: The quantity of data for each test case is not as important as having test cases that address all types of operating conditions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Data representing expected conditions in actual processing
choices.justification: Selecting the right kind of data is key in testing a computer system. The data should not only include valid and invalid data but also be representative of actual processing; quality is more important than quantity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Completing the test on schedule
choices.justification: It is more important to have adequate test data than to complete the testing on schedule.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A random sample of actual data
choices.justification: It is unlikely that a random sample of actual data covers all test conditions and provides a reasonable representation of actual data.

questions.text: The MOST important point of consideration for an information systems (IS) auditor while reviewing an enterprise’s project portfolio is that it:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: does not exceed the existing IT budget.
choices.justification: Managing the project portfolio within the agreed budget is important, and it should be identified if the project portfolio exceeds the IT budget. However, it is not as critical as ensuring that it is aligned with the business plan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is aligned with the investment strategy.
choices.justification: Project portfolio alignment has an explicit directive and strategic goal in aligning existing projects to business strategies and ensuring continuous alignment to continue investing in the properly aligned projects and portfolios. Thus, the project portfolio should be aligned with the investment strategy, but it is more important that it is aligned with the business plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: has been approved by the IT steering committee.
choices.justification: Appropriate approval of the project portfolio should be granted. However, not every enterprise has an IT steering committee, and this is not as critical as ensuring that the projects are aligned with the business plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: is aligned with the business plan.
choices.justification: Portfolio management takes a holistic view of an enterprise’s overall IT strategy, which, in turn, should be aligned with the business strategy. A business plan provides the justification for each of the projects in the project portfolio, and that is the major consideration for an information systems (IS) auditor.

questions.text: The most common reason for the failure of information systems to meet the needs of users is that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: user needs are constantly changing.
choices.justification: Although changing user needs influences the success or failure of many projects, the core problem is usually a lack of getting the initial requirements correct at the beginning of the project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the growth of system requirements was forecast inaccurately.
choices.justification: Projects may fail as the needs of the users increase; however, this can be mitigated through better change control procedures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the hardware system limits the number of concurrent users.
choices.justification: Rarely do hardware limitations affect the usability of the project if the requirements were correctly documented at the beginning of the project.

choices.label: D
choices.text: user participation in defining the system’s requirements was inadequate.
choices.justification: Lack of adequate user involvement, especially in the system’s requirements phase, usually results in a system that does not fully or adequately address the needs of the user. Only users can define what their needs are and, therefore, what the system should accomplish.

questions.text: An organization recently deployed a customer relationship management application that was developed in-house. Which of the following is the BEST option to ensure that the application operates as designed?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: user acceptance testing (UAT)
choices.justification: user acceptance testing (UAT) verifies that the system functionality is deemed acceptable by the end users of the system; however, a review of UAT does not validate whether the system is performing as designed because UAT is performed on a subset of system functionality. The UAT review is a part of the postimplementation review.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Project risk assessment
choices.justification: Although a risk assessment highlights the risk of the system, it does not include an analysis to verify that the system is operating as designed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Postimplementation review
choices.justification: The purpose of a postimplementation review is to evaluate how successfully the project results match original goals, objectives and deliverables. The postimplementation review also evaluates how effective the project management practices were in keeping the project on track.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Management approval of the system
choices.justification: Management approval of the system can be based on reduced functionality and does not verify that the system is operating as designed. Management approval is a part of postimplementation review.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor who is auditing the software acquisition process will ensure that the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: contract is reviewed and approved by the legal counsel before it is signed.
choices.justification: and approving the contract is one of the most important steps in the software acquisition process. An information systems (IS) auditor should verify that legal counsel reviewed and approved the contract before management signs the contract.

choices.label: B
choices.text: requirements cannot be met with the systems already in place.
choices.justification: Existing systems may meet the requirements, but management may choose to acquire software for other reasons.

choices.label: C
choices.text: requirements are found to be critical for the business.
choices.justification: Not all of the requirements in the contract need to support critical business needs; some requirements may be in the contract for ease-of-use or other purposes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: user participation is adequate in the process.
choices.justification: User participation is not necessarily required in the software acquisition process. Instead, users most likely participate in requirements definition and user acceptance testing.

questions.text: Normally, it is essential to involve which of the following stakeholders in the initiation stage of a project?
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: System owners
choices.justification: System owners are the information systems (project) sponsors or chief advocates. They normally are responsible for initiating and funding projects to develop, operate and maintain information systems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System users
choices.justification: System users are the individuals who use or are affected by the information system. Their requirements are crucial in the requirements definition, design and testing stages of a project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: System designers
choices.justification: System designers translate business requirements and constraints into technical solutions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: System builders
choices.justification: System builders construct the system based on the specifications from the systems designers. In most cases, the designers and builders are the same personnel.

questions.text: When reviewing input controls, an information systems (IS) auditor observes that, in accordance with corporate policy, procedures allow supervisory override of data validation edits. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: not be concerned, because there may be other compensating controls to mitigate the risk.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor should not assume that compensating controls exist.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ensure that overrides are automatically logged and subject to review.
choices.justification: If input procedures allow overrides of data validation and editing, automatic logging should occur. A management individual who did not initiate the override should review this log.

choices.label: C
choices.text: verify whether all such overrides are referred to senior management for approval.
choices.justification: The log may be reviewed by another manager but does not require senior management approval.

choices.label: D
choices.text: recommend that overrides not be permitted.
choices.justification: If the overrides are policy-compliant, there is no need for senior management approval or a blanket prohibition.

questions.text: Following good practices, formal plans for implementation of new information systems are developed during the:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: development phase.
choices.justification: The implementation plans are updated during the development of the system, but the plans were already addressed during the design phase.

choices.label: B
choices.text: design phase.
choices.justification: The method of implementation may affect the design of the system. Therefore, planning for implementation should begin well in advance of the actual implementation date. A formal implementation plan should be constructed in the design phase and revised as the development progresses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: testing phase.
choices.justification: The testing phase focuses on testing the system and is not concerned with implementation planning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: deployment phase.
choices.justification: The deployment phase implements the system according to the plans set out earlier in the design phase.

questions.text: Many IT projects experience problems because the development time and/or resource requirements are underestimated. Which of the following techniques provides the GREATEST assistance in developing an estimate of project duration?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Function point analysis
choices.justification: Function point analysis is a technique for determining the size of a development task based on the number of function points. Function points are factors such as inputs, outputs, inquiries and logical internal files. Although this helps determine the size of individual activities, it does not assist in determining project duration, because there are many overlapping tasks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Program evaluation review technique (PERT) chart
choices.justification: The program evaluation review technique (PERT) method works on the principle of obtaining project timelines based on project events for three likely scenarios—worst, best and normal. The timeline is calculated by a predefined formula and identifies the critical path, which identifies the key activities that must be prioritized. A PERT chart helps determine project duration after all the activities and the work involved with those activities are known.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rapid application development
choices.justification: Rapid application development is a methodology that enables organizations to develop strategically important systems faster, while reducing development costs and maintaining quality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Object-oriented system development
choices.justification: Object-oriented design is an approach to systems development that proposes the use of system objects to build new systems and rebuild old ones. An important goal of object-oriented development is to change the nature of traditional software development from designing and writing models from scratch to building systems through the assembly of reusable software objects. Object-oriented system development is the process of solution specification and modeling but does not assist in calculating project duration.

questions.text: When a new system is to be implemented within a short time frame, it is MOST important to:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: finish writing user manuals.
choices.justification: The completion of the user manuals is less important than the need to test the system adequately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: perform user acceptance testing (UAT).
choices.justification: It is most important to complete the user acceptance testing to ensure that the system to be implemented is working correctly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: add last-minute enhancements to functionalities.
choices.justification: Adding another enhancement increases the time to complete a project because the code must be frozen and testing must be completed, then any other changes as future enhancements must be made.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ensure that the code has been documented and reviewed.
choices.justification: It is appropriate to have the code documented and reviewed, but, unless the acceptance testing is completed, there is no guarantee that the system will work correctly and meet user requirements.

questions.text: Which of the following system and data conversion strategies provides the GREATEST redundancy?
questions.taskStatement: Apply project management methodologies to the audit process

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct cutover
choices.justification: Direct cutover has high risk because it does not provide for a period of testing and adjustments, nor does it provide an easy fallback option if the new system fails.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pilot study
choices.justification: A pilot study approach is performed incrementally, making rollback procedures difficult to execute.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Phased approach
choices.justification: A phased approach is performed incrementally, making rollback procedures difficult to execute.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Parallel run
choices.justification: Parallel runs are the safest—although the most expensive—approach because both the old and new systems are run, thus incurring what might appear to be double costs.

questions.text: During a postimplementation review of an enterprise resource management system, an information systems (IS) auditor is MOST likely to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: review access control configuration.
choices.justification: access control configuration is the first task performed to determine whether security has been appropriately mapped in the system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: evaluate interface testing.
choices.justification: Because a postimplementation review is done after user acceptance testing and actual implementation, an information systems (IS) auditor does not evaluate interface testing. Evaluating interface testing is part of the implementation process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: review detailed design documentation.
choices.justification: The issue of reviewing detailed design documentation is not generally relevant to an enterprise resource management system, because these systems are usually vendor packages with user manuals. Further, because the system has been implemented, the IS auditor would only check the detailed design if there appeared to be a gap between design and functionality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: evaluate system testing.
choices.justification: System testing should be performed before final user signoff. The IS auditor should not need to review the system tests after implementation.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing a series of completed projects finds that the implemented functionality often exceeded requirements and most of the projects ran significantly over budget. Which of these areas of the organization’s project management process is the MOST likely cause of this issue?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Project scope management
choices.justification: Because the implemented functionality is greater than what was required, the most likely cause of the budget issue is failure to effectively manage project scope. Project scope management is defined as the processes required to ensure that the project includes all of the required work, and only the required work, to complete the project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Project time management
choices.justification: Project time management is defined as the processes required to ensure timely completion of the project. The issue noted in the question does not mention whether projects were completed on time, so this is not the most likely cause.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Project risk management
choices.justification: Project risk management is defined as the processes concerned with identifying, analyzing and responding to project risk. Although the budget overruns mentioned above represent one form of project risk, they appear to be caused by implementing too much functionality, which relates more directly to project scope.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Project procurement management
choices.justification: Project procurement management is defined as the processes required to acquire goods and services from outside the performing organization. Although purchasing goods and services that are too expensive can cause budget overruns, in this case, the key to the question is that implemented functionality is greater than what was required, which is more likely related to project scope.

domain: Information Systems Acquisition, Development, and Implementation

questions.text: Which of the following is an advantage of prototyping?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: The finished system normally has strong internal controls.
choices.justification: Prototyping often has poor internal controls because the focus is primarily on functionality, not on security.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prototype systems can provide significant time and cost savings.
choices.justification: Prototype systems can provide significant time and cost savings through better user interaction and the ability to rapidly adapt to changing requirements; however, they also have several disadvantages, including loss of overall security focus, project oversight and implementation of a prototype that is not yet ready for production.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Change control is often less complicated with prototype systems.
choices.justification: Change control becomes much more complicated with prototyping.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prototyping ensures that functions or extras are not added to the intended system.
choices.justification: Prototyping often leads to functions or extras being added to the system that were not originally intended.

questions.text: Which of the following types of testing determines whether a new or modified system can operate in its target environment without adversely impacting other existing systems?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Parallel testing
choices.justification: Parallel testing is the process of feeding data into two systems—the modified system and an alternate system—and comparing the results. In this approach, the old and new systems operate concurrently for a period and perform the same processing functions. This allows a new system to be tested without affecting existing systems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pilot testing
choices.justification: Pilot testing takes place first at one location and is then extended to other locations. The purpose is to see if the new system operates satisfactorily in one place before implementing it at other locations. In most cases, the cutover to the new system will disable existing systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Interface/integration testing
choices.justification: Interface/integration testing is a hardware or software test that evaluates the connection of two or more components that pass information from one area to another. The objective is to take unit-tested modules and build an integrated structure. This does not test in a true production environment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sociability testing
choices.justification: The purpose of sociability testing is to confirm that a new or modified system can operate in its target environment without adversely impacting existing systems. This should cover the platform that will perform primary application processing and interfaces with other systems, and changes to the desktop in a client-server or web development.

questions.text: During the information systems (IS) audit of large payment systems of an organization, it was noticed that during code coverage testing, the quality assurance team analyzed a nonproduction environment using logging and tracing tools. Which of the following code issues is MOST likely to be missed because of this action?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Race condition
choices.justification: The changes from a testing environment with instrumentation inserted into the code and the production environment for the code can mask timing-related issues like race conditions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bounds checking
choices.justification: Bounds checking is a coding issue rather than an environmental issue.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Input validation
choices.justification: Input validation is a coding issue rather than an environmental issue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pointer manipulation
choices.justification: Pointer manipulation is a coding issue rather than an environmental issue.

questions.text: A failure discovered in which of the following testing stages would have the GREATEST impact on the implementation of new application software?
questions.taskStatement: Determine whether the organization has defined ownership of IT risk, controls, and standards

choices.label: A
choices.text: System testing
choices.justification: System testing is undertaken by the development team to determine if the combined units of software work together and that the software meets user requirements per specifications. A failure here is expensive but easier to fix than a failure found later in the testing process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acceptance testing
choices.justification: Acceptance testing is the final stage before the software is installed and available for use. Software failure at the acceptance testing level has the greatest impact on implementation because this can result in delays and cost overruns.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Integration testing
choices.justification: Integration testing examines the units/modules as one integrated system. A failure here is expensive and requires re-work of the modules but is not as expensive as a problem found just prior to implementation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unit testing
choices.justification: Unit testing examines the individual units or components of the software. A failure here is expensive and requires re-work of the modules but is not as expensive as a problem found just prior to implementation. System, integration and unit testing are all performed by the developers at various stages of development; the impact of failure is comparatively less for each than failure at the acceptance testing stage.

questions.text: During the review of a web-based software development project, an information systems (IS) auditor realizes that coding standards are not enforced and code reviews are rarely carried out. These issues will MOST likely increase the likelihood of a successful:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: buffer overflow.
choices.justification: Poorly written code, especially in web-based applications, is often exploited by hackers using buffer overflow techniques.

choices.label: B
choices.text: brute force attack.
choices.justification: A brute force attack is used to crack passwords, but this is not related to coding standards.

choices.label: C
choices.text: distributed denial of service (DDoS) attack.
choices.justification: A distributed denial of service (DDoS) attack floods its target with numerous packets to prevent it from responding to legitimate requests. This is not related to coding standards.

choices.label: D
choices.text: war dialing attack.
choices.justification: War dialing uses modem-scanning tools to hack private branch exchanges or other telecommunications services.

questions.text: Which testing approach is MOST appropriate to ensure that internal application interface errors are identified as soon as possible?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bottom-up testing
choices.justification: A bottom-up approach to testing begins with atomic units, such as programs and modules, and works upward until a complete system test has taken place.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sociability testing
choices.justification: Sociability testing takes place at a later stage in the development process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Top-down testing
choices.justification: The top-down approach to testing ensures that interface errors are detected early and that testing of major functions is conducted early.

choices.label: D
choices.text: System testing
choices.justification: System tests take place at a later stage in the development process.

questions.text: Which of the following data validation edits is effective in detecting transposition and transcription errors?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Range check
choices.justification: A range check is checking data that matches a predetermined range of allowable values.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Check digit
choices.justification: A check digit is a numeric value that is calculated mathematically and is appended to data to ensure that the original data have not been altered (e.g., an incorrect, but valid, value substituted for the original). This control is effective in detecting transposition and transcription errors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Validity check
choices.justification: A validity check is programmed checking of the data validity in accordance with predetermined criteria.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Duplicate check
choices.justification: In a duplicate check, new or fresh transactions are matched to those previously entered to ensure that they are not already in the system.

questions.text: The PRIMARY objective of performing a postincident review is that it presents an opportunity to:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: improve the internal control process.
choices.justification: A postincident review examines the cause and response to an incident. The lessons learned from the review can be used to improve internal controls. Understanding the purpose and structure of postincident reviews and follow-up procedures enables the information security manager to continuously improve the security program. Improving the incident response plan based on the incident review is an internal (corrective) control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: harden the network to industry good practices.
choices.justification: A postincident review may result in improvements to controls, but its primary purpose is not to harden a network.

choices.label: C
choices.text: highlight the importance of incident response management to management.
choices.justification: The purpose of postincident review is to ensure that the opportunity is presented to learn lessons from the incident. It is not intended as a forum to educate management.

choices.label: D
choices.text: improve employee awareness of the incident response process.
choices.justification: An incident may be used to emphasize the importance of incident response, but that is not the intention of the postincident review.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes the concept of overfitting in machine learning (ML)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate potential opportunities and risks associated with emerging technologies, regulations, and industry practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overfitting happens when a model fails to capture the underlying patterns and relationships in the training data.
choices.justification: Underfitting occurs when a model is too simple and fails to capture the underlying patterns in the data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Overfitting occurs when advanced algorithms are underused in machine learning (ML).
choices.justification: The use of advanced algorithms is not directly related to overfitting.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Overfitting occurs when the training data are insufficient to train a ML model effectively.
choices.justification: In this case, the model may not generalize well due to insufficient training data, but this is not an example of overfitting.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Overfitting occurs when a model performs well on the training data but fails to generalize to new, unseen data.
choices.justification: Overfitting happens when a model becomes overly complex and starts fitting the noise or random fluctuations in the training data, which leads to poor performance on new, unseen data.

questions.text: What is the PRIMARY goal of natural language processing in the field of artificial intelligence?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate potential opportunities and risks associated with emerging technologies, regulations, and industry practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: To create algorithms that mimic human cognitive processes and decision making
choices.justification: Mimicking human cognitive processes and decision making is a broader objective of artificial intelligence and is not specific to natural language processing (NLP).

choices.label: B
choices.text: To build intelligent systems capable of autonomously learning from data
choices.justification: Autonomously learning from data is a goal of machine learning, which is a subfield of artificial intelligence, but not the primary focus of NLP.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To develop machines capable of understanding and generating human language
choices.justification: The primary goal of NLP is to enable machines to understand, interpret and generate human language in a way that is similar to how humans process language. NLP involves tasks such as language translation, sentiment analysis, text summarization and speech recognition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To design robots and physical agents that can interact with the environment
choices.justification: Designing robots and physical agents that interact with the environment falls within the scope of robotics and embodied artificial intelligence (AI), which are different areas of research than NLP.

questions.text: During the system testing phase of an application development project, the information systems (IS) auditor should review the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate audit processes as part of quality assurance and improvement programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: conceptual design specifications.
choices.justification: A conceptual design specification is a document prepared during the requirements definition phase. The system testing is based on a test plan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: vendor contract.
choices.justification: A vendor contract is prepared during a software acquisition process and may be reviewed to ensure that all the deliverables in the contract have been delivered, but the most important area of review is the error reports.

choices.label: C
choices.text: error reports.
choices.justification: Testing is crucial in determining that user requirements have been validated. The information systems (IS) auditor should be involved in this phase, review error reports for their precision in recognizing erroneous data and review the procedures for resolving errors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: program change requests.
choices.justification: Program change requests are reviewed normally as a part of the postimplementation phase.

questions.text: A proposed transaction processing application will have many data capture sources and outputs in paper and electronic form. To ensure that transactions are not lost during processing, an information systems (IS) auditor should recommend the inclusion of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: validation controls.
choices.justification: Validation controls can check for required fields, format and type of data, and other criteria that must be met before the data can be submitted to the server. This helps ensure that the data is accurate and complete and that the application can process them correctly. Input and output validation controls are, therefore, valid controls but do not detect and report lost transactions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: internal credibility checks.
choices.justification: Internal credibility checks are valid controls to detect errors in processing but do not detect and report lost transactions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: clerical control procedures.
choices.justification: A clerical procedure can be used to summarize and compare inputs and outputs; however, an automated process is less susceptible to error.

choices.label: D
choices.text: automated systems balancing.
choices.justification: Automated systems balancing is the best way to ensure that no transactions are lost because any imbalance between total inputs and total outputs is reported for investigation and correction.

questions.text: An advantage of using a bottom-up versus a top-down approach to software testing is that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: interface errors are detected earlier.
choices.justification: Interface errors will not be found until later in the testing process—during integration or system testing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: confidence in the system is achieved earlier.
choices.justification: Confidence in the system cannot be obtained until the testing is completed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: errors in critical modules are detected earlier.
choices.justification: The bottom-up approach to software testing begins with the testing of atomic units, such as programs and modules, and works upward until complete system testing has taken place. The advantage of using a bottom-up approach to software testing is that errors in critical modules are found earlier.

choices.label: D
choices.text: major functions and processing are tested earlier.
choices.justification: Bottom-up testing tests individual components and major functions and processing is not adequately tested until systems and integration testing is completed.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the software development process for an organization. Which of the following functions are appropriate for the end users to perform?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Program output testing
choices.justification: A user can test program output by checking the program input and comparing it with the system output. This task, although usually done by the programmer, can also be done effectively by the user.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System configuration
choices.justification: System configuration is usually too technical to be accomplished by a user and this situation can create security issues and introduce a separation of duties issue.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Program logic specification
choices.justification: Program logic specification is a very technical task that is normally performed by a programmer. A user performing this task can introduce a separation of duties issue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Performance tuning
choices.justification: Performance tuning also requires high levels of technical skill and cannot be effectively accomplished by a user. A user performing this task can introduce a separation of duties issue.

questions.text: The use of object-oriented design and development techniques MOST likely:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: facilitates the ability to reuse modules.
choices.justification: One of the major benefits of object-oriented design and development is the ability to reuse modules.

choices.label: B
choices.text: improves system performance.
choices.justification: Object-oriented design is not intended as a method of improving system performance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: enhances control effectiveness.
choices.justification: Control effectiveness is not an objective of object-oriented design; control effectiveness may be reduced through this approach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: speeds up the system development life cycle (SDLC).
choices.justification: The use of object-oriented design may speed up the system development life cycle (SDLC) for future projects through the reuse of modules, but object-oriented design does not speed up development of the initial project.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing a proposed application software acquisition should ensure that the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: operating system (OS) being used is compatible with the existing hardware platform.
choices.justification: If the operating system (OS) is currently being used, it is compatible with the existing hardware platform; if it were incompatible, it would not operate properly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: planned OS updates have been scheduled to minimize negative impacts on enterprise needs.
choices.justification: The planned OS updates should be scheduled to minimize negative impacts on the organization, but this is not an issue when considering the acquisition of new software.

choices.label: C
choices.text: OS has the latest versions and updates.
choices.justification: The installed OS should be equipped with the most recent versions and updates (with sufficient history and stability). Because the OS is installed, it is not a consideration at the time of considering acquisition of a new application.

choices.label: D
choices.text: product is compatible with the current or planned OS.
choices.justification: In reviewing the proposed application, the auditor should ensure that the products to be purchased are compatible with the current or planned OS.

questions.text: The BEST time for an information systems (IS) auditor to assess the control specifications of a new application software package that is being considered for acquisition is during:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: the internal lab testing phase.
choices.justification: During testing, the information systems (IS) auditor ensures that the security requirements are met. This is not the time to assess the control specifications.

choices.label: B
choices.text: testing and prior to user acceptance.
choices.justification: The control specifications drive the security requirements that are built into the contract and should be assessed before the product is acquired and tested.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the requirements gathering phase.
choices.justification: The best time for the involvement of an IS auditor is at the beginning of the requirements definition of the development or acquisition of applications software, providing maximum opportunity for review of the vendors and their products. Early engagement of an IS auditor also minimizes the potential of a business commitment to a given solution that might be inadequate and more difficult to overcome as the process continues.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the implementation phase.
choices.justification: During the implementation phase, the IS auditor may check whether the controls have been enabled; however, this is not the time to assess the control requirements.

questions.text: An organization is implementing an enterprise resource planning (ERP) application. Of the following, who is PRIMARILY responsible for overseeing the project to ensure that it is progressing in accordance with the project plan and that it will deliver the expected results?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Project sponsor
choices.justification: A project sponsor is typically the senior manager in charge of the primary business unit that the application will support. The sponsor provides funding for the project and works closely with the project manager to define the critical success factors or metrics for the project. The project sponsor is not responsible for reviewing the progress of the project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System development project team
choices.justification: A system development project team completes the assigned tasks, works according to the instructions of the project manager and communicates with the user project team. The system development project team is not responsible for overseeing the progress of the project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Project steering committee
choices.justification: A project steering committee that provides an overall direction for the enterprise resource planning (ERP) implementation project is responsible for reviewing the project’s progress to ensure that it will deliver the expected results.

choices.label: D
choices.text: User project team
choices.justification: A user project team (UPT) completes the assigned tasks, communicates effectively with the system development team and works according to the advice of the project manager. A UPT is not responsible for reviewing the progress of the project.

questions.text: Which of the following is the PRIMARY purpose for conducting parallel testing?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: To determine whether the system is cost-effective
choices.justification: Parallel testing may show that the old system is more cost-effective than the new system, but this is not the primary reason for parallel testing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To enable comprehensive unit and system testing
choices.justification: Unit and system testing are completed before parallel testing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To highlight errors in the program interfaces with files
choices.justification: Program interfaces with files are tested for errors during system testing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To ensure the new system meets user requirements
choices.justification: The purpose of parallel testing is to ensure that the implementation of a new system will meet user requirements by comparing the results of the old system with the new system to ensure correct processing.

questions.text: A large industrial organization is replacing an obsolete legacy system and evaluating whether to buy a custom solution or develop a system in-house. Which of the following will MOST likely influence the decision?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Technical skills and knowledge within the organization related to sourcing and software development
choices.justification: Critical core competencies will most likely be carefully considered before outsourcing the planning phase of the application.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Privacy requirements applied to the data processed by the application
choices.justification: Privacy regulations apply to both solutions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the legacy system being replaced was developed in-house
choices.justification: While individuals with knowledge of the legacy system are helpful, they may not have the technical skills to build a new system. Therefore, this is not the primary factor influencing the make versus buy decision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The users not devoting reasonable time to defining the functionalities of the solution
choices.justification: Unclear business requirements (functionalities) similarly affect either development process but are not the primary factor influencing the make versus buy decision.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST likely benefit of implementing a standardized infrastructure?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Improved cost-effectiveness of IT service delivery and operational support
choices.justification: A standardized IT infrastructure provides a consistent set of platforms and operating systems across the organization. This standardization reduces the time and effort required to manage a set of disparate platforms and operating systems. In addition, the implementation of enhanced operational support tools (e.g., password management tools, patch management tools and auto provisioning of user access) is simplified. These tools can help the organization reduce the cost of IT service delivery and operational support.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased security of the IT service delivery center
choices.justification: A standardized infrastructure results in a more homogeneous environment, which is more prone to attacks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduced level of investment in the IT infrastructure
choices.justification: While standardization can reduce support costs, the transition to a standardized kit can be expensive; therefore, the overall level of IT infrastructure investment is not likely to be reduced.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced need for testing future application changes
choices.justification: A standardized infrastructure may simplify testing of changes, but it does not reduce the need for such testing.

questions.text: The PRIMARY objective of conducting a postimplementation review for a business process automation project is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT vendor selection and contract management processes meet business, legal, and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: ensure that the project meets the intended business requirements.
choices.justification: Ensuring that the project meets the intended business requirements is the primary objective of a postimplementation review.

choices.label: B
choices.text: evaluate the adequacy of controls.
choices.justification: Evaluating the adequacy of controls may be part of the review but is not the primary objective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: confirm compliance with technological standards.
choices.justification: Confirming compliance with technological standards is normally not part of the postimplementation review, because this compliance should be addressed during the design and development phase.

choices.label: D
choices.text: confirm compliance with regulatory requirements.
choices.justification: Confirming compliance with regulatory requirements is normally not part of the postimplementation review, because this compliance should be addressed during the design and development phase.

questions.text: A legacy payroll application was migrated to a new application. Which of the following stakeholders should be PRIMARILY responsible for reviewing and signing off on the accuracy and completeness of the data before going live?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Information systems (IS) auditor
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor should ensure that there is a review and sign-off by the data owner during the data conversion stage of the project. An IS auditor is not responsible for reviewing and signing-off on the accuracy of the converted data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Database administrator (DBA)
choices.justification: A database administrator’s (DBA’s) primary responsibility is to maintain the integrity of the database and make the database available to users. A DBA is not responsible for reviewing migrated data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Project manager
choices.justification: A project manager provides day-to-day management and leadership of the project but is not responsible for the accuracy and integrity of the data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Data owner
choices.justification: During the data conversion stage of a project, the data owner is primarily responsible for reviewing and signing off that the data are migrated completely and accurately and are valid.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor who is invited to a project development meeting notes that no project risk has been documented. When the IS auditor raises this issue, the project manager responds that it is too early to identify risk and that, if risk starts impacting the project, a risk manager will be hired. The appropriate response of the IS auditor is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: stress the importance of spending time at this point in the project to consider and document risk and to develop contingency plans.
choices.justification: The majority of project risk can be identified before a project begins, allowing mitigation/avoidance plans to be put in place to deal with this risk. A project should have a clear link back to corporate strategy, enterprise risk management and tactical plans to support this strategy. The process of setting corporate strategy, setting objectives and developing tactical plans should include the consideration of risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: accept the project manager’s position because the project manager is accountable for the outcome of the project.
choices.justification: The project manager cannot accept responsibility for risk acceptance. The risk must be addressed continuously—starting as early in the process as possible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: offer to work with the risk manager when one is appointed.
choices.justification: Appointing a risk manager is a good practice, but waiting until the project has been impacted by risk is misguided. Risk management needs to be forward looking; allowing risk to evolve into issues that adversely impact the project represents a failure of risk management. With or without a risk manager, people within and outside of the project team need to be consulted and encouraged to comment when they believe new risk has emerged or risk priorities have changed. The information systems (IS) auditor has an obligation to the project sponsor and the organization to advise on appropriate project management practices. Waiting for the possible appointment of a risk manager represents an unnecessary and dangerous delay in implementing risk management.

choices.label: D
choices.text: inform the project manager that the IS auditor will conduct a review of the risk at the completion of the requirements definition phase of the project.
choices.justification: IS auditors cannot provide risk review without impairing their independence.

questions.text: The reason for establishing a stop or freezing point on the design of a new system is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: prevent further changes to a project in process.
choices.justification: The stop point is intended to provide greater control over changes but not to prevent them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: indicate the point at which the design is to be completed.
choices.justification: The stop point is used for project control but not to create an artificial fixed point that requires the design of the project to cease.

choices.label: C
choices.text: require that changes after that point be evaluated for cost-effectiveness.
choices.justification: Projects often tend to expand, especially during the requirements definition phase. This expansion often grows to a point where the originally anticipated cost benefits are diminished because the cost of the project has increased. When this occurs, it is recommended that the project be stopped or frozen to allow a review of all the cost benefits and the payback period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: provide the project management team with more control over the project design.
choices.justification: A stop point is used to control requirements, not project design.

questions.text: Documentation of a business case used in an IT development project should be retained until:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: the end of the system’s life cycle.
choices.justification: A business case should be used throughout the life cycle of the product. It serves as an anchor for new management personnel, helps to maintain focus and provides valuable information on estimates versus actuals. Questions, such as “Why do we do that?”, “What was the original intent?” and “How did we perform against the plan?”, can be answered, and lessons for developing future business cases can be learned.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the project is approved.
choices.justification: The business case should be retained even after project approval to provide ability to review and validate the business case once the project is implemented.

choices.label: C
choices.text: user acceptance of the system.
choices.justification: The business case will be retained throughout the system development life cycle for later reference and validation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the system is in production.
choices.justification: After the system is in production, the business case can be validated to ensure that the promised costs and benefits were correct.

questions.text: When reviewing an active project, an information systems (IS) auditor observed that the business case was no longer valid because of a reduction in anticipated benefits and increased costs. The IS auditor should recommend that the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: project be discontinued.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor should not recommend discontinuing or completing the project before reviewing an updated business case.

choices.label: B
choices.text: business case be updated and possible corrective actions be identified.
choices.justification: The IS auditor should recommend that the business case be kept current throughout the project because it is a key input to decisions made throughout the life of any project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: project be returned to the project sponsor for re-approval.
choices.justification: The project cannot be returned to the sponsor until the business case has been updated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: project be completed and the business case be updated later.
choices.justification: An IS auditor should not recommend completing the project before reviewing an updated business case and ensuring approval from the project sponsor.

questions.text: The knowledge base of an expert system that uses questionnaires to lead the user through a series of choices before a conclusion is reached is known as:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: rules.
choices.justification: Rules refer to the expression of declarative knowledge using if-then relationships.

choices.label: B
choices.text: decision trees.
choices.justification: Decision trees use questionnaires to lead a user through a series of choices until a conclusion is reached.

choices.label: C
choices.text: semantic nets.
choices.justification: Semantic nets consist of a graph in which nodes represent physical or conceptual objects and the arcs describe the relationship between the nodes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: dataflow diagrams.
choices.justification: A dataflow diagram is used to map the progress of data through a system and examine logic, error handling and data management.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing system development for a health care organization with two application environments—production and test. During an interview, the auditor notes that production data are used in the test environment to test program changes. What is the MOST significant potential risk from this situation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: The test environment may not have adequate controls to ensure data accuracy.
choices.justification: The accuracy of data used in the test environment is not of significant concern if these data are representative of the production environment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The test environment may produce inaccurate results due to use of production data.
choices.justification: Using production data in the test environment does not cause test results to be inaccurate ; it has the opposite effect. Using production data improves the accuracy of testing processes, because the data most closely mirrors the production environment. Despite this, the risk of data disclosure or unauthorized access in the test environment is significant; therefore, production data should not be used in the test environment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hardware in the test environment may not be identical to the production environment.
choices.justification: Hardware in the test environment should mirror the production environment to ensure that testing is reliable. However, this does not relate to the risk of using live data in a test environment and is not the correct answer.
