choices.label: D
choices.text: The test environment may not have adequate access controls implemented to ensure data confidentiality.
choices.justification: In many cases, the test environment is not configured with the same access controls that are enabled in the production environment. For example, programmers may have privileged access to the test environment (for testing purposes), but not to the production environment. If the test environment does not have adequate access control, the production data are subject to risk of unauthorized access and/or data disclosure. This is the most significant risk of the choices listed and is especially important in a health care organization where patient data confidentiality is critical and privacy laws in many countries impose strict penalties on misuse of these data.

domain: Information Systems Acquisition, Development, and Implementation

questions.text: A new application has been purchased from a vendor and is about to be implemented. Which of the following choices is a key consideration when implementing the application?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preventing the compromise of the source code during the implementation process.
choices.justification: Protecting the executable or object code during the implementation process is essential to prevent compromise, rather than solely focusing on the source code.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensuring that vendor default accounts and passwords have been disabled.
choices.justification: Disabling vendor default accounts and passwords is a critical part of implementing a new application.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Removing the old copies of the program from escrow to avoid confusion.
choices.justification: Because this is a new application, there should not be any problem with older versions in escrow, making this consideration less relevant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Verifying that the vendor is meeting support and maintenance agreements.
choices.justification: Although support and maintenance agreements are important, they may not be a key consideration during the initial implementation phase.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes the role of an information systems (IS) auditor in improving quality assurance?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Internal auditor
choices.justification: The internal auditor’s role is to provide independent assurance that an organization’s risk management, governance and internal control processes are operating effectively. This assurance is achieved by performing audits, which are quality control, and not necessarily related to quality assurance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: er
choices.justification: is primarily a quality control activity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Workshop facilitator
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor may conduct workshops as a consultant in improving quality assurance, which is part of the planning activity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Consultant
choices.justification: Audit is primarily a quality control activity; however, the auditor can help an organization in improving quality assurance by guiding it on control effectiveness. This is the role of a consultant.

questions.text: Which of the following carries the LOWEST risk when managing failures while transitioning from legacy applications to new applications?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Phased changeover
choices.justification: Phased changeover involves the changeover from the old system to the new system in a phased manner. Therefore, at no time will the old system and the new system both be fully operational as one integrated system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abrupt changeover
choices.justification: In the abrupt changeover, processing switches from the old system to the new system on a cutoff date and time, and the old system is discontinued after the changeover to the new system takes place. Therefore, the old system is not available as a backup if there are problems when the new system is implemented.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rollback procedure
choices.justification: Rollback procedures involve restoring all systems to their previous working state; however, the parallel changeover is the better strategy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Parallel changeover
choices.justification: Parallel changeover involves running the old system, then running both the old and new systems in parallel, and finally fully changing to the new system after gaining confidence in the new system's functionality.

questions.text: While evaluating the out-of-scope section in a project plan, an information systems (IS) auditor should ascertain whether the section:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: effectively describes unofficial project objectives.
choices.justification: Out-of-scope items are not part of the project. There should be no unofficial project objectives. Reasonable objectives should be considered by the project leadership and either accepted (in scope) or rejected (out of scope).

choices.label: B
choices.text: effectively describes the project’s boundaries.
choices.justification: The purpose of the out-of-scope section is to make clear to readers what items are not considered project objectives so that all project stakeholders understand the project boundaries and what is in scope versus out of scope. This applies to all types of projects, including individual audits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: clearly states the project’s nice-to-have objectives.
choices.justification: Out-of-scope items are not part of the project, but nice-to-have items may be included in the project objectives. However, they may be the last priority on the list of all project objectives.

choices.label: D
choices.text: provides the necessary flexibility to the project team.
choices.justification: Out-of-scope items are not part of the project; the project team’s flexibility regarding project objectives should be managed through a robust change request process. This is particularly important to avoid scope creep.

questions.text: An airline is currently redesigning its reservation system. The configuration team has configured the customer registration process and moved it to the controlled environment. What step did they execute?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Checking in
choices.justification: Checking in is the process of moving an item to the controlled environment. When a change is required (and supported by a change control form), the configuration manager will check out the item.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recording the baseline
choices.justification: Recording the baseline is a benefit that the enterprise achieves by using configuration management tools related to releases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Registering configuration item changes
choices.justification: Registering configuration item changes is a benefit that the enterprise achieves by using configuration management tools related to releases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checking out
choices.justification: The process of checking out is meant to prevent or manage simultaneous code edits, with hardware, network and system architects reviewing and approving the changes or updates to the hardware asset and the inventory tracking systems.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor assesses the project management process for an internal software development project. Concerning the software functionality, the IS auditor should look for sign-off by:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: the project manager.
choices.justification: The project manager provides day-to-day management and leadership of the project and ensures that project activities align with the overall direction. The project manager cannot sign off on project requirements; that would violate separation of duties.

choices.label: B
choices.text: systems development management.
choices.justification: Systems development management provides technical support for hardware and software environments.

choices.label: C
choices.text: business unit management.
choices.justification: Business unit management assumes ownership of the project and the resulting system. It is responsible for acceptance testing and confirming that the required functions are available in the software.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the quality assurance team.
choices.justification: The quality assurance team ensures the quality of the project by measuring adherence to the organization’s system development life cycle. They will conduct testing but not sign off on the project requirements.

questions.text: Which of the following would be of MOST concern for an information systems (IS) auditor reviewing continuous integration and continuous deployment (CICD) implementation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Supporting documentation was not always updated prior to deployment.
choices.justification: Although having all support documentation updated when performing a change is important, this is not the biggest concern for an information systems (IS) auditor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Developers do not have write access to change logs.
choices.justification: Developers should not have access to the change logs that recorded their events and actions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Standard maintenance changes were not documented.
choices.justification: Standard maintenance changes not being documented is the main concern for the IS auditor because documentation of all changes is one of the controls for change management in continuous integration and continuous deployment (CICD). At least, minimum information (e.g., description of the change, success criteria, risk and rollback procedure) should be included for all changes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Retention policies were not defined for change logs.
choices.justification: Retention policies for change logs allow the enterprise to guarantee that logs are kept for the necessary time. Although not having retention policies can be a concern, this is not the main concern of those listed in this question about CICD implementation.

questions.text: What kind of software application testing is considered the final stage of testing and typically includes users outside of the development team?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alpha testing
choices.justification: Alpha testing is the testing stage just before beta testing. Alpha testing is typically performed by programmers and business analysts, instead of users. Alpha testing is used to identify bugs or glitches that can be fixed before beta testing begins with external users.

choices.label: B
choices.text: White box testing
choices.justification: White box testing is performed much earlier in the software development life cycle than alpha or beta testing. White box testing is used to assess the effectiveness of software program logic, where test data are used to determine procedural accuracy of the programs being tested. This testing stage determines if the program operates the way it is supposed to at a functional level. White box testing does not typically involve external users.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Regression testing
choices.justification: Regression testing is the process of re-running a portion of a test scenario to ensure that changes or corrections have not introduced more errors (i.e., the same tests are run after multiple successive program changes to ensure that the fix for one problem did not break another part of the program). Regression testing is not the last stage of testing and does not typically involve external users.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta testing
choices.justification: Beta testing is the final stage of testing and typically includes users outside of the development area. Beta testing is a form of user acceptance testing and generally involves a limited number of users who are external to the development effort.

questions.text: An enterprise is developing a new procurement system that is behind schedule. As a result, the time originally planned for the test phase is proposed to be shortened. The project manager asks the information systems (IS) auditor for recommendations to mitigate the risk associated with reduced testing. Which of the following is a suitable risk mitigation strategy?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Test and release a pilot with reduced functionality.
choices.justification: Testing and releasing a pilot with reduced functionality reduces risk in several ways. Reduced functionality should result in fewer test cases to run, fewer defects to fix and retest and less regression testing. A pilot release that is made available to a select group of users will reduce the risk associated with a full implementation. All of the benefits of releasing the system to the full user population will not be realized, but some benefits should start to be realized. Additionally, some useful comments from real users should be obtained to guide what extra functionality and other improvements need to be included in an entire release.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fix and retest the highest-severity functional defects.
choices.justification: When testing starts, a significant number of defects are likely to exist. Focusing only on the highest-severity functional defects has the risk that other important aspects, such as usability problems and nonfunctional performance and security requirements, will be ignored. The system may go live, but users may struggle to use the system as intended to realize business benefits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eliminate planned testing by the development team and proceed straight to acceptance testing.
choices.justification: Eliminating testing by development is not a good solution. Before system acceptance testing begins, some prior testing should occur to establish that the system is ready to proceed to acceptance evaluation. If prior testing by development does not occur, there is a considerable risk that the software will have a significant number of low-level defects, such as transactions that cause the system to hang and unintelligible error messages. These defects can prove frustrating for users or testers tasked with acceptance testing and, ultimately, can cause the overall test time to increase rather than decrease.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement a test tool to automate defect tracking.
choices.justification: The use of a defect tracking tool can help in improving test efficiency, but it does not address the fundamental risk caused by reducing the testing effort on a system in which quality is uncertain. Given the build problems experienced, there is reason to suspect that quality problems can exist.

questions.text: Which is the MAIN benefit for an enterprise that has implemented continuous integration and continuous deployment (CICD) change management?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensures that all changes are documented
choices.justification: Ensuring that all changes are documented is one key control for CICD change management, but it is not the main benefit for the enterprise.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increases speed with which software is created and delivered
choices.justification: Increasing the speed for the creation and delivery of software is the main goal of implementing continuous integration and continuous deployment (CICD). The other options are pieces required to implement CICD.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Automates testing to detect success or failure of an integration
choices.justification: Testing automation is a core feature of CICD, but it is not the greatest benefit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Logs all actions taken by developers
choices.justification: Logging all actions taken by developers is one key control for CICD change management but it is not the main benefit.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is asked to review implementing a customer relationship management system for a large organization. The IS auditor discovers that the project incurred significant over-budget expenses, and scope creep caused the project to miss key dates. Which of the following should the IS auditor recommend for future projects?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Project management training
choices.justification: Although project management training is a good practice, it does not necessarily prevent scope creep without the use of a software baseline and a robust requirements change process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A software baseline
choices.justification: Using a software baseline provides a cut-off point for the system's design and allows the project to proceed as scheduled without being delayed by scope creep.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A balanced scorecard
choices.justification: A balanced scorecard is a coherent set of performance measures organized into four categories—traditional financial perspective, customer perspective, internal business process perspective, and learning and growth perspective. It does not prevent scope creep.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Automated requirements software
choices.justification: Use of automated requirements software does not decrease the risk of scope creep.

questions.text: While reviewing an ongoing project, the information systems (IS) auditor notes that the development team has spent eight hours of activity on the first day against a budget of 24 hours (over three days). The projected time to complete the remainder of the activity is 20 hours. The IS auditor should report that the project:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: is behind schedule.
choices.justification: earned value analysis (EVA) is based on the premise that if a project task is assigned 24 hours for completion, it can be reasonably completed during that time frame. According to EVA, the project is behind schedule because the value of the eight hours spent on the task should be only four hours, considering that 20 hours of effort remain to be completed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is ahead of schedule.
choices.justification: The project is not ahead of schedule because the work remaining exceeds the time allotted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: is on schedule.
choices.justification: The project is not on schedule because only 16 hours remain to do 20 hours of work.

choices.label: D
choices.text: cannot be evaluated until the activity is completed.
choices.justification: The amount of work left has been evaluated at 20 hours, and the time left on the project is 16 hours, so the auditor can evaluate the current status.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST practice of configuration and release management in software development?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ad hoc software deployments
choices.justification: Ad hoc software deployments make software changes without proper planning or documentation and are not considered good practice in configuration and release management. Ad hoc deployments can lead to inconsistencies, version conflicts and difficulties in managing and tracking changes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Automated deployment pipelines
choices.justification: Automated deployment pipelines are an important aspect of configuration and release management. They involve automating the deployment process, including building, testing and deploying software to different environments. Automated pipelines ensure consistency, reduce manual errors and enable frequent and efficient releases. Automated pipelines also provide visibility into the deployment process and allow for quick rollbacks in case of issues or failures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lack of version control
choices.justification: Lack of version control is a detrimental practice in configuration and release management. Version control systems, such as Git, are crucial for tracking changes, managing different versions of software and enabling collaboration among development teams. Version control systems provide a structured and controlled approach to managing code changes, making it easier to revert to previous versions if needed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Manual tracking of configuration changes
choices.justification: Manual tracking of configuration changes is not an optimal practice in configuration and release management. Manual tracking is prone to human errors, can be time consuming and lacks the efficiency and accuracy offered by automated tools and systems. Configuration management tools, such as Ansible® or Puppet®, automate the tracking of configuration changes and help maintain consistency across different environments.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST relevant to an information systems (IS) auditor evaluating how the project manager has monitored the progress of the project?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Critical path diagrams
choices.justification: Critical path diagrams are used to determine the critical path for the project that represents the shortest possible time required for completing the project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Program evaluation review technique diagrams
choices.justification: Program evaluation review technique diagrams are a critical path method technique in which three estimates (as opposed to one) of timelines required to complete activities are used to determine the critical path.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Function point analysis
choices.justification: Function point analysis is a technique used to determine the size of a development task, based on the number of function points.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gantt charts
choices.justification: Gantt charts help to identify activities that have been completed early or late through comparison to a baseline. Progress of the entire project can be read from the Gantt chart to determine whether the project is behind, ahead of or on schedule.

questions.text: Which of the following helps an information systems (IS) auditor evaluate the quality of new software that is developed and implemented?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reporting of the mean time between failures over time
choices.justification: The mean time between repetitive failures includes the inefficiency in fixing the first reported failures and is a reflection of the performance of the response team or help desk team in fixing the reported issues.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Overall mean time to repair failures
choices.justification: The mean time to repair is a reflection on the performance of the response team or help desk team in addressing reported issues.

choices.label: C
choices.text: First report of the mean time between failures
choices.justification: The mean time between failures that are first reported represents flaws in the software that users in the production environment report. This information helps the information systems (IS) auditor evaluate the quality of the developed and implemented software.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Overall response time to correct failures
choices.justification: The response time reflects the agility of the response or help desk teams in addressing reported issues.

questions.text: Before implementing controls in a newly developed system, management should PRIMARILY ensure that the controls:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: satisfy a requirement in addressing a risk.
choices.justification: The purpose of a control is to mitigate a risk; therefore, the primary consideration when selecting a control is that it effectively mitigates an identified risk. When designing controls, it is necessary to consider all the aspects in the presented options. In an ideal situation, controls that address all these aspects are the best controls. Realistically, it may not be possible to design them all and the cost may be prohibitive; therefore, it is necessary to consider the controls that are related primarily to the treatment of existing risk in the organization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: do not reduce productivity.
choices.justification: Controls often affect productivity and performance; however, this must be balanced against the benefit obtained from the implementation of the control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: are based on a minimized cost analysis.
choices.justification: The most important reason for a control is to mitigate a risk—and the selection of a control is usually based on a cost-benefit analysis, not on selecting just the least expensive control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: are detective or corrective.
choices.justification: A good control environment includes preventive, detective and corrective controls.

questions.text: Assignment of process ownership is essential in system development projects because it:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: enables the tracking of the development completion percentage.
choices.justification: Process ownership assignment does not have a feature to track the completion percentage of deliverables.

choices.label: B
choices.text: optimizes the design cost of user acceptance test cases.
choices.justification: Whether the design cost of test cases will be optimized is not determined from the assignment of process ownership. It may help to some extent; however, there are many other factors involved in the design of test cases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: minimizes the gaps between requirements and functionalities.
choices.justification: For gap minimization, a specific requirements analysis framework should be in place and then applied; however, a gap may be found between the design and the as-built system that can lead to system functionality not meeting requirements. This will be identified during user acceptance testing. Process ownership alone does not have the capability to minimize requirement gaps.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ensures that system design is based on business needs.
choices.justification: The involvement of process owners ensures that the system will be designed according to the needs of the business processes that depend on system functionality. A sign-off on the design by the process owners is crucial before development begins.

questions.text: Code erroneously excluded from a production release was subsequently moved into the production environment, bypassing normal change procedures. Which of the following choices is of MOST concern to the information systems (IS) auditor performing a postimplementation review?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: The code was missed during the initial implementation.
choices.justification: Although missing a component of a release is indicative of a process deficiency, it is of more concern that the missed change was promoted into the production environment without management approval.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The change did not have change management approval.
choices.justification: Change management approval of changes mitigates the risk of unauthorized changes being introduced to the production environment. Unauthorized changes might result in disruption of systems or fraud. It is, therefore, imperative to ensure that each change has appropriate change management approval.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The error was discovered during the postimplementation review.
choices.justification: Most release/change control errors are discovered during postimplementation review. It is of greater concern that the change was promoted without management approval after it was discovered.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The release team used the same change order number.
choices.justification: Using the same change order number is not a relevant concern.

domain: Information Systems Acquisition, Development, and Implementation
category: System Migration, Infrastructure Deployment, and Data Conversion

questions.text: An organization undertakes a business process reengineering project in support of a new and direct marketing approach to its customers. Which of the following is an information systems (IS) auditor’s main concern about the new process?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether key controls are in place to protect assets and information resources
choices.justification: The audit team must advocate the inclusion of the key controls and verify that the controls are in place before implementing the new process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether the system addresses corporate customer requirements
choices.justification: The system must meet the requirements of all customers, not just corporate customers. This is not the information systems (IS) auditor’s main concern.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the system can meet the performance goals
choices.justification: The system must meet performance requirements, but this is of secondary concern to the need to ensure that key controls are in place.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whether the system supports segregation/separation of duties (SoD)
choices.justification: Separation of duties (SoD) is a key control—but only one of the controls that should be in place to protect the assets of the organization.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST critical for commercial enterprises that are exchanging data through system interfaces?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Data integrity
choices.justification: Data integrity is the overall accuracy, completeness and consistency of data, which is part of data authentication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Data confidentiality
choices.justification: Data confidentiality is the protection of data from unauthorized access but does not have the highest priority. The goal of integrity is the most important in information security, especially for commercial enterprises and their financial transactions, which is part of data authentication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Data authentication
choices.justification: Data authentication is the process of confirming the origin and integrity of data. It is critical that enterprises can rely on the origin and integrity of data exchanged through system interfaces.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Data availability
choices.justification: Data availability is a measure of how often enterprise data are available to be used by authorized parties and does not have the highest priority.

questions.text: After an organization finishes the business process reengineering (BPR) of all its critical operations, an information systems (IS) auditor is MOST likely to focus on a review of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: pre-business process reengineering (BPR) process flowcharts.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor must review the process as it is today, not as it was in the past.

choices.label: B
choices.text: post-BPR process flowcharts.
choices.justification: An IS auditor’s task is to identify and ensure that key controls have been incorporated into the reengineered process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: BPR project plans.
choices.justification: Business process reengineering (BPR) project plans are a step within a BPR project.

choices.label: D
choices.text: continuous improvement and monitoring plans.
choices.justification: Continuous improvement and monitoring plans are steps within a BPR project.

questions.text: Which of the following should be developed during the requirements definition phase of a software development project to address aspects of software testing?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Test data covering critical applications
choices.justification: Test data is usually created during the system testing phase.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Detailed test plans
choices.justification: Detailed test plans are created during system testing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Quality assurance (QA) test specifications
choices.justification: Quality assurance (QA) test specifications are set out later in the development process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: User acceptance test (UAT) specifications
choices.justification: A key objective in any software development project is to ensure that the developed software will meet the business objectives and the requirements of the user. The users should be involved in the requirements definition phase of a development project and user acceptance testing (UAT) specification should be developed during this phase.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST helps an information systems (IS) auditor evaluate the quality of programming activities related to future maintenance capabilities?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Programming language
choices.justification: The programming language may be a concern if it is not a commonly used language; however, program coding standards are more important.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Development environment
choices.justification: The development environment may be relevant to evaluate the efficiency of the program development process but not future maintenance of the program.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Version control system
choices.justification: A version control system helps manage software code revisions but does not ensure that coding standards are consistently applied.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Program coding standards
choices.justification: Program coding standards are required for efficient program maintenance and modifications. To enhance the quality of programming activities and future maintenance capabilities, program coding standards should be applied. Program coding standards are essential to writing, reading and understanding code simply and clearly, without referring to design specifications.

questions.text: Due to a reorganization, a business application system will be extended to other departments. Which of the following should be of the GREATEST concern for an information systems (IS) auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Process owners have not been identified.
choices.justification: When one application is expanded to multiple departments, it is important to ensure the mapping between the process owner and system functions. The absence of a defined process owner may cause issues with monitoring or authorization controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The billing cost allocation method has not been determined.
choices.justification: Cost allocation is the method business owners use to calculate profitability for the purpose of financial reporting. The billing cost allocation method of application usage is of less importance than identifying process owners.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Multiple application owners exist.
choices.justification: The fact that multiple application owners exist is not a concern for an information systems (IS) auditor if process owners have been identified, because the process owners are responsible for the specific process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A training program does not exist.
choices.justification: The lack of a training program is only a minor concern for the IS auditor.

questions.text: An organization has contracted with an external consulting firm to implement a commercial financial system to replace its existing system that was developed in-house. In reviewing the proposed development approach, which of the following is of GREATEST concern?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acceptance testing is to be managed by users.
choices.justification: Acceptance is normally managed by the user area because users must be satisfied that the new system will meet their requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A quality plan is not part of the contracted deliverables.
choices.justification: A quality plan is an essential element of all projects. It is critical that the contracted supplier is required to produce such a plan. The quality plan for the proposed development contract should be comprehensive and encompass all phases of the development and include which business functions will be included and when.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Not all business functions will be available on initial implementation.
choices.justification: If the system is large, a phased-in approach to implementing the application is a reasonable approach.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prototyping is being used to confirm that the system meets business requirements.
choices.justification: Prototyping is a valid method of ensuring that the system will meet business requirements.

questions.text: Information for detecting unauthorized input from a user workstation is BEST provided by the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: console log printout.
choices.justification: A console log printout is not the best source because it does not record activity from a specific terminal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: transaction journal.
choices.justification: The transaction journal records all transaction activity, which then can be compared to the authorized source documents to identify any unauthorized input.

choices.label: C
choices.text: automated suspense file listing.
choices.justification: An automated suspense file listing lists only transaction activity where an edit error occurred.

choices.label: D
choices.text: user error report.
choices.justification: The user error report lists only input that resulted in an edit error and does not record improper user input.

questions.text: An enterprise development team does not follow generally accepted system development life cycle practices. Which of the following is MOST likely to cause problems for software development projects?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Functional verification of the prototypes is assigned to end users.
choices.justification: Prototypes are verified by users.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The project is implemented while minor issues from user acceptance testing are open.
choices.justification: User acceptance testing is seldom completely successful. If errors are not critical, they may be corrected after implementation without seriously affecting usage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Project responsibilities are not formally defined at the beginning of a project.
choices.justification: Errors or lack of attention in the initial phases of a project may cause costly errors and inefficiencies in later phases. Proper planning is required at the beginning of a project.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Program documentation is inadequate.
choices.justification: Although a concern, lack of adequate program documentation is not as big a risk as the lack of assigned responsibilities during the initial stages of the project.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST approach to ensure that sufficient test coverage will be achieved for a project with a strict end date and a fixed time to perform testing?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Requirements should be tested in terms of importance and frequency of use.
choices.justification: This approach maximizes the usefulness of testing by concentrating on the most important aspects of the system and on the areas where defects represent the greatest risk to user acceptance. A further extension of this approach is to also consider the technical complexity of requirements because complexity tends to increase the likelihood of defects.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Test coverage should be restricted to functional requirements.
choices.justification: The problem with testing only functional requirements is that nonfunctional requirement areas, such as usability and security, which are important to the overall quality of the system, are ignored.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Automated tests should be performed using scripting.
choices.justification: Increasing the efficiency of testing by automating test execution is a good idea. However, by itself, this approach does not ensure the appropriate targeting of test coverage and is not an effective alternative.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of required test runs should be reduced by retesting only defect fixes.
choices.justification: Retesting only defect fixes has a considerable risk that it will not detect instances in which defect fixes may have caused the system to regress (i.e., introduced errors in parts of the system that were previously working correctly). For this reason, it is a good practice to undertake formal regression testing after defect fixes have been implemented.

questions.text: An organization is replacing a payroll program that it developed in-house, with the relevant subsystem of a commercial enterprise resource planning (ERP) system. Which of the following represents the HIGHEST potential risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Undocumented approval of some project changes
choices.justification: Undocumented changes (leading to scope creep) are a risk, but the greatest risk is the loss of data integrity when migrating data from the old system to the new system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Faulty migration of historical data from the old system to the new system
choices.justification: The most significant risk after a payroll system conversion is loss of data integrity resulting in the organization being unable to pay employees in a timely and accurate manner. Loss of data integrity can also result in incorrect records of past payments. As a result, maintaining data integrity and accuracy during migration is paramount.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Incomplete testing of the standard functionality of the ERP subsystem
choices.justification: A lack of testing is always a risk; however, in this case, the new payroll system is a subsystem of an existing commercially available (and therefore probably well-tested) system.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Duplication of existing payroll permissions on the new ERP subsystem
choices.justification: Setting up the new system, including access permissions and payroll data, always presents some level of risk; however, the greatest risk is related to the migration of data from the old system to the new system.

questions.text: The waterfall life cycle model of software development is MOST appropriately used when:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: requirements are well understood and are expected to remain stable, like the business environment in which the system will operate.
choices.justification: Historically, the waterfall model has been best suited to stable conditions and well-defined requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: requirements are well understood and the project is subject to time pressures.
choices.justification: When the degree of uncertainty of the system to be delivered and the conditions in which it will be used rises, the waterfall model has not been successful. In these circumstances, the various forms of iterative development life cycle give the advantage of breaking down the scope of the overall system to be delivered, making the requirements gathering and design activities more manageable. The ability of an iterative development life cycle to deliver working software earlier also acts to alleviate uncertainty and may allow an earlier realization of benefits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the project intends to apply an object-oriented design and programming approach.
choices.justification: The choice of a design and programming approach is not, itself, a determining factor of the type of software development life cycle that is appropriate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the project will involve the use of new technology.
choices.justification: The use of new technology in a project introduces a significant element of risk. An iterative form of development, particularly one of the agile or exploratory methods that focuses on early development of actual working software, is likely to be the better option to manage this uncertainty.

domain: Information Systems Acquisition, Development, and Implementation

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST cost-effective recommendation for reducing the number of defects encountered during software development projects?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase the time allocated for system testing.
choices.justification: Allowing more time for testing may discover more defects; however, little is revealed about why the quality problems are occurring, and the costs of the extra testing and rectifying the defects found will be greater than if they had been discovered earlier in the development process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement formal software inspections.
choices.justification: Inspections of code and design are a proven software quality technique. An advantage of this approach is that defects are identified before they propagate through the development life cycle. This reduces the cost of correction because less rework is involved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increase the development staff.
choices.justification: The ability of the development staff can have a bearing on the quality of what is produced; however, replacing staff can be expensive and disruptive, and the presence of a competent staff cannot guarantee quality in the absence of effective quality management processes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Require the sign-off of all project deliverables.
choices.justification: Sign-off of deliverables may help detect defects if signatories are diligent about reviewing deliverable content; however, this is difficult to enforce and may occur too late in the process to be cost-effective. Deliverable reviews normally do not go down to the same level of detail as software inspections.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is conducting a postimplementation review of an enterprise’s network. Which of the following findings would be of MOST concern?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wireless mobile devices are not password-protected.
choices.justification: Although mobile devices that are not password-protected are a risk, this is not as significant as unsecured network devices.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Default passwords are not changed when installing network devices.
choices.justification: The most significant risk in this case is that the factory default passwords are not changed on critical network equipment. This can allow anyone to change the configurations of network equipment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An outbound web proxy does not exist.
choices.justification: The use of a web proxy is a good practice but may not be required depending on the enterprise.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All communication links do not use encryption.
choices.justification: Encryption is a good control for data security but is not appropriate to use for all communication links due to cost and complexity.

questions.text: Functionality is a characteristic associated with evaluating the quality of software products throughout their life cycle and is BEST described as the set of attributes that apply to the:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: existence of a set of functions and their specified properties.
choices.justification: Functionality is the set of attributes that apply to the existence of a set of functions and their specified properties. The functionality of a system represents the tasks, operations and purpose of the system in achieving its objective (i.e., supporting a business requirement).

choices.label: B
choices.text: ability of the software to be transferred from one environment to another.
choices.justification: The ability of the software to be transferred from one environment to another refers to portability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: capability of software to maintain its level of performance under stated conditions.
choices.justification: The capability of software to maintain its level of performance under stated conditions refers to reliability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: relationship between the performance of the software and the amount of resources used.
choices.justification: The relationship between the performance of the software and the amount of resources used refers to efficiency.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the software development capabilities of an organization that has adopted the agile methodology. The IS auditor would be the MOST concerned if:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: certain project iterations produce proof-of-concept deliverables and unfinished code.
choices.justification: The agile software development methodology is an iterative process where each iteration (sprint) produces functional code. If a development team is producing code for demonstration purposes, this is an issue because the following iterations of the project build on the code developed in the prior sprint.

choices.label: B
choices.text: application features and development processes are not extensively documented.
choices.justification: One focus of agile methodology is to rely more on team knowledge and produce functional code quickly. These characteristics result in less extensive documentation or documentation embedded in the code itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: software development teams continually re-plan each step of their major projects.
choices.justification: After each sprint, agile development teams re-plan the project so that unfinished tasks are performed and resources can be reallocated as needed. The continual re-planning is a key component of agile development methodology.

choices.label: D
choices.text: project managers do not manage project resources, leaving that to project team members.
choices.justification: The management of agile software development is different from conventional development approaches in that leaders act as facilitators and allow team members to determine how to manage their own resources to get each sprint completed. Because the team members are performing the work, they are in a good position to understand how much time/effort is required to complete a sprint.

questions.text: By evaluating application development projects against the capability maturity model, an information systems (IS) auditor should be able to verify that:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: reliable products are guaranteed.
choices.justification: Although the likelihood of success should increase as the software processes mature toward the optimizing level, mature processes do not guarantee a reliable product.

choices.label: B
choices.text: programmers’ efficiency is improved.
choices.justification: The capability maturity model (CMM) does not evaluate technical processes such as programming efficiency.

choices.label: C
choices.text: security requirements are designed.
choices.justification: The CMM does not evaluate security requirements or other application controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: predictable software processes are followed.
choices.justification: By evaluating the organization’s development projects against the CMM, an information systems (IS) auditor determines whether the development organization follows a stable, predictable software development process.

questions.text: What is the BEST method to facilitate successful user testing and acceptance of a new enterprise resource planning payroll system that replaces an existing legacy system?
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: Multiple testing
choices.justification: Multiple testing will not compare results from the old and new systems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Parallel testing
choices.justification: Parallel testing is the best method for testing data results and system behavior because it allows the users to compare results from both systems before decommissioning the legacy system. Parallel testing also results in better user adoption of the new system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Integration testing
choices.justification: Integration testing assesses how the system interacts with other systems, and end users do not perform this type of testing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prototype testing
choices.justification: Prototype testing is used during design and development to ensure that user input is received; however, this method is not used for acquired systems or during user acceptance testing.

questions.text: During the development of an application, quality assurance (QA) testing and user acceptance testing (UAT) were combined. The MAJOR concern for an information systems (IS) auditor reviewing the project is:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: increased maintenance.
choices.justification: The method of testing used does not affect the maintenance of the system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: improper documentation of testing.
choices.justification: Quality assurance (QA)and user acceptance testing (UAT) are often led by business representatives according to a defined test plan. The combination of these two tests does not affect documentation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: improper acceptance of a program.
choices.justification: The major risk of combining QA testing and UAT is that the users may apply pressure to accept a program that meets their needs even though it does not meet QA standards.

choices.label: D
choices.text: delays in problem resolution.
choices.justification: The method of testing should not affect the timelines for problem resolution.

questions.text: Which of the following software testing methods provides the BEST feedback on how the software will perform in the live environment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alpha testing
choices.justification: Alpha testing is often performed only by users within the organization who are developing the software. Alpha testing generally involves a software version that does not contain all the features of the final product and may be a simulated test.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Regression testing
choices.justification: Regression testing is used to determine whether system changes have introduced new errors to existing functionality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta testing
choices.justification: Beta testing follows alpha testing and involves real-world exposure with external user involvement. Beta testing is the last stage of testing and involves sending the product's beta version to independent beta test sites or offering it free to interested users.

choices.label: D
choices.text: White box testing
choices.justification: White box testing is used to assess the effectiveness of program logic.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is performing a postimplementation review of an organization’s system and identifies output errors within an accounting application. The IS auditor determines that this was caused by input errors. Which of the following controls should the IS auditor recommend to management?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recalculations
choices.justification: A sample of transactions may be recalculated manually to ensure that processing is accomplishing the anticipated task. Recalculations are performed after the output phase.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Limit checks
choices.justification: Processing controls should be implemented as close as possible to the point of data entry. Limit checks are one type of input validation check that provides a preventive control to ensure that invalid data cannot be entered, because values must fall within a predetermined limit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Run-to-run totals
choices.justification: Run-to-run totals provide the ability to verify data values through the stages of application processing. Run-to-run total verification ensures that data read into the computer are accepted and then applied to the updating process. Run-to-run totals are performed after the output phase.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reconciliations
choices.justification: Reconciliation of file totals should be performed on a routine basis. Reconciliations may be performed through the use of a manually maintained account, a file control record or an independent control file. Reconciliations are performed after the output phase.

questions.text: From a risk management point of view, the BEST approach when implementing a large and complex IT infrastructure is:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: a major deployment after proof of concept.
choices.justification: A major deployment poses a higher risk of implementation failure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prototyping and a one-phase deployment.
choices.justification: Prototyping may reduce development failure, but a large environment usually requires a phased approach.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a deployment plan based on sequenced phases.
choices.justification: When developing a large and complex IT infrastructure, a good practice is to use a phased approach to fit the entire system together. This provides greater assurance of quality results.

choices.label: D
choices.text: to simulate the new infrastructure before deployment.
choices.justification: It is not usually feasible to simulate a large and complex IT infrastructure prior to deployment.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is asked to participate in project initiation meetings for a critical project. The IS auditor’s MAIN concern should be that the:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: complexity and risk associated with the project have been analyzed.
choices.justification: Understanding complexity and risk, and actively managing these throughout a project are critical to a successful outcome.

choices.label: B
choices.text: resources needed throughout the project have been determined.
choices.justification: The resources needed will be dependent on the complexity of the project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: technical deliverables have been identified.
choices.justification: It is too early in the project to identify the technical deliverables.

choices.label: D
choices.text: contract for external parties involved in the project has been completed.
choices.justification: Not all projects require contracts with external parties.

questions.text: Two months after a major application implementation, management, which assumes that the project went well, requests that an information systems (IS) auditor perform a review of the completed project. The IS auditor’s PRIMARY focus should be to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: determine whether user feedback on the system has been documented.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor should check whether user feedback has been provided, but this is not the most important area for audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: assess whether the planned cost benefits are being measured, analyzed and reported.
choices.justification: It is important to assess the effectiveness of the project; however, assuring that the production environment is adequately controlled after the implementation is of primary concern.

choices.label: C
choices.text: review controls built into the system to assure that they are operating as designed.
choices.justification: Because management is assuming that the implementation went well, the primary focus of the IS auditor is to test the controls built into the application to assure that they are functioning as designed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: review subsequent program change requests.
choices.justification: change requests may be a good idea, but this is more important if the application is perceived to have a problem.

questions.text: Which of the following techniques BEST helps an information systems (IS) auditor gain reasonable assurance that a project can meet its target date?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Estimation of the actual end date based on the completion percentages and estimated time to complete, taken from status reports
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor cannot count on the accuracy of data in status reports for reasonable assurance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Confirmation of the target date based on interviews with experienced managers and staff involved in the completion of the project deliverables
choices.justification: Interviews are a valuable source of information but do not necessarily identify any project challenges, because the people being interviewed are involved in the project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Extrapolation of the overall end date based on completed work packages and current resources
choices.justification: Direct observation of results is better than estimations and qualitative information gained from interviews or status reports. Project managers and involved staff tend to underestimate the time needed for completion and the necessary time buffers for dependencies between tasks, while overestimating the completion percentage for tasks underway (i.e., 80:20 rule).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Calculation of the expected end date based on current resources and remaining available project budget
choices.justification: The calculation based on remaining budget does not consider the speed at which the project has been progressing.

questions.text: When two or more systems are integrated, the information systems (IS) auditor must review input/output controls in the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: systems receiving the output of other systems.
choices.justification: A responsible control is to protect downstream systems from contamination from an upstream system. This requires a system that sends data to review its output and the receiving system to review its input.

choices.label: B
choices.text: systems sending output to other systems.
choices.justification: Systems sending data to other systems should ensure that the data they send are correct, but that verification does not protect the receiving system from transmission errors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: systems sending and receiving data.
choices.justification: Both systems must be reviewed for input/output controls because the output for one system is the input for the other.

choices.label: D
choices.text: interfaces between the two systems.
choices.justification: The interfaces must be set up correctly and provide error controls, but a good practice is to review the data before sending and after receipt.

questions.text: Which of the following should be included in a feasibility study for a project to implement an electronic data interchange (EDI) process?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encryption algorithm format
choices.justification: Encryption algorithms are too detailed for this phase. They would only be outlined, and any cost or performance implications would be shown.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Detailed internal control procedures
choices.justification: Internal control procedures are too detailed for this phase. They would only be outlined, and any cost or performance implications would be shown.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Necessary communication protocols
choices.justification: The communications protocols must be included because there may be significant cost implications if new hardware and software are involved, and risk implications if the technology is new to the organization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proposed trusted third-party agreement
choices.justification: Third-party agreements are too detailed for this phase. They would only be outlined, and any cost or performance implications would be shown.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is assigned to audit a software development project that is more than 80 percent complete but has already overrun time by 10 percent and costs by 25 percent. Which of the following actions should the IS auditor take?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: Report that the organization does not have effective project management.
choices.justification: The organization may have effective project management practices and still be behind schedule or over budget.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recommend that the project manager be changed.
choices.justification: There is no indication that the project manager should be changed without looking into the reasons for the overrun.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the IT governance structure.
choices.justification: The organization may have sound IT governance and still be behind schedule or over budget.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the business case and project management.
choices.justification: Before making any recommendations, an information systems (IS) auditor needs to understand the project and the factors that have contributed to bringing the project over budget and over schedule.

questions.text: During a postimplementation review, which of the following activities should be performed?
questions.taskStatement: Conduct post-implementation reviews of systems to determine whether project deliverables, controls, and requirements are met

choices.label: A
choices.text: User acceptance testing (UAT)
choices.justification: User acceptance testing (UAT) supports the process of ensuring that the system is production ready and satisfies all documented requirements. User acceptance testing should be performed prior to the implementation (perhaps during the development phase), not after the implementation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Return on investment (ROI) analysis
choices.justification: Following implementation, a cost-benefit analysis or return on investment (ROI) should be reperformed to verify that the original business case benefits are delivered and business value is created.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activation of audit trails
choices.justification: An audit trail is a detailed record of the activities on a database or system. The audit trail should be activated during the implementation of the application, not during the postimplementation review.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Updates of the state of enterprise architecture (EA) diagrams
choices.justification: Although updating the enterprise architecture (EA) diagrams is a good practice, it is not normally part of a postimplementation review.

domain: Information Systems Acquisition, Development, and Implementation

questions.text: During the requirements definition stage of a proposed enterprise resource planning system, the project sponsor requests that the procurement and accounts payable modules be linked. Which of the following test methods is the BEST to perform?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Unit testing
choices.justification: Unit testing is a technique that is used to test program logic within a particular program or module and does not specifically address the linkage between software modules. Integration testing is the best answer.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Integration testing
choices.justification: Integration testing is a hardware or software test that evaluates the connection of two or more components that pass information from one area to another. The objective is to take unit-tested modules and build an integrated structure dictated by design.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sociability testing
choices.justification: Sociability testing confirms that the new or modified system can operate in its target environment without adversely impacting existing systems and does not specifically address the linkage between software modules. Integration testing is the best answer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Quality assurance (QA) testing
choices.justification: Quality assurance (QA) testing is primarily used to ensure that the logic of the application is correct and does not specifically address the linkage between software modules. Integration testing is the best answer.

questions.text: During the audit of an acquired software package, an information systems (IS) auditor finds that the software purchase was based on information obtained through the Internet, rather than from responses to a request for proposal. The IS auditor should FIRST:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: test the software for compatibility with existing hardware.
choices.justification: Because the software package has already been acquired, it is most likely that it is in use and, therefore, compatible with existing hardware. Further, the first responsibility of the information systems (IS) auditor is to ensure that the purchasing procedures have been approved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: perform a gap analysis.
choices.justification: Because there was no request for proposal, there may be no documentation of the expectations of the product and nothing against which to measure a gap. The first task for the IS auditor is to ensure that the purchasing procedures were approved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: review the licensing policy.
choices.justification: The licensing policy should be reviewed to ensure proper licensing but only after the purchasing procedures are checked.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ensure that the procedure had been approved.
choices.justification: In the case of a deviation from the predefined procedures, an IS auditor should first ensure that the procedure followed for acquiring the software is consistent with the business objectives and has been approved by the appropriate authorities.

questions.text: A small company cannot segregate duties between its development processes and its change control function. What is the BEST way to ensure that the tested code is the code that is moved into production?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Release management software
choices.justification: Automated release management software can prevent unauthorized changes by moving code into production without any manual intervention.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Manual code comparison
choices.justification: Manual code comparison can detect whether the wrong code has been moved into production; however, code comparison does not prevent the code from being migrated and is not as good a control as using release management software. In addition, manual code comparison is not always efficient and requires highly skilled personnel.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Regression testing in preproduction
choices.justification: Regression testing ensures that changes do not break the current system functionality or unwittingly overwrite previous changes. Regression testing does not prevent untested code from moving into production.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Management approval of changes
choices.justification: Although management should approve every change to production, approvals do not prevent untested code from being migrated into the production environment.

questions.text: During which of the following phases in system development are user acceptance testing (UAT) plans normally prepared?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Feasibility study
choices.justification: The feasibility study is too early for such detailed user involvement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Requirements definition
choices.justification: During requirements definition, the project team works with the users to define their precise objectives and functional needs. The users should be working with the team to consider and document how the system functionality can be tested to ensure that it meets their stated needs. An information systems (IS) auditor should know at what point user testing should be planned to ensure that it is most effective and efficient.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implementation planning
choices.justification: The implementation planning phase is when the tests are conducted. It is too late in the process to develop the test plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Postimplementation review
choices.justification: User acceptance testing (UAT) should be completed prior to implementation.

questions.text: Which of the following controls helps prevent duplication of vouchers during data entry?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Range check
choices.justification: A range check works over a range of numbers. Even if the same voucher number reappears, it will satisfy the range and, therefore, not be useful.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transposition and substitution
choices.justification: Transposition and substitution are used in encoding but will not help in establishing unique voucher numbers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sequence check
choices.justification: A sequence check involves increasing the order of numbering and validates whether the vouchers are in sequence, and, thus, prevents duplicate vouchers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cyclic redundancy check
choices.justification: A cyclic redundancy check is used for completeness of data received over the network but is not useful in application-code-level validations.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is involved in the reengineering process that aims to optimize IT infrastructure. Which of the following will BEST identify the issues to be resolved?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Self-assessment
choices.justification: Self-assessment may be one of the viable options with which to start; however, the results only indicate current conditions, not desired state, and tend to become subjective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reverse engineering
choices.justification: Reverse engineering is a technique applied to analyze how a device or program works and is not appropriate here.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prototyping
choices.justification: Prototyping is applied to ensure that user requirements are met prior to engaging in the development process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gap analysis
choices.justification: Gap analysis is the best method to identify issues that need to be addressed in the reengineering process. Gap analysis indicates which parts of current processes conform to good practices (desired state) and which parts do not.

questions.text: Regression testing is undertaken PRIMARILY to ensure that:
