questions.taskStatement: Evaluate IT resource and project management for alignment with the organization's strategies and objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: system functionality meets customer requirements.
choices.justification: Validation testing is used to test the functionality of the system against detailed requirements to ensure that software construction is traceable to customer requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a new system can operate in the target environment.
choices.justification: Sociability testing is used to see whether the system can operate in the target environment without adverse impacts on the existing systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: applicable development standards have been maintained.
choices.justification: Software quality assurance and code reviews are used to determine whether development standards are maintained.

choices.label: D
choices.text: applied changes have not introduced new errors.
choices.justification: Regression testing is used to test for the introduction of new errors in the system after changes have been applied.

questions.text: When auditing the proposed acquisition of a new computer system, an information systems (IS) auditor should FIRST ensure that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: a clear business case has been approved by management.
choices.justification: The first concern of an information systems (IS) auditor is to ensure that the proposal meets the needs of the business. This should be established by a clear business case.

choices.label: B
choices.text: corporate security standards will be met.
choices.justification: Compliance with security standards is essential, but it is too early in the procurement process for this to be an IS auditor’s first concern.

choices.label: C
choices.text: users will be involved in the implementation plan.
choices.justification: Having users involved in the implementation process is essential, but it is too early in the procurement process for this to be an IS auditor’s first concern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the new system will meet all required user functionality.
choices.justification: Meeting the needs of the users is essential, and this should be included in the business case presented to management for approval.

questions.text: Which of the following has the MOST significant impact on the success of an application system implementation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prototyping application development methodology
choices.justification: The prototyping application development technique reduces the time to deploy systems primarily by using faster development tools that allow a user to see a high-level view of the workings of the proposed system within a short period of time. The use of any one development methodology has a limited impact on the success of the project.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compliance with applicable external requirements
choices.justification: Compliance with applicable external requirements has an impact on the implementation success, but the impact is not as significant as the impact of the overall organizational environment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Overall organizational environment
choices.justification: The overall organizational environment has the most significant impact on the success of application systems implemented. This includes the alignment between IT and the business, the maturity of the development processes and the use of change control and other project management tools.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Software reengineering technique
choices.justification: The software reengineering technique is a process of updating an existing system by extracting and reusing design and program components. This is used to support major changes in the way an organization operates. Its impact on the success of the application systems that are implemented is small compared with the impact of the overall organizational environment.

questions.text: A project development team is considering using production data for its test deck. The team removed sensitive data elements before loading it into the test environment. Which of the following additional concerns should an information systems (IS) auditor have with this practice?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Not all functionality will be tested.
choices.justification: A primary risk of using production data in a test deck is that not all transactions or functionality may be tested if there are no data that meet the requirement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Production data are introduced into the test environment.
choices.justification: The presence of production data in a test environment is not a concern if the sensitive elements have been scrubbed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Specialized training is required.
choices.justification: Creation of a test deck from production data does not require specialized knowledge, so this is not a concern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The project may run over budget.
choices.justification: The risk of a project running over budget is always a concern, but it is not related to the practice of using production data in a test environment.

questions.text: An advantage of using sanitized live transactions for test data is that:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct periodic review of information systems and enterprise architecture (EA) to determine alignment with organizational objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: all transaction types are included.
choices.justification: Sanitized production data may not contain all transaction types. The test data may need to be modified to ensure that all data types are represented.

choices.label: B
choices.text: every error condition is likely to be tested.
choices.justification: Not all error types are sure to be tested because most production data only contains certain types of errors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: no special routines are required to assess the results.
choices.justification: The results can be tested using normal routines, but that is not a significant advantage of using sanitized live data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: test transactions are representative of live processing.
choices.justification: Test data is representative of live processing; however, it is important that all sensitive information in the live transaction file is sanitized to prevent improper data disclosure.

questions.text: Which of the following considerations is the MOST important while evaluating a business case for the acquisition of a new accounting application?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether the business cases related to information systems meet business objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Total cost of ownership of the application
choices.justification: Total cost of ownership of the application is important to understand the resource and budget requirements in the short and long term; however, decisions should be based on benefits realization from this investment. Therefore, return on investment (ROI) is the most important consideration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The resources required for implementation
choices.justification: The resources required for implementation of the application are an important consideration; however, decisions should be based on benefits realization from this investment. Therefore, ROI should be carefully considered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Return on investment (ROI) for the enterprise
choices.justification: The proposed ROI benefits, and targets or metrics that can be measured, are the most important aspects of a business case. While reviewing the business case, it should be verified that the proposed ROI is achievable, does not make unreasonable assumptions and can be measured for success. (Benefits realization should look beyond project cycles to longer-term cycles that consider the total benefits and total costs throughout the life of the new system.)

choices.label: D
choices.text: The cost and complexity of security requirements
choices.justification: The cost and complexity of security requirements are important considerations, but they need to be weighed against the proposed benefits of the application. Therefore, ROI is more important.

domain: Information Systems Acquisition, Development, and Implementation

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST indicator that a newly developed system will be used after it is in production?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Regression testing
choices.justification: Regression test results do not assist with the user experience and are primarily concerned with new functionality or processes and whether those changes altered or broke previous functionality.

choices.label: B
choices.text: User acceptance testing
choices.justification: User acceptance testing is undertaken to provide confidence that a system or system component operates as intended, to provide a basis for evaluating the implementation of the requirements, or to demonstrate the effectiveness or efficiency of the system or component. If the testing results are poor, then the system is unlikely to be adopted by the users.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sociability testing
choices.justification: Sociability test results indicate how the application works with other components within the environment and is not indicative of the user experience.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Parallel testing
choices.justification: Parallel testing is performed when the comparison of two applications is needed but does not provide feedback on user satisfaction.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST helps identify deviations from the project plan?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Project management framework
choices.justification: Establishment of a project management framework identifies the scope and boundaries of managing projects and the consistent method to be applied when initiating a project but does not define the criteria used to measure project success.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Project management approach
choices.justification: A project management approach defines guidelines for project management processes and deliverables but does not define the criteria used to measure project success.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Project resource plan
choices.justification: A project resource plan defines project team members' responsibilities, relationships, authorities and performance criteria but does not wholly define the criteria used to measure project success.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Project performance criteria
choices.justification: Project performance criteria must be established as a baseline to identify deviations from the project plan. Successful completion of the project plan is indicative of project success.

questions.text: The project steering committee is ultimately responsible for the following:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Day-to-day management and leadership of the project
choices.justification: Day-to-day management and leadership of the project is the function of the project manager.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Allocating the funding for the project
choices.justification: Providing the funding for the project is the function of the project sponsor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Project deliverables, costs and timetables
choices.justification: The project steering committee provides overall direction; ensures appropriate representation of the major stakeholders in the project outcome; and takes ultimate responsibility for the deliverables, costs and timetables.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensuring that system controls are in place
choices.justification: Ensuring that system controls are in place is the function of the project security officer.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST method of controlling scope creep in a system development project?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Defining penalties for changes in requirements
choices.justification: Although defining penalties for changes in requirements may help to prevent scope creep, software baselining is a better way to accomplish this goal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Establishing a software baseline
choices.justification: Software baselining, the cutoff point in the design phase, occurs after a rigorous review of user requirements. Any changes thereafter will undergo strict formal change control and approval procedures. Scope creep refers to uncontrolled change within a project resulting from improperly managed requirements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Adopting a matrix project management structure
choices.justification: In a matrix project organization, management authority is shared between the project manager and the department heads. Adopting a matrix project management structure will not address the problem of scope creep.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identifying the critical path of the project
choices.justification: Although the critical path is important, it will change over time and will not control scope creep.

questions.text: To minimize the cost of a software project, quality management techniques should be applied:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: as close to their writing (i.e., point of origination) as possible.
choices.justification: Quality assurance (QA) should start as early as possible but continue through the entire development process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: primarily at project start to ensure that the project is established in accordance with organizational governance standards.
choices.justification: Only performing QA during the start of the project will not detect problems that appear later in the development cycle.

choices.label: C
choices.text: continuously throughout the project with an emphasis on finding and fixing defects primarily through testing to maximize the defect detection rate.
choices.justification: Although quality management is important to properly establish a software development project, quality management should be effectively practiced throughout the project. The major source of unexpected costs on most software projects is rework. The general rule is that the earlier in the development life cycle that a defect occurs, and the longer it takes to find and fix that defect, the more effort will be needed to correct it. A well-written quality management plan is a good start, but it must also be actively applied. Simply relying on testing to identify defects is a relatively costly and less effective way of achieving software quality. For example, an error in requirements discovered in the testing phase can result in scrapping significant amounts of work.

choices.label: D
choices.text: mainly at project close-down to capture lessons learned that can be applied to future projects.
choices.justification: Capturing lessons learned will be too late for the current project. Additionally, applying quality management techniques throughout a project is likely to yield its own insights into the causes of quality problems and assist in staff development.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing an enterprise’s system-development testing policy. Which of the following statements concerning use of production data for testing would the IS auditor consider to be MOST appropriate?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Senior information systems (IS) and business management must approve use before production data can be used for testing.
choices.justification: There is risk associated with the use of production data for testing. These include compromising customer or employee confidentiality (which may also involve breaching legislation) and corrupting production of the data. Additionally, there are certain cases in which effective testing requires specifically designed data. There are other cases in which using production data provides insights that are difficult or impossible to get from manufactured test data. One example is testing of interfaces to legacy systems. Management information systems are a further example where access to real data is likely to enhance testing. Some flexibility on the use of production data is likely to be the best option. In addition to obtaining senior management approval, conditions that mitigate the risk associated with using production data can be agreed on, such as masking names and other identifying fields to protect privacy.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Production data can be used if they are copied to a secure test environment.
choices.justification: Copying production data to a secure environment is a good practice, but this should only be done with the approval of management. Management must accept the risk of using production data for testing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Production data can never be used. All test data must be developed and based on documented test cases.
choices.justification: Creating a complete set of test data is an ideal situation but is not always possible due to the volume of test data that would be required.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Production data can be used if confidentiality agreements are in place.
choices.justification: Production data can only be used with management’s permission. After permission is obtained, the use of confidentiality agreements may be appropriate and required.

questions.text: The BEST method of confirming the accuracy of a system tax calculation is by:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: review and analysis of the source code of the calculation programs.
choices.justification: A review of source code is not an effective method of ensuring that the calculation is being computed correctly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: recreating program logic using generalized audit software to calculate monthly totals.
choices.justification: Recreating program logic may lead to errors, and monthly totals are not accurate enough to ensure correct computations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: preparing simulated transactions for processing and comparing the results to predetermined results.
choices.justification: Preparing simulated transactions for processing and comparing the results to predetermined results is the best method for confirming the accuracy of a tax calculation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: automatic flowcharting and analysis of the source code of the calculation programs.
choices.justification: Flowcharting and analysis of source code are not effective methods to address the accuracy of individual tax calculations.

questions.text: Which of the following should be an information systems (IS) auditor’s PRIMARY concern after discovering that the scope of an IS project has changed and an impact study has not been performed?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Time and cost implications caused by the change
choices.justification: Any scope change might have an impact on the duration and cost of the project; that is the reason why an impact study is conducted and the client is informed of the potential impact on the schedule and cost.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Risk that regression tests will fail
choices.justification: A change in scope does not necessarily impact the risk that regression tests will fail.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Users not agreeing with the change
choices.justification: An impact study does not determine whether users will agree with a change in scope.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Project team not having the skills to make the necessary change
choices.justification: Conducting an impact study can identify a lack of resources, such as the project team lacking the skills necessary to make the change; however, this is only part of the impact on the overall timelines and cost to the project due to the change.

questions.text: Results of a postimplementation review indicate that only 75 percent of the users can log in to the application concurrently. Which of the following could have BEST discovered the identified weakness of the application?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Load testing
choices.justification: Load testing evaluates the performance of the software under normal and peak conditions. Because this application is not supporting normal numbers of concurrent users, the load testing must not have been adequate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stress testing
choices.justification: Stress testing determines the capacity of the software to cope with an abnormal number of users or simultaneous operations. Because the number of concurrent users in this question is within normal limits, the answer is load testing, not stress testing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recovery testing
choices.justification: Recovery testing evaluates the ability of a system to recover after a failure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Volume testing
choices.justification: Volume testing evaluates the impact of incremental volume of records (not users) on a system.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds that user acceptance testing of a new system is being repeatedly interrupted by defect fixes from the developers. Which of the following is the BEST recommendation for an IS auditor to make?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Consider the feasibility of a separate user acceptance environment.
choices.justification: A separate environment, or environments, is normally necessary for testing to be efficient and effective and to ensure the integrity of production code. It is important that the development and test code bases be separate. When defects are identified, they can be fixed in the development environment, without interrupting testing, before being migrated in a controlled manner to the test environment. A separate test environment can also be used as the final staging area from which code is migrated to production. Separate environments enforce a separation between development and production code. The logistics of setting up and refreshing customized test data is easier if a separate environment is maintained.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Schedule user testing to occur at a given time each day.
choices.justification: If developers and testers are sharing the same environment, they must work effectively at separate times of the day. It is unlikely that this situation would provide optimum productivity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implement a source code version-control tool.
choices.justification: Use of a source code control tool is a good practice, but it does not properly mitigate the lack of an appropriate test environment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only retest high-priority defects.
choices.justification: Even low-priority fixes run the risk of introducing unintended results when combined with the rest of the system code. To prevent this, regular regression testing covering all code changes should occur. A separate test environment makes the logistics of regression testing easier to manage.

questions.text: The purpose of a checksum on an amount field in an electronic data interchange communication of financial transactions is to ensure:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: integrity.
choices.justification: A checksum that is calculated on an amount field and included in the electronic data interchange communication can be used to identify unauthorized modifications.

choices.label: B
choices.text: authenticity.
choices.justification: Authenticity cannot be established by a checksum alone and needs other controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: authorization.
choices.justification: Authorization cannot be established by a checksum alone and needs other controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: nonrepudiation.
choices.justification: Nonrepudiation can be ensured by using digital signatures.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing the IT project management process is reviewing a feasibility study for a critical project to build a new data center. The IS auditor is MOST concerned about which of the following findings?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's security awareness training program

choices.label: A
choices.text: How the project fits into the overall project portfolio has not been determined.
choices.justification: Although projects must be assigned a priority and managed as a portfolio, this step most likely occurs after the feasibility study determines that the project is viable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The organizational impact of the project has not been assessed.
choices.justification: The feasibility study determines the strategic benefits of the project. Therefore, the feasibility study’s results determine the organizational impact—a comparison report of costs, benefits, risks, etc. The project portfolio is a part of measuring the organizational strategy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Not all IT stakeholders have been given an opportunity to provide input.
choices.justification: A feasibility study is ordinarily conducted by those with the knowledge to make the decision because the involvement of the entire IT organization is not needed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The environmental impact of the data center has not been considered.
choices.justification: The environmental impact should be part of the feasibility study; however, the organizational impact is more important.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor should ensure that review of online electronic funds transfer reconciliation procedures includes:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: vouching.
choices.justification: Vouching is usually performed during the funds transfer, not during the reconciliation effort.

choices.label: B
choices.text: authorizations.
choices.justification: In online processing, authorizations are normally done automatically by the system, not during the reconciliation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: corrections.
choices.justification: Correction entries should be reviewed during a reconciliation; however, they are normally done by an individual other than the person entrusted to do reconciliations and are not as important as tracing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: tracing.
choices.justification: Tracing is a transaction reconciliation effort that involves following the transaction from the original source to its final destination. In electronic funds transfer transactions, the direction on tracing may start from the customer-printed copy of the receipt, proceed to checking the system audit trails and logs, and end with checking the master file records for daily transactions.

questions.text: A new business application requires deviation from the standard configuration of the operating system (OS). What activity should the information systems (IS) auditor recommend to the security manager as a FIRST response?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initial rejection of the request because it is against the security policy
choices.justification: The security policy may be waived with management approval to meet business requirements; it is not up to the security manager to refuse the deviation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Approval of the exception to policy to meet business needs
choices.justification: The security manager may make a case for deviation from the policy, but this should be based on a risk assessment and compensating controls. The deviation itself should be approved in accordance with a defined exception handling process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assessment of the risk and identification of compensating controls
choices.justification: Before approving any exception, the security manager should first check for compensating controls and assess the possible risk due to deviation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Revision of the OS baseline configuration
choices.justification: Updating or revising the baseline configuration is not associated with requests for deviations.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing a new web-based order entry system the week before it goes live. The IS auditor identified that the application, as designed, may be missing several critical controls regarding how the system stores customer credit card information. The IS auditor should FIRST:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: determine whether system developers have proper training on adequate security measures.
choices.justification: Although it is important for programmers to understand security, it is more important that the security requirements were properly stated in the project plan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: determine whether system administrators have disabled security controls for any reason.
choices.justification: System administrators may have made changes to the controls, but it is assumed that the auditor is reviewing the system as designed a week prior to implementation, so the administrators have not yet configured the system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: verify that security requirements have been properly specified in the project plan.
choices.justification: If there are significant security issues identified by an information systems (IS) auditor, the first question is whether the security requirements were correct in the project plan. Depending on whether the requirements were included in the plan affects the recommendations the auditor would make.

choices.label: D
choices.text: validate whether security controls are based on requirements which are no longer valid.
choices.justification: It is possible that security requirements will change over time based on new threats or vulnerabilities, but if critical controls are missing, this points toward a faulty design that was based on incomplete requirements.

questions.text: What is the PRIMARY reason that an information systems (IS) auditor verifies that the process of postimplementation review of an application was completed after a release?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: To make sure that users are appropriately trained
choices.justification: Postimplementation review does not target verifying user training needs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To verify that the project was within budget
choices.justification: Project costs are monitored during development and are not the primary reason for a postimplementation review.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To check that the project meets expectations
choices.justification: The objective of a postimplementation review is to reveal whether the implementation of a system has achieved planned objectives (i.e., meets business objectives and risk acceptance criteria).

choices.label: D
choices.text: To determine whether proper controls were implemented
choices.justification: Although an information systems (IS) auditor would be interested in ensuring that proper controls were implemented, the most important consideration is that the project meets expectations.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor found time constraints and expanded needs to be the root causes for recent violations of corporate data definition standards in a new business intelligence project. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate suggestion for an auditor to make?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Achieve standards alignment through an increase of resources devoted to the project.
choices.justification: Provided that data architecture, technical and operational requirements are sufficiently documented, the alignment to standards can be treated as a specific work package assigned to new project resources.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Align the data definition standards after completion of the project.
choices.justification: The use of nonstandard data definitions lowers the efficiency of the new development and increases the risk of errors in critical business decisions. Changing data definition standards after project conclusion creates risk and is not a viable solution.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delay the project until compliance with standards can be achieved.
choices.justification: Delaying the project is an inappropriate suggestion because of business requirements or the likely damage to entire project profitability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enforce standard compliance by adopting punitive measures against violators.
choices.justification: Punishing the violators is outside the authority of the auditor and inappropriate until the reason for the violations have been determined.

questions.text: The PRIMARY purpose of a postimplementation review is to ascertain that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate potential opportunities and risks associated with emerging technologies, regulations, and industry practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: the lessons learned have been documented.
choices.justification: It is important to ensure that lessons learned during the project are not forgotten; however, it is more important to ascertain whether the project solved the problem it was designed to address.

choices.label: B
choices.text: future enhancements can be identified.
choices.justification: Identifying future enhancements is not the primary objective of a postimplementation review.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the project has been delivered on time and budget.
choices.justification: Although it is important to review whether the project was completed on time and budget, it is more important to determine whether the project met the business needs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: project objectives have been met.
choices.justification: A project manager performs a postimplementation review to obtain feedback regarding the project deliverables and business needs and to determine whether the project has successfully met them.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing IT projects for a large enterprise and wants to determine whether the IT projects undertaken in a given year are those which have been assigned the highest priority by the business and which will generate the greatest business value. Which of the following is MOST relevant?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capability maturity model (CMM)
choices.justification: A capability maturity model (CMM) is a means of assessing the relative maturity of the IT processes within an organization, running from Level 0 (Incomplete—processes are not implemented or fail to achieve their purpose) to Level 5 (Optimizing—metrics are defined and measured, and continuous improvement techniques are in place). It does not help determine the optimal portfolio of capital projects, because it assesses the maturity of the processes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Portfolio management
choices.justification: Portfolio management is designed to assist in the definition, prioritization, approval and running of a set of projects within a given organization. These tools offer data capture, workflow and scenario planning functionality, which can help identify the optimum set of projects (from the full set of ideas) to take forward within a given budget.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Configuration management
choices.justification: A configuration management database, which stores the configuration details for an organization’s IT systems, is an important tool for IT service delivery and, in particular, change management. A configuration management database may provide information that would influence the prioritization of projects but is not designed for that purpose.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Project management body of knowledge
choices.justification: The project management body of knowledge is a methodology for the management and delivery of projects. It offers no specific guidance or assistance for optimizing a project portfolio.

questions.text: The development of an application has been outsourced to an offshore vendor. Which of the following should be of GREATEST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: The right to audit clause was not included in the contract.
choices.justification: The lack of the right to audit clause presents a risk to the organization; however, the risk is not as consequential as the lack of a business case.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The business case was not established.
choices.justification: Because the business case was not established, it is likely that the business rationale, risk and risk mitigation strategies for outsourcing the application development were not fully evaluated and the appropriate information was not provided to senior management for formal approval. This situation presents the biggest risk to the organization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There was no source code escrow agreement.
choices.justification: If the source code is held by the provider and not provided to the organization, the lack of source code escrow presents a risk to the organization; however, the risk is not as consequential as the lack of a business case.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The contract does not cover change management procedures.
choices.justification: The lack of change management procedures presents a risk to the organization, especially with the possibility of extraordinary charges for any required changes; however, the risk is not as consequential as the lack of a business case.

questions.text: The MAJOR consideration for an information systems (IS) auditor reviewing an organization’s IT project portfolio is the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: IT budget.
choices.justification: The IT budget is important to ensure that the resources are being used in the best manner, but this is secondary to the importance of reviewing the business plan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: existing IT environment.
choices.justification: The existing IT environment is important and used to determine gap analysis but is secondary to the importance of reviewing the business plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: business plan.
choices.justification: One of the most important determining factors for which projects get funded is how well a project meets an organization’s strategic objectives. Portfolio management takes a holistic view of a company’s overall IT strategy. IT strategy should be aligned with the business strategy and, hence, reviewing the business plan should be the major consideration.

choices.label: D
choices.text: investment plan.
choices.justification: The investment plan is important to set out project priorities, but secondary to the importance of reviewing the business plan.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: An organization completed a business impact analysis (BIA) as part of business continuity planning. The NEXT step in the process is to develop:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: a business continuity strategy.
choices.justification: A business continuity strategy is the next phase because it identifies the best way to recover. The criticality of the business process, the cost, the time required to recover, and security must be considered during this phase.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a test and exercise plan.
choices.justification: The recovery strategy and plan development precede the test plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a user training program.
choices.justification: Training can only be developed once the business continuity plan (BCP) is in place.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the business continuity plan (BCP).
choices.justification: A strategy must be determined before the BCP is developed.

questions.text: Which of the following reports is the MOST appropriate source of information for an information systems (IS) auditor to validate that an (internet service provider (ISP)) has been complying with an enterprise service level agreement for the availability of outsourced telecommunication services?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Downtime reports on the telecommunication services generated by the ISP
choices.justification: The internet service provider (ISP)-generated downtime reports are produced by the same entity that is being monitored. As a result, it will be necessary to review these reports for possible bias and/or errors against other data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A utilization report of automatic failover services generated by the enterprise
choices.justification: The information provided by these reports is indirect evidence of the extent that the backup telecommunication services were used. These reports may not indicate compliance with the service level agreement, just that the failover systems had been used.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A bandwidth utilization report provided by the ISP
choices.justification: Utilization reports are used to measure the usage of bandwidth, not uptime.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Downtime reports on the telecommunication services generated by the enterprise
choices.justification: The enterprise should use internally generated downtime reports to monitor the service provided by the ISP and, as available, to compare with the reports provided by the ISP.

questions.text: Determining the service delivery objective (SDO) should be based PRIMARILY on:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: the minimum acceptable operational capability.
choices.justification: The service delivery objective (SDO) is the level of service to be reached during the alternate process mode until the normal situation is restored. This is directly related to the business needs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the cost-effectiveness of the restoration process.
choices.justification: The cost-effectiveness of the restoration process is not the main consideration of determining the SDO.

choices.label: C
choices.text: meeting the recovery time objectives (RTOs).
choices.justification: Meeting the recovery time objective (RTO) may be one of the considerations in determining the SDO, but it is a secondary factor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the allowable interruption window.
choices.justification: The allowable interruption window may be one of the factors secondary to determining the SDO.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST mitigates the risk arising from using reciprocal agreements as a recovery alternative?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Perform disaster recovery exercises annually.
choices.justification: Although disaster recovery exercises are important but difficult to perform in a reciprocal agreement, the greater risk is geographic proximity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure that partnering organizations are separated geographically.
choices.justification: If the two partnering organizations are in close geographic proximity, this can lead to both organizations being subjected to the same environmental disaster, such as an earthquake.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Regularly perform a business impact analysis (BIA).
choices.justification: A business impact analysis (BIA) will help both organizations identify critical applications, but separation is a more important consideration when entering reciprocal agreements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Select a partnering organization with similar systems.
choices.justification: Selecting a partnering organization with similar systems is a good idea, but separation is a more important consideration when entering reciprocal agreements.

questions.text: The MAIN purpose of service level management (SLM) is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: set expectations between users and providers.
choices.justification: The objective of service level management (SLM) is to negotiate, document and manage (i.e., provide and monitor) the services in the manner in which the customer requires those services.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ensure that services are managed to deliver the highest achievable level of availability.
choices.justification: SLM does not necessarily ensure that services are delivered at the highest achievable level of availability (e.g., redundancy and clustering). Although maximizing availability might be necessary for some critical services, it cannot be applied as a general rule of thumb.

choices.label: C
choices.text: keep the costs associated with any service at a minimum.
choices.justification: SLM cannot ensure that costs for all services will be kept at a low or minimum level because costs associated with a service will directly reflect the customer’s requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: monitor and report any legal noncompliance to business management.
choices.justification: Monitoring and reporting legal noncompliance is not a primary objective of SLM.

questions.text: During an HR audit, an information systems (IS) auditor is informed that there is a verbal agreement between the IT and HR departments about the level of IT services expected. In this situation, what should the IS auditor do FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Postpone the audit until the agreement is documented.
choices.justification: There is no reason to postpone an audit because a service agreement is not documented, unless that is all that is being audited. The agreement can be documented after it has been established that there is an agreement in place.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Report the existence of the undocumented agreement to senior management.
choices.justification: Reporting to senior management is not necessary at this stage of the audit because this is not a serious immediate vulnerability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Confirm the content of the agreement with both departments.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor should first confirm and understand the current practice before making any recommendations. Part of this is to ensure that both parties agree with the terms of the agreement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Draft a service level agreement for the two departments.
choices.justification: Drafting a service level agreement is not the IS auditor’s responsibility.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor of a health care organization is reviewing contractual terms and conditions of a third-party cloud provider being considered to host patient health information. Which of the following contractual terms is the GREATEST risk to the customer organization?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: Data ownership is retained by the customer organization.
choices.justification: The customer organization would want to retain data ownership and, therefore, this would not be a risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The third-party provider reserves the right to access data to perform certain operations.
choices.justification: Organizations must review the regulatory environment in which the cloud provider operates because it may have requirements or restrictions of its own. Some service providers reserve the right to access customer information (third-party access) to perform certain transactions and provide certain services. In the case of protected health information, regulations may restrict certain access. Organizations must then determine whether the cloud provider provides appropriate controls to ensure data security.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bulk data withdrawal mechanisms are undefined.
choices.justification: An organization may eventually wish to discontinue its service with a third-party cloud-based provider. The organization would then want to remove its data from the system and ensure that the service provider clears the system (including any backups) of its data. Some providers do not offer automated or bulk data withdrawal mechanisms, which the organization needs to migrate its data. These aspects should be clarified prior to using a third-party provider.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The customer organization is responsible for backup, archiving and restoration.
choices.justification: An organization may need to plan its own data recovery processes and procedures if the service provider does not make this available or the organization has doubts about the service provider’s processes. This would only be a risk if the customer organization could not perform these activities.

questions.text: During an implementation review of a recent application deployment, it was determined that several incidents were assigned incorrect priorities and, because of this, failed to meet the business service level agreement (SLA). What is the GREATEST concern?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: The support model was not approved by senior management.
choices.justification: Although senior management involvement is important, the more critical issue is whether the support model was not properly developed and implemented.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The incident resolution of time specified in the SLA is not realistic.
choices.justification: Although the incident resolution time specified in the service level agreement (SLA) may not always be attainable, the more critical issue is whether the support model was not properly developed and implemented.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There are inadequate resources to support the applications.
choices.justification: Although adequate support resources are important, the more critical issue is whether the support model was not properly developed and implemented.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The support model was poorly developed and implemented contributing to inaccurate prioritization and SLA failures.
choices.justification: The greatest concern for the information systems (IS) auditor is that the support model was not developed and implemented correctly to prevent or react to potential outages. Incidents can cost the business a significant amount of money and a support model should be implemented with the project. This should be a step within the system development life cycle (SDLC) and procedures and, if it is missed on one project, it may be a symptom of an overall breakdown in process.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing a new outsourcing contract with a service provider would be MOST concerned if which of the following was missing?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: A clause providing a right to audit the service provider
choices.justification: The absence of a right to audit clause or other forms of attestation that the supplier is compliant with a certain standard would potentially prevent the information systems (IS) auditor from investigating any aspect of supplier performance moving forward, including control deficiencies, poor performance, and adherence to legal requirements. The absence of this clause would be a major concern for the IS auditor because it would be difficult for the organization to assess whether the appropriate controls were implemented.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A clause defining penalty payments for poor performance
choices.justification: Although a clear definition of penalty payment terms is desirable, not all contracts require the payment of penalties for poor performance. When performance penalties are required, these penalties are often subject to negotiation on a case-by-case basis. Therefore, the absence of this information is not as significant as a lack of right to audit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Predefined service level report templates
choices.justification: Although the inclusion of service level report templates is desirable, if the requirement for service level reporting is included in the contract, the absence of predefined templates for reporting is not a significant concern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A clause regarding supplier limitation of liability
choices.justification: The absence of a limitation of liability clause for the service provider exposes the provider to unlimited liability. This is to the advantage of the outsourcing organization so, although the IS auditor might highlight the absence of such a clause, it does not constitute a major concern.

questions.text: When determining the acceptable time period for the resumption of critical business processes:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: only downtime costs need to be considered.
choices.justification: Downtime costs cannot be looked at in isolation. The quicker information assets can be restored and business processing resumed, the smaller the downtime costs. However, the expenditure needed to have the redundant capability that is required to rapidly recover information resources might be prohibitive for nonessential business processes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: recovery operations should be analyzed.
choices.justification: Recovery operations alone do not determine the acceptable time period for the resumption of critical business processes, and indirect downtime costs should be considered in addition to the direct cash outflows incurred due to business disruption.

choices.label: C
choices.text: both downtime costs and recovery costs need to be evaluated.
choices.justification: Downtime costs and recovery costs need to be evaluated in determining the acceptable time period before the resumption of critical business processes. The outcome of the business impact analysis should be a recovery strategy that represents the optimal balance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: indirect downtime costs should be ignored.
choices.justification: The indirect costs of a serious disruption to normal business activity (e.g., loss of customer and supplier goodwill and loss of market share) may actually be more significant than direct costs over time, thus reaching the point where business viability is threatened.

questions.text: An organization is considering using a new IT service provider. From an audit perspective, which of the following is the MOST important item to review?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: References from other clients for the service provider
choices.justification: A due diligence activity, such as reviewing references from other clients, is a good practice, but the service level agreement (SLA) is most critical because it defines what specific levels of performance are required and makes the provider contractually obligated to deliver what was promised.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The physical security of the service provider site
choices.justification: A due diligence activity, such as reviewing physical security controls, is a good practice, but the SLA is most critical because it defines what specific levels of security are required and makes the provider contractually obligated to deliver what was promised.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The proposed service level agreement (SLA) with the service provider
choices.justification: An SLA is a guarantee that the provider will deliver the services according to the contract. When contracting with a service provider, it is a good practice to enter into an SLA with the provider. The information systems (IS) auditor will want to ensure that performance and security requirements are clearly stated in the SLA.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Background checks of the service provider’s employees
choices.justification: A due diligence activity, such as the use of background checks for the service provider’s employees, is a good practice, but the SLA is most critical because it defines what specific levels of security and labor practices are required and makes the provider contractually obligated to deliver what was promised.

questions.text: Which of the following issues should be a MAJOR concern to an information systems (IS) auditor who is reviewing a service level agreement (SLA)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT service management practices align with organizational requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: A service adjustment resulting from an exception report took a day to implement.
choices.justification: Resolving issues related to exception reports is an operational issue that should be addressed in the service level agreement (SLA); however, a response time of one day may be acceptable, depending on the terms of the SLA.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The complexity of application logs used for service monitoring made the review difficult.
choices.justification: The complexity of application logs is an operational issue, which is not related to the SLA.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Key performance indicators (KPIs) were not included in the SLA.
choices.justification: Lack of service measures, such as key performance indicators (KPIs), makes it difficult to gauge the efficiency and effectiveness of the IT services being provided. Although KPIs can vary based on the nature of the service and the agreement between parties, commonly included KPIs in SLAs include response time, uptime, service reliability, etc.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The document is updated on an annual basis.
choices.justification: Although it is important that the document be current, depending on the term of the agreement, it may not be necessary to change the document more frequently than annually.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST helps an information systems (IS) auditor to detect the problems encountered by application executions during IT operations?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Operating system logs
choices.justification: Operating system logs help in identifying problems encountered by operating systems and may not report application-related problems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Incident reports
choices.justification: Application execution problems may not be reported as incidents and would not appear in incident reports unless the interruption is significant enough to be considered an incident.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exception reports
choices.justification: Exception reports are automated reports that list the problems encountered by the application during execution.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Computer operator logs
choices.justification: Computer operator logs are manually entered by the operator during operations; these logs may or may not identify specific problems with applications.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: During an audit of a business continuity plan (BCP), an information systems (IS) auditor found that, although all departments were housed in the same building, each department had a separate BCP. The IS auditor recommended that the BCPs be reconciled. Which of the following areas should be reconciled FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evacuation plan
choices.justification: Protecting human resources during a disaster-related event should be addressed first. Having separate business continuity plans (BCPs) can result in conflicting evacuation plans, thus jeopardizing the safety of staff and clients.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recovery priorities
choices.justification: Recovery priorities may be unique to each department and can be addressed separately, but still should be reviewed for possible conflicts and/or the possibility of cost reduction, but only after the issue of human safety has been analyzed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Backup storages
choices.justification: Backup strategies are not critical to the integration of the plans for the various departments. Life safety is always the first priority.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Call tree
choices.justification: Communication during a crisis is always a challenge, but the call tree is not as important as ensuring life safety first.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST important to determine the recovery point objective (RPO) for a critical process in an enterprise?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Number of hours of acceptable downtime
choices.justification: The recovery time objective (RTO) is the amount of time allowed for the recovery of a business function or resource after a disaster.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total cost of recovering critical systems
choices.justification: The determination of the recovery point objective (RPO) already takes cost into consideration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Extent of data loss that is acceptable
choices.justification: The RPO determines acceptable data loss and the earliest acceptable recovery point in time, quantifying the permissible data loss in case of interruption.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acceptable reduction in the level of service
choices.justification: The service delivery objective (SDO) is directly related to the business needs. The SDO is the level of services to be reached during the alternate process mode until the normal situation is restored.

questions.text: While performing a review of a critical third-party application, an information systems (IS) auditor is MOST concerned with discovering:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: inadequate procedures for ensuring adequate system portability.
choices.justification: Procedures to ensure that systems are developed so that they can be ported to other system platforms help ensure that the system can continue functioning without affecting the business process if changes to the infrastructure occur. This is less important than the availability of the software.

choices.label: B
choices.text: inadequate operational documentation for the system.
choices.justification: Inadequate operational documentation is a risk but is less significant than the risk of unavailability of the software.

choices.label: C
choices.text: an inadequate alternate service provider listing.
choices.justification: Although alternate service providers can be used if a vendor goes out of business, having access to the source code via a software escrow agreement is more important.

choices.label: D
choices.text: an inadequate software escrow agreement.
choices.justification: The inclusion of a clause in the agreement that requires software code to be placed in escrow helps to ensure that the customer can continue to use the software and/or obtain technical support if a vendor goes out of business.

questions.text: An optimized disaster recovery plan (DRP) for an organization should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: reduce the length of the recovery time and the cost of recovery.
choices.justification: One of the objectives of a disaster recovery plan (DRP) is to reduce the duration and cost of recovering from a disaster.

choices.label: B
choices.text: increase the length of the recovery time and the cost of recovery.
choices.justification: A DRP increases the cost of operations before and after the disaster occurs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: reduce the duration of the recovery time and increase the cost of recovery.
choices.justification: A DRP should reduce the time to return to normal operations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: not affect the recovery time or the cost of recovery.
choices.justification: A DRP should reduce the cost that could result from a disaster.

questions.text: Which of the following is the PRIMARY objective of the business continuity plan (BCP) process?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: To provide assurance to stakeholders that business operations will continue in the event of disaster.
choices.justification: The business continuity plan (BCP) does not provide assurance of continuing operations; however, it helps the organization to respond to disruptions to critical business processes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To establish an alternate site for IT services to meet predefined recovery time objectives (RTOs).
choices.justification: Establishment of an alternate site is more relevant to disaster recovery than the BCP.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To manage risk while recovering from an event that adversely affected operations.
choices.justification: The BCP process primarily focuses on managing and mitigating risk during recovery of operations due to an event that affected operations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To meet the regulatory compliance requirements in the event of natural disaster.
choices.justification: The regulatory compliance requirements may help establish the recovery time objective (RTO) requirements.

questions.text: Integrating the business continuity plan (BCP) into IT project management aids in:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: the testing of the business continuity requirements.
choices.justification: Testing the business continuity plan’s (BCP) requirements is not related to IT project management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the development of a more comprehensive set of requirements.
choices.justification: Integrating the BCP into the development process ensures complete coverage of the requirements through each phase of the project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the development of a transaction flowchart.
choices.justification: A transaction flowchart aids in analyzing an application’s controls but does not affect business continuity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ensuring the application meets the users’ needs.
choices.justification: A BCP will not directly address the detailed processing needs of the users.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST ensures continuity of a wide area network (WAN) across the enterprise?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Built-in alternative routing
choices.justification: Alternative routing ensures that the network will continue if a communication device fails or if a link is severed because message rerouting can be automatic.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Complete full system backup daily
choices.justification: System backup will not afford protection for a networking failure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A repair contract with a service provider
choices.justification: The repair contract will almost always result in some lost time and is not as effective as permanent alternative routing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A duplicate machine alongside each server
choices.justification: Standby servers will not provide continuity if a link is severed.

questions.text: For effective implementation after a business continuity plan (BCP) has been developed, it is MOST important that the BCP be:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: stored in a secure offsite facility.
choices.justification: If the business continuity plan (BCP) is kept in a safe place, it will not reach the users, users will never implement the BCP and, thus, the BCP will be ineffective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: approved by senior management.
choices.justification: Senior management approval is a prerequisite for designing and approving the BCP but is less important than making sure that the plan is available to all key personnel to ensure that the plan will be effective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: communicated to appropriate personnel.
choices.justification: The implementation of a BCP will be effective only if appropriate personnel are informed and aware of all the aspects of the BCP.

choices.label: D
choices.text: made available through the enterprise’s intranet.
choices.justification: Making a BCP available on an enterprise’s intranet does not guarantee that personnel will be able to access, read or understand it.

questions.text: Which of the following is an appropriate test method to apply to a business continuity plan (BCP)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pilot
choices.justification: A pilot test is used for implementing a new process or technology and is not appropriate for a business continuity plan (BCP).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paper
choices.justification: A paper test (sometimes called a desk check) is appropriate for testing a BCP. It is a walk-through of the entire BCP, or part of the BCP, involving major players in the BCP’s execution who determine what may happen in a particular disaster.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Unit
choices.justification: A unit test is used to test new software components and is not appropriate for a BCP.

choices.label: D
choices.text: System
choices.justification: A system test is an integrated test used to test a new IT system but is not appropriate for a BCP.

questions.text: While observing a full simulation of the business continuity plan (BCP), an information systems (IS) auditor notices that the notification systems within the organizational facilities can be severely impacted by infrastructure damage. The BEST recommendation that the IS auditor can provide to the organization is to ensure that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: the salvage team is trained to use the notification system.
choices.justification: The salvage team would not be able to use a severely damaged notification system, even if they are trained to use it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the notification system provides for the recovery of the backup.
choices.justification: The recovery of the backups has no bearing on the notification system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: redundancies are built into the notification system.
choices.justification: If the notification system has been severely impacted by the damage, redundancy is the best control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the notification systems are stored in a vault.
choices.justification: Storing the notification system in a vault is of little value if the building is damaged.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST important for an information systems (IS) auditor to verify while conducting a business continuity audit?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Data backups are performed on a timely basis.
choices.justification: Performing data backups is necessary for a business continuity plan, but the information systems (IS) auditor will always be most concerned with human safety.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A recovery site is contracted for and available as needed.
choices.justification: A recovery site is important for business continuity, but life safety is always the first priority.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Human safety procedures are in place.
choices.justification: The most important element in any business continuity process is the protection of human life. This takes precedence over all other aspects of the plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Insurance coverage is adequate and premiums are current.
choices.justification: Insurance coverage is not as important as life safety.

questions.text: The PRIMARY objective of testing a business continuity plan (BCP) is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: familiarize employees with the business continuity plan (BCP).
choices.justification: Familiarizing employees with the business continuity plan (BCP) is a secondary benefit of a test.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ensure that all residual risk is addressed.
choices.justification: It is not cost-effective to address all residual risk in a BCP.

choices.label: C
choices.text: exercise all possible disaster scenarios.
choices.justification: It is not practical to test all possible disaster scenarios.

choices.label: D
choices.text: identify limitations of the BCP.
choices.justification: Testing the BCP provides the best evidence of any limitations that may exist.

questions.text: Which of the following business continuity plan (BCP) tests involves participation of relevant members of the crisis management/response team to practice proper coordination?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tabletop
choices.justification: The primary purpose of tabletop testing is to practice proper coordination because it involves all or some of the crisis team members and is focused more on coordination and communication issues than on technical process details.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Functional
choices.justification: Functional testing involves mobilization of personnel and resources at various geographic sites. This is a more in-depth functional test and not primarily focused on coordination and communication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Full-scale
choices.justification: A full-scale test is a comprehensive and realistic simulation of a disaster scenario, involving multiple teams and resources. It aims to test the entire business continuity plan (BCP) in a real-world environment. Although coordination among various teams is essential in a full-scale test, it does not specifically mention the participation of the crisis management/response team or emphasize practicing proper coordination.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Desk check
choices.justification: A desk check is a review and analysis of the BCP documents by individuals or a small group. It is typically a more passive and theoretical exercise, involving the review of plans, policies and procedures without active participation or coordination among team members.

questions.text: During the design of a business continuity plan (BCP), the business impact analysis (BIA) identifies critical processes and supporting applications. This will PRIMARILY influence the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: responsibility for maintaining the BCP.
choices.justification: The responsibility for maintaining the business continuity plan (BCP) is decided after the selection or design of the appropriate recovery strategy and development of the plan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: criteria for selecting a recovery site provider.
choices.justification: The criteria for selecting a recovery site provider are decided after the selection or design of the appropriate recovery strategy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: recovery strategy.
choices.justification: The most appropriate strategy is selected based on the relative risk level, timelines and criticality identified in the business impact analysis (BIA).

choices.label: D
choices.text: responsibilities of key personnel.
choices.justification: The responsibilities of key personnel are decided after the selection or design of the appropriate recovery strategy during the plan development phase.

questions.text: Which of the following should be of MOST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor reviewing the business continuity plan (BCP)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: The disaster levels are based on scopes of damaged functions but not on duration.
choices.justification: Although failure to consider duration can be a problem, it is not as significant as scope, and neither is as critical as the need to identify someone with the authority to invoke the business continuity plan (BCP).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The difference between low-level disaster and software incidents is not clear.
choices.justification: The difference between incidents and low-level disasters is always unclear and frequently revolves around the amount of time required to correct the damage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The overall BCP is documented, but detailed recovery steps are not specified.
choices.justification: The lack of detailed steps should be documented, but their absence does not mean a lack of recovery if someone has invoked the BCP.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The responsibility for declaring a disaster is not identified.
