choices.justification: If nobody declares the disaster, the BCP is not invoked, making all other concerns less important.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST ensures uninterrupted operations in an organization with IT operation centers in several countries?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Distribution of key procedural documentation
choices.justification: Procedural documentation should always be up-to-date and distributed to major locations. However, documents alone are insufficient if employees do not know their role in the plan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reciprocal agreement between business partners
choices.justification: A reciprocal agreement is an emergency processing agreement between two or more enterprises with similar equipment or applications. Typically, participants of a reciprocal agreement promise to provide processing time to each other when an emergency arises. Although it is integral to business continuity to have a location for business operations, it does not necessarily need to be a reciprocal agreement. For example, in some cases, business operations may be carried out from each employee’s home.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Strong senior management leadership
choices.justification: Senior management may not be readily available to provide leadership during a disaster. Therefore, it is most important that employees fully understand their roles in the business continuity plan (BCP).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Employee training on the BCP
choices.justification: During a disaster, the chain of command might be interrupted. Therefore, it is important that employees know their roles in the BCP, including where to report and how to perform their job functions. Employee training on the plan is especially important for businesses with offices that are geographically separated because there is a greater chance of communication disruption.

questions.text: Which of the following contributes MOST to an effective business continuity plan (BCP)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: The document is circulated to all interested parties.
choices.justification: The business continuity plan (BCP) circulation ensures that the BCP document is received by all users. Although essential, this does not contribute significantly to the success of the BCP.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Planning involves all user departments.
choices.justification: The involvement of user departments in the BCP is crucial for the identification of the business processing priorities and the development of an effective plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The plan is approved by senior management.
choices.justification: A BCP approved by senior management does not necessarily ensure the effectiveness of the BCP.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An audit is performed by an external information systems (IS) auditor.
choices.justification: An audit does not necessarily improve the quality of the BCP.

questions.text: Which of the following would be a MAJOR concern for an information systems (IS) auditor reviewing a business continuity plan (BCP)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: The plan is approved by the chief information officer.
choices.justification: Ideally, the board of directors should approve the plan to ensure acceptability, but it is possible to delegate approval authority to the chief information officer. Pragmatically, lack of documenting test results can have more significant consequences.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The plan contact lists have not been updated.
choices.justification: The contact lists are an important part of the business continuity plan (BCP); however, they are not as important as documenting the test results.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Test results are not adequately documented.
choices.justification: The effectiveness of a BCP can best be determined through tests. If results of tests are not documented, then there is no basis for feedback, updates, etc.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The training schedule for recovery personnel is not included.
choices.justification: If test results are documented, a need for training will be identified and the BCP will be updated.

questions.text: During a review of a business continuity plan (BCP), an information systems (IS) auditor noticed that the point at which a situation is declared to be a crisis has not been defined. The MAJOR risk associated with this is that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: response and recovery actions are initiated too early.
choices.justification: Without a defined threshold for declaring a crisis, there is a possibility that response and recovery actions may be initiated prematurely, resulting in unnecessary costs and disruptions. However, the likelihood of initiating actions too early is not as critical as the risk of delayed response in the absence of a clear crisis declaration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: activating the appropriate response and recovery actions are delayed during a crisis.
choices.justification: The absence of a clear definition for declaring a situation as a crisis creates the risk of delays in activating the appropriate response and recovery actions. Without a predefined threshold or criteria for identifying a crisis, there is uncertainty and potential confusion about when to escalate the response, mobilize resources and implement critical measures. This can lead to valuable time being lost, ineffective decision-making, and delays in mitigating the impacts of the crisis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: crisis situations may be underestimated or overlooked.
choices.justification: In the absence of a defined point for declaring a crisis, there is a risk that crisis situations may be underestimated or overlooked. However, it is not as significant a concern as delayed reactions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the BCP may become too rigid.
choices.justification: Without a defined crisis declaration point, there is a risk that the business continuity plan (BCP) may become too rigid. This can lead to unnecessary delays and inefficiencies in responding to incidents, because the plan may require adherence to predefined steps without considering the actual severity and impact of the situation. However, this risk is not as significant as the risk of delayed response.

questions.text: With respect to business continuity strategies, an information systems (IS) auditor interviews key stakeholders in an organization to determine whether they understand their roles and responsibilities. The IS auditor is attempting to evaluate the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: clarity and simplicity of the business continuity plans (BCPs).
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor should interview key stakeholders to evaluate how well they understand their roles and responsibilities. When all stakeholders have a detailed understanding of their roles and responsibilities in the event of a disaster, an IS auditor can deem the business continuity plan to be clear and simple.

choices.label: B
choices.text: adequacy of the BCPs.
choices.justification: To evaluate adequacy, the IS auditor should review the plans and compare them to appropriate standards and the results of tests of the plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: effectiveness of the BCPs.
choices.justification: To evaluate effectiveness, the IS auditor should review the results from previous tests or incidents. This is the best determination for the evaluation of effectiveness. An understanding of roles and responsibilities by key stakeholders will assist in ensuring the BCP is effective.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ability of IS and end-user personnel to respond effectively in emergencies.
choices.justification: To evaluate the response, the IS auditor should review results of continuity tests. This will provide the IS auditor with assurance that target and recovery times are met. Emergency procedures and employee training need to be reviewed to determine whether the organization has implemented plans to allow for an effective response.

questions.text: Depending on the complexity of an organization’s business continuity plan (BCP), it may be developed as a set of plans to address various aspects of business continuity and disaster recovery. In such an environment, it is essential that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: each plan is consistent with one another.
choices.justification: Depending on the complexity of an organization, there can be more than one plan to address various aspects of business continuity and disaster recovery, but the plans must be consistent to be effective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: all plans are integrated into a single plan.
choices.justification: The plans do not necessarily have to be integrated into one single plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: each plan is dependent on one another.
choices.justification: Although each plan may be independent, each plan must be consistent with other plans to have a viable business continuity planning strategy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the sequence for implementation of all plans is defined.
choices.justification: It may not be possible to define a sequence in which plans have to be implemented because it may be dependent on the nature of disaster, criticality, recovery time, etc.

questions.text: A medium-sized organization, whose IT disaster recovery measures have been in place and regularly tested for years, has just developed a formal business continuity plan (BCP). A basic BCP tabletop exercise has been performed successfully. Which testing should an information systems (IS) auditor recommend be performed next to verify the adequacy of the new BCP?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Full-scale test with relocation of all departments, including IT, to the contingency site.
choices.justification: A full-scale test in the situation described might fail because it would be the first time that the plan is actually exercised, and a number of resources (including IT) and time would be wasted.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Walk-through test of a series of predefined scenarios with all critical personnel involved.
choices.justification: The walk-through test is a basic type of testing. Its intention is to make key staff familiar with the plan and discuss critical plan elements, rather than verifying its adequacy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IT disaster recovery test with business departments involved in testing the critical applications.
choices.justification: The recovery of applications should always be verified and approved by the business instead of being purely IT-driven. The IT plan has been tested repeatedly so a disaster recovery test would not help in verifying the administrative and organizational parts of the business continuity plan (BCP), which are not IT-related.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Functional test of a scenario with limited IT involvement.
choices.justification: After a tabletop exercise has been performed, the next step is a functional test, which includes the mobilization of staff to exercise the administrative and organizational functions of a recovery. Because the IT part of the recovery has been tested for years, it is more efficient to verify and optimize the BCP before actually involving IT in a full-scale test. The full-scale test would be the last step of the verification process before entering into a regular annual testing schedule.

questions.text: While designing the business continuity plan (BCP) for an airline reservation system, the MOST appropriate method of data transfer/backup at an offsite location is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: shadow file processing.
choices.justification: In shadow file processing, exact duplicates of the files are maintained at the same site or at a remote site. The two files are processed concurrently. This is used for critical data files, such as airline booking systems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: electronic vaulting.
choices.justification: Electronic vaulting electronically transmits data either to direct access storage, an optical disc or another storage medium; this is a method used by banks. This method is not usually in real time, unlike a shadow file system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: hard-disk mirroring.
choices.justification: Hard-disk mirroring provides redundancy in case the primary hard disk fails. All transactions and operations occur on two hard disks in the same server.

choices.label: D
choices.text: hot-site provisioning.
choices.justification: A hot site is an alternate site ready to take over business operations within a few hours of any business interruption and is not a method for backing up data.

questions.text: A live test of a mutual agreement for IT system recovery was carried out, including a four-hour test of intensive usage by the business units. The test was successful, but gives only partial assurance that the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: system and the IT operations team can sustain operations in the emergency environment.
choices.justification: The applications have been operated intensively, but the capability of the system and the IT operations team to sustain and support this environment (ancillary operations, batch closing, error corrections, output distribution, etc.) is only partially tested.

choices.label: B
choices.text: resources and the environment can sustain the transaction load.
choices.justification: Because the test involved intensive usage, the backup seems to be able to handle the transaction load.

choices.label: C
choices.text: connectivity to the applications at the remote site meets response time requirements.
choices.justification: Because users were able to connect to and use the system, the response time must have been satisfactory.

choices.label: D
choices.text: workflow of actual business operations can use the emergency system in case of a disaster.
choices.justification: The live test of the mutual agreement for IT system recovery, including the intensive usage by business units, only partially ensures that the workflow of actual business operations can use the emergency system in case of a disaster.

questions.text: Which of the following stakeholders is the MOST important in terms of developing a business continuity plan (BCP)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Process owners
choices.justification: Process owners are essential in identifying the critical business functions, recovery times and resources needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Application owners
choices.justification: A business continuity plan (BCP) is concerned with the continuity of business processes, while applications may or may not support critical business processes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Board of directors
choices.justification: The board of directors might approve the plan, but they are typically not involved in the details of developing the BCP.

choices.label: D
choices.text: IT management
choices.justification: IT management identifies the IT resources, servers and infrastructure needed to support the critical business functions as defined by the business process owners.

questions.text: The activation of an enterprise’s business continuity plan (BCP) should be based on predetermined criteria that address the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: duration of the outage.
choices.justification: The initiation of a business continuity plan (BCP) (action) should primarily be based on the maximum period for which a business function can be disrupted before the disruption threatens the achievement of organizational objectives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: type of outage.
choices.justification: The type of outage is not as important to the activation of the plan as the length or duration of the outage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: probability of the outage.
choices.justification: The probability of the outage is relevant to the frequency of incidents, not the need to activate the plan. The plan is designed to be activated after an event of a certain duration occurs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: cause of the outage.
choices.justification: The cause of the outage may affect the response plan to be activated, but not the decision to activate the plan. The plan will be activated any time an event of a predetermined duration occurs.

questions.text: Which of the following ensures the availability of transactions in the event of a disaster?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Send hourly backups containing transactions offsite.
choices.justification: Sending hourly backups containing transactions offsite is not in real time and, therefore, can result in the loss of one hour of transactional data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Send daily backups containing transactions offsite.
choices.justification: Sending daily backups containing transactions offsite is not in real time and, therefore, can result in the loss of one day’s worth of transactional data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capture transactions to multiple storage devices.
choices.justification: Capturing transactions to multiple storage devices does not ensure availability at an offsite location.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transmit transactions offsite in real time.
choices.justification: The only way to ensure the availability of all transactions is to perform a real-time transmission to an offsite facility.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor notes during an audit that an organization’s business continuity plan (BCP) does not adequately address information confidentiality during the recovery process. The IS auditor should recommend that the plan be modified to include:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: the level of information security required when business recovery procedures are invoked.
choices.justification: Clearly define the level of information security required when invoking business recovery procedures to ensure confidentiality is maintained.

choices.label: B
choices.text: information security roles and responsibilities in the crisis management structure.
choices.justification: During a time of crisis, the security needs of the organization may increase because many usual controls such as segregation/separation of duties (SoD) are missing. Having security roles in the crisis management plan is important, but that is not the best answer to this scenario.

choices.label: C
choices.text: information security resource requirements.
choices.justification: Identifying the resource requirements for information security, as part of the business continuity plan (BCP), is important, but it is more important to set out the security levels that are required for protected information.

choices.label: D
choices.text: change management procedures for information security that can affect business continuity arrangements.
choices.justification: Change management procedures can help keep a BCP up to date but are not relevant to this scenario.

questions.text: Which of the following activities should the business continuity manager perform FIRST after the replacement of hardware at the primary information processing facility (IPF)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verify compatibility with the hot site.
choices.justification: Before validating that the new hardware is compatible with the recovery site, the business continuity manager should update the listing of all equipment and IT assets included in the business continuity plan (BCP).

choices.label: B
choices.text: the implementation report .
choices.justification: The implementation report is of limited value to the business continuity manager because the equipment has been installed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform a walk-through of the disaster recovery plan (DRP).
choices.justification: The walk-through of the plan should only be done after the asset inventory has been updated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Update the IT assets inventory .
choices.justification: An IT assets inventory is the basic input for the BCP/disaster recovery plan (DRP), and the plan must be updated to reflect changes in the IT infrastructure.

questions.text: The BEST method for assessing the effectiveness of a business continuity plan (BCP) is to review the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: plans and compare them to appropriate standards.
choices.justification: Comparisons to standards gives some assurance that the plan addresses the critical aspects of a business continuity plan (BCP) but does not reveal anything about its effectiveness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: results from previous tests.
choices.justification: Previous test results provide evidence of the effectiveness of the BCP.

choices.label: C
choices.text: emergency procedures and employee training.
choices.justification: emergency procedures provides insight into some aspects of the plan but falls short of providing assurance of the plan's overall effectiveness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: offsite storage and environmental controls.
choices.justification: offsite storage and environmental controls provide insight into some aspects of the plan but fall short of providing assurance of the plan overall effectiveness.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor can verify that an organization’s business continuity plan (BCP) is effective by reviewing the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: alignment of the BCP with industry good practices.
choices.justification: Alignment of the business continuity plan (BCP) with industry good practices does not provide the assurance of the effectiveness of the BCP.

choices.label: B
choices.text: results of business continuity tests performed by information systems (IS) and end-user personnel.
choices.justification: The effectiveness of the BCP can best be evaluated by reviewing the results from previous business continuity tests for thoroughness and accuracy in accomplishing their stated objectives.

choices.label: C
choices.text: offsite facility, its contents, security and environmental controls.
choices.justification: The offsite facility, its contents, security and environmental controls do not provide assurance of the effectiveness of the BCP. Only testing will provide an accurate assessment of the effectiveness of the BCP.

choices.label: D
choices.text: annual financial cost of the BCP activities versus the expected benefit of the implementation of the plan.
choices.justification: The annual financial cost of the BCP activities versus the expected benefit of implementation of the plan does not provide assurance of the effectiveness of the BCP. Only testing will provide an accurate assessment of the effectiveness of the BCP.

questions.text: The PRIMARY objective of business continuity and disaster recovery plans should be to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: safeguard critical information systems (IS) assets.
choices.justification: Safeguarding critical information systems (IS) assets is a secondary objective of a business continuity and disaster recovery plan. The first priority is always life safety.

choices.label: B
choices.text: provide for continuity of operations.
choices.justification: Providing continuity of operations is a secondary objective of a business continuity and disaster recovery plan. The first priority is always life safety.

choices.label: C
choices.text: minimize the loss to an organization.
choices.justification: Minimizing the loss to an organization is a secondary objective of a business continuity and disaster recovery plan. The first priority is always life safety.

choices.label: D
choices.text: protect human life.
choices.justification: Because human life is invaluable, the main priority of any business continuity and disaster recovery plan should be to protect people.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST method to ensure that the business continuity plan (BCP) remains up to date?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: The group walks through the different scenarios of the plan from beginning to end.
choices.justification: A structured walk-through test gathers representatives from each department who will review the plan and identify weaknesses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The group ensures that specific systems can actually perform adequately at the alternate offsite facility.
choices.justification: The ability of the group to ensure that specific systems can actually perform adequately at the alternate offsite facility is a parallel test and does not involve group meetings.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The group is aware of full-interruption test procedures.
choices.justification: Group awareness of full-interruption test procedures is the most intrusive test to regular operations and the business.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Interdepartmental communication is promoted to better respond in the case of a disaster.
choices.justification: Although improving communication is important, it is not the most valued method to ensure that the plan is up to date.

questions.text: An organization has just completed its annual risk assessment. Regarding the business continuity plan (BCP), what should an information systems (IS) auditor recommend as the next step for the organization?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: and evaluate the BCP for adequacy.
choices.justification: The business continuity plan (BCP) should be reviewed every time a risk assessment is completed for the organization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform a full simulation of the BCP.
choices.justification: Performing a simulation should be completed after the BCP has been deemed adequate for the organization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Train and educate employees regarding the BCP.
choices.justification: Training of the employees should be performed after the BCP has been deemed adequate for the organization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Notify critical contacts in the BCP.
choices.justification: There is no reason to notify the BCP contacts at this time.

questions.text: An offsite information processing facility (IPF) with electrical wiring, air conditioning and flooring, but no computer or communications equipment, is a:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: cold site.
choices.justification: A cold site is ready to receive equipment but does not offer any components at the site in advance of the need.

choices.label: B
choices.text: warm site.
choices.justification: A warm site is an offsite backup facility that is partially configured with network connections and selected peripheral equipment—such as disk units, controllers and central processing units— to operate an information processing facility (IPF).

choices.label: C
choices.text: dial-up site.
choices.justification: A dial-up site is used for remote access, but not for offsite information processing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: duplicate processing facility.
choices.justification: A duplicate IPF is a dedicated, fully developed recovery site that can back up critical applications.

questions.text: When developing a business continuity plan (BCP), which of the following tools should be used to gain an understanding of the organization’s business processes?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Business continuity self-audit
choices.justification: Business continuity self-audit is a tool for evaluating the adequacy of the business continuity plan (BCP) but not for gaining an understanding of the business.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Resource recovery analysis
choices.justification: Resource recovery analysis is a tool for identifying the components necessary for a business resumption strategy but not for gaining an understanding of the business.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk assessment
choices.justification: Risk assessment and business impact assessment are tools for understanding the business as a part of a BCP.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gap analysis
choices.justification: The role that gap analysis can play in a BCP is to identify deficiencies in a plan but not to gain an understanding of the business.

questions.text: A financial institution that processes millions of transactions each day has a central communications processor (switch) for connecting to automated teller machines. Which of the following is the BEST contingency plan for the communications processor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reciprocal agreement with another organization
choices.justification: Reciprocal agreements make an organization dependent on the other organization and raise privacy, competition and regulatory issues.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alternate processor in the same location
choices.justification: Having an alternate processor in the same location resolves the equipment problem but is not effective if the failure is caused by environmental conditions (e.g., power disruption).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alternate processor at another network node
choices.justification: The unavailability of the central communications processor disrupts all access to the banking network. This can be caused by an equipment, power or communications failure. Having a duplicate processor in another location that can be used for alternate processing is the best solution.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Duplex communication links
choices.justification: The installation of duplex communication links is only appropriate if the failure is limited to the communication link.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST way to ensure that incident response activities are consistent with the requirements of business continuity?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Draft and publish a clear practice for enterprise-level incident response.
choices.justification: Publishing an enterprise-level incident response plan is effective only if business continuity aligned itself to incident response. Incident response supports business continuity, not the other way around.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Establish a cross-departmental working group to share perspectives.
choices.justification: Sharing perspectives is valuable, but a working group does not necessarily lead to ensuring that the interface between plans is workable.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Develop a scenario and perform a structured walk-through.
choices.justification: A structured walk-through including both incident response and business continuity personnel provides the best opportunity to identify gaps or misalignments between the plans.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Develop a project plan for end-to-end testing of disaster recovery.
choices.justification: A project plan developed for disaster recovery does not necessarily address deficiencies in business continuity or incident response.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: When reviewing an enterprise’s preventive maintenance process for systems at a data center, what is the MOST important practice that should be in place for an information systems (IS) auditor to be able to ensure that adequate maintenance is being performed on all critical computing, power and cooling systems?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proper background checks on all service personnel are conducted.
choices.justification: Although the trustworthiness of the service personnel is important, it is normal practice for these individuals to be escorted and supervised by the data center personnel. It is also expected that the service provider performs this background check, not the customer.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Service personnel are escorted at all times when performing their work.
choices.justification: Escorting service personnel is common and a good practice, but the greater risk in this case is if work is performed during critical processing times.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maintenance is scheduled during noncritical processing times.
choices.justification: The biggest risk to normal operations in a data center is if an incident or mishap were to happen during critical peak processing times; therefore, it is prudent to ensure that no type of system maintenance is performed at these critical times.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Verification of maintenance being performed is done independently.
choices.justification: Independent verification confirms documented maintenance activities, ensuring their effectiveness in sustaining critical systems. It provides assurance and detects any maintenance process gaps or deficiencies.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor has been tasked by an automated manufacturing facility for the risk-based audit of its distributed control supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) systems. Which of the following should be the PRIMARY task for the auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the role and/or impact of automatization and/or decision-making systems for an organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evaluation of communication architecture and connectivity interfaces
choices.justification: Communication channels over the Internet and even USB interfaces in air-gapped systems increase the risk of malware exposure (e.g., Stuxnet).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evaluation of functioning of monitoring terminals, sensors and actuators
choices.justification: Monitors, sensors and actuator evaluation is not of the same gravity in risk-based audit as communication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assessment of total cost of ownership (TCO) for the SCADA systems
choices.justification: Evaluation of total cost of ownership (TCO)/cost-effectiveness is not the primary concern to an information systems (IS) auditor compared to malware exposure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evaluation of usability of automated controls for enterprise engineers
choices.justification: Usability evaluation is of lesser evaluation in the risk-based audit.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST important when an operating system (OS) patch is to be applied to a production environment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Successful regression testing by the developer
choices.justification: Although testing is important for any patch, in this case, it should be assumed that the operating system (OS) vendor tested the patch before releasing it. Before this OS patch is put into production, the organization should do system testing to ensure that no issues will occur.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Approval from the information asset owner
choices.justification: It is most important that information owners approve any changes to production systems to ensure that no serious business disruption takes place as the result of the patch release.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Approval from the security officer
choices.justification: The security officer does not normally need to approve every OS patch.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patch installation at alternate sites
choices.justification: Security patches need to be deployed consistently across the organization, including alternate sites. However, approval from the information asset owner is still the most important consideration.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST ensures the integrity of a server’s operating system?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protecting the server in a secure location
choices.justification: Protecting the server in a secure location is a good practice, but it does not ensure that a user will not try to exploit logical vulnerabilities and compromise the operating system (OS).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Setting a boot password
choices.justification: Setting a boot password is a good practice but does not ensure that a user will not try to exploit logical vulnerabilities and compromise the OS.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hardening the server configuration
choices.justification: Hardening a system means to configure it in the most secure manner (i.e., install latest security patches, properly define access authorization for users and administrators, disable insecure options and uninstall unused services) to prevent nonprivileged users from gaining the right to execute privileged instructions and, thus, take control of the entire machine, jeopardizing the integrity of the OS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implementing activity logging
choices.justification: Activity logging has two weaknesses in this scenario—it is a detective control (not a preventive one), and the attacker who already gained privileged access can modify logs or disable them.

questions.text: When reviewing a hardware maintenance program, an information systems (IS) auditor should assess whether:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: the schedule of all unplanned maintenance is maintained.
choices.justification: Unplanned maintenance cannot be scheduled.

choices.label: B
choices.text: it is in line with historical trends.
choices.justification: Hardware maintenance programs do not necessarily need to be in line with historic trends.

choices.label: C
choices.text: it has been approved by the IS steering committee.
choices.justification: Maintenance schedules normally are not approved by the steering committee.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the program is validated against vendor specifications.
choices.justification: Although maintenance requirements vary based on complexity and performance workloads, a hardware maintenance schedule should be validated against the vendor-provided specifications.

questions.text: The GREATEST advantage of using web services for the exchange of information between two systems is:
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: secure communication.
choices.justification: Communication is not necessarily more secure using web services.

choices.label: B
choices.text: improved performance.
choices.justification: The use of web services does not necessarily increase performance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: efficient interfacing.
choices.justification: Web services facilitate the interoperable exchange of information between two systems regardless of the operating system or programming language used.

choices.label: D
choices.text: enhanced documentation.
choices.justification: There is no documentation benefit in using web services.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor examining the security configuration of an operating system (OS) should review the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: transaction logs.
choices.justification: Transaction logs are used to track and analyze transactions related to an application or system interface, but that is not the primary source of audit evidence in an operating system (OS) audit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: authorization tables.
choices.justification: Authorization tables are used to verify implementation of logical access controls and will not be of much help when reviewing control features of an OS.

choices.label: C
choices.text: parameter settings.
choices.justification: Configuration parameters allow a standard piece of software to be customized for diverse environments and are important in determining how a system runs. The parameter settings should be appropriate to an organization’s workload and control environment. Improper implementation and/or monitoring of OSs can result in undetected errors and corruption of the data being processed, and lead to unauthorized access and inaccurate logging of system usage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: routing tables.
choices.justification: Routing tables do not contain information about the OS and, therefore, provide no information to aid in the evaluation of controls.

questions.text: Which of the following network components is PRIMARILY set up to serve as a security measure by preventing unauthorized traffic between different segments of the network?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Firewalls
choices.justification: Firewall systems are the primary tool that enables an enterprise to prevent unauthorized access between networks. An enterprise may choose to deploy one or more systems that function as firewalls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Routers
choices.justification: Routers can filter packets based on parameters, such as source address, but are not primarily a security tool.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Layer 2 switches
choices.justification: Based on Media Access Control addresses, layer 2 switches separate traffic without determining whether it is authorized or unauthorized traffic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Virtual local area networks (VLANs)
choices.justification: A virtual local area network (VLAN) is a functionality of some switches that allows them to control traffic between different ports even though they are in the same physical local access network. Nevertheless, they do not effectively deal with authorized versus unauthorized traffic.

questions.text: When reviewing the implementation of a local area network, an information systems (IS) auditor should FIRST review the:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: node list.
choices.justification: Verification of nodes from the node list follows the review of the network diagram.

choices.label: B
choices.text: acceptance test report.
choices.justification: The review of the acceptance test report follows the verification of nodes from the node list.

choices.label: C
choices.text: network diagram.
choices.justification: To properly review a local area network implementation, an information systems (IS) auditor should first verify the network diagram to identify risk or single points of failure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: users list.
choices.justification: The users list is reviewed after the acceptance test report.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is evaluating network performance for an organization that is considering increasing its Internet bandwidth due to a performance degradation during business hours. Which of the following is MOST likely the cause of the performance degradation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate risk associated with shadow IT and end-user computing (EUC) to determine effectiveness of compensating controls

choices.label: A
choices.text: Malware on servers
choices.justification: The existence of malware on the organization’s server can contribute to network performance issues, but the degraded performance would not likely be restricted to business hours.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Firewall misconfiguration
choices.justification: Firewall misconfiguration can contribute to network performance issues, but the degraded performance would not likely be restricted to business hours.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased spam received by the email server
choices.justification: The existence of spam on the organization’s email server can contribute to network performance issues, but the degraded performance would not likely be restricted to business hours.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unauthorized network activities
choices.justification: Unauthorized network activities—such as employee use of file or music sharing sites, online gambling or personal email containing large files or photos—can contribute to network performance issues. Because the information systems (IS) auditor found the degraded performance during business hours, this is the most likely cause.

questions.text: Which of the following is a prevalent risk in the development of end-user computing applications?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate risk associated with shadow IT and end-user computing (EUC) to determine effectiveness of compensating controls

choices.label: A
choices.text: Applications may not be subject to testing and IT general controls.
choices.justification: End-user computing is defined as the ability of end users to design and implement their own information system using computer software products. End-user developed applications may not be subjected to an independent outside review by systems analysts and frequently are not created in the context of a formal development methodology. These applications may lack appropriate standards, controls, quality assurance procedures and documentation. A risk of end-user applications is that management may rely on them as much as traditional applications.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Development and maintenance costs may be increased.
choices.justification: End-user computing systems typically result in reduced application development and maintenance costs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Application development time may be increased.
choices.justification: End-user computing systems typically result in a reduced development cycle time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decision-making may be impaired due to diminished responsiveness to requests for information.
choices.justification: End-user computing systems normally increase flexibility and responsiveness to management’s information requests because the system is being developed directly by the user community.

questions.text: An employee received a digital photo frame as a gift and connected it to his/her work PC to transfer digital photos. The PRIMARY risk that this scenario introduces is that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate risk associated with shadow IT and end-user computing (EUC) to determine effectiveness of compensating controls

choices.label: A
choices.text: the photo frame storage media can be used to steal enterprise data.
choices.justification: Although any storage device can be used to steal data, the damage caused by malware can be widespread and severe for the enterprise, which is the more significant risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the drivers for the photo frame may be incompatible and crash the user’s PC.
choices.justification: Although device drivers may be incompatible and crash the user’s PC, the damage caused by malware can be widespread and severe for the enterprise.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the employee may bring inappropriate photographs into the office.
choices.justification: Although inappropriate content can result, the damage caused by malware can be widespread and severe for the enterprise.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the photo frame can be infected with malware.
choices.justification: Any storage device can be a vehicle for infecting other computers with malware. Some devices have been found to be infected in the factory during the manufacturing process. Controls should exist to prohibit employees from connecting any storage media devices to their enterprise-issued PCs.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: A consulting firm created a file transfer protocol (FTP) site for the purpose of receiving financial data and communicated the site’s address, user ID and password to the financial services enterprise in separate email messages. The enterprise is to transmit its data to the FTP site after manually encrypting the data. The information systems (IS) auditor’s GREATEST concern with this process is that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: The users may not remember to manually encrypt the data before transmission.
choices.justification: If the data is not encrypted, an unauthorized external party may download sensitive enterprise data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The site credentials were sent to the financial services enterprise via email.
choices.justification: Although the possibility exists that the logon information was captured from the emails, data should be encrypted, so the theft of the data would not allow the attacker to read them.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Personnel at the consulting firm may obtain access to sensitive data.
choices.justification: Some of the employees at the consulting firm will have access to the sensitive data and the consulting firm must have procedures in place to protect the data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The use of a shared user ID to the FTP site does not allow for user accountability.
choices.justification: Tracing accountability is of minimal concern compared to the compromise of sensitive data.

questions.text: In a distributed system, which of the following BEST allows different components or modules to communicate and coordinate their activities?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Message queue interface
choices.justification: A message queue interface is a specific type of interface that is used for asynchronous communication between applications through message queues, but it is not a general-purpose interface for coordinating activities in a distributed system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Application programming interface (API)
choices.justification: An application programming interface (API) allows components or modules in a distributed system to communicate and coordinate their activities. An API defines a set of rules and protocols that enable the interaction and exchange of data between software components.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Remote procedure call
choices.justification: A Remote Procedure Call (RPC) is a communication protocol that allows a program to execute a procedure or function in a different address space, but it is not specifically focused on coordinating activities in a distributed system.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Communication infrastructure interface
choices.justification: Communication infrastructure interface is an interface between the application platform and the communication infrastructure.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: Which of the following allows software systems or applications to access and interact with a cloud-based infrastructure or service provider?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP)
choices.justification: Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) is a network management protocol used for monitoring and managing network devices, but it is not designed specifically for accessing cloud-based infrastructure or service providers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Remote procedure call
choices.justification: A Remote Procedure Call (RPC) is a communication protocol that allows a program to execute a procedure or function in a different address space, but it is not designed specifically for accessing cloud-based infrastructure or service providers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Circuit-level gateway
choices.justification: A circuit-level gateway is a telecommunication gateway for transferring data from different networks but is not designed specifically for accessing cloud-based infrastructure or service providers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Application programming interface (API)
choices.justification: An application programming interface (API) allows software systems or applications to access and interact with a cloud-based infrastructure or service provider. APIs provide a set of rules and protocols that enable developers to integrate their applications with cloud services, access resources and perform various operations.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: During fieldwork, an information systems (IS) auditor experienced a system crash caused by a security patch installation. To provide reasonable assurance that this event will not recur, the IS auditor should ensure that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: only systems administrators perform the patch process during nonbusiness hours.
choices.justification: Although system administrators normally install patches, it is more important that changes be made according to a formal procedure that includes testing and implementing the change during nonproduction times.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the client’s change management and patching processes have proper controls.
choices.justification: The change management process, which includes procedures regarding implementing changes during production hours, helps to ensure that this type of event does not recur. An information systems (IS) auditor should review the change management process, including patch management procedures, to verify that the process has adequate controls and to make suggestions accordingly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: patches are validated using parallel testing in production.
choices.justification: Although patches normally undergo testing, it is often impossible to test all patches thoroughly. It is more important that changes are made during nonproduction times and that a backout plan is in place in case of problems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: an approval process of the patch, including a risk assessment, is developed.
choices.justification: An approval process alone cannot directly prevent this type of incident from happening. There should be a complete change management process that includes testing, scheduling and approval.

questions.text: During the review of data file change management controls, which of the following BEST helps to decrease the research time needed to investigate exceptions?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: One-for-one checking
choices.justification: One-for-one checking is a control procedure in which an individual document agrees with a detailed listing of documents processed by the system. It would take a long time to complete the research using this procedure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Data file security
choices.justification: Data file security controls prevent access by unauthorized users in their attempt to alter data files. This would not help identify the transactions posted to an account.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transaction logs
choices.justification: Transaction logs generate an audit trail by providing a detailed list of date of input, time of input, user ID, terminal location, etc. Research time can be reduced in investigating exceptions because the review can be performed on the logs rather than on the entire transaction file. Transaction logs also help to determine which transactions have been posted to an account—by a particular individual during a particular period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: File updating and maintenance authorization
choices.justification: File updating and maintenance authorization is a control procedure to update the stored data and ensure accuracy and security of stored data. This does provide evidence regarding the individuals who update the stored data; however, it is not effective in the given situation to determine transactions posted to an account.

questions.text: An organization recently installed a security patch that crashed the production server. To minimize the probability of this occurring again, an information systems (IS) auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: apply the patch according to the patch’s release notes.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor should not apply the patch. That is an administrator responsibility.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ensure that a good change management process is in place.
choices.justification: An IS auditor must review the change management process, including patch management procedures, and verify that the process has adequate controls and make suggestions accordingly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: thoroughly test the patch before sending it to production.
choices.justification: Testing of the patch is the responsibility of the development or production support team, not the auditor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: approve the patch after doing a risk assessment.
choices.justification: The IS auditor is not authorized to approve a patch. That is a responsibility of a steering committee.

questions.text: When auditing a database environment, an information systems (IS) auditor will be MOST concerned if the database administrator (DBA) is performing which of the following functions?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Performing database changes according to change management procedures
choices.justification: Performing database changes according to change management procedures is a normal function of the database administrator (DBA) and is compliant with the organization's procedures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Installing patches or upgrades to the operating system
choices.justification: Installing patches or upgrades to the operating system is a function that should be performed by a systems administrator, not by a DBA. If a DBA is performing this function, there is risk based on inappropriate separation of duties.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sizing table space and consulting on table join limitations
choices.justification: A DBA is expected to support the business by helping design, create and maintain databases and the interfaces to the databases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Performing backup and recovery procedures
choices.justification: The DBA often performs or supports database backup and recovery procedures.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the change management process for an enterprise resource planning application. Which of the following is the BEST method for testing program changes?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Select a sample of change tickets and review them for authorization.
choices.justification: Selecting a sample of change tickets and reviewing them for authorization helps test for authorization controls; however, it does not identify program changes that were made without supporting change tickets.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform a walk-through by tracing a program change from start to finish.
choices.justification: Performing a walk-through assists the information systems (IS) auditor in understanding the process but does not ensure that all changes adhere to the normal process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trace a sample of modified programs to support change tickets.
choices.justification: Tracing modified programs to change tickets is the best way to test change management controls and detect undocumented changes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use query software to analyze all change tickets for missing fields.
choices.justification: Using query software to analyze all change tickets for missing fields does not identify program changes that were made without supporting change tickets.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is asked to audit the change management process for all IT operational systems. Which of the following documents will BEST aid the auditor in defining the scope for the audit project?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enterprise architecture (EA)
choices.justification: The enterprise architecture (EA) document provides the IT-environment information that the information systems (IS) auditor needs to be able to define the scope of the audit on the change management process for all IT systems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Control catalog
choices.justification: The control catalog is required for an auditor to be able to plan the testing of controls, which is the next step after defining scope.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Risk register
choices.justification: The risk register helps the IS auditor to determine the systems to audit, based on the priority of their associated risk and is useful during audit planning but not in defining the scope of the audit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: IT organizational chart
choices.justification: The IT organizational chart helps the IS auditor to understand the process flow, which is useful during audit planning but is not the most helpful in defining the scope of the audit.

questions.text: The purpose of code signing is to provide assurance that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: the software has not been subsequently modified.
choices.justification: Code signing ensures that the executable code came from a reputable source and has not been modified after being signed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the application can safely interface with another signed application.
choices.justification: The signing of code does not ensure that it will integrate with other applications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the signer of the application is trusted.
choices.justification: Code signing provides assurance of the source but does not ensure that the source is trusted. The code signing will, however, ensure that the code has not been modified.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the private key of the signer has not been compromised.
choices.justification: The compromise of the sender’s private key results in a loss of trust and is not the purpose of code signing.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor evaluates the effectiveness of the change management process in an organization. What is the MOST important control that the IS auditor should look for to ensure system availability?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Changes are authorized by IT managers at all times.
choices.justification: Changes are usually required to be signed off by a business analyst, member of the change control board or other authorized representatives, not necessarily by IT management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: User acceptance testing (UAT) is performed and properly documented.
choices.justification: User acceptance testing (UAT) is important but not a critical element of change control and does not usually address the topic of availability as asked in the question.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Test plans and procedures exist and are closely followed.
choices.justification: The most important control for ensuring system availability is to implement a sound test plan and procedures that are followed consistently.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Capacity planning is performed as part of each development project.
choices.justification: While capacity planning should be considered in each development project, it will not ensure system availability, nor is it part of the change control process.

questions.text: Which of the following is a control that can be implemented to reduce risk of internal fraud if application programmers are allowed to move programs into the production environment in a small enterprise?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Post-implementation functional testing
choices.justification: Independent postimplementation testing would not be as effective because the system can be accepted by the end user without detecting the undocumented functionality.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Registration and review of changes
choices.justification: An independent review of the changes to the program in production can identify potential unauthorized changes, versions or functionality that the programmer put into production.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Validation of user requirements
choices.justification: An independent review of user requirements is not as effective, because the system can meet user requirements and still include undocumented functionalities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: User acceptance testing (UAT)
choices.justification: An independent review of user acceptance is not as effective because the system can be accepted by the end users, and the undocumented functionalities can remain undetected.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing the application change management process for a large multinational organization should be MOST concerned when:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: test systems run different configurations than production systems.
choices.justification: Although, ideally, production and test systems should be configured identically, there may be reasons why this does not occur. The more significant concern is whether the configuration management database was not maintained.

choices.label: B
choices.text: change management records are paper based.
choices.justification: Paper-based change management records are inefficient to maintain and not easy to review in large volumes; however, they do not present a concern from a control point of view if they are properly and diligently maintained.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the configuration management database is not maintained.
choices.justification: The configuration management database (CMDB) is used to track configuration item (CI) and the dependencies between them. An out-of-date CMDB in a large multinational organization can result in incorrect approvals being obtained or leave out critical dependencies during the test phase.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the test environment is installed on the production server.
choices.justification: Although it is not ideal to have the test environment installed on the production server, it is not a control-related concern. If the test and production environments are kept separate, they can be installed on the same physical server(s).

questions.text: During the review of an in-house developed application, the GREATEST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor is if a:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: user raises a change request and tests it in the test environment.
choices.justification: Having a user involved in testing changes is common practice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: programmer codes a change in the development environment and tests it in the test environment.
choices.justification: Having a programmer code a change in development and then separately test the change in a test environment is a good practice and preferable over testing in production.

choices.label: C
choices.text: manager approves a change request and then reviews it in production.
choices.justification: Having a manager review a change to make sure it was done correctly is an acceptable practice.

choices.label: D
choices.text: manager initiates a change request and subsequently approves it.
choices.justification: Initiating and approving a change request by the same person violates the principle of separation of duties, because one should not approve their own requests.

questions.text: Which of the following tests performed by an information systems (IS) auditor is the MOST effective in determining compliance with organizational change control procedures?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: software migration records and verify approvals.
choices.justification: Software migration records may not have all changes listed—changes could have been made that were not included in the migration records.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identify changes that have occurred and verify approvals.
choices.justification: The most effective method is to determine what changes have been made (check logs and modified dates) and then verify that they have been approved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: change control documentation and verify approvals.
choices.justification: Change control records may not have all changes listed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure that only appropriate staff can migrate changes into production.
choices.justification: Ensuring that only appropriate staff can migrate changes into production is a key control process but, alone, does not verify compliance.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor discovers that developers have operator access to the command line of a production environment operating system. Which of the following controls would BEST mitigate the risk of undetected and unauthorized program changes to the production environment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Commands typed on the command line are logged.
choices.justification: Having a log alone is not a control; reviewing the log for command line activities is a control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hash keys are calculated periodically for programs and matched against hash keys calculated for the most recent authorized versions of the programs.
choices.justification: Periodically matching hash keys enables the detection of changes to files.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Access to the operating system command line is granted through an access restriction tool with preapproved rights.
choices.justification: Although access restriction through an access control tool may provide some level of control, the fact that developers already have command line access suggests that this control may be bypassed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Software development tools and compilers have been removed from the production environment.
choices.justification: Removing the tools from the production environment may limit the ability to make unauthorized changes, but it does not address the risk of undetected changes or changes made through other means.

questions.text: A vendor released several critical security patches over the past few months, which put a strain on the ability of the administrators to keep the patches tested and deployed in a timely manner. The administrators asked if they could reduce the testing of the patches. What approach should the organization take?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue the current process of testing and applying patches.
choices.justification: Applying security software patches promptly is critical to maintaining the security of the servers; further, testing the patches is important because the patches may affect other systems and business operations. Because the vendor recently released several critical patches in a short time, the organization may choose to wait to see if this is a temporary problem before making a revision to policy or procedures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduce testing and ensure that an adequate back-out plan is in place.
choices.justification: Reduced testing increases the risk of business operation disruption due to a faulty or incompatible patch. Although a back-out plan does help mitigate this risk, a thorough testing up front is the more appropriate option.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delay patching until resources for testing are available.
choices.justification: Applying security software patches promptly is critical to maintaining the security of the servers. Delaying patching increases the risk of a security breach due to system vulnerability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rely on the vendor’s testing of the patches.
choices.justification: The testing done by the vendor may not be applicable to the systems and environment of the organization that needs to deploy the patches.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor observed that users are occasionally granted the authority to change system data. This elevated system access yet is required for smooth functioning of business operations. Which of the following controls does the IS auditor MOST likely recommend for long-term resolution?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Redesign the controls related to data authorization.
choices.justification: Data authorization controls should be driven by the policy. Although there may be some technical controls that can be adjusted, if the data changes happen infrequently, then an exception process is the better choice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement additional separation of duties controls.
choices.justification: Although adequate separation of duties is important, the information systems (IS) auditor must first review policy to see if there is a formal documented process for this type of temporary access controls to enforce separation of duties.

choices.label: C
choices.text: policy to see if a formal exception process is required.
choices.justification: If the users are granted access to change data in support of the business requirements, the policy should be followed. If there is no policy for the granting of extraordinary access, then one should be designed to ensure that no unauthorized changes are made.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement additional logging controls.
choices.justification: Audit trails are needed whenever temporary elevated access is required. However, this is not the first step the auditor should take in reviewing the overall process.

questions.text: Recovery procedures for an information processing facility (IPF) are BEST based on:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: recovery time objective (RTO)
choices.justification: The recovery time objective (RTO) is the amount of time allowed for the recovery of a business function or resource after a disaster occurs; the RTO is the desired recovery time frame based on maximum tolerable outage (MTO) and available recovery alternatives.

choices.label: B
choices.text: recovery point objective (RPO)
choices.justification: The recovery point objective (RPO) has the greatest influence on the recovery strategies for given data. It is determined based on the acceptable data loss in case of a disruption of operations. The RPO effectively quantifies the permissible amount of data loss in case of interruption.

choices.label: C
choices.text: maximum tolerable outage (MTO)
choices.justification: MTO is the amount of time allowed for the recovery of a business function or resource after a disaster occurs; it represents the time by which the service must be restored before the organization is faced with the threat of collapse.

choices.label: D
choices.text: information security policy.
choices.justification: An information security policy does not address recovery procedures.

questions.text: In a small organization, developers may release emergency changes directly to production. Which of the following will BEST control the risk in this situation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Approve and document the emergency changes promptly after release.
choices.justification: It may be appropriate to allow programmers to make emergency changes if they are documented and approved after the fact.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement a segregated environment for production access and restrict developer access outside of specific time frames.
choices.justification: Limiting developer access to production within specific time frames may help establish some controls, but it does not directly address the risk of unauthorized or unapproved emergency changes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implement a defined emergency change management process that includes secondary approval.
choices.justification: Obtaining secondary approval before releasing to production is not as critical in an emergency situation as documenting the changes promptly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement strict access controls and permissions on the production machine to prevent unauthorized changes.
