choices.justification: A validity check is the most useful check for the verification of passwords because it verifies that the required format has been used—for example, not using a dictionary word and including nonalphabetical characters. An effective password must have several different types of characters: alphabetical, numeric and special.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Field check
choices.justification: The implementation of a field check is not as effective as a validity check that verifies that all password criteria have been met.

questions.text: Which of the following would an information systems (IS) auditor consider to be MOST helpful when evaluating the effectiveness and adequacy of a preventive computer maintenance program?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: System downtime log
choices.justification: A system downtime log provides evidence regarding the effectiveness and adequacy of computer preventive maintenance programs. The log is a detective control, but because it is validating the effectiveness of the maintenance program, it is validating a preventive control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vendor reliability figures
choices.justification: Vendor reliability figures are not an effective measure of a preventive maintenance program.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Regularly scheduled maintenance log
choices.justification: the log is a good detective control to ensure that maintenance is being done; however, only the system downtime will indicate whether the preventive maintenance is actually working well.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Written preventive maintenance schedule
choices.justification: A schedule is a good control to ensure that maintenance is scheduled and that no items are missed in the maintenance schedule; however, it is not a guarantee that the work is actually being done.

questions.text: What is the PRIMARY role of the information systems (IS) auditor in controlling self-assessment programs?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate controls at all stages of the information systems development life cycle

choices.label: A
choices.text: Assessor
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor leads and guides the auditees in assessing their environment by providing insights about the objectives of controls based on risk assessment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Participant
choices.justification: The IS auditor is the facilitator, and the management client participates in the control self-assessment (CSA) program.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Facilitator
choices.justification: When CSA programs are established, auditors become internal control professionals and assessment facilitators.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Manager
choices.justification: The business unit managers take responsibility for and ownership of internal control systems under their authority.

questions.text: During a production system change control audit, an information systems (IS) auditor finds that the change management process is not formally documented and that some migration procedures failed. What should the IS auditor do next?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's enterprise risk management (ERM) program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recommend redesigning the change management process.
choices.justification: Although it may be necessary to redesign the change management process, this cannot be done until a root cause analysis is conducted to determine why the current process is not being followed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gain more assurance on the findings through root cause analysis.
choices.justification: A change management process is critical to IT production systems. Before recommending that the organization take any other action (e.g., stopping migrations or redesigning the change management process), the information systems (IS) auditor should gain assurance that the incidents reported are related to deficiencies in the change management process and not caused by some process other than change management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recommend that program migration be stopped until the change process is documented.
choices.justification: A business relies on being able to make changes when necessary, and security patches must often be deployed promptly. It is not feasible to halt all changes until a new process is developed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Document the finding and present it to management.
choices.justification: The audit results, including the findings of noncompliance, will be delivered to management after a root cause analysis of the issue has been completed.

questions.text: An organization has a business process with a recovery time objective (RTO) equal to zero and a recovery point objective (RPO) close to one minute. This implies that the process can tolerate:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: a data loss of up to one minute, but the processing must be continuous.
choices.justification: The recovery time objective (RTO) measures organization tolerance for downtime, and the recovery point objective (RPO) measures how much data loss can be accepted.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a one-minute processing interruption but cannot tolerate any data loss.
choices.justification: A processing interruption of one minute exceeds the zero RTO set by the organization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a processing interruption of one minute or more.
choices.justification: A processing interruption of one minute or more exceeds the continuous availability requirements of an RTO of zero.

choices.label: D
choices.text: both a data loss and processing interruption longer than one minute.
choices.justification: An RPO of one minute only allows data loss of one minute.

questions.text: The MAIN criterion for determining the severity level of a service disruption incident is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: cost of recovery.
choices.justification: The cost of recovery can be minimal, yet the service downtime can have a major impact.

choices.label: B
choices.text: negative public opinion.
choices.justification: Negative public opinion is a symptom of an incident; it is a factor in determining impact but not the most important one.

choices.label: C
choices.text: geographic location.
choices.justification: Geographic location does not determine the severity of the incident.

choices.label: D
choices.text: downtime.
choices.justification: The longer the period of time a client cannot be serviced, the greater the severity (impact) of the incident.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: Which of the following choices BEST ensures accountability when updating data directly in a production database?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: of audit logs
choices.justification: Detailed audit logs that contain the user ID of the individual who performed the change and the data before and after the change are the best evidence of database changes. A review of these logs identifies the individual who changed the data (ensuring accountability) and the correctness of the change.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Principle of least privilege
choices.justification: Although access to production databases should be controlled by the principle of least privilege, this does not identify who made the change or indicate if the change was made correctly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Approved validation plan
choices.justification: Having an approved validation plan indicates that the change was made correctly but does not show who made the change in production. Only a system-generated audit log can prove accountability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Separation of duties
choices.justification: Separation of duties only ensures that the user making the data change is different than the individual who approved the data change. It does not identify the individual who made the change, nor does it ensure that the data change was correct.

questions.text: A database administrator (DBA) has identified a performance problem with some tables, which can be solved through denormalization. This situation will increase the risk of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: concurrent access.
choices.justification: Denormalization may impact other aspects of database performance or data consistency, but it does not directly increase the risk of concurrent access issues.

choices.label: B
choices.text: deadlocks.
choices.justification: Although denormalization may have performance implications, it is not directly linked to an increased risk of deadlocks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: unauthorized access to data.
choices.justification: Unauthorized access to data is primarily addressed through access controls and security mechanisms, and denormalization does not directly impact this risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a loss of data integrity.
choices.justification: Denormalization compromises the structure that ensures data integrity, leading to an increased risk of data inconsistency and loss of data integrity.

questions.text: During the audit of a database server, which of the following poses the GREATEST security risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: The password on the administrator account does not expire.
choices.justification: A nonexpiring password is a risk and an exposure but not as serious a risk as a weak password or the continued use of default settings.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Default global security settings for the database remain unchanged.
choices.justification: Default security settings for the database can allow issues such as blank user passwords or passwords that are the same as the username. This poses the greatest security risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Old data have not been purged.
choices.justification: Failure to purge old data may present a performance issue and data management concern but is not an immediate security concern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Database activity is not fully logged.
choices.justification: Incomplete logging of database activity is a potential risk but not as serious a risk as default settings.

questions.text: A new business requirement required changing database vendors. Which of the following areas should the information systems (IS) auditor PRIMARILY examine concerning this implementation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Integrity of the data
choices.justification: Critical issues when migrating data from one database to another is the integrity of the data and ensuring that the data are migrated completely and correctly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Timing of the cutover
choices.justification: The timing of the cutover is important, but because the data are being migrated to a new database, duplication should not be an issue.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Authorization level of users
choices.justification: The authorization of the users is not as relevant as the authorization of the application, because the users will interface with the database through an application and not directly interface with the database.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Normalization of the data
choices.justification: Normalization is used to design the database and is not necessarily related to database migration.

questions.text: What is the BEST backup strategy for a large database with data supporting online sales?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Weekly full backup with daily incremental backup
choices.justification: Weekly full backup and daily incremental backup is a poor backup strategy for online transactions. Because this system supports online sales, it can be difficult to recreate lost data and this solution may result in a loss of up to one day’s worth of data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Daily full backup
choices.justification: A full backup normally requires a couple of hours, and, therefore, it can be impractical to conduct a full backup every day.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Clustered servers
choices.justification: Clustered servers provide a redundant processing capability but are not a backup.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mirrored hard disks
choices.justification: Mirrored hard disks ensure that all data are backed up to more than one disk so that a failure of one disk will not result in loss of data.

questions.text: The database administrator (DBA) suggests that database efficiency can be improved by denormalizing some tables. This will result in:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: loss of confidentiality.
choices.justification: Denormalization should not cause loss of confidentiality even though confidential data may be involved. The database administrator (DBA) should ensure that access controls to the databases remain effective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: increased redundancy.
choices.justification: Normalization is a design or optimization process for a relational database that increases redundancy. Redundancy, which is usually considered positive for resource availability, is negative in a database environment because it demands additional and otherwise unnecessary data handling efforts. Denormalization is sometimes advisable for functional reasons.

choices.label: C
choices.text: unauthorized accesses.
choices.justification: Denormalization pertains to the structure of the database, not the access controls. It should not result in unauthorized access.

choices.label: D
choices.text: application malfunctions.
choices.justification: Denormalization may require some changes to the calls between databases and applications but should not cause application malfunctions.

questions.text: Which of the following security measures BEST ensures the integrity of information stored in a data warehouse?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Validated daily backups
choices.justification: Backups address availability, not integrity. Validated backups ensure that the backup will work when needed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Change management procedures
choices.justification: Adequate change management procedures protect the data warehouse and the systems with which the data warehouse interfaces from unauthorized changes but are not usually concerned with the data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Data dictionary maintenance
choices.justification: Data dictionary maintenance procedures provide for the definition and structure of data that are input to the data warehouse. This will not affect the integrity of data already stored.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A read-only restriction
choices.justification: Applying read-only restriction to a data warehouse prevents data manipulation, because most of the data stored there are historical and do not require changes.

questions.text: Which of the following controls provides the GREATEST assurance of database integrity?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Audit log procedures
choices.justification: Audit log procedures enable recording of all events that have been identified and help in tracing the events. However, they only point to the event and do not ensure completeness or accuracy of the database contents.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Table link/reference checks
choices.justification: Performing table link/reference checks serves to detect table linking errors (such as completeness and accuracy of the contents of the database) and, thus, provides the greatest assurance of database integrity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Query/table access time checks
choices.justification: Querying/monitoring table access time checks helps designers improve database performance but not integrity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rollback and rollforward database features
choices.justification: Rollback and rollforward database features ensure recovery from an abnormal disruption. They ensure the integrity of the transaction that was being processed at the time of disruption, but do not provide assurance on the integrity of the contents of the database.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds that a database administrator (DBA) has read-and-write access to production data. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: accept the DBA access as a common practice.
choices.justification: Although granting access to production data to the database administrator (DBA) may be a common practice, the information systems (IS) auditor should evaluate the relevant controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: assess the controls relevant to the DBA function.
choices.justification: When reviewing privileged accounts, the auditor should look for compensating controls that may address a potential exposure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: recommend the immediate revocation of the DBA access to production data.
choices.justification: The DBA should have access based on the principle of least privilege; unless care is taken to validate what access is required, revocation may remove access positively at the DBA requires to do their job.

choices.label: D
choices.text: review user access authorizations approved by the DBA.
choices.justification: Granting user authorizations is the responsibility of the data owner, not the DBA, and access to production data is not generally associated with user access authorizations.

questions.text: During an application audit, an information systems (IS) auditor is asked to provide assurance of the database's referential integrity. Which of the following should be reviewed?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Field definition
choices.justification: Field definitions describe the table layout but are not directly related to referential integrity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Master table definition
choices.justification: Master table definition describes the database structure but is not directly related to referential integrity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Composite keys
choices.justification: Composite keys describe how the keys are created but are not directly related to referential integrity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Foreign key structure
choices.justification: Referential integrity in a relational database refers to consistency between coupled (linked) tables. Referential integrity is usually enforced by the combination of a primary key or candidate key (alternate key) and a foreign key. For referential integrity to hold, any field in a table that is declared a foreign key should contain only values from a parent table’s primary key or a candidate key.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds out-of-range data in some tables of a database. Which of the following controls should the IS auditor recommend to avoid this situation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Log all table update transactions
choices.justification: Logging all table update transactions provides audit trails and is a detective control but will not prevent the introduction of inaccurate data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement integrity constraints in the database
choices.justification: Implementing integrity constraints in the database is a preventive control because data are checked against predefined tables or rules, which prevents any undefined data from being entered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implement before and after image reporting
choices.justification: Before and after image reporting makes it possible to trace the impact that transactions have on computer records and is a detective control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use tracing and tagging
choices.justification: Tracing and tagging is used to test application systems and controls but is not a preventive control that can avoid out-of-range data.

questions.text: During an application audit, the information systems (IS) auditor finds several problems related to corrupt data in the database. Which of the following is a corrective control that the IS auditor should recommend?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Define the standards, and closely monitor them for compliance.
choices.justification: Establishing standards is a preventive control, and monitoring for compliance is a detective control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ensure that only authorized personnel can update the database.
choices.justification: Ensuring that only authorized personnel can update the database is a preventive control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Establish controls to handle concurrent access problems.
choices.justification: Establishing controls to handle concurrent access problems is a preventive control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proceed with restore procedures.
choices.justification: Proceeding with restore procedures is a corrective control. Restore procedures can be used to recover databases to their last-known archived version.

questions.text: Which of the following situations would increase the likelihood of fraud?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Application programmers are implementing changes to data in the production database.
choices.justification: Production programs are used for processing enterprise data. It is imperative that controls on changes to production programs are stringent. Lack of control in this area can result in application programs being modified to manipulate the data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administrators are implementing vendor patches to vendor-supplied software without following change control procedures.
choices.justification: The lack of change control is a serious risk—but if the changes are only vendor-supplied patches to vendor software then the risk is minimal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Operations support staff members are implementing changes to batch schedules.
choices.justification: The implementation of changes to batch schedules by operations support staff affects the scheduling of the batches only; it does not impact the live data unless jobs are run in the wrong sequence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Database administrators (DBA) are implementing changes to data structures.
choices.justification: Database administrators (DBA) are required to implement changes to data structures. This is required for reorganization of the database to allow for additions, modifications or deletions of fields or tables in the database.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing database controls discovered that changes to the database during normal working hours were handled through a standard set of procedures. However, changes made after normal hours required only an abbreviated number of steps. In this situation, which of the following is considered an adequate set of compensating controls?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Allow changes to be made only with the database administrator (DBA) user account.
choices.justification: The use of the database administrator (DBA) user account without logging permits uncontrolled changes to be made to databases after access to the account was obtained.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Make changes to the database after granting access to a normal user account.
choices.justification: A normal user account should not have access to a database. This permits uncontrolled changes to any of the databases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use the DBA user account to make changes, log the changes and review the change log the following day.
choices.justification: The use of a DBA user account is normally set up to log all changes made and is most appropriate for changes made outside of normal hours. The use of a log, which records the changes, allows changes to be reviewed. Because an abbreviated number of steps are used, this represents an adequate set of compensating controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use the normal user account to make changes, log the changes and review the change log the following day.
choices.justification: Users should not be able to make changes. Logging only provides information on changes made but does not limit changes to only those who were authorized.

questions.text: If a database is restored using before-image dumps, where should the process begin following an interruption?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Before the last transaction
choices.justification: When using before images, it is important to note that the last transaction in the dump may not have updated the database before the dump was taken.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After the last transaction
choices.justification: The last transaction will not have updated the database and must be reprocessed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: At the first transaction after the latest checkpoint
choices.justification: Program checkpoints are irrelevant in this situation. Checkpoints are used in application failures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: At the last transaction before the latest checkpoint
choices.justification: Program checkpoints are irrelevant in this situation. Checkpoints are used in application failures.

questions.text: A laptop computer belonging to an enterprise database administrator (DBA) and containing a file of production database passwords has been stolen. What should the organization do FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Send a report to the information systems (IS) audit department.
choices.justification: Although the information systems (IS) audit department should be notified, this should not be the first action.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Change the name of the database administrator (DBA) account.
choices.justification: Changing the database administrator (DBA) account name can impact production database servers and, thus, would not be a good idea.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Suspend the DBA account.
choices.justification: Suspending the DBA account can impact the production database servers and may not be effective if there is more than one DBA account sharing the same database password. The thief may guess the account names of the other DBAs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Change the database password.
choices.justification: The password should be changed immediately because there is no way to know whether it has been compromised.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST effective when determining the correctness of individual account balances migrated from one database to another?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compare the hash total before and after the migration.
choices.justification: The hash total only validates the data integrity at a batch level rather than at a transaction level.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Verify that the number of records is the same for both databases.
choices.justification: Databases are composed of records that can contain multiple fields. The number of records does not allow an information systems (IS) auditor to ascertain whether some of these fields have been successfully migrated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform sample testing of the migrated account balances.
choices.justification: Performing sample testing of the migrated account balances involves the comparison of a selection of individual transactions from the database before and after the migration.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compare the control totals of all of the transactions.
choices.justification: Comparing the control totals does not imply that the records are complete or that individual values are accurate.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing database security for an organization. Which of the following is the MOST important consideration for database hardening?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: The default configurations are changed.
choices.justification: Default database configurations, such as default passwords and services, need to be changed; otherwise, the database can be easily compromised by malicious code and by intruders.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All tables in the database are denormalized.
choices.justification: The denormalization of a database is related more to performance than to security.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stored procedures and triggers are encrypted.
choices.justification: Limiting access to stored procedures is a valid security consideration but not as critical as changing default configurations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The service port used by the database server is changed.
choices.justification: Changing the service port used by the database is a component of the configuration changes that can be made to the database, but other more critical configuration changes should be made first.

questions.text: Which interface allows different software applications to access and manipulate a relational database management system (RDBMS)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Application programming interface (API)
choices.justification: An application programming interface (API) allows different software applications to access and manipulate a relational database management system (RDBMS). The API provides a set of functions, methods or protocols that enable developers to interact with the database, perform queries and manage data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Structured query language (SQL)
choices.justification: Structured Query Language (SQL) is a programming language specifically designed for managing and manipulating relational databases, but it is not an interface. SQL is typically used with an API to interact with the database.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Object-relational mapping
choices.justification: Object-relational mapping is a technique that allows developers to map between objects in an application and relational database tables, but it is not an interface for accessing the RDBMS.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Java database connectivity
choices.justification: Java database connectivity is a Java API that provides methods for connecting to and interacting with a database using the Java programming language, but it is specific to Java and not a general-purpose interface.

questions.text: During a database review, an information systems (IS) auditor notices that some tables in the database are not normalized. The IS auditor should next:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: recommend that the database be normalized.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor should not immediately recommend normalizing the database until further investigation and analysis takes place.

choices.label: B
choices.text: review the conceptual data model.
choices.justification: the conceptual data model may not directly provide information about normalization or the justification for the level of normalization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: review the stored procedures.
choices.justification: the stored procedures may not directly provide information about normalization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: review the justification.
choices.justification: If the database is not normalized, the IS auditor should review the justification because, in some situations, denormalization is recommended for performance reasons.

questions.text: Segmenting a highly sensitive database results in:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: reduced exposure.
choices.justification: Segmenting data reduces the quantity of data exposed to a particular vulnerability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: reduced threat.
choices.justification: The threat may remain constant, but each segment represents a different vector against which it must be directed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: less criticality.
choices.justification: Criticality is a data attribute and is not affected by the manner in which it is segmented.

choices.label: D
choices.text: less sensitivity.
choices.justification: Sensitivity is a data attribute and is not affected by the manner in which it is segmented.

questions.text: Online banking transactions are being posted to the database when processing suddenly comes to a halt. The integrity of the transaction processing is BEST ensured by:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: database integrity checks.
choices.justification: Database integrity checks are important to ensure database consistency and accuracy. These include isolation, concurrency and durability controls, but the most important issue here is atomicity—the requirement for transactions to complete entirely and commit, or else roll back to the last known good point.

choices.label: B
choices.text: validation checks.
choices.justification: Validation checks prevent introduction of corrupt data but do not address system failure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: input controls.
choices.justification: Input controls are important to protect the integrity of input data but do not address system failure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: database commits and rollbacks.
choices.justification: Database commits save completed transaction processing, while rollback reverses partially completed transaction processing if the transaction fails.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds out-of-range data in some tables of a database. Which of the following controls would BEST avoid this situation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Log all table update transactions.
choices.justification: Logging all table update transactions is a detective control that would not help avoid invalid data entry.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement before-and-after image reporting.
choices.justification: Implementing before-and-after image reporting is a detective control that would not help avoid the situation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use tracing and tagging.
choices.justification: Tracing and tagging are used to test application systems and controls and cannot prevent out-of-range data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement integrity constraints in the database.
choices.justification: Implementing integrity constraints in the database is a preventive control because data are checked against predefined tables or rules, preventing any undefined data from being entered.

questions.text: In a relational database with referential integrity, the use of which of the following keys would prevent deletion of a row from a customer table if the customer number of that row is stored with live orders on the orders table?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Foreign key
choices.justification: In a relational database with referential integrity, the use of foreign keys would prevent events such as primary key changes and record deletions, resulting in orphaned relations within the database.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Primary key
choices.justification: It should not be possible to delete a row from a customer table when the customer number (primary key) of that row is stored with live orders on the orders table (the foreign key to the customer table). A primary key works in one table so it is not able to provide/ referential integrity by itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Secondary key
choices.justification: Secondary keys that are not foreign keys are not subject to referential integrity checks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Public key
choices.justification: A public key is related to encryption and not linked in any way to referential integrity.

questions.text: A new database is being set up in an overseas location to provide information to the general public and to increase the speed at which the information is made available. The overseas database is to be housed at a data center and will be updated in real time to mirror the information stored locally. Which of the following areas of operations should be considered as having the HIGHEST risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Confidentiality of the information stored in the database
choices.justification: Confidentiality of the information stored in the database is not a major concern, because the information is intended for public use.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The hardware being used to run the database application
choices.justification: The business objective is to make the information available to the public in a timely manner. Because the database is physically located overseas, hardware failures that are left unfixed can reduce the availability of the system to users.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Backups of the information in the overseas database
choices.justification: Backups of the information in the overseas database are not a major concern, because the overseas database is a mirror of the local database; thus, a backup copy exists locally.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remote access to the backup database
choices.justification: Remote access to the backup database does not impact availability.

questions.text: A database administrator (DBA) who needs to make emergency changes to a database after normal working hours should log in:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether effective processes are in place to support end users

choices.label: A
choices.text: with their named account to make the changes.
choices.justification: Logging in using the named user account before using the database administrator (DBA) account provides accountability by noting the person making the changes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: with the shared DBA account to make the changes.
choices.justification: The DBA account is typically a shared user account. The shared account makes it difficult to establish the identity of the support user who is performing the database update.

choices.label: C
choices.text: to the server administrative account to make the changes.
choices.justification: The server administrative accounts are shared and may be used by multiple support users. In addition, the server privilege accounts may not have the ability to perform database changes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: to the user’s account to make the changes.
choices.justification: The use of a normal user account does not have sufficient privileges to make changes on the database.

questions.text: The information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing an enterprise’s HR database implementation. The IS auditor discovers that the database servers are clustered for high availability, all default database accounts have been removed and database audit logs are kept and reviewed on a weekly basis. What other area should the IS auditor check to ensure that the databases are appropriately secured?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Database administrators are restricted from access to HR data.
choices.justification: Database administrators have access to all data on the server, but there is no practical control to prevent that; therefore, this is not a concern.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Database logs are encrypted.
choices.justification: Database audit logs normally do not contain any confidential data; therefore, encrypting the log files is not required.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Database stored procedures are encrypted.
choices.justification: If a stored procedure contains a security sensitive function, such as encrypting data, it can be a requirement to encrypt the stored procedure. However, this is less critical than ensuring initialization parameters are correct.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Database initialization parameters are appropriate.
choices.justification: When a database is opened, many of its configuration options are governed by initialization parameters. These parameters are usually governed by a file that contains many settings. The system initialization parameters address many global database settings, including authentication, remote access and other critical security areas. To effectively audit a database implementation, the information systems (IS) auditor must examine the database initialization parameters.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor analyzing the audit log of a database management system (DBMS) finds that some transactions were partially executed as a result of an error and have not been rolled back. Which of the following transaction processing features has been violated?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Consistency
choices.justification: Consistency ensures that the database is in a proper state when the transaction begins and ends and that the transaction has not violated integrity rules.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Isolation
choices.justification: Isolation means that, while in an intermediate state, the transaction data are invisible to external operations. This prevents two transactions from attempting to access the same data at the same time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Durability
choices.justification: Durability guarantees that a successful transaction will persist and cannot be undone.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atomicity
choices.justification: Atomicity guarantees that either the entire transaction is processed or none of it is.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds that database administrators (DBAs) have access to the log location on the database server and the ability to purge logs from the system. What is the BEST audit recommendation to ensure that DBA activity is effectively monitored?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Change permissions to prevent DBAs from purging logs.
choices.justification: Changing the database administrator (DBA) permissions to prevent DBAs from purging logs may not be feasible and does not adequately protect the availability and integrity of the database logs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Forward database logs to a centralized log server to which the DBAs do not have access.
choices.justification: To protect the availability and integrity of the database logs, it is most feasible to forward the database logs to a centralized log server to which the DBAs do not have access.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Require that critical changes to the database are formally approved.
choices.justification: Requiring that critical changes to the database are formally approved does not adequately protect the availability and integrity of the database logs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Back up database logs to magnetic media.
choices.justification: Backing up database logs to magnetic media does not adequately protect the availability and integrity of the database logs.

questions.text: The objective of concurrency control in a database system is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: restrict updating of the database to authorized users.
choices.justification: Access controls restrict updating of the database to authorized users.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ensure integrity when two processes attempt to update the same data simultaneously.
choices.justification: Concurrency controls prevent data integrity problems, which can arise when two update processes access the same data item simultaneously.

choices.label: C
choices.text: prevent inadvertent or unauthorized disclosure of data in the database.
choices.justification: Controls, such as passwords, prevent the inadvertent or unauthorized disclosure of data from the database.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ensure the accuracy, completeness, and consistency of data.
choices.justification: Quality controls, such as edits, ensure the accuracy, completeness, and consistency of data maintained in the database.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: What is the MOST effective control for enforcing accountability among database users who are accessing sensitive information?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Implement a log management process.
choices.justification: Accountability means knowing what is being done by whom. The best way to enforce the principle is to implement a log management process that creates and stores logs with pertinent information, such as username, type of transaction and hour.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement a two-factor authentication.
choices.justification: Implementing a two-factor authentication prevents unauthorized access to the database but does not record the activity of the user when using the database.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use table views to access sensitive data.
choices.justification: Using table views restricts users from seeing data that they should not be able to see but does not record what users did with data that they were allowed to see.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Separate database and application servers.
choices.justification: Separating database and application servers may help with better administration or even in implementing access controls but does not address the accountability issues.

questions.text: Which of the following is a MAJOR challenge in implementing a log management system?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Complexity of the IT infrastructure
choices.justification: Addressing the complexity of the IT resources that generate logs is a major challenge because the performance of many resources deteriorates when logging is enabled. Therefore, it is necessary to determine the type of logs to be captured from each resource.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multiple formats of logs collected
choices.justification: The multiplicity of log formats can be challenging; however, this can be addressed using appropriate parsing techniques.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cost of storage due to the volume of logs
choices.justification: Storage cost is a comparatively lesser challenge and can be managed with appropriate budget and retention periods.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Addressing false-positive alerts
choices.justification: Addressing false positives during the initial periods of implementation can be addressed by appropriate planning and implementing tools in a phased manner.

questions.text: Which of the following would an information systems (IS) auditor use to determine if unauthorized modifications were made to production programs?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's management of IT policies and practices, including compliance with legal and regulatory requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: System log analysis
choices.justification: System log analysis identifies changes and activity on a system but does not identify whether the change was authorized unless conducted as a part of a compliance test.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compliance testing
choices.justification: To determine that only authorized modifications are made to production programs requires the change management process to be reviewed to evaluate the existence of a trail of documentary evidence. Compliance testing helps to verify that the change management process has been applied consistently.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Forensic analysis
choices.justification: Forensic analysis is a specialized technique for criminal investigation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Analytical review
choices.justification: An analytical review assesses the general control environment of an organization.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor performing an application maintenance audit would review the log of program changes for the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: authorization of program changes.
choices.justification: The auditor wants to ensure that only authorized changes have been made to the application. The auditor would therefore review the log of program changes to verify that all changes have been approved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: creation date of a current object module.
choices.justification: The creation date of the current object module does not indicate earlier changes to the application.

choices.label: C
choices.text: number of program changes actually made.
choices.justification: The auditor reviews the system to notice the number of changes actually made but then verifies that all the changes were authorized.

choices.label: D
choices.text: creation date of a current source program.
choices.justification: The creation date of the current source program does not identify earlier changes.

questions.text: Which of the following is a MAIN benefit of monitoring operational logs?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: It provides early warnings of possible risk materialization.
choices.justification: Monitoring operational logs provides early warnings about possible risk materialization before it becomes an incident; however, all operational logs do not provide such information.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It helps information systems (IS) auditors in detecting problems encountered during operations.
choices.justification: Auditors use sampling methods to review logs that are identified by certain criteria (e.g., operation termination, login to sensitive accounts and transactions beyond defined thresholds). Monitoring such operational logs helps in detecting problems in the identified area, but the logs are limited and provide limited benefit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It gives insight into the health and performance of the IT infrastructure.
choices.justification: Operational log monitoring primarily helps in providing insight about the IT infrastructure and provides data to build performance metrics for various IT resources (e.g., central processing unit (CPU) performance, storage capacity usage, network bandwidth usage and maximum number of transactions).

choices.label: D
choices.text: It provides audit trails for transactions processed during operations.
choices.justification: Audit trails are more useful for ensuring appropriate processing of a transaction and they may not identify problems in processing.

questions.text: Which of the following activities performed by a database administrator (DBA) should be performed by a different person?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deleting database activity logs
choices.justification: Because database activity logs record activities performed by the database administrator (DBA), deleting them should be performed by an individual other than the DBA. This is a compensating control to aid in ensuring an appropriate separation of duties and is associated with the DBA’s role.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implementing database optimization tools
choices.justification: Implementing database optimization tools is part of the DBA’s normal job function.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Monitoring database usage
choices.justification: Monitoring database usage is part of the DBA’s normal job function.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defining backup and recovery procedures
choices.justification: Defining backup and recovery procedures is part of the DBA’s normal job function.

questions.text: Which of the following should the information systems (IS) auditor review to ensure the correct version of a data file is used for a production run?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Incident or error reports related to the production run
choices.justification: Operator problem reports may provide information about errors or issues encountered during the production run, which can be useful for identifying potential data file version problems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Schedules detailing the tasks performed by operators
choices.justification: Operator work schedules are not directly relevant to verifying the correct version of a data file for a production run.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Logs containing relevant system activity and events
choices.justification: System logs and analysis programs can be used by an information systems (IS) auditor to ensure that the correct file version was used for a production run.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reports documenting the distribution of the produced output
choices.justification: Output distribution reports primarily focus on identifying and distributing application reports and may not directly contribute to verifying the correct version of a data file used for a production run.

questions.text: The reliability of an application system’s audit trail may be questionable if:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: user IDs are recorded in the audit trail.
choices.justification: An audit trail must record the identity of the person or process involved in the logged activity to establish accountability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the security administrator has read-only rights to the audit file.
choices.justification: Restricting the administrator to read-only access will protect the audit file from alteration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: date and time stamps are recorded when an action occurs.
choices.justification: Data and time stamps should be recorded in the logs to enable the reconstruction and correlation of events on multiple systems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: users can amend audit trail records when correcting system errors.
choices.justification: An audit trail is not effective if the details in it can be amended.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: During a disaster recovery test, an information systems (IS) auditor observes that the performance of the disaster recovery site’s server is slow. To find the root cause of this, the IS auditor should FIRST review the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: event error log generated at the disaster recovery site.
choices.justification: If the issue cannot be clarified, the information systems (IS) auditor should then review the event error log.

choices.label: B
choices.text: disaster recovery test plan.
choices.justification: The disaster recovery test plan does not identify any issues related to system performance unless the test was poorly designed and inefficient, but that would come after checking the configuration.

choices.label: C
choices.text: disaster recovery plan (DRP).
choices.justification: the disaster recovery plan (DRP) is unlikely to provide any information about system performance issues.

choices.label: D
choices.text: configurations and alignment of the primary and disaster recovery sites.
choices.justification: Because the configuration of the system is the most probable cause, the IS auditor should review that first.

questions.text: To address an organization’s disaster recovery requirements, backup intervals should not exceed the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: service level objective.
choices.justification: Organizations try to set service level objectives to meet established business targets. The resulting time for the service level objective relates to recovery of service, not to recovery of data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: recovery time objective (RTO).
choices.justification: The recovery time objective (RTO) defines the time period after the disaster in which normal business functionality needs to be restored.

choices.label: C
choices.text: maximum acceptable outage (MAO).
choices.justification: The maximum acceptable outage (MAO) is the maximum amount of system downtime that is tolerable. It can be used as a synonym for maximum tolerable period of disruption or maximum allowable downtime. However, the RTO denotes an objective/target, while the MAO constitutes a vital necessity for an organization’s survival.

choices.label: D
choices.text: recovery point objective (RPO).
choices.justification: Recovery point objective (RPO) defines the point in time to which data must be restored after a disaster to resume processing transactions. Backups should be performed in a way that the latest backup is no older than this maximum time frame. If the backups are not done frequently enough, then too much data are likely to be lost.

questions.text: In a disaster recovery situation, which of the following is the MOST important metric to ensure that data are synchronized between critical systems?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: recovery point objective (RPO)
choices.justification: Establishing a common recovery point objective (RPO) is most critical for ensuring that interdependencies between systems are properly synchronized. It ensures that systems do not contain data from different points in time that may result in accounting transactions that cannot be reconciled and a loss of referential integrity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: recovery time objective (RTO)
choices.justification: recovery time objectives (RTOs) are not as important to synchronize because they normally vary depending on the level of effort and resources required to restore a system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: recovery service resilience
choices.justification: Recovery service resilience measures the fault tolerance due to data exceptions and ability to restart and recover from internal failures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: recovery service scalability
choices.justification: Recovery service scalability refers to the capacity constraints and limitations that a recovery solution may have relative to the original system configuration.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the most recent disaster recovery plan of an organization. Which approval is the MOST important when determining the availability of system resources required for the plan?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Executive management
choices.justification: Although executive management’s approval is essential, the IT department is responsible for managing system resources and their availability as related to disaster recovery (DR).

choices.label: B
choices.text: IT management
choices.justification: Because a disaster recovery plan (DRP) is based on the recovery and provisioning of IT services, IT management’s approval is most important to verify that the system resources will be available in the event that a disaster event is triggered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Board of directors
choices.justification: The board of directors may review and approve the DRP, but the IT department is responsible for managing system resources and their availability as related to DR.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Steering committee
choices.justification: The steering committee determines the requirements for DR (recovery time objective and recovery point objective); however, the IT department is responsible for managing system resources and their availability as related to DR.

questions.text: During an information systems (IS) audit of the disaster recovery plan of a global enterprise, the auditor observes that some remote offices have very limited local IT resources. Which of the following observations is the MOST critical for the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: A test has not been made to ensure that local resources can maintain security and service standards when recovering from a disaster or incident.
choices.justification: Regardless of the capability of local IT resources, the most critical risk is the lack of testing that would identify quality issues in the recovery process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The corporate business continuity plan (BCP) plan does not accurately document the systems that exist at remote offices.
choices.justification: The corporate business continuity plan (BCP) may not include disaster recovery plan (DRP) details for remote offices. It is important to ensure that the local plans have been tested.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Corporate security measures have not been incorporated into the test plan.
choices.justification: Security is an important issue because many controls may be missing during a disaster. However, not having a tested plan is more important.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A test has not been made to ensure that backups from the remote offices are usable.
choices.justification: The backups cannot be trusted until they have been tested. However, this should be done as part of the overall tests of the DRP.

questions.text: What component requires frequent updating to ensure the continued effectiveness of a disaster recovery plan (DRP)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contact information of key personnel
choices.justification: In the event of a disaster, it is important to have a current updated list of personnel who are key to the operation of the plan.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Server inventory documentation
choices.justification: Asset inventory is important and should be linked to the change management process of the organization, but having access to key people may compensate for outdated records.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Individual roles and responsibilities
choices.justification: Individual roles and responsibilities are important, but, in a disaster, many people can fill different roles depending on their experience.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Procedures for declaring a disaster
choices.justification: The procedures for declaring a disaster are important because they can affect response, customer perception and regulatory issues, but not as important as having the right people there when needed.

questions.text: When developing a disaster recovery plan (DRP), what criteria should be considered when determining the acceptable downtime?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Annual loss expectancy
choices.justification: The acceptable downtime is not determined by the annual loss expectancy (ALE); ALE is related to risk management calculations, not disaster recovery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Service delivery objective
choices.justification: The service delivery objective is relevant to business continuity, but it is not determined by acceptable downtime.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Quantity of orphan data
choices.justification: The quantity of orphan data is relevant to business continuity, but it is not determined by acceptable downtime.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Maximum tolerable outage (MTO)
choices.justification: Recovery time objective (RTO) is determined based on the acceptable downtime in the case of a disruption of operations. It indicates the maximum tolerable outage (MTO) that an organization considers to be acceptable before a system or process must resume following a disaster.

questions.text: Which of the following disaster recovery testing techniques is the MOST efficient way to determine the effectiveness of the plan?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preparedness tests
choices.justification: Preparedness tests involve simulation of the entire environment (in phases) at a relatively low cost and help the team to better understand and prepare for the actual test scenario.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paper tests
choices.justification: Paper tests in a walk-through test the entire plan, but there is no simulation and less is learned. It also is difficult to obtain evidence that the team has understood the test plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Full operational tests
choices.justification: Full operational tests require approval from management, are not easy or practical to test in most scenarios and may trigger a real disaster.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Actual service disruption
choices.justification: An actual service disruption is not recommended in most cases unless required by regulation or policy.

questions.text: An organization with a number of offices across a wide geographical area has developed a disaster recovery plan (DRP). Using actual resources, which of the following is the MOST cost-effective test of the DRP?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Full operational test
choices.justification: A full operational test is conducted after the paper and preparedness test and is quite expensive.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Preparedness test
choices.justification: A preparedness test offers a more practical approach by assessing the readiness of actual resources and systems. It provides a higher level of confidence that the organization's critical components are prepared for recovery without incurring the potential disruptions and costs associated with a full operational test.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Paper test
choices.justification: A paper test is a structured walk-through of the disaster recovery plan (DRP) and should be conducted before a preparedness test, but a paper test (desk check) is not sufficient to test the viability of the plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regression test
choices.justification: A regression test is not a DRP test and is used in software development and maintenance.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is auditing an IT disaster recovery plan (DRP). The IS auditor should PRIMARILY ensure that the plan covers:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: a resilient IT infrastructure.
choices.justification: A resilient IT infrastructure is typically required to minimize interruptions to IT services; however, if a critical business function does not require high availability of IT, this may not be required for all disaster recovery plan (DRP) elements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: alternate site information.
choices.justification: Although the selection of an alternate site is important, the more critical issue is the prioritization of resources based on impact and recovery time objective (RTO) of business functions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: documented disaster recovery test results.
choices.justification: Documented DRP test results are helpful when maintaining the DRP; however, the DRP must first and foremost be aligned with business requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: analysis and prioritization of business functions.
choices.justification: The DRP must primarily focus on recovering critical business functions in the event of disaster within predefined RTOs; thus, it is necessary to align the recovery of IT services based on the criticality of business functions.

questions.text: What is the PRIMARY risk of using reciprocal agreements for disaster recovery between two business units in an organization?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: The documents lack legal sufficiency.
choices.justification: Inadequate agreements between two business units is a risk, but generally a lesser one than the risk that both organizations will suffer a disaster at the same time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both entities are vulnerable to the same incident.
choices.justification: The use of reciprocal disaster recovery is based on the probability that both organizations will not suffer a disaster at the same time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IT systems have non-identical configurations.
choices.justification: Although incompatible IT systems can create problems, it is a less significant risk than both organizations suffering from the same disaster at the same time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: One party experiences more frequent disruptions than the other.
choices.justification: Although one party may use the other’s resources more frequently, this can be addressed by contractual provisions and is not a major risk.

questions.text: Which of the following choices would MOST likely ensure that a disaster recovery effort is successful?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: The tabletop test was performed.
choices.justification: Performing a tabletop test is extremely helpful but does not ensure that the recovery process is working properly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Data restoration was completed.
choices.justification: The most reliable method to determine whether a backup is valid is to restore it to a system. A data restore test should be performed at least annually to verify that the process is working properly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recovery procedures are approved.
choices.justification: Approved recovery procedures do not ensure that data can be successfully restored.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Appropriate staff resources are committed.
choices.justification: Although having appropriate staff resources is appropriate, without data, the recovery will not be successful.

questions.text: After a disaster declaration, the media creation date at a warm recovery site is based on the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: recovery point objective (RPO)
choices.justification: The recovery point objective (RPO) defines the acceptable data loss in a disruption, indicating the earliest acceptable data recovery point. It quantifies the permissible data loss in case of interruption. The media creation date reflects the RPO for data restoration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: recovery time objective (RTO)
choices.justification: The recovery time objective (RTO) is the amount of time allowed for the recovery of a business function or resource after a disaster occurs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: service delivery objective (SDO)
choices.justification: The service delivery objective (SDO) is directly related to the business needs and is the level of service to be reached during the alternate process mode until the normal situation is restored.

choices.label: D
choices.text: maximum tolerable outage (MTO)
choices.justification: The maximum tolerable outage (MTO) is the maximum time that an organization can support processing in alternate mode.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST reason for integrating the testing of noncritical systems in the disaster recovery plan (DRP) with the business continuity plan (BCP)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: To ensure that the DRPs is aligned to the business impact analysis (BIA).
choices.justification: The disaster recovery plan (DRP) should be aligned with the business impact analysis (BIA); however, this has no impact on integrating the testing of noncritical systems in the DRPs with the business continuity plan (BCP).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infrastructure recovery personnel can be assisted by business subject matter experts.
choices.justification: Infrastructure personnel will be focused on restoring the various platforms that make up the infrastructure, and it is not necessary for business subject matter experts to be involved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The BCPs may assume the existence of capabilities that are not in the DRPs.
choices.justification: The BCPs may assume the existence of capabilities that are not part of the DRPs, such as allowing employees to work from home during the disaster; however, IT may not have made sufficient provisions for these capabilities (e.g., they cannot support a large number of employees working from home). Although the noncritical systems are important, it is possible that they are not part of the DRPs. For example, an organization may use an online system that does not interface with the internal systems. If the business function using the system is a critical process, the system should be tested, and it may not be part of the DRP. Therefore, DRP and BCP testing should be integrated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To provide business executives with knowledge of disaster recovery capabilities.
choices.justification: Although business executives may be interested in the benefits of disaster recovery, testing is not the best way to accomplish this task.

questions.text: Of the following alternatives, the FIRST approach to developing a disaster recovery strategy is to assess whether:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's problem and incident management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: all threats can be completely removed.
choices.justification: It is impossible to remove all existing and future threats.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a cost-effective, built-in resilience can be implemented.
choices.justification: It is critical to initially identify information assets that can be made more resilient to disasters (e.g., diverse routing, alternate paths or multiple communication carriers). Preventing a problem is always better than planning to address a problem when it happens.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the recovery time objective (RTO) can be optimized.
choices.justification: The optimization of the recovery time objective (RTO) comes later in the development of the disaster recovery strategy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the cost of recovery can be minimized.
choices.justification: Efforts to minimize the cost of recovery come later in the development of the disaster recovery strategy.

questions.text: Which of the following would an information systems (IS) auditor consider to be the MOST important to review when conducting a disaster recovery audit?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hot site is contracted for and available as needed.
choices.justification: A hot site is important, but it is of no use if there are no data backups for it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A business continuity manual is available and current.
choices.justification: A business continuity manual is advisable but not most important in a disaster recovery audit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Insurance coverage is adequate and premiums are current.
choices.justification: Insurance coverage should be adequate to cover costs but is not as important as having the data backup.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Data backups are performed timely and stored offsite.
choices.justification: Without data to process, all other components of the recovery effort are of no value. Even in the absence of a plan, recovery efforts of any type would not be practical without data to process.

questions.text: Management considered two projections for its disaster recovery plan (DRP): plan A with two months to fully recover and plan B with eight months to fully recover. The recovery point objectives (RPOs) are the same in both plans. It is reasonable to expect that plan B projected higher:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: downtime costs.
choices.justification: Because management considered a longer time window for recovery in plan B, downtime costs included in the plan are likely to be higher.

choices.label: B
choices.text: resumption costs.
choices.justification: Because the recovery time for plan B is longer, resumption costs can be expected to be lower.

choices.label: C
choices.text: recovery costs.
choices.justification: Because the recovery time for plan B is longer, recovery costs can be expected to be lower.

choices.label: D
choices.text: walk-through costs.
choices.justification: Walk-through costs are not a part of disaster recovery.

questions.text: A large chain of shops with electronic funds transfer at point-of-sale devices has a central communications processor for connecting to the banking network. Which of the following is the BEST disaster recovery plan (DRP) for the communications processor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the readiness of information systems for implementation and migration into production

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offsite storage of daily backups
choices.justification: Offsite storage of backups does not help, because electronic funds transfer tends to be an online process and offsite storage will not replace the dysfunctional processor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alternative standby processor onsite
