choices.justification: The provision of an alternate processor onsite is fine for an equipment problem but does not help in the case of a power outage and may require technical expertise to cutover to the alternate equipment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Installation of duplex communication links
choices.justification: Installation of duplex communication links is most appropriate if only the communication link fails.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alternative standby processor at another network node
choices.justification: Having an alternative standby processor at another network node is the best solution. The unavailability of the central communications processor disrupts all access to the banking network, disrupting operations for all of the shops. This can be caused by failure of equipment, power or communications.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST helps define disaster recovery strategies?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Annual loss expectancy and exposure factor
choices.justification: Annual loss expectancy and exposure factor are more related to risk in general.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maximum tolerable downtime and data loss
choices.justification: Two of the key outcomes of the business impact analysis are the recovery time objective (RTO) and recovery point objective (RPO)—maximum tolerable downtime and data loss—that further help in identifying the recovery strategies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Existing server and network redundancies
choices.justification: Existing server and network redundancies are good to know, but the RTO and RPO are needed to design the right recovery strategies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Data backup and offsite storage requirements
choices.justification: Data backup and offsite storage requirements are an important aspect of a business continuity plan (BCP), but these alone will not help in defining the disaster recovery strategies.

questions.text: When reviewing a disaster recovery plan, an information systems (IS) auditor should be MOST concerned with the lack of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: process owner involvement.
choices.justification: Process owner involvement is a critical part of the business impact analysis (BIA), which is used to create the disaster recovery plan. If the information systems (IS) auditor determines that process owners were not involved, this is a significant concern.

choices.label: B
choices.text: well-documented testing procedures.
choices.justification: Although well-documented testing procedures are important, unless process owners are involved there is no way to know whether the priorities and critical elements of the plan are valid.

choices.label: C
choices.text: an alternate processing facility.
choices.justification: An alternate processing facility may be a requirement to meet the needs of the business; however, such a decision needs to be based on the BIA.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a well-documented data classification scheme.
choices.justification: A data classification scheme is important to ensure that controls over data are appropriate; however, this is a lesser concern than a lack of process owner involvement.

questions.text: Which of the following issues should be the GREATEST concern to the information systems (IS) auditor when reviewing an IT disaster recovery test?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Due to the limited test time window, only the most essential systems were tested, and other systems were tested separately during the rest of the year.
choices.justification: This is not a concern because over the course of the year, all the systems were tested.

choices.label: B
choices.text: During the test, some of the backup systems were defective or not working, causing the test of these systems to fail.
choices.justification: The purpose of the test is to test the backup plan. When the backup systems are not working, then the plan cannot be counted on in a real disaster. This is the most serious problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The procedures to shut down and secure the original production site before starting the backup site required far more time than planned.
choices.justification: In a real disaster, there is no need for a clean shutdown of the original production environment because the first priority is to bring the backup site up.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Every year, the same employees perform the test, and the recovery plan documents are not used because every step is well known by all participants.
choices.justification: A disaster recovery test should test the plan, processes, people and IT systems. Therefore, if the plan is not used, its accuracy and adequacy cannot be verified. Disaster recovery should not rely on key staff because a disaster can occur when they are not available. However, the fact that the test works is less serious than the failure of the systems and infrastructure that the recovery plan counts on. Good practice rotates different people through the test and ensures that the plan itself is followed and tested.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is conducting a review of the disaster recovery procedures for a data center. Which of the following indicators BEST shows that the procedures meet the requirements?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Documented procedures were approved by management.
choices.justification: Management approval does not necessarily mean that the disaster recovery procedures are sufficient to meet the needs of the business.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Procedures were reviewed and compared with industry good practices.
choices.justification: Although it is useful to compare the procedures with documented industry good practices, a tabletop exercise (paper test) is a better indicator that the procedures meet requirements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A tabletop exercise using the procedures was conducted.
choices.justification: Conducting a tabletop exercise (paper-based test) of the procedures with all responsible members best ensures that the procedures meet the requirements. This type of test can identify missing or incorrect procedures because representatives responsible for performing the tasks are present.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recovery teams and their responsibilities are documented.
choices.justification: The documentation of recovery teams and their responsibilities is part of the procedures and does not necessarily validate that the procedures are correct and complete thus meeting requirements.

questions.text: There are several methods of providing telecommunication continuity. The method of routing traffic through split-cable or duplicate-cable facilities is called:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: alternative routing.
choices.justification: Alternative routing is a method of routing information via an alternate medium, such as copper cable or fiber optics. This involves the use of different networks, circuits or end points if the normal network is unavailable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: diverse routing.
choices.justification: Diverse routing routes traffic through split-cable facilities or duplicate-cable facilities. This can be accomplished with different and/or duplicate cable sheaths. If different cable sheaths are used, the cable may be in the same conduit and, therefore, subject to the same interruptions as the cable it is backing up. The communication service subscriber can duplicate the facilities by having alternate routes, although the entrance to and from the customer premises may be in the same conduit. The subscriber can obtain diverse routing and alternate routing from the local carrier, including dual-entrance facilities. This type of access is time-consuming and costly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: long-haul network diversity.
choices.justification: Long-haul network diversity is a diverse, long-distance network using different packet switching circuits among the major long-distance carriers. It ensures long-distance access if any carrier experiences a network failure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: last-mile circuit protection.
choices.justification: Last-mile circuit protection is a redundant combination of local carrier T-1s (E-1s in Europe), microwave and/or coaxial cable access to the local communications loop. This enables the facility to have access during a local carrier communication disaster. Alternate local-carrier routing is also used.

questions.text: Which of the following scenarios provides the BEST disaster recovery plan (DRP) to implement for critical applications?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Daily data backups that are stored offsite and a hot site located 140 km from the main data center.
choices.justification: The absence of real-time backups may pose a challenge depending on the recovery point objective (RPO).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Daily data backups that are stored onsite in a fireproof safe.
choices.justification: Having data backups is necessary, but not having a replication site would be insufficient for the critical application.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Real-time data replication between the main data center and the hot site located 500 m from the main site.
choices.justification: Depending on the type of disaster, a hot site should normally be located more than 500 meters from the main facility. Having real-time backups may be the best option, depending on the data RPO.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Daily data backups that are stored offsite with a warm site located 70 km from the main data center.
choices.justification: A warm site may take days to recover, and, therefore, it may not be a suitable solution.

questions.text: In which of the following situations is it MOST appropriate to implement data mirroring as the recovery strategy?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Disaster tolerance is high.
choices.justification: Data mirroring is a data recovery technique, and disaster tolerance addresses the allowable time for an outage of the business.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The recovery time objective (RTO) is high.
choices.justification: The recovery time objective (RTO) is an indicator of disaster tolerance. Data mirroring addresses data loss, not the RTO.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The recovery point objective (RPO) is low.
choices.justification: The recovery point objective (RPO) indicates the latest point in time at which it is possible to recover the data. This determines how often the data must be backed up to minimize data loss. If the RPO is low, then the organization does not want to lose much data and must use a process such as data mirroring to prevent data loss.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The RPO is high.
choices.justification: If the RPO is high, then a less expensive backup strategy can be used; data mirroring should not be implemented as the data recovery strategy.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor evaluating the resilience of a high-availability network should be MOST concerned if:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: the setup is geographically dispersed.
choices.justification: Dispersed geographic locations provide backup if a site has been destroyed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the servers are clustered in one site.
choices.justification: A clustered setup in one site makes the entire network vulnerable to natural disasters or other disruptive events.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a hot site is ready for activation.
choices.justification: A hot site is also a good alternative for a single-point-of-failure site.

choices.label: D
choices.text: diverse routing is implemented for the network.
choices.justification: Diverse routing provides telecommunications backup if a network is not available.

questions.text: To ensure structured disaster recovery, it is MOST important that the business continuity plan (BCP) and disaster recovery plan (DRP) are:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: stored at an alternate location.
choices.justification: Storing the business continuity plan (BCP) at an alternate location is useful in the case of complete site outage; however, the BCP is not useful during a disaster without adequate tests.

choices.label: B
choices.text: communicated to all users.
choices.justification: Communicating to users is not of much use without actual tests.

choices.label: C
choices.text: tested regularly.
choices.justification: If the BCP is tested regularly, the BCP and disaster recovery plan (DRP) team are adequately aware of the process and that helps in structured disaster recovery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: updated regularly.
choices.justification: Even if the plan is updated regularly, it is of less use during an actual disaster if it is not adequately tested.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is performing a review of the disaster recovery hot site used by a financial institution. Which of the following is the GREATEST concern?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: System administrators use shared accounts which never expire at the hot site.
choices.justification: Although it is not a good practice for security administrators to share accounts that do not expire, the greater risk in this scenario is running out of disk space.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Disk space utilization data are not kept current.
choices.justification: Not knowing how much disk space is in use and, therefore, how much is needed at the disaster recovery site can create major issues in the case of a disaster.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Physical security controls at the hot site are less robust than at the main site.
choices.justification: Physical security controls are important, and this is a concern, but the more important concern is running out of disk space. The physical characteristics of the disaster recovery site may call for different controls that may appear to be less robust than the main site; however, such a risk can be addressed through policy and procedures or by adding additional personnel if needed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Servers at the hot site do not have the same specifications as at the main site.
choices.justification: If the servers at the hot site are capable of running the programs that are required in a disaster recovery situation, the precise capabilities of the servers at the hot site is not a major risk. It is necessary to ensure that software configuration and settings match the servers at the main site, but it is not unusual for newer and more powerful servers to exist at the main site for everyday production use while the standby servers are less powerful.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is useful while drafting a disaster recovery plan (DRP)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Downtime costs decrease as the recovery point objective (RPO) increases.
choices.justification: Downtime costs are not related to the recovery point objective (RPO). The RPO defines the data backup strategy, which is related to recovery costs rather than to downtime costs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Downtime costs increase with time.
choices.justification: Downtime costs—such as loss of sales, idle resources and salaries—increase with time. A disaster recovery plan (DRP) should be drawn up to achieve the lowest downtime costs possible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recovery costs are independent of time.
choices.justification: Recovery costs decrease with the time allowed for recovery. For example, recovery costs to recover business operations within two days will be higher than recovery costs within seven days. The essence of an effective DRP is to minimize uncertainty and increase predictability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recovery costs can only be controlled on a short-term basis.
choices.justification: With good planning, recovery costs can be predicted and contained.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST critical element to execute a disaster recovery plan (DRP) effectively?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Offsite storage of backup data
choices.justification: Remote storage of backups is the most critical disaster recovery plan (DRP) element of the items listed because access to backup data is required to restore systems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Up-to-date list of key disaster recovery contacts
choices.justification: Having a list of key contacts is important but not as important as having adequate data backup.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Availability of a replacement data center
choices.justification: A DRP may use a replacement data center or some other solution, such as a mobile site, reciprocal agreement, or outsourcing agreement.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Clearly defined recovery time objective
choices.justification: Having a clearly defined recovery time objective is especially important for business continuity planning, but the core element of disaster recovery (the recovery of IT infrastructure and capability) is data backup.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is performing an audit in the data center when the fire alarm begins sounding. The audit scope includes disaster recovery, so the auditor observes the data center staff respond to the alarm. Which of the following is the MOST important action for the data center staff to complete in this scenario?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Notify the local fire department of the alarm condition.
choices.justification: Life safety is always the first priority, and notifying the fire department of the alarm is not typically necessary because most data center alarms are configured to automatically report to the local authorities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prepare to activate the fire suppression system.
choices.justification: Fire suppression systems are designed to operate automatically, and activating the system when staff are not yet evacuated can create confusion and panic, leading to injuries or even fatalities. Manual triggering of the system can be necessary under certain conditions, but only after all other data center personnel are safely evacuated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensure all persons in the data center are evacuated.
choices.justification: In an emergency, safety of life is always the first priority; therefore, the complete and orderly evacuation of the facility staff is the most important activity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Remove all backups from the data center.
choices.justification: Removal of backups from the data center is not an appropriate action because it can delay the evacuation of personnel. Most organizations have copies of backups in offsite storage to mitigate the risk of data loss for this type of disaster.

questions.text: A hot site should be implemented as a recovery strategy when the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: disaster downtime tolerance is low.
choices.justification: Disaster downtime tolerance is the time gap during which the business can accept non-availability of IT facilities. If this time gap is low, recovery strategies that can be implemented within a short period of time, such as a hot site, should be used.

choices.label: B
choices.text: recovery point objective (RPO) is high.
choices.justification: The recovery point objective (RPO) is the earliest point in time at which it is possible to recover the data. A high RPO means that the process would result in greater losses of data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: recovery time objective (RTO) is high.
choices.justification: A high recovery time objective (RTO) means that additional time would be available for the recovery strategy, thus making other recovery alternatives—such as warm or cold sites—viable alternatives.

choices.label: D
choices.text: maximum tolerable downtime is long.
choices.justification: If the maximum tolerable downtime is long, then a warm or cold site is a more cost-effective solution.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor discovers that the disaster recovery plan (DRP) for an organization does not include a critical application hosted in the cloud. Management response states that the cloud vendor is responsible for disaster recovery (DR) and DR-related testing. What is the NEXT course of action for the IS auditor to pursue?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Plan an audit of the cloud vendor.
choices.justification: Auditing the cloud vendor is useful; however, this is only useful if the vendor is contractually required to provide disaster recovery (DR) services.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the vendor contract to determine its DR capabilities.
choices.justification: DR services can only be expected from the vendor when explicitly listed in the contract with well-defined recovery time objectives and recovery point objectives. Without the contractual language, the vendor is not required to provide DR services.

choices.label: C
choices.text: an independent auditor’s report of the cloud vendor.
choices.justification: An independent auditor’s report, such as Statements on Standards for Attestation Engagements 16, on DR capabilities can be reviewed to ascertain the vendor’s DR capabilities; however, this is only fruitful if the vendor is contractually required to provide DR services.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Request a copy of the DRP from the cloud vendor.
choices.justification: A copy of DR policies can be requested to review their adequacy; however, this will only be useful if the vendor is contractually required to provide DR services.

questions.text: Which of the following represents the GREATEST risk created by a reciprocal agreement for disaster recovery between two organizations?
questions.taskStatement: Determine whether the organization has defined ownership of IT risk, controls, and standards

choices.label: A
choices.text: Developments may result in hardware and software incompatibility.
choices.justification: If one organization updates its hardware and software configuration, it may mean it is no longer compatible with the other party's systems in the agreement. This may mean that each organization is unable to use the facilities at the other organization to recover their processing following a disaster.

choices.label: B
choices.text: may not be available when needed.
choices.justification: unavailable when needed are an intrinsic risk in any reciprocal agreement, but this is a contractual matter and not the greatest risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The recovery plan cannot be live tested.
choices.justification: The plan can be tested by paper-based walk-throughs and possibly by agreement between the organizations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The security infrastructures in each organization may be different.
choices.justification: The difference in security infrastructures, although a risk, is not insurmountable.

questions.text: The cost of ongoing operations when a disaster recovery plan (DRP) is in place, compared to not having a DRP, will MOST likely:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: increase.
choices.justification: Due to the additional cost of testing, maintaining and implementing disaster recovery plan (DRP) measures, the cost of normal operations for any organization will always increase after a DRP implementation (i.e., the cost of normal operations during a nondisaster period is more than the cost of operations during a nondisaster period when no DRP was in place).

choices.label: B
choices.text: decrease.
choices.justification: The implementation of a DRP always results in additional costs to the organization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: remain the same.
choices.justification: The implementation of a DRP always results in additional costs to the organization.

choices.label: D
choices.text: be unpredictable.
choices.justification: The costs of a DRP are fairly predictable and consistent.

questions.text: The MAIN purpose for periodically testing offsite disaster recovery facilities is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: protect the integrity of the data in the database.
choices.justification: The testing of an offsite facility does nothing to protect the integrity of the database. It may test the validity of backups but does not protect their integrity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: eliminate the need to develop detailed contingency plans.
choices.justification: Testing an offsite location validates the value of the contingency plans and is not used to eliminate detailed plans.

choices.label: C
choices.text: ensure the continued compatibility of the contingency facilities.
choices.justification: The main purpose of offsite hardware testing is to ensure the continued compatibility of the contingency facilities so that assurance can be gained that the contingency plans will work in an actual disaster.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ensure that program and system documentation remains current.
choices.justification: Program and system documentation should be reviewed continuously to ensure that content is up-to-date. A test of an offsite facility may ensure that the documentation for that site is current, but this is not the purpose of testing an offsite facility.

questions.text: Which of the following is a continuity plan test that simulates a system crash and uses actual resources to cost-effectively obtain evidence about the plan’s effectiveness?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Paper test
choices.justification: A paper test is a walk-through of the plan, involving major players who attempt to determine what might happen in a particular type of service disruption in the plan’s execution. A paper test usually precedes the preparedness test.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Post-test
choices.justification: A post-test is actually a test phase and is comprised of a group of activities, such as returning all resources to their proper place, disconnecting equipment, returning personnel and deleting all organization data from third-party systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Preparedness test
choices.justification: A preparedness test is a localized version of a full test, wherein resources are expended in the simulation of a system crash. This test is performed regularly on different aspects of the plan and can be a cost-effective way to gradually obtain evidence about the plan’s effectiveness. It also provides a means to improve the plan in increments.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Walk-through
choices.justification: A walk-through is a test involving a simulated disaster situation that tests the preparedness and understanding of management and staff rather than the actual resources.

questions.text: A disaster recovery plan (DRP) for an organization’s financial system specifies that the recovery point objective (RPO) is zero and the recovery time objective (RTO) is 72 hours. Which of the following is the MOST cost-effective solution?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hot site that can be operational in eight hours with asynchronous backup of the transaction logs
choices.justification: A hot site meets the recovery time objective (RTO) but incurs higher costs than necessary.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Distributed database systems in multiple locations updated asynchronously.
choices.justification: Asynchronous updates of the database in distributed locations do not meet the recovery point objective (RPO).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Synchronous updates of the data and standby active systems in a hot site
choices.justification: Synchronous updates of the data and standby active systems in a hot site meet the RPO and RTO requirements but are costlier than a warm site solution.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Synchronous remote copy of the data in a warm site that can be operational in 48 hours
choices.justification: The synchronous copy of the data storage achieves the RPO, and a warm site operational in 48 hours meets the required RTO.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing an organization’s disaster recovery plan (DRP) should PRIMARILY verify that it is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: tested every six months.
choices.justification: The plan must be subjected to regular testing, but the period between tests depends on the nature of the organization, the amount of change in the organization and the relative importance of information systems (IS). Three months, or even annually, may be appropriate in different circumstances.

choices.label: B
choices.text: regularly reviewed and updated.
choices.justification: The plan should be reviewed at appropriate intervals, depending on the nature of the business and the rate of change of systems and personnel. Otherwise, it may become out-of-date and may no longer be effective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: approved by the chief executive officer.
choices.justification: Although the disaster recovery plan (DRP) should receive the approval of senior management, it need not be the chief executive officer if another executive officer is equally or more appropriate. For a purely IS-related plan, the executive responsible for technology may approve the plan.

choices.label: D
choices.text: communicated to every department head in the organization.
choices.justification: Although a business continuity plan (BCP) is likely to be circulated throughout an organization, the IS DRP will usually be a technical document and only relevant to IS and communication staff.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST reasonable option for recovering a noncritical system?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warm site
choices.justification: A warm site is generally available at a medium cost, requires less time to become operational and is suitable for sensitive operations that should be recovered in a moderate amount of time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mobile site
choices.justification: A mobile site is a vehicle ready with all necessary computer equipment that can be moved to any location, depending upon the need. The need for a mobile site relies on the scale of operations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hot site
choices.justification: A hot site is contracted for a shorter time period at a higher cost, and it is better suited for recovery of vital and critical applications.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cold site
choices.justification: Generally, a cold site is contracted for a longer period at a lower cost. It is generally used for noncritical applications because it requires more time to make a cold site operational.

questions.text: Which of the following provides the BEST evidence of an organization’s disaster recovery capability readiness?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Disaster recovery plan (DRP)
choices.justification: Having a plan is important, but a plan cannot be considered effective until it has been tested.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Customer references for the alternate site provider
choices.justification: Customer references may aid in choosing an alternate site provider but will not ensure the effectiveness of the plan.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Processes for maintaining the DRP
choices.justification: A disaster recovery plan must be kept up-to-date through a regular maintenance and review schedule, but this is not as important as testing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Results of tests and exercises
choices.justification: Only tests and exercises demonstrate the adequacy of the plans and provide reasonable assurance of an organization’s disaster recovery capability readiness.

questions.text: The organization’s disaster recovery plan (DRP) should address early recovery of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: all information systems processes.
choices.justification: A disaster recovery plan (DRP) recovers most-critical systems first, according to business priorities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: all financial processing applications.
choices.justification: Depending on business priorities, financial systems may or may not be the first to be recovered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: only those applications designated by the information systems (IS) manager.
choices.justification: The business manager, not the information systems (IS) manager, determines priorities for system recovery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: processing in priority order, as defined by business management.
choices.justification: Business management should know which systems are critical and what they need to process well in advance of a disaster. It is management’s responsibility to develop and maintain the plan. Adequate time will not be available for this determination after the disaster occurs. IS and the information processing facility are service organizations that exist for the purpose of assisting the general user management in successfully performing their jobs.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: While conducting an audit on the customer relationship management application, the information systems (IS) auditor observes that it takes a significantly long time for users to log on to the system during peak business hours as compared with other times of the day. After a user is logged on, the average response time for the system is within acceptable limits. Which of the following choices should the IS auditor recommend?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: No action should be taken because the system meets current business requirements.
choices.justification: The information systems (IS) auditor should not recommend taking no action, because a delayed login process has a negative impact on employee productivity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: IT should increase the network bandwidth to improve performance.
choices.justification: Network bandwidth may or may not be the root cause of this issue. Performance measurement criteria may help determine the cause, which can then be remediated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Users should be provided with detailed manuals to use the system properly.
choices.justification: Because the problem is related to logging on and not to processing, additional training for users is not effective in this case.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Establish performance measurement criteria for the authentication servers.
choices.justification: Performance criteria for the authentication servers would help to quantify acceptable thresholds for system performance, which can be measured and remediated.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST ensures that users have uninterrupted access to a critical, heavily used web-based application?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Disk mirroring
choices.justification: Disk mirroring provides real-time replication of disk drives but does not ensure uninterrupted system availability in the event a server crashes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: redundant array of inexpensive disks (RAID)
choices.justification: redundant array of inexpensive disks (RAID) technology improves resiliency but does not protect against failure of a network interface card or central processing unit processor failure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dynamic domain name system (DNS)
choices.justification: Dynamic domain name system (DNS) is a method used to assign a host name to an Internet Protocol address that is dynamic. This is a useful technology but does not help ensure availability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Load balancing
choices.justification: Load balancing distributes traffic across multiple servers, ensuring uninterrupted system availability and consistent response time for web applications. It also redirects traffic to functional servers if a server fails.

questions.text: While reviewing the process for continuous monitoring of the capacity and performance of IT resources, an information systems (IS) auditor should PRIMARILY ensure that the process is focused on which of the following?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adequately monitoring service levels of IT resources and services
choices.justification: Continuous monitoring helps to ensure that service level agreements (SLAs) are met, but this is not the primary focus of monitoring. It is possible that even if a system were offline, it would meet the requirements of an SLA. Therefore, accurate availability monitoring is more important.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Providing data to enable timely planning for capacity and performance requirements
choices.justification: data gained from capacity and performance monitoring is an input to the planning process, the primary focus is to monitor availability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Providing accurate feedback on IT resource capacity
choices.justification: Accurate capacity monitoring of IT resources is a critical element of a continuous monitoring process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Properly forecasting performance, capacity and throughput of IT resources
choices.justification: Although continuous monitoring helps management to predict likely IT resource capabilities, the more critical issue is that availability monitoring is accurate.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST directly affected by network performance monitoring tools?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Integrity
choices.justification: Network monitoring tools can be used to detect errors that are propagating through a network, but their primary focus is on network reliability so that the network is available when required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Availability
choices.justification: Network monitoring tools allow observation of network performance and problems. This allows the administrator to take corrective action when network problems are observed. Therefore, the characteristic most directly affected by network monitoring is availability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Completeness
choices.justification: Network monitoring tools do not measure completeness of the communication. This is measured by the end points in the communication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Confidentiality
choices.justification: A network monitoring tool can violate confidentiality by allowing a network administrator to observe nonencrypted traffic. This requires careful protection and policies regarding the use of network monitoring tools.

questions.text: The PRIMARY benefit of an IT manager monitoring technical capacity is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: identify the need for new hardware and storage procurement.
choices.justification: This is one benefit of monitoring technical capacity because it can help forecast future demands, not just react to system failures. However, the primary responsibility of the IT manager is to meet the overall requirement to ensure that IT is meeting the service level expectations of the business.

choices.label: B
choices.text: determine the future capacity need based on usage.
choices.justification: Determining future capacity is one definite benefit of technical capability monitoring.

choices.label: C
choices.text: ensure that the service level requirements are met.
choices.justification: Capacity monitoring has multiple objectives; however, the primary objective is to ensure compliance with the internal service level agreement between the business and IT.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ensure that systems operate at optimal capacity.
choices.justification: IT management is interested in ensuring that systems are operating at optimal capacity, but their primary obligation is to ensure that IT is meeting the service level requirements of the business.

questions.text: When reviewing system parameters, an information systems (IS) auditor’s PRIMARY concern should be that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: they are set to meet both security and performance requirements.
choices.justification: The primary concern is to find the balance between security and performance. Recording changes in an audit trail and periodically reviewing them is a detective control; however, if parameters are not set according to business rules, monitoring of changes may not be an effective control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: changes are recorded in an audit trail and periodically reviewed.
choices.justification: changes to ensure that they are supported by appropriate documents is also a detective control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: changes are authorized and supported by appropriate documents.
choices.justification: If parameters are set incorrectly, the related documentation and the fact that these are authorized does not reduce the impact.

choices.label: D
choices.text: access to parameters in the system is restricted.
choices.justification: Restriction of access to parameters ensures that only authorized staff can access the parameters; however, if the parameters are set incorrectly, restricting access will still have an adverse impact.

questions.text: Doing which of the following during peak production hours can result in unexpected downtime?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether effective processes are in place to support end users

choices.label: A
choices.text: Performing data migration
choices.justification: Performing data migration may impact performance but does not cause downtime.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Performing preventive maintenance on electrical systems
choices.justification: Preventive maintenance activities should be scheduled for nonpeak times of the day, and preferably during a maintenance window time period. A mishap or incident caused by a maintenance worker can result in unplanned downtime.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Promoting applications from development to the staging environment
choices.justification: Promoting applications into a staging environment (not production) should not affect system operations in any significant manner.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reconfiguring a standby router in the data center
choices.justification: Reconfiguring a standby router should not cause unexpected downtime because the router is not operational and any problems should not affect network traffic.

questions.text: A lower recovery time objective (RTO) results in:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: higher disaster tolerance.
choices.justification: Disaster tolerance relates to the length of time that critical business processes can be interrupted. A higher disaster tolerance allows for a longer outage and, therefore, longer recovery time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: higher cost.
choices.justification: recovery time objective (RTO) is based on the acceptable down time in case of a disruption of operations. The lower the RTO, the higher the cost of recovery strategies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: reduced system interruptions.
choices.justification: The lower the disaster tolerance, the narrower the interruption window. The interruption window is the length of the outage of critical processes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: more permissive data loss.
choices.justification: Permissive data loss relates to recovery point objective (RPO), not disaster tolerance.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor needs to review the procedures used to restore a software application to its state prior to an upgrade. Therefore, the auditor needs to assess the following:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: Problem management procedures
choices.justification: Problem management procedures are used to track user feedback and issues related to the operation of an application for trend analysis and problem resolution.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Software development procedures
choices.justification: Software development procedures, such as the software development life cycle (SDLC), are used to manage the creation or acquisition of new or modified software.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Back-out procedures
choices.justification: Back-out procedures are used to restore a system to a previous state and are an important element of the change control process. The other choices are not related to the change control process, which specifies what procedures should be followed when software is being upgraded but does not work and requires a fallback to its former state.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Incident management procedures
choices.justification: Incident management procedures are used to manage errors or problems with system operation. A help desk usually uses them. One of the incident management procedures may be how to follow a fallback plan.

questions.text: Contractual provisions for a hot, warm or cold site should PRIMARILY cover which of the following considerations?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Physical security measures
choices.justification: Physical security measures are not always part of the contract, although they are an important consideration when choosing a third-party site.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total number of subscribers
choices.justification: The total number of subscribers is a consideration, but more important is whether the agreement limits the number of subscribers in a building or in a specific area. It is also good to know if other subscribers are competitors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Number of subscribers permitted to use a site at one time
choices.justification: The contract should specify the number of subscribers permitted to use the site at any one time. The contract can be written to give preference to certain subscribers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: References by other users
choices.justification: The references that other users can provide are a consideration taken before signing the contract; they are not part of the contractual provisions.

questions.text: Which of the following must exist to ensure the viability of a duplicate information processing facility (IPF)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: The site is near the primary site to ensure quick and efficient recovery.
choices.justification: The site chosen should not be subject to the same natural disaster as the primary site. Being close may be a risk or an advantage, depending on the type of expected disaster.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The site contains the most advanced hardware available.
choices.justification: A reasonable compatibility of hardware/software must exist to serve as a basis for backup. The latest or newest hardware may not adequately serve this need.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The workload of the primary site is monitored to ensure adequate backup is available.
choices.justification: Resource availability must be assured. The workload of the primary site must be monitored to ensure that availability at the alternate site for emergency backup use is sufficient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The hardware is tested when it is installed to ensure it is working properly.
choices.justification: Testing the hardware when the site is established is essential, but regular testing of the actual backup data is necessary to ensure that the operation will continue to perform as planned.

questions.text: An organization implemented an online customer help desk application using a software as a service (SaaS) operating model. An information systems (IS) auditor is asked to recommend the best control to monitor the service level agreement (SLA) with the SaaS vendor regarding availability. What is the BEST recommendation that the IS auditor can provide?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the monitoring and reporting of IT key performance indicators (KPIs) and IT key risk indicators (KRIs)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the SaaS vendor to provide a weekly report on application uptime.
choices.justification: Weekly application availability reports are useful, but these reports represent only the vendor’s perspective. While monitoring these reports, the organization can raise concerns of inaccuracy; however, without internal monitoring, such concerns cannot be substantiated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement an online polling tool to monitor the application and record outages.
choices.justification: Implementing an online polling tool to monitor and record application outages is the best option for an organization to monitor the software as a service application availability. Comparing internal reports with the vendor’s service level agreement (SLA) reports ensures that the vendor’s monitoring of the SLA is accurate and that all conflicts are appropriately resolved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Log all application outages reported by users and aggregate the outage time weekly.
choices.justification: Logging the outage times reported by users is helpful but does not give a true picture of all outages of the online application. Some outages may go unreported, especially if the outages are intermittent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Contract an independent third party to provide weekly reports on application uptime.
choices.justification: Contracting a third party to implement availability monitoring is not a cost-effective option. Additionally, this results in a shift from monitoring the software as a service (SaaS) vendor to monitoring the third party.

questions.text: Which of the following is widely accepted as one of the critical components in networking management?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Configuration and change management
choices.justification: Configuration management is widely accepted as one of the key components of any network because it establishes how the network will function internally and externally. It also deals with the management of configuration and monitoring performance. Change management ensures that the setup and management of the network are done properly, including managing changes to the configuration, removing default passwords and possibly hardening the network by disabling unneeded services.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Topological mappings
choices.justification: Topological mappings provide outlines of the components of the network and its connectivity. This is important to address issues such as single points of failure and proper network isolation, but it is not the most critical component of network management.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Application of monitoring tools
choices.justification: Application monitoring is not a critical part of network management.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proxy server troubleshooting
choices.justification: Proxy server troubleshooting is used for troubleshooting purposes, and managing a proxy is only a small part of network management.

questions.text: Which of the following should the information systems (IS) auditor review to ensure that servers are optimally configured to support processing requirements?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's change, configuration, release, and patch management programs

choices.label: A
choices.text: Benchmark test results
choices.justification: Benchmark tests are designed to compare system performance using standardized criteria; however, benchmark testing does not provide the best data to ensure the optimal configuration of servers in an organization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Server logs
choices.justification: A server log contains data showing activities performed on the server but does not contain the utilization data required to ensure the optimal configuration of servers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Downtime reports
choices.justification: A downtime report identifies the elapsed time when a computer is not operating correctly because of machine failure but is not useful in determining optimal server configurations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Server utilization data
choices.justification: Monitoring server utilization identifies underused servers and monitors overall server utilization. Underused servers do not provide the business with optimal cost-effectiveness. By monitoring server usage, IT management can take appropriate measures to raise the utilization ratio and provide the most effective return on investment.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST limits the impact of server failures in a distributed environment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Redundant pathways
choices.justification: Redundant pathways will minimize the impact of channel communications failures but will not address the problem of server failure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clustering
choices.justification: Clustering allows two or more servers to work as a unit so that when one of them fails, the other takes over.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dial backup lines
choices.justification: Dial backup lines will minimize the impact of channel communications failures but not a server failure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Standby power
choices.justification: Standby power provides an alternative power source in the event of an energy failure but does not address the problem of a server failure.

questions.text: While reviewing the IT infrastructure, an information systems (IS) auditor notices that storage resources are continuously being added. The IS auditor should:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's storage, backup, and restoration policies and processes

choices.label: A
choices.text: recommend the use of disk mirroring.
choices.justification: A disk mirroring solution increases storage requirements. This is not advisable until a proper capacity management plan is in place.

choices.label: B
choices.text: review the adequacy of offsite storage.
choices.justification: Offsite storage is unrelated to the problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: review the capacity management process.
choices.justification: Capacity management is the planning and monitoring of computer resources to ensure that available IT resources are used efficiently and effectively. This looks at capacity from a strategic viewpoint and allows a plan to forecast and purchase additional equipment in a planned manner.

choices.label: D
choices.text: recommend the use of a compression algorithm.
choices.justification: Although data compression may save disk space, it can affect system performance. This is not the first choice—the auditor should recommend more investigation into the increased demand for storage before providing any recommended solutions.

questions.text: Business units are concerned about the performance of a newly implemented system. Which of the following should an information systems (IS) auditor recommend?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Develop a baseline and monitor system usage.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor should recommend the development of a performance baseline and monitor the system’s performance against the baseline to develop empirical data upon which decisions for modifying the system can be made.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Define alternate processing procedures.
choices.justification: Alternate processing procedures will not alter a system’s performance, and no changes should be made until the reported issue has been examined more thoroughly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prepare the maintenance manual.
choices.justification: A maintenance manual will not alter a system’s performance or address the user concerns.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement the changes users have suggested.
choices.justification: Implementing changes without knowledge of the cause(s) for the perceived poor performance may not result in a more efficient system.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience

questions.text: Which of the following distinguishes a business impact analysis (BIA) from a risk assessment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inventory of critical assets
choices.justification: An inventory of critical assets is completed in a risk assessment and a business impact analysis (BIA).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identification of vulnerabilities
choices.justification: The identification of vulnerabilities is relevant in a risk assessment and a BIA.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Listing of threats
choices.justification: A listing of threats is relevant in a risk assessment and a BIA.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Determination of acceptable downtime
choices.justification: A determination of acceptable downtime is made only in a BIA.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing an organization’s disaster recovery plan (DRP) implementation. The project was completed on time and on budget. During the review, the auditor uncovers several areas of concern. Which of the following presents the GREATEST risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the role and/or impact of automatization and/or decision-making systems for an organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Testing of the DRP has not been performed.
choices.justification: Although testing a disaster recovery plan (DRP) is a critical component of a successful disaster recovery strategy, this is not the biggest risk; the biggest risk comes from a plan that is not properly designed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The disaster recovery strategy does not specify the use of a hot site.
choices.justification: The use of a hot site is a strategic determination based on tolerable downtime, cost and other factors. Although using a hot site may be considered a good practice, this is a very costly solution that may not be required for the organization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The business impact analysis (BIA) was conducted, but the results were not used.
choices.justification: The risk of not using the business impact analysis (BIA) results for disaster recovery planning means that the DRP may not be designed to recover the most critical assets in the correct order. As a result, the plan may not be adequate to allow the organization to recover from a disaster.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The disaster recovery project manager for the implementation has recently left the organization.
choices.justification: If the DRP is designed and documented properly, the loss of an experienced project manager should have minimal impact. The risk of a poorly designed plan that may not meet the requirements of the business is much more significant than the risk posed by the loss of the project manager.

questions.text: As part of the business continuity planning process, which of the following should be identified FIRST in the business impact analysis (BIA)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Risk, such as single point-of-failure and infrastructure risk
choices.justification: Risk should be identified after the critical business processes have been identified.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Threats to critical business processes
choices.justification: The identification of threats to critical business processes can only be determined after the critical business processes have been identified.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Critical business processes for ascertaining the priority for recovery
choices.justification: The identification of critical business processes should be addressed first so that the priorities and timelines for recovery can be documented.

choices.label: D
choices.text: required for resumption of business
choices.justification: Identification of resources required for business resumption occur after the identification of critical business processes.

questions.text: After completing the business impact analysis (BIA), what is the NEXT step in the business continuity planning process?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Test and maintain the plan.
choices.justification: After selecting a strategy, a specific business continuity plan (BCP) can be developed, tested and implemented.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Develop a specific plan.
choices.justification: After selecting a strategy, a specific BCP can be developed, tested and implemented.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Develop recovery strategies.
choices.justification: After the business impact analysis (BIA) is completed, the next phase in the BCP development is to identify the various recovery strategies and select the most appropriate strategy for recovering from a disaster that will meet the timelines and priorities defined through the BIA.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement the plan.
choices.justification: After selecting a strategy, a specific BCP can be developed, tested and implemented.

questions.text: Which of the following tasks should be performed FIRST when preparing a disaster recovery plan (DRP)?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Develop a recovery strategy.
choices.justification: Developing a recovery strategy will come after performing a business impact analysis (BIA).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform a business impact analysis (BIA).
choices.justification: The first step in any disaster recovery plan (DRP) is to perform a BIA.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Map software systems, hardware and network components.
choices.justification: The BIA identifies critical business processes and the systems that support those processes. Mapping software systems, hardware and network components will come after performing a BIA.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Appoint recovery teams with defined personnel, roles and hierarchy.
choices.justification: Appointing recovery teams with defined personnel, roles and hierarchy will come after performing a BIA.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST helps prioritize the recovery of IT assets when planning for a disaster?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Incident response plan
choices.justification: An incident response plan is an organized approach to addressing and managing a security breach or attack. The plan defines what constitutes an incident and the process to follow when an incident occurs. It does not prioritize recovery during a disaster.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Business impact analysis (BIA)
choices.justification: Incorporating the business impact analysis (BIA) into the IT disaster recovery planning process is critical to ensure that IT assets are prioritized to align with the business.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Threat and risk analysis
choices.justification: Identifying threats and analyzing risk to the business is an important part of disaster planning, but it does not determine the priority of recovery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recovery time objective (RTO)
choices.justification: The recovery time objective (RTO) is the amount of time allowed for the recovery of a business function or resource after a disaster occurs. This is included as part of the BIA and used to represent the prioritization of recovery.

questions.text: To optimize an organization’s business continuity plan, an information systems (IS) auditor should recommend a business impact analysis to determine:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: the business processes that generate the most financial value for the organization and, therefore, must be recovered first.
choices.justification: It is a common mistake to overemphasize financial value rather than urgency. For example, although the processing of incoming mortgage loan payments is important from a financial perspective, it can be delayed for a few days in the event of a disaster. Wiring funds to close on a loan, although not generating direct revenue, is far more critical because of the possibility of regulatory problems, customer complaints and reputation issues.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the priorities and order for recovery to ensure alignment with the organization’s business strategy.
choices.justification: The business strategy (which is often a long-term view) does not have a direct impact at this point in time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the business processes that must be recovered following a disaster to ensure the organization’s survival.
choices.justification: To ensure the organization’s survival following a disaster, it is important to recover the most critical business processes first.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the priorities and order of recovery, which will recover the greatest number of systems in the shortest time frame.
choices.justification: The mere number of recovered systems does not have a direct impact at this point in time. The important action is to recover systems that would impact business survival.

questions.text: The PRIMARY purpose of a business impact analysis (BIA) is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: define recovery strategies.
choices.justification: One of the primary outcomes of a business impact analysis (BIA) is the recovery time objective (RTO)e and the recovery point objective (RPO), which help in defining the recovery strategies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: identify the alternate site.
choices.justification: A BIA, itself, does not help in identifying the alternate site. That is determined during the recovery strategy phase of the project.

choices.label: C
choices.text: improve recovery testing.
choices.justification: A BIA, itself, does not help improve recovery testing. That is done during the implementation and testing phase of the project.

choices.label: D
choices.text: calculate the annual loss expectancy.
choices.justification: The annual loss expectancy of critical business assets and processes is determined during risk assessment and is reviewed in the BIA, but this is not the primary advantage.

questions.text: Which of the following groups is the BEST source of information for determining the criticality of application systems as part of a business impact analysis (BIA)?
questions.taskStatement: Determine whether the organization has defined ownership of IT risk, controls, and standards

choices.label: A
choices.text: Business processes owners
choices.justification: Business process owners have the most relevant information to contribute because the business impact analysis (BIA) is designed to evaluate criticality and recovery timelines, based on business needs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: IT management
choices.justification: Although IT management must be involved, it may not be fully aware of the business processes that need to be protected.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Senior business management
choices.justification: Although senior management must be involved, it may not be fully aware of the criticality of applications that need to be protected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Industry experts
choices.justification: The BIA depends on the organization's unique business needs, and the advice of industry experts is of limited value.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST method for determining the criticality of each application system in the production environment?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's ability to continue business operations

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interview the application programmers.
choices.justification: Interviews with the application programmers provide limited information related to the criticality of the systems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform a gap analysis.
choices.justification: A gap analysis is relevant to system development and project management but does not determine application criticality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the most recent application audits.
choices.justification: The audits may not contain the required information about application criticality or may not have been done recently.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform a business impact analysis (BIA).
choices.justification: A business impact analysis (BIA) gives the impact of the loss of each application. A BIA is conducted with representatives of the business that can accurately describe the criticality of a system and its importance to the business.

domain: Information Systems Operations and Business Resilience
category: Job Scheduling and Production Process Automation

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST method for an information systems (IS) auditor to verify that critical production servers are running the latest security updates released by the vendor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure that automatic updates are enabled on critical production servers.
choices.justification: Ensuring that automatic updates are enabled on production servers may be a valid way to manage the patching process; however, this does not provide assurance that all servers are being patched appropriately.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Verify manually that the patches are applied on a sample of production servers.
choices.justification: Verifying patches manually on a sample of production servers is less effective than automated testing and introduces a significant audit risk. Manual testing is also difficult and time consuming.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the change management log for critical production servers.
choices.justification: The change management log may not be updated on time and may not accurately reflect the patch update status on servers. A better testing strategy is to test the server for patches, rather than examining the change management log.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Run an automated tool to verify the security patches on production servers.
choices.justification: An automated tool can immediately provide a report on which patches have been applied and which are missing.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST helps to detect errors in data processing?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Programmed edit checks
choices.justification: Automated controls, such as programmed edit checks, are preventive controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Well-designed data entry screens
choices.justification: Automated controls, such as well-designed data entry screens, are preventive controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Separation of duties
choices.justification: Enforcing separation of duties primarily ensures that a single individual does not have the authority to both create and approve a transaction; this is not considered to be a method to detect errors, but a method to help prevent errors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hash totals
choices.justification: The use of hash totals is an effective method to reliably detect errors in data processing. A hash total indicates an error in data integrity.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor has been assigned to conduct a test that compares job run logs to computer job schedules. Which of the following observations would be the GREATEST concern to the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: There are a growing number of emergency changes.
choices.justification: Emergency changes are acceptable if they are properly documented as part of the process.

choices.label: B
choices.text: There were instances when some jobs were not completed on time.
choices.justification: Instances of jobs not being completed on time are a potential issue and should be investigated, but it is not the greatest concern.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There were instances when some jobs were overridden by computer operators.
choices.justification: The overriding of computer processing jobs by computer operators can lead to unauthorized changes to data or programs. This is a control concern; thus, it is always critical.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence shows that only scheduled jobs were run.
choices.justification: The audit should find that all scheduled jobs were run and that any exceptions were documented. This is not a violation.

questions.text: A batch transaction job failed in production; however, the same job returned no issues during user acceptance testing (UAT). Analysis of the production batch job indicates that it was altered after UAT. What control provides a mitigation for this risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether IT operations and maintenance practices support the organization's objectives

choices.label: A
choices.text: Improve regression test cases.
choices.justification: Improving the quality of the testing is not applicable in this case, because the more important issue is that developers have access to the production environment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activate audit trails for a limited period after release.
choices.justification: Activating audit trails or performing additional logging may be useful; however, the more important issue is that developers have access to the production environment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conduct an application user access review.
choices.justification: Conducting an application user access review does not identify developers’ access to code because they are not included in this review.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement a segregation/separation of duties (SoD) policy.
choices.justification: To ensure proper segregation/separation of duties (SoD), developers should be restricted to the development environment only. If code needs to be modified after user acceptance testing, the process must be restarted in development.

category: Information System Attack Methods and Techniques

questions.text: What method might an information systems (IS) auditor use to test wireless security at branch office locations?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: War dialing
choices.justification: War dialing is a technique for gaining access to a computer or a network through the dialing of defined blocks of telephone numbers, with the hope of getting an answer from a modem.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Social engineering
choices.justification: Social engineering is a technique used to gather information that can assist an attacker in gaining logical or physical access to data or resources. Social engineering exploits human weaknesses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: War driving
choices.justification: War driving is a technique for locating and gaining access to wireless networks by driving or walking around a building with a wireless-equipped computer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Password cracking
choices.justification: Password crackers are tools used to guess users’ passwords by trying combinations and dictionary words. After a wireless device has been identified, password crackers may be used to try to attack it.

questions.text: Which of the following types of penetration tests simulates a real attack and is used to test incident handling and response capability of the target?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blind testing
choices.justification: Blind testing is also known as black-box testing. This refers to a test where the penetration tester is not given any information and is forced to rely on publicly available information. This test simulates a real attack, except that the target organization is aware of the test being conducted.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Targeted testing
choices.justification: Targeted testing is also known as white-box testing. This refers to a test where the penetration tester is provided with information and the target organization is also aware of the testing activities.
