In some cases, the tester is also provided with a limited-privilege account to be used as a starting point.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Double-blind testing
choices.justification: Double-blind testing is also known as zero-knowledge testing. This refers to a test where the penetration tester is not given any information and the target organization is not given any warning—both parties are blind to the test. This is the best scenario for testing response capability because the target will react as if the attack were real.

choices.label: D
choices.text: External testing
choices.justification: External testing refers to a test where an external penetration tester launches attacks on the target’s network perimeter from outside the target network (typically from the Internet).

questions.text: A perpetrator looking to gain access to, and gather information about, encrypted data being transmitted over a network would MOST likely use:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: eavesdropping.
choices.justification: In eavesdropping, which is a passive attack, the intruder gathers the information flowing through the network with the intent of acquiring message contents for personal analysis or for third parties. Encrypted traffic is generally protected against eavesdropping

choices.label: B
choices.text: spoofing.
choices.justification: Spoofing is an active attack. In spoofing, a user receives an email that appears to have originated from one source when it actually was sent from another source.

choices.label: C
choices.text: traffic analysis.
choices.justification: In traffic analysis, which is a passive attack, an intruder determines the nature of the traffic flow between defined hosts and, through an analysis of session length, frequency and message length, the intruder is able to guess the type of communication taking place. This typically is used when messages are encrypted, and eavesdropping would not yield any meaningful results.

choices.label: D
choices.text: masquerading.
choices.justification: In masquerading, the intruder presents an identity other than the original identity. This is an active attack.

questions.text: Two-factor authentication can be circumvented through which of the following attacks?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Denial-of-service (DoS)
choices.justification: A denial-of-service (DoS) attack does not have a relationship to authentication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Man-in-the-middle
choices.justification: A man-in-the-middle attack is similar to piggybacking in that the attacker pretends to be the legitimate destination and then retransmits whatever is sent by the authorized user plus additional transactions after authentication has been accepted. This is done in many instances of bank fraud.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Key logging
choices.justification: Key logging can circumvent single-factor authentication but not two-factor authentication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Brute force
choices.justification: Brute force can circumvent single-factor authentication but not two-factor authentication.

questions.text: The use of residual biometric information to gain unauthorized access is an example of which of the following attacks?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Replay
choices.justification: Residual biometric characteristics, such as fingerprints left on a biometric capture device, may be reused by an attacker to gain unauthorized access.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Brute force
choices.justification: A brute force attack involves feeding the biometric capture device numerous different biometric samples.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cryptographic
choices.justification: A cryptographic attack targets the algorithm or the encrypted data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mimic
choices.justification: In a mimic attack, the attacker reproduces characteristics similar to those of the enrolled user such as forging a signature or imitating a voice.

questions.text: Web application developers occasionally use hidden fields on web pages to save information about a client session. This technique is used to store session variables that enable persistence across web pages, such as maintaining the contents of a shopping cart on a retail website application. The MOST likely web-based attack due to this practice is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: parameter tampering.
choices.justification: Web application developers sometimes use hidden fields to save information about a client session or to submit hidden parameters, such as the language of the end user, to the underlying application. Because hidden form fields do not display in the browser, developers may feel safe passing unvalidated data in the hidden fields (to be validated later). This practice is not safe because an attacker can intercept, modify and submit requests, which can discover information or perform functions that the web developer never intended. The malicious modification of web application parameters is known as parameter tampering.

choices.label: B
choices.text: cross-site scripting.
choices.justification: Cross-site scripting involves the compromise of the web page to redirect users to content on the attacker website. The use of hidden fields has no impact on the likelihood of a cross-site scripting attack because these fields are static content that cannot ordinarily be modified to create this type of attack.

choices.label: C
choices.text: cookie poisoning.
choices.justification: Web applications use cookies to save session state information on the client machine so that the user does not need to log on every time a page is visited. Cookie poisoning refers to the interception and modification of session cookies to impersonate the user or steal logon credentials. The use of hidden fields has no relation to cookie poisoning.

choices.label: D
choices.text: stealth commanding.
choices.justification: Stealth commanding is the hijacking of a web server by the installation of unauthorized code. Although the use of hidden forms may increase the risk of server compromise, the most common server exploits involve vulnerabilities of the server operating system or web server.

questions.text: An organization is developing a new web-based application to process orders from customers. Which of the following security measures should be taken to protect this application from hackers?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure that ports 80 and 443 are blocked at the firewall.
choices.justification: Port 80 must be open for a web application to work, and port 443 must be open for a Secured Hypertext Transmission Protocol to operate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inspect file and access permissions on all servers to ensure that all files have read-only access.
choices.justification: For customer orders to be placed, some data must be saved to the server. No customer orders can be placed on a read-only server.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perform a web application security review.
choices.justification: Performing a web application security review is a necessary effort to uncover security vulnerabilities that can be exploited by hackers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Make sure that only the internet protocol (IP) addresses of existing customers are allowed through the firewall.
choices.justification: Restricting internet protocol (IP) addresses might be appropriate for some types of web applications but is not the best solution because a new customer cannot place an order until the firewall rules were changed to allow the customer to connect.

questions.text: During an information systems (IS) audit of a global enterprise, the IS auditor discovers that the enterprise uses Voice-over Internet Protocol as the sole means of voice connectivity among all offices. Which of the following presents the MOST significant risk for the enterprise’s Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) infrastructure?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Network equipment failure
choices.justification: The use of Voice-over Internet Protocol does not introduce any unique risk with respect to equipment failure, and redundancy can be used to address network failure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Distributed denial-of-service attack
choices.justification: A distributed denial of service (DDoS) attack would potentially disrupt the enterprise’s ability to communicate among its offices and have the highest impact. In a traditional voice network, a DDoS attack would only affect the data network, not voice communications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Premium-rate fraud (toll fraud)
choices.justification: Toll fraud occurs when someone compromises the phone system and makes unauthorized long-distance calls. Although toll fraud may cost the business money, the more severe risk is the disruption of service.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Social engineering attack
choices.justification: Social engineering, which involves gathering sensitive information to launch an attack, can be exercised over any kind of telephony.

questions.text: If inadequate, which of the following is the MOST likely contributor to a denial-of-service (DoS) attack?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Router configuration and rules
choices.justification: Improper router configuration and rules can lead to denial-of-service (DoS) attacks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Design of the internal network
choices.justification: An inefficient design of the internal network may also lead to a DoS attack, but this is not as high a risk as router misconfiguration errors.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Updates to the router system software
choices.justification: Updates to router software have led to a DoS attack in the past, but this is a subset of router configuration and rules.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Audit testing and review techniques
choices.justification: Audit testing and review techniques can cause a DoS attack if tests disable systems or applications, but this is not the most likely risk.

questions.text: Consider the following diagram: To detect attack attempts that the firewall is unable to recognize, an information systems (IS) auditor should recommend placing a network intrusion detection system between the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's security awareness training program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Firewall and the organization’s network.
choices.justification: Attack attempts that cannot be recognized by the firewall will be detected if a network-based intrusion detection system (IDS) is placed between the firewall and the organization’s network.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Internet and the firewall.
choices.justification: A network-based IDS placed between the Internet and the firewall will detect attack attempts, whether they are or are not noticed by the firewall.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Internet and the web server.
choices.justification: Placing an IDS outside of the web server will identify attacks directed at the web server but will not detect attacks missed by the firewall.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Web server and the firewall.
choices.justification: Placing the IDS after the web server would identify attacks that have made it past the web server but will not indicate whether the firewall would have been able to detect the attacks.

questions.text: Distributed denial-of-service (DDoS ) attacks on Internet sites are typically evoked by hackers using which of the following?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: Logic bombs
choices.justification: Logic bombs are programs designed to destroy or modify data at a specific event or time in the future.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Phishing site
choices.justification: Phishing is an attack, normally via email, pretending to be an authorized person or organization requesting information.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Spyware
choices.justification: Spyware is a program that picks up information from PC drives by making copies of their contents.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Botnets
choices.justification: A botnet is a number of Internet-connected devices, each of which is running one or more bots. Botnets can be used to perform distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks, steal data and send spam, and allow the attacker to access the device and its connection.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST control to mitigate the risk of pharming attacks to an Internet banking application?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: User registration and password policies
choices.justification: User registration and password policies cannot mitigate pharming attacks because they do not prevent manipulation of domain name system (DNS) records.

choices.label: B
choices.text: User security awareness
choices.justification: User security awareness cannot mitigate pharming attacks because it does not prevent manipulation of DNS records.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use of intrusion detection/intrusion prevention systems
choices.justification: The use of intrusion detection/intrusion prevention systems cannot mitigate pharming attacks because they do not prevent manipulation of DNS records.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Domain name system (DNS) server security hardening
choices.justification: The pharming attack redirects the traffic to an unauthorized website by exploiting vulnerabilities of the DNS server. To avoid this kind of attack, it is necessary to eliminate any known vulnerability that can allow DNS poisoning. Older versions of DNS software are vulnerable to this kind of attack and should be patched.

questions.text: Which of the following potentially blocks hacking attempts?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intrusion detection system (IDS)
choices.justification: An intrusion detection system (IDS) is a detective control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Honeypot system
choices.justification: A honeypot solution captures intruder activity or traps the intruders when they attempt to explore a simulated target.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intrusion prevention system (IPS)
choices.justification: An intrusion prevention system (IPS) is deployed as an inline device on a network or host that can detect and block hacking attempts.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Network security scanner
choices.justification: A network security scanner identifies vulnerabilities but does not remediate them.

questions.text: Information systems (IS) management recently replaced its existing wired local area network with a wireless infrastructure to accommodate the increased use of mobile devices within the enterprise. This will increase the risk of which of the following attacks?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Port scanning
choices.justification: Port scanning often targets the external firewall of the enterprise. Use of wireless will not affect this.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Back door
choices.justification: A back door is an opening implanted into, or left in, software that enables an unauthorized entry into a system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Man-in-the-middle
choices.justification: Man-in-the-middle attacks intercept a message and can read, replace or modify it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: War driving
choices.justification: A war driving attack uses a wireless Ethernet card, set in promiscuous mode, and a powerful antenna to penetrate wireless systems from outside.

questions.text: Which of the following intrusion detection systems (IDSs) will MOST likely generate false alarms resulting from normal network activity?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Statistical-based
choices.justification: A statistical-based intrusion detection system (IDS) relies on a definition of known and expected behavior of systems. Because normal network activity may, at times, include unexpected behavior (e.g., a sudden massive download by multiple users), these activities will be flagged as suspicious.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Signature-based
choices.justification: A signature-based IDS is limited to its predefined set of detection rules, just like a virus scanner. Signature-based systems traditionally have low levels of false positives but may be weak at detecting new attacks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neural network
choices.justification: A neural network combines statistical- and signature-based IDSs to create a hybrid and better system.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Host-based
choices.justification: Host-based is another type of IDS, but it would not be used to monitor network activity.

questions.text: Which of the following presents an inherent risk with no distinct identifiable preventive controls?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Piggybacking
choices.justification: Piggybacking is the act of following an authorized person through a secured door and can be prevented using deadman doors. Logical piggybacking is an attempt to gain access through someone who has the rights (e.g., electronically attaching to an authorized telecommunication link to possibly intercept transmissions). This can be prevented by encrypting the message.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Viruses
choices.justification: Viruses are malicious program code inserted into another executable code that can self-replicate and spread from computer to computer via sharing of computer disks, transfer of logic over telecommunication lines or direct contact with an infected machine. Antivirus software can be used to protect the computer against viruses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Data diddling
choices.justification: Data diddling involves changing data before they are entered into the computer. It is one of the most common abuses because it requires limited technical knowledge and occurs before computer security can protect the data. There are only compensating controls for data diddling.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unauthorized application shutdown
choices.justification: The shutdown of an application can be initiated through terminals or microcomputers connected directly (online) or indirectly (dial-up line) to the computer. Only individuals knowing the high-level logon ID and password can initiate the shutdown process, which is effective if there are proper access controls.

questions.text: An Internet-based attack using password sniffing can:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: enable one party to act as if they are another party.
choices.justification: Spoofing attacks can be used to enable one party to act as if they are another party.

choices.label: B
choices.text: cause modification to the contents of certain transactions.
choices.justification: Data modification attacks can be used to modify the contents of certain transactions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: be used to gain access to systems containing proprietary information.
choices.justification: Password sniffing attacks can be used to gain access to systems on which proprietary information is stored.

choices.label: D
choices.text: result in major problems with billing systems and transaction processing agreements.
choices.justification: Repudiation of transactions can cause major problems with billing systems and transaction processing agreements.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a passive cybersecurity attack?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Traffic analysis
choices.justification: Cybersecurity threats/vulnerabilities are divided into passive and active attacks. A passive attack is one that monitors or captures network traffic but does not in any way modify, insert or delete the traffic. Examples of passive attacks include network analysis, eavesdropping and traffic analysis.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Masquerading
choices.justification: Because masquerading alters the data by modifying the origin, it is an active attack.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Denial-of-service
choices.justification: Because a denial-of–service attack floods the network with traffic or sends malformed packets over the network, it is an active attack.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Email spoofing
choices.justification: Because email spoofing alters the email header, it is an active attack.

questions.text: Which of the following is MOST indicative of the effectiveness of an information security awareness program?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Employees report more information regarding security incidents.
choices.justification: Although the promotion of security awareness is a preventive control, it can also be a detective measure because it encourages people to identify and report possible security violations. The reporting of incidents implies that employees are acting because of the awareness program.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All employees have signed the information security policy.
choices.justification: The existence of evidence that all employees have signed the security policy does not ensure that security responsibilities have been understood and applied.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Most employees have attended an awareness session.
choices.justification: One of the objectives of the security awareness program is to inform the employees of what is expected of them and their responsibilities, but this knowledge does not ensure that employees will perform their activities in a secure manner.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Information security responsibilities have been included in job descriptions.
choices.justification: The documentation of roles and responsibilities in job descriptions is not an indicator of the effectiveness of the awareness program.

questions.text: A hacker can obtain passwords without the use of computer tools or programs through the technique of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: social engineering.
choices.justification: Social engineering is based on the divulgence of private information through dialogues, interviews, inquiries, etc., in which a user may be indiscreet regarding their or someone else’s personal data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: sniffers.
choices.justification: A sniffer is a computer tool to monitor the traffic in networks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: back doors.
choices.justification: Back doors are computer programs left by hackers to exploit vulnerabilities.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trojan horses.
choices.justification: Trojan horses are computer programs that pretend to supplant a real program; thus, the functionality of the program is not authorized and is usually malicious in nature.

questions.text: The MOST likely explanation for a successful social engineering attack is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's security awareness training program

choices.label: A
choices.text: computer error.
choices.justification: Social engineering focuses on human behavior.

choices.label: B
choices.text: judgment error.
choices.justification: Social engineering is fundamentally about obtaining from someone a level of trust that is not warranted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: expertise.
choices.justification: Generally, social engineering attacks do not require significant expertise; often, the attacker is not proficient in information technology or systems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: technology.
choices.justification: Technology may facilitate social engineering, but it is fundamentally about obtaining human trust.

questions.text: During a review of intrusion detection logs, an information systems (IS) auditor notices traffic coming from the Internet, which appears to originate from the internal internet protocol (IP) address of the enterprise payroll server. Which of the following malicious activities would MOST likely cause this type of result?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Denial-of-service (DoS) attack
choices.justification: A denial-of-service (DoS) attack is designed to limit the availability of a resource and is characterized by a high number of requests that require response from the resource (usually a website). The target spends so many resources responding to the attack requests that legitimate requests are not serviced. These attacks are most commonly launched from networks of compromised computers (botnets) and may involve attacks from multiple computers at once.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spoofing
choices.justification: Spoofing is a form of impersonation where one computer tries to take on the identity of another computer. When an attack originates from the external network but uses an internal network address, the attacker is most likely trying to bypass firewalls and other network security controls by impersonating (or spoofing) the payroll server’s internal network address. By impersonating the payroll server, the attacker may be able to access sensitive internal resources.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Port scanning
choices.justification: Port scanning is a reconnaissance technique that is designed to gather information about a target before a more active attack. Port scanning might be used to determine the internal address of the payroll server but would not normally create a log entry that indicated external traffic from an internal server address.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Man-in-the-middle attack
choices.justification: A man-in-the-middle attack is a form of active eavesdropping where the attacker intercepts a computerized conversation between two parties and then allows the conversation to continue by relaying the appropriate data to both parties, while simultaneously monitoring the same data passing through the attacker’s conduit. This type of attack does not register as an attack originating from the payroll server, but, instead, it might be designed to hijack an authorized connection between a workstation and the payroll server.

questions.text: To protect a Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) infrastructure against a denial-of-service (DoS) attack, it is MOST important to secure the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: access control servers.
choices.justification: Securing the access control server may prevent account alteration or lockout but is not the primary protection against denial-of-service (DoS) attacks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: session border controllers.
choices.justification: Session border controllers enhance security in the access network and in the core. In the access network, they hide a user’s real address and provide a managed public address. This public address can be monitored, minimizing the opportunities for scanning and DoS attacks. Session border controllers permit access to clients behind firewalls while maintaining the firewall’s effectiveness. In the core, session border controllers protect the users and the network. They hide network topology and users’ real addresses. They can also monitor bandwidth and quality of service.

choices.label: C
choices.text: backbone gateways.
choices.justification: Backbone gateways are isolated and not readily accessible to hackers, so this is not a location of DoS attacks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: intrusion detection system (IDS).
choices.justification: Intrusion detection systems (IDSs) monitor traffic, but do not protect against DoS attacks.

questions.text: An information security policy stating that “a fixed time duration of inactivity must initiate a password-enabled screensaver” addresses which of the following attack methods?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Piggybacking
choices.justification: Piggybacking refers to unauthorized persons following, either physically or virtually, authorized persons into restricted areas. Initiating a screensaver that requires user to reenter the password prevents someone from tailgating an authorized person.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dumpster diving
choices.justification: This policy only refers to the user’s computer, not dumpster diving (looking through the organization’s trash for valuable information).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shoulder surfing
choices.justification: Shoulder surfing refers to observing a user while entering/viewing sensitive data on the screen. This may enable the observer to obtain login information or other sensitive information.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impersonation
choices.justification: Impersonation refers to someone acting as an employee in an attempt to retrieve desired information.

questions.text: An enterprise discovers that the computer of the chief financial officer has been infected with malware that includes a keystroke logger and a rootkit. The FIRST action to take is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: contact the appropriate law enforcement authorities to begin an investigation.
choices.justification: Although contacting law enforcement may be needed, the first step is to halt data flow by disconnecting the computer from the network.

choices.label: B
choices.text: immediately ensure that no additional data are compromised.
choices.justification: The first step is to disconnect the computer from the network, thus ensuring that no additional data are compromised. Then, using proper forensic techniques, capture the information stored in temporary files, network connection information, programs loaded into memory and other information on the machine.

choices.label: C
choices.text: disconnect the PC from the network.
choices.justification: The most important task is to prevent further data compromise and preserve evidence by disconnecting the computer from the network.

choices.label: D
choices.text: update the antivirus signature on the PC to ensure that the malware or virus is detected and removed.
choices.justification: Preserve the machine in a forensically sound condition and do not make any changes to it except to disconnect it from the network. Otherwise, evidence would be destroyed by powering off the PC or updating the software on the PC. Information stored in temporary files, network connection information, programs loaded into memory and other information may be lost.

questions.text: Which of the following results in a denial-of-service (DoS) attack?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Brute force attack
choices.justification: A brute force attack is typically a text attack that exhausts all possible key combinations used against encryption keys or passwords.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ping of death
choices.justification: The use of Ping with a packet size higher than 65 kilobyte (KB) and no fragmentation flag on will cause a denial of service.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leapfrog attack
choices.justification: A leapfrog attack, the act of telneting through one or more hosts to preclude a trace, makes use of user ID and password information obtained illicitly from one host to compromise another host.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Negative acknowledgment attack
choices.justification: A negative acknowledgment is a penetration technique that capitalizes on a potential weakness in an operating system that does not handle asynchronous interrupts properly, leaving the system in an unprotected state during such interrupts.

questions.text: What is a risk associated with attempting to control physical access to sensitive areas, such as computer rooms, using card keys or locks?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Unauthorized individuals wait for controlled doors to open and walk in behind those authorized.
choices.justification: Piggybacking (or tailgating) can compromise the physical access controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The contingency plan for the enterprise cannot effectively test controlled access practices.
choices.justification: The testing of controlled access is of minimal concern in a disaster recovery environment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Access cards, keys and pads can be easily duplicated allowing easy compromise of the control.
choices.justification: Duplicating access control cards or keys is technically challenging.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Removing access for those who are no longer authorized is complex.
choices.justification: An access control system should have easily followed procedures for managing user access throughout the access life cycle.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST reliably effective method for dealing with the spread of a network worm that exploits vulnerability in a protocol?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Install the latest vendor security patches immediately.
choices.justification: Installing the latest patches will improve the situation only if a patch has been released that addresses the particular vulnerability in the protocol. Also, patches should not be installed prior to testing, because patching systems can create new vulnerabilities or impact performance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Block the protocol traffic in the perimeter firewall.
choices.justification: Blocking the protocol on the perimeter does not stop the worm from spreading if it is introduced via portable media.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Block the protocol traffic between internal network segments.
choices.justification: Blocking the protocol helps to slow the spread, but also prohibits any software that uses it from working between segments.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stop the services that the protocol uses.
choices.justification: Stopping the services is the most effective way to prevent a worm from spreading, because it directly addresses the means of propagation at the lowest practical level.

questions.text: The FIRST step in a successful attack to a system is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: gathering information.
choices.justification: Successful attacks start by gathering information about the target system. This is done in advance so that the attacker gets to know the target systems and the potential vulnerabilities that can be exploited in the attack.

choices.label: B
choices.text: gaining access.
choices.justification: When attackers discover potential vulnerabilities through information gathering, they attempt to gain access.

choices.label: C
choices.text: denying services.
choices.justification: An attacker usually launches a denial of service as one of the last steps in the attack.

choices.label: D
choices.text: evading detection.
choices.justification: When attackers have gained access and possibly infected the victim with a rootkit, they delete audit logs and take other steps to hide their tracks.

questions.text: What is the BEST approach to mitigate the risk of a phishing attack?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's security awareness training program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intrusion detection
choices.justification: Intrusion detection systems (IDSs) capture network or host traffic for analysis and may detect malicious activity but are not generally effective against phishing attacks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Security assessment
choices.justification: Assessing security does not mitigate the risk. Phishing is based on social engineering and often distributed through email.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Strong authentication
choices.justification: Phishing attacks can be mounted in various ways, often through email; strong two-factor authentication cannot mitigate most types of phishing attacks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: User education
choices.justification: The best way to mitigate the risk of phishing is to educate users to take caution with suspicious Internet communications and not to trust them until verified. Users may require regular training to recognize suspicious web pages and email because the means and methods of threat actors evolve.

questions.text: Which of the following features of a public key infrastructure is MOST closely associated with proving that an online transaction was authorized by a specific customer?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nonrepudiation
choices.justification: Nonrepudiation, achieved with digital signatures, prevents the senders from later denying that they generated and sent the message.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encryption
choices.justification: Encryption plays a role in creating digital signatures, which are used to provide nonrepudiation, but encryption is also used for other purposes, whereas nonrepudiation is entirely concerned with ensuring that specific actions can be traced to specific actors in a manner beyond reasonable doubt.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Authentication
choices.justification: Authentication is necessary to establish the identification of all parties to a communication but does not play a central role in the scenario described.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Integrity
choices.justification: Integrity ensures that transactions are accurate but does not provide the identification of the customer.

questions.text: The information security policy that states “each individual must have his/her badge read at every controlled door” addresses which of the following attack methods?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Piggybacking
choices.justification: Piggybacking refers to unauthorized persons following authorized persons, either physically or virtually, into restricted areas. This policy addresses the polite behavior problem of holding doors open for a stranger. If every employee must have their badge read at every controlled door, no unauthorized person can enter the sensitive area.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shoulder surfing
choices.justification: Shoulder surfing (looking over the shoulder of a person to view sensitive information on a screen or desk) would not be prevented by the implementation of this policy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dumpster diving
choices.justification: Dumpster diving, looking through organization’s trash for valuable information, can be done outside the enterprise’s physical perimeter; therefore, this policy would not address this attack method.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impersonation
choices.justification: Impersonation refers to an unauthorized person acting as an employee, trying to retrieve the desired information. Some forms of social engineering attacks can join an impersonation attack and piggybacking, but this information security policy does not address the impersonation attack.

questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes the role of a directory server in a public key infrastructure?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encrypts the information transmitted over the network
choices.justification: Encrypting the information transmitted over the network is a role performed by a security server.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Makes other users’ certificates available to applications
choices.justification: A directory server makes other users’ certificates available to applications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Facilitates the implementation of a password policy
choices.justification: Facilitating the implementation of a password policy is not relevant to public key infrastructure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stores certificate revocation lists
choices.justification: Storing certificate revocation lists is a role performed by a security server.

questions.text: A certificate (certification) authority (CA) can delegate the processes of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: revocation and suspension of a subscriber’s certificate.
choices.justification: Revocation and suspension of the subscriber certificate are functions of the subscriber certificate life cycle management, which the certificate (certification) authority (CA) must perform.

choices.label: B
choices.text: generation and distribution of the CA public key.
choices.justification: Generation and distribution of the CA public key is a part of the CA key life cycle management process and, as such, cannot be delegated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: establishing a link between the requesting entity and its public key.
choices.justification: Establishing a link between the requesting entity and its public key is a function of a registration authority. This may or may not be performed by a CA; therefore, this function can be delegated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: issuing and distributing subscriber certificates.
choices.justification: Issuance and distribution of the subscriber certificate are functions of the subscriber certificate life cycle management, which the CA must perform.

questions.text: When using public key encryption to secure data being transmitted across a network:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: the keys used to encrypt and decrypt the data are public.
choices.justification: The public and private keys always work as a pair—if a public key is used to encrypt a message, the corresponding private key MUST be used to decrypt the message.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the key used to encrypt the data is private, but the key used to decrypt the data is public.
choices.justification: If the message is encrypted with a private key, that will provide proof of origin but not message security or confidentiality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the key used to encrypt the data is public, but the key used to decrypt the data is private.
choices.justification: Public key encryption, also known as asymmetric key cryptography, uses a public key to encrypt the message and a private key to decrypt it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the keys used to encrypt and decrypt the data are private.
choices.justification: Using two private keys is not possible with asymmetric encryption.

questions.text: In a public key infrastructure, a registration authority:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: verifies information supplied by the subject requesting a certificate.
choices.justification: A registration authority is responsible for verifying information supplied by the subject requesting a certificate and verifies the requestor’s right to request a certificate on behalf of themselves or their organization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: issues the certificate after the required attributes are verified and the keys are generated.
choices.justification: Certification authorities, not registration authorities, issue certificates after verification of the information has been completed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: digitally signs a message to achieve nonrepudiation of the signed message.
choices.justification: The sender who has control of his/her private key signs the message, not the registration authority.

choices.label: D
choices.text: registers signed messages to protect them from future repudiation.
choices.justification: Registering signed messages is not a task performed by registration authorities.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST significant function of an enterprise public key infrastructure and certificate authority employing X.509 digital certificates?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: It provides the public/private key set for the encryption and signature services used by email and file space.
choices.justification: Although some email applications depend on public key infrastructure (PKI)-issued certificates for nonrepudiation, the purpose of PKI is to provide authentication of the individual and link an individual with their private key. The certificate (certification) authority (CA) does not ordinarily create the user’s private key.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It binds a digital certificate and its public key to an individual subscriber’s identity.
choices.justification: PKI is primarily used to gain assurance that protected data or services originated from a legitimate source. The process to ensure the validity of the subscriber identity by linking to the digital certificate/public key is strict and rigorous.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It provides the authoritative source for employee identity and personal details.
choices.justification: Personal details are not stored in or provided by components in the PKI.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It provides the authoritative authentication source for object access.
choices.justification: Authentication services within operating systems and applications may be built on PKI-issued certificates, but PKI does not provide authentication services for object access.

questions.text: Digital signatures require the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: signer to have a public key and the receiver to have a private key.
choices.justification: If a sender encrypts a message with a public key, it will provide confidential transmission to the receiver with the private key.

choices.label: B
choices.text: signer to have a private key and the receiver to have a public key.
choices.justification: Digital signatures are intended to verify to a recipient the integrity of the data and the identity of the sender. The digital signature standard is based on the sender encrypting a digest of the message with their private key and the receiver validating the message with the public key.

choices.label: C
choices.text: signer and receiver to have a public key.
choices.justification: Asymmetric key cryptography always works with key pairs. Therefore, a message encrypted with a public key can only be opened with a private key.

choices.label: D
choices.text: signer and receiver to have a private key.
choices.justification: If both the sender and receiver have a private key there would be no way to validate the digital signature.

questions.text: Which of the following manages the digital certificate life cycle to ensure that adequate security and controls exist in digital signature applications related to ecommerce?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Registration authority
choices.justification: A registration authority is an optional entity that is responsible for the administrative tasks associated with registering the end entity that is the subject of the certificate issued by the certificate (certification) authority (CA).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Certificate authority (CA)
choices.justification: The CA maintains a directory of digital certificates for the reference of those receiving them. It manages the certificate life cycle, including certificate directory maintenance and certificate revocation list (CRL) maintenance and publication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Certification revocation list
choices.justification: A CRL is an instrument for checking the continued validity of the certificates for which the CA has responsibility. A certificate that is put on a CRL can no longer be trusted.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Certification practice statement
choices.justification: A certification practice statement is a detailed set of rules governing the certificate authority’s operations.

questions.text: Which of the following would an information systems (IS) auditor consider a weakness when performing an audit of an enterprise that uses a public key infrastructure with digital certificates for its business-to-consumer transactions via the Internet?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Customers are widely dispersed geographically, but the certificate (certification) authority (CA) are not.
choices.justification: It is common to use a single certificate (certification) authority (CA). They do not need to be geographically dispersed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Customers can make their transactions from any computer or mobile device.
choices.justification: The use of public key infrastructure and certificates allows flexible secure communications from many devices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The CA has several data processing subcenters to administer certificates.
choices.justification: The CA will often have redundancy and failover capabilities to alternate data centers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The enterprise is the owner of the CA.
choices.justification: If the CA belongs to the same enterprise, this poses a risk. The management of a CA must be based on trusted and secure procedures. If the enterprise has not set in place the controls to manage the registration, distribution and revocation of certificates, this can lead to a compromise of the certificates and loss of trust.

questions.text: The purpose of a mantrap controlling access to a computer facility is PRIMARILY to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: prevent piggybacking.
choices.justification: The intended purpose of a mantrap controlling access to a computer facility is primarily to prevent piggybacking.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prevent toxic gases from entering the data center.
choices.justification: Preventing toxic gases from entering the data center can be accomplished with a single self-closing door.

choices.label: C
choices.text: starve a fire of oxygen.
choices.justification: Starving a fire of oxygen can be accomplished with a single self-closing fire door.

choices.label: D
choices.text: prevent rapid movement in or out of the facility.
choices.justification: A rapid exit may be necessary in some circumstances (e.g., a fire).

questions.text: Which of the following public key infrastructure (PKI) elements describes the procedure for disabling a compromised private key?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Certificate revocation list
choices.justification: The certificate revocation list is a list of certificates that have been revoked before their scheduled expiration date.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Certification practice statement
choices.justification: The certification practice statement is the how-to document used in policy-based public key infrastructure (PKI).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Certificate policy
choices.justification: The certificate policy sets the requirements that are subsequently implemented by the certification practice statement (CPS).

choices.label: D
choices.text: PKI disclosure statement
choices.justification: The PKI disclosure statement covers critical items, such as the warranties, limitations and obligations that legally bind each party.

questions.text: Neural networks are effective in detecting fraud because they can:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: discover new trends because they are inherently linear.
choices.justification: Neural networks are inherently nonlinear.

choices.label: B
choices.text: solve problems where large and general sets of training data are not obtainable.
choices.justification: Neural networks do not work well at solving problems for which sufficiently large and general sets of training data are not obtainable.

choices.label: C
choices.text: address problems that require consideration of a large number of input variables.
choices.justification: Neural networks can be used to attack problems that require consideration of numerous input variables. They are capable of capturing relationships and patterns often missed by other statistical methods, but they do not discover new trends.

choices.label: D
choices.text: make assumptions about the shape of any curve relating variables to the output.
choices.justification: Neural networks make no assumption about the shape of any curve relating variables to the output.

questions.text: When protecting an enterprise’s IT systems, which of the following is normally the next line of defense after the network firewall has been compromised?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Personal firewall
choices.justification: Personal firewalls are later in the defensive strategy, being located on the endpoints.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antivirus programs
choices.justification: Antivirus programs are installed on endpoints and on the network, but the next layer of defense after a firewall is an intrusion detection system (IDS)/intrusion prevention system (IPS).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intrusion detection system (IDS)
choices.justification: An IDS is the next line of defense after the firewall. It detects anomalies in the network/server activity and tries to detect the perpetrator.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Virtual local area network (VLAN) configuration
choices.justification: Virtual local area network (VLAN) configurations are not intended to compensate for a compromise of the firewall. They are an architectural good practice.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing a software-based firewall configuration. Which of the following represents the GREATEST vulnerability?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: An implicit deny rule as the last rule in the rule base
choices.justification: Configuring a firewall with an implicit deny rule is common practice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Installation on an operating system configured with default settings
choices.justification: Default settings of most equipment—including operating systems—are often published and provide an intruder with predictable configuration information, which allows easier system compromise. To mitigate this risk, firewall software should be installed on a system using a hardened operating system that has limited functionality, providing only the services necessary to support the firewall software.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rules permitting or denying access to systems or networks
choices.justification: A firewall configuration should have rules allowing or denying access according to policy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Configuration as a virtual private network (VPN) endpoint
choices.justification: A firewall is often set up as the endpoint for a virtual private network (VPN).

questions.text: An enterprise is planning to install a network-based intrusion detection system (IDS) to protect the website that it hosts. Where should the device be installed?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: On the local network
choices.justification: Although an intrusion detection system (IDS) can be installed on the local network to ensure that systems are not subject to internal attacks, an enterprise’s public web server would not normally be installed on the local network, but rather in the demilitarized zone (DMZ).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Outside the firewall
choices.justification: It is not unusual to place a network IDS outside of the firewall just to watch the traffic that is reaching the firewall, but this would not be used to specifically protect the web application.

choices.label: C
choices.text: In the demilitarized zone
choices.justification: Network-based IDSs detect attack attempts by monitoring network traffic. A public web server is typically placed on the protected network segment known as the DMZ. An IDS installed in the DMZ detects and reports on malicious activity originating from the Internet and the internal network, thus allowing the administrator to act.

choices.label: D
choices.text: On the server that hosts the website
choices.justification: A host-based IDS would be installed on the web server, but a network-based IDS would not.

questions.text: When reviewing an intrusion detection system, an information systems (IS) auditor should be MOST concerned about which of the following?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: High number of false-positive alarms
choices.justification: Although the number of false positives is a serious issue, the problem will be known and can be corrected.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low coverage of network traffic
choices.justification: Cybersecurity attacks might not be timely identified if only a small portion of network traffic is analyzed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Network performance downgrade
choices.justification: An intrusion detection system (IDS) might decrease an overall network performance; however, it is a secondary risk in this case.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Default detection settings
choices.justification: It is a good practice to customize IDS settings to specific network perimeter, however, there is a higher likelihood of missing the attacks due to insufficient network coverage.

questions.text: After installing a network, an enterprise implemented a vulnerability assessment tool to identify possible weaknesses. Which type of reporting poses the MOST serious risk associated with such tools?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Differential
choices.justification: The differential reporting function provided by this tool compares scan results over a period of time.

choices.label: B
choices.text: False-positive
choices.justification: False-positive reporting is one in which the system falsely reports a vulnerability. Controls may be in place, but are evaluated as weak, which should prompt a rechecking of the controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: False-negative
choices.justification: False-negative reporting on weaknesses means the control weaknesses in the network are not identified and, therefore, may not be addressed, leaving the network vulnerable to attack.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Less-detail
choices.justification: Less-detail reporting requires additional tools or analysis to determine the existence and severity of vulnerabilities.

questions.text: When conducting a penetration test of an IT system, an enterprise should be MOST concerned with:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: the confidentiality of the report.
choices.justification: A penetration test report is a sensitive document because it lists the vulnerabilities of the target system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: finding all weaknesses in the system.
choices.justification: Finding all possible weaknesses is not possible in complex information systems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: restoring systems to the original state.
choices.justification: After the test is completed, the systems must be restored to their original state. In performing the test, changes may have been made to firewall rules, user IDs may have been created, or false files may have been uploaded. These must all be cleaned up before the test is completed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: logging changes made to production systems.
choices.justification: All changes made should be recorded, but the most important concern is to ensure that the changes are reversed at the end of the test.

questions.text: A firewall is being deployed at a new location. Which of the following is the MOST important factor in ensuring a successful deployment?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: logs frequently
choices.justification: A regular review of log files does not start until the deployment has been completed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Testing and validating the rules
choices.justification: A mistake in the rule set can render a firewall ineffective or insecure. Therefore, testing and validating the rules is the most important factor in ensuring a successful deployment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Training a local administrator at the new location
choices.justification: Training a local administrator may not be necessary if the firewalls are managed from a central location.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sharing firewall administrative duties
choices.justification: Having multiple administrators is a good idea, but not the most important for successful deployment.

questions.text: The MOST important factor in planning a black box penetration test is:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: the documentation of the planned testing procedure.
choices.justification: A penetration test should be carefully planned and executed, but the most important factor is proper approvals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a realistic evaluation of the environment architecture to determine scope.
choices.justification: In a black box penetration test, the environment is not known to the testing organization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: knowledge by the management staff of the client organization.
choices.justification: Black box penetration testing assumes no prior knowledge of the infrastructure to be tested. Testers simulate an attack from someone who is unfamiliar with the system. It is important to have management knowledge of the proceedings so that if the test is identified by the monitoring systems, the legality of the actions can be determined quickly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: scheduling and deciding on the timed length of the test.
choices.justification: A test must be scheduled to minimize the risk of affecting critical operations; however, this is part of working with the management of the organization.

questions.text: Which of the following antispam filtering methods has the LOWEST possibility of false-positive alerts?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rule-based
choices.justification: Rule-based filtering will trigger false-positive alert each time a key word is met in the message.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Checksum based
choices.justification: The advantage of this type of checksum-based filtering is that it lets ordinary users help identify spam, and not just administrators, thus, vastly increasing the pool of spam fighters. The disadvantage is that spammers can insert unique invisible gibberish—known as hashbusters—into the middle of each of their messages, thus making each message unique and having a different checksum. This leads to a race between the developers of the checksum software and the developers of the spam-generating software.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heuristic filtering
choices.justification: A heuristic is a technique designed for solving a problem more quickly when classic methods are too slow, or for finding an approximate solution when classic methods fail to find any exact solution. This is achieved by trading optimality, completeness, accuracy or precision for speed. In a way, it can be considered a shortcut.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Statistic-based
choices.justification: Statistical filtering analyzes the frequency of each word within the message and then evaluates the message as a whole. Therefore, it can ignore a suspicious keyword if the entire message is within normal bounds. However, statistical filtering is prone to false-positive alerts.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor selects a server for a penetration test that will be carried out by a technical specialist. Which of the following is MOST important?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: The tools used to conduct the test
choices.justification: The choice of tools is important to ensure a valid test and prevent system failure; however, the permission of the owner is most important.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Certifications held by the IS auditor
choices.justification: Whether the information systems (IS) auditor holds certifications is not relevant to the effectiveness of the test.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Permission from the data owner of the server
choices.justification: The data owner should be informed of the risk associated with a penetration test, the timing of the test, what types of tests are to be conducted and other relevant details.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An intrusion detection system (IDS) is enabled
choices.justification: An intrusion detection system (IDS) is not required for a penetration test.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor evaluates security controls’ effectiveness by simulating real-world attacks during an audit review. Which of the following security testing techniques is the auditor using?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vulnerability scanning
choices.justification: Vulnerability scanning is the process of discovering, analyzing and reporting on security flaws and vulnerabilities. Vulnerability scans are conducted via automated vulnerability scanning tools to identify potential risk exposures and attack vectors across enterprise networks, hardware, software and systems, and does not require a simulation of real-world attacks.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Security auditing
choices.justification: A security audit is a systematic and comprehensive evaluation of an enterprise’s information systems, networks and physical infrastructure and does not require a simulation of real-world attacks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Code review
choices.justification: Code reviews are methodical assessments of code designed to identify bugs, increase code quality and help developers learn the source code. Code review does not require a simulation of real-world attacks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Penetration testing
choices.justification: Penetration testing focuses on evaluating the effectiveness of security controls by simulating real-world attacks. Penetration testing involves active testing and exploitation of vulnerabilities in the system to assess the enterprise’s resilience against attacks and identify areas for improvement.

questions.text: Which of the following antivirus software implementation strategies is the MOST effective in an interconnected enterprise network?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate risk associated with shadow IT and end-user computing (EUC) to determine effectiveness of compensating controls

choices.label: A
choices.text: Server-based antivirus software
choices.justification: An effective antivirus solution must be a combination of server-, network- and perimeter-based scanning and protection.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enterprise-based antivirus software
choices.justification: An important means of controlling the spread of viruses is to deploy an enterprisewide antivirus solution that will monitor and analyze traffic at many points. This provides a layered defense model that is more likely to detect malware regardless of how it comes into the enterprise— through a USB or portable storage, a network, an infected download or malicious web application.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Workstation-based antivirus software
choices.justification: Only checking for a virus on workstations is not adequate because malware can infect many network devices or servers as well.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perimeter-based antivirus software
choices.justification: Because malware can enter an enterprise through many different methods, only checking for malware at the perimeter is not enough to protect the enterprise.

questions.text: When reviewing the configuration of network devices, an information systems (IS) auditor should FIRST identify:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's problem and incident management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: the good practices for the type of network devices deployed.
choices.justification: After understanding the devices in the network, a good practice for using the device should be reviewed to ensure that there are no anomalies within the configuration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: whether components of the network are missing.
choices.justification: Identification of which component is missing can only be known after reviewing and understanding the topology and a good practice for deployment of the device in the network.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the importance of the network devices in the topology.
choices.justification: The first step is to understand the importance and role of the network device within the enterprise ’s network topology.

choices.label: D
choices.text: whether subcomponents of the network are being used appropriately.
choices.justification: Identification of which subcomponent is being used inappropriately can only be known after reviewing and understanding the topology and a good practice for deployment of the device in the network.

questions.text: When installing an intrusion detection system (IDS), which of the following is MOST important?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: Properly locating it in the network architecture
choices.justification: Properly locating an intrusion detection system (IDS) in the network is the most important step during installation. A poorly located IDS can leave key areas of the network unprotected.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Preventing denial-of-service (DoS) attacks
choices.justification: A network IDS monitors network traffic and a host-based IDS monitors activity on the host, but it has no capability of preventing a denial-of-service (DoS) attack.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identifying messages that need to be quarantined
choices.justification: Configuring an IDS can be a challenge because it may require the IDS to learn what normal activity is, but the most important part of the installation is to install it in the right places.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Minimizing the rejection errors
choices.justification: An IDS is only a monitoring device and does not reject traffic. Rejection errors apply to a biometric device.

questions.text: Over the long term, which of the following has the greatest potential to improve the security incident response process?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's problem and incident management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: A walk-through review of incident response procedures
choices.justification: A walk-through is a good first step to evaluate the incident response plan, but the lessons learned from incidents will provide more meaningful long-term benefits.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Simulation exercises performed by the security incident response team
choices.justification: Simulation exercises to find the gaps and shortcomings in the actual incident response processes help to improve the process over time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ongoing security training for users
choices.justification: Training the users and members of the incident response team improves the effectiveness of the team but learning from the lessons of previous incidents generates the greatest benefit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Documenting responses to an incident
choices.justification: Documenting all incidents is important to allow later analysis and review but is not as important as the results of the analysis.

questions.text: Which of the following types of penetration tests effectively evaluates the incident handling and response capability of the system administrator?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's problem and incident management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Targeted testing
choices.justification: In targeted testing, penetration testers are provided with information related to target and network design and the target’s IT team is aware of the testing activities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Internal testing
choices.justification: Internal testing refers to attacks and control circumvention attempts on the target from within the perimeter. The system administrator is typically aware of the testing activities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Double-blind testing
choices.justification: In double-blind testing, the penetration tester has little or limited knowledge about the target system, and personnel at the target site are not informed that a test is being performed. Because the administrator and security staff at the target are not aware of the test, it can effectively evaluate the incident handling and response capability of the system administrator.

choices.label: D
choices.text: External testing
choices.justification: External testing is a generic term that refers to attacks and control circumvention attempts on the target from outside the target system. The system administrator may or may not be aware of the testing activities, so this is not the correct answer. (Note: Rather than concentrating on specific terms, certified information systems auditor (CISA) candidates should understand the differences between various types of penetration testing.)

questions.text: Which of the following is the MAIN reason an enterprise should have an incident response plan? The plan helps to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's problem and incident management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: ensure prompt communication of adverse events to relevant management.
choices.justification: Incident response plans generally deal with a wide range of possible issues. Although it is important to have proper communication of an adverse event within the enterprise, the primary objective is to reduce impact of incidents.

choices.label: B
choices.text: contain costs related to maintaining disaster recovery plan capabilities.
choices.justification: An effective incident response plan can minimize damage to the enterprise, which minimizes costs, but the main purpose of the incident response plan is to minimize damage. Possible damage can include nonfinancial metrics, such as damage to the enterprise’s reputation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: ensure that customers are promptly notified of issues, such as security breaches.
choices.justification: Although an incident response plan includes elements such as when and how to contact customers about a significant incident, the primary purpose of the plan is to minimize the impact.

choices.label: D
choices.text: minimize the duration and impact of system outages and security incidents.
choices.justification: An incident response plan helps minimize the impact of an incident because it provides a controlled response to incidents. The phases of the plan include planning, detection, evaluation, containment, eradication, escalation, response, recovery, reporting, post-incident review and a review of lessons learned.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST important action in recovering from a cybersecurity incident?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's problem and incident management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activating an incident response team
choices.justification: Hopefully, the incident response team and procedures were set up prior to the incident. The first step is to activate the team, contain the incident and keep the business operational.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Appointing cyberforensic investigators
choices.justification: When a cybersecurity incident is suspected, cyberforensic investigators should be used to set up alarms, catch intruders within the network, and track and trace them over the Internet. The use of cyberforensic experts is only done after the incident is identified.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Executing a business continuity plan (BCP)
choices.justification: The most important objective in recovering from a cyberincident is to keep the business operational, but most attacks will not require the activation or use of the business continuity plan (BCP).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Preserving evidence
choices.justification: The primary objective for the business is to stay in business. In a noncriminal investigation this may even mean that some evidence is lost.
