choices.justification: A VPN does support security policies related to secure communications, but its primary purpose is to protect data in transit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Detecting misuse or mistakes
choices.justification: A VPN does not check the content of packets, so it cannot detect misuse or mistakes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regulating access
choices.justification: A VPN is not used to regulate access. A user may have to log in to use a VPN, but that is not the purpose of the VPN.

questions.text: The reason a certification and accreditation process is performed on critical systems is to ensure that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: security compliance has been technically evaluated.
choices.justification: Certified and accredited systems are systems that have had their security compliance technically evaluated for running in a specific environment and configuration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: data have been encrypted and are ready to be stored.
choices.justification: Certification tests security functionality, including encryption where that is required, but that is not the primary objective of the certification and accreditation (C&A) process.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the systems have been tested to run on different platforms.
choices.justification: Certified systems are evaluated to run in a specific environment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the systems have followed the phases of a waterfall model.
choices.justification: A waterfall model is a software development methodology and not a reason for performing a C&A process.

questions.text: An enterprise stores and transmits sensitive customer information within a secure wired network. It has implemented an additional wireless local area network (WLAN) to support general-purpose staff computing needs. A few employees with WLAN access have legitimate business reasons for also accessing customer information. Which of the following represents the BEST control to ensure separation of the two networks?
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: Establish two physically separate networks.
choices.justification: Although having two physically separate networks ensures the security of customer data, it makes it impossible for authorized wireless users to access that data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implement virtual local area network (VLAN) segmentation.
choices.justification: Although a virtual local area network (VLAN) would provide separation of the two networks, it is possible, with sufficient knowledge, for an attacker to gain access to one VLAN from the other.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Install a dedicated router between the two networks.
choices.justification: A dedicated router between the two networks would separate them; however, this would be less secure than a firewall.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Install a firewall between the networks.
choices.justification: In this case, a firewall can be used as a strong control to allow authorized users on the wireless network to access the wired network.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor performing a telecommunication access control review should be concerned PRIMARILY with the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: maintenance of access logs of usage of various system resources.
choices.justification: The maintenance of access logs of usage of system resources is a detective control. A preventive control should be used first.

choices.label: B
choices.text: authorization and authentication of the user prior to granting access to system resources.
choices.justification: The authorization and authentication of users before granting them access to system resources (networks, servers, applications, etc.) is the most significant aspect in a telecommunication access control review because it is a preventive control. Weak controls at this level can affect all other aspects of security.

choices.label: C
choices.text: adequate protection of stored data on servers by encryption or other means.
choices.justification: The adequate protection of data being stored on servers by encryption or other means is a method of protecting stored information and is not a network access issue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: accountability system and the ability to identify any terminal accessing system resources.
choices.justification: The accountability system and the ability to identify any terminal accessing system resources deal with controlling access through the identification of a terminal or device attempting to connect to the network. This is called node authentication and is not as good as authenticating the user sitting at that node.

questions.text: After reviewing its business processes, a large enterprise is deploying a new web application based on Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technology. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate approach for implementing access control that will facilitate security management of the VoIP web application?
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fine-grained access control
choices.justification: Fine-grained access control on VoIP web applications does not scale to enterprisewide systems because it is primarily based on individual user identities and their specific technical privileges.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Role-based access control
choices.justification: Authorization in this case can best be addressed by role-based access control (RBAC) technology. RBAC controls access according to job roles or functions. RBAC is easy to manage and can enforce strong and efficient access controls in large-scale web environments including VoIP implementation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Access control lists
choices.justification: Access control lists on Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) web applications do not scale to enterprisewide systems because they are primarily based on individual user identities and their specific technical privileges.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Network/service access control
choices.justification: Network/service addresses VoIP availability but does not address application-level access or authorization.

questions.text: Java applets and Active X controls are distributed programs that execute in the background of a client web browser. This practice is considered reasonable when:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: a firewall exists.
choices.justification: There should always be a firewall on an Internet connection; however, whether to allow active models is a decision made depending on the source of the module.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a secure web connection is used.
choices.justification: A secure web connection provides confidentiality. Neither a secure web connection nor a firewall can identify an executable file as friendly.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the source of the executable file is certain.
choices.justification: Acceptance of these mechanisms should be based on established trust. The control is provided by only knowing the source and then allowing the acceptance of the applets. Hostile applets can be received from anywhere.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the host website is part of the enterprise.
choices.justification: Hosting the website as part of the enterprise is impractical. The client will accept the program if the parameters are established to do so.

questions.text: The BEST filter rule for protecting a network from being used as an amplifier in a denial-of-service attack is to deny all:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: outgoing traffic with source addresses external to the network.
choices.justification: Outgoing traffic with an internet protocol (IP) source address different than the internal IP range in the network is invalid. In most cases, it signals a denial-of-service attack originated by an internal user or by a previously compromised internal machine; in both cases, applying this filter will stop the infected machine from participating in the attack.

choices.label: B
choices.text: incoming traffic with discernible spoofed internet protocol (IP) source addresses.
choices.justification: Denying incoming traffic does not prevent an internal machine from participating in an attack on an outside target.

choices.label: C
choices.text: incoming traffic that includes options set in the Internet Protocol.
choices.justification: Incoming traffic has the IP options set according to the type of traffic. This is a normal condition.

choices.label: D
choices.text: incoming traffic whose destination address belongs to critical hosts.
choices.justification: Denying incoming traffic to internal hosts prevents legitimate traffic.

questions.text: A characteristic of User Datagram Protocol (UDP) in network communications is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: packets may arrive out of order.
choices.justification: User Datagram Protocol (UDP) uses a simple transmission model without implicit handshaking routines for providing reliability, ordering or data integrity. Thus, UDP provides an unreliable service, and datagrams may arrive out of order, appear duplicated or get dropped.

choices.label: B
choices.text: increased communication latency.
choices.justification: The advantage of UDP is that the lack of error checking allows for reduced latency. Time-sensitive applications, such as online video or audio, often use UDP because of the reduced latency of this protocol.

choices.label: C
choices.text: incompatibility with packet broadcast.
choices.justification: UDP is compatible with packet broadcast (sending to all on the local network) and multicasting (sending to all subscribers).

choices.label: D
choices.text: error correction may slow down processing.
choices.justification: UDP assumes that error checking and correction is either not necessary or performed in the application, avoiding the overhead of such processing at the network interface level.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor performing detailed network assessments and access control reviews should FIRST:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: determine the points of entry into the network.
choices.justification: In performing detailed network assessments and access control reviews, an information systems (IS) auditor should first determine the points of entry to the system and review the points of entry, accordingly, for appropriate controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: evaluate users’ access authorization.
choices.justification: Evaluation of user access authorization is an implementation issue for appropriate controls for the points of entry.

choices.label: C
choices.text: assess users’ identification and authorization.
choices.justification: Assessment of user identification and authorization are implementation issues for appropriate controls for the points of entry.

choices.label: D
choices.text: evaluate the domain-controlling server configuration.
choices.justification: Evaluation of the domain-controlling server configuration is not the first area to be reviewed. It will be reviewed after the network entry points have been identified.

questions.text: Validated digital signatures in an email software application will:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: help detect unauthorized email.
choices.justification: Validated electronic signatures are based on qualified certificates that are created by a certificate authority, with the technical standards required to ensure the key can neither be forced nor reproduced in a reasonable time. Such certificates are only delivered through a registration authority after proof of identity has been passed. Using strong signatures in email traffic, nonrepudiation can be assured, and a sender can be tracked. The recipient can configure his/her email server or client to automatically delete emails from specific senders.

choices.label: B
choices.text: provide confidentiality.
choices.justification: For confidentiality issues, one must use encryption, not a signature.

choices.label: C
choices.text: add to the workload of gateway servers.
choices.justification: Without any filters directly applied on mail gateway servers to block traffic without strong signatures, the workload will not increase. Using filters directly on a gateway server will result in overhead less than antivirus software imposes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: significantly reduce available bandwidth.
choices.justification: Digital signatures are only a few bytes in size and will not slash bandwidth. Even if gateway servers were to check certificate revocation lists, there is little overhead.

questions.text: Which of the following line media would provide the BEST security for a telecommunication network?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Broadband network digital transmission
choices.justification: The secure use of broadband communications is subject to whether the network is shared with other users, the data are encrypted and the risk of network interruption.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Baseband network
choices.justification: A baseband network is one that is usually shared with many other users and requires encryption of traffic but still may allow some traffic analysis by an attacker.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dialup
choices.justification: A dial-up line is fairly secure because it is a private connection, but it is too slow to be considered for most commercial applications today.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dedicated lines
choices.justification: Dedicated lines are set apart for a particular user or organization. Because there is no sharing of lines or intermediate entry points, the risk of interception or disruption of telecommunications messages is lower.

questions.text: In transport mode, the use of the encapsulation security payload (ESP), protocol is advantageous over the authentication header protocol because it provides:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: connectionless integrity.
choices.justification: Both forms of Internet Protocol security (IPSec), authentication header (AH) and encapsulation security payload (ESP), provide connectionless integrity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: data origin authentication.
choices.justification: Both AH and ESP authenticate data origin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: antireplay service.
choices.justification: The time stamps used in IPSec prevent replay attacks.

choices.label: D
choices.text: confidentiality.
choices.justification: Only the ESP protocol provides confidentiality via encryption.

questions.text: The potential for unauthorized system access by way of terminals or workstations within an enterprise’s facility is increased when:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: connecting points are available in the facility to connect laptops to the network.
choices.justification: Any person with wrongful intentions can connect a laptop to the network. The insecure connecting points make unauthorized access possible if the individual has knowledge of a valid user ID and password. The other choices are controls for preventing unauthorized network access.

choices.label: B
choices.text: users take precautions to keep their passwords confidential.
choices.justification: If system passwords are not readily available for intruders to use, they must guess, introducing an additional factor and requires time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: terminals with password protection are located in insecure locations.
choices.justification: System passwords provide protection against unauthorized use of terminals located in insecure locations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: terminals are located within the facility in small clusters under the supervision of an administrator.
choices.justification: Supervision is a very effective control when used to monitor access to a small operating unit or production resources.

questions.text: An organization requests that an information systems (IS) auditor provide a recommendation to enhance the security and reliability of its Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) system and data traffic. Which of the following meets this objective?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate whether effective processes are in place to support end users

choices.label: A
choices.text: VoIP infrastructure needs to be segregated using virtual local area networks.
choices.justification: Segregating the Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) traffic using virtual local area networks (VLANs) best protects the VoIP infrastructure from network-based attacks, potential eavesdropping and network traffic issues (which helps to ensure uptime).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Buffers need to be introduced at the VoIP endpoints.
choices.justification: The use of packet buffers at VoIP endpoints is a method to maintain call quality, not a security method.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ensure that end-to-end encryption is enabled in the VoIP system.
choices.justification: Encryption is used when VoIP calls use the Internet (not the local area network (LAN)) for transport because the assumption is that the physical security of the building and the Ethernet switch and VLAN security are adequate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure that emergency backup power is available for all parts of the VoIP infrastructure.
choices.justification: The design of the network and the proper implementation of VLANs are more critical than ensuring that all devices are protected by emergency power.

questions.text: Which of the following types of firewalls provide the GREATEST degree and granularity of control?
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: Screening router
choices.justification: Screening routers and packet filters work at the protocol, service and/or port level. This means that they analyze packets from layers 3 and 4 and not from higher levels.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Packet filter
choices.justification: A packet filter works at too low a level of the communication stack to provide granular control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Application gateway
choices.justification: The application gateway is similar to a circuit gateway, but it has specific proxies for each service. To handle web services, it has a hypertext transmission protocol (HTTP) proxy that acts as an intermediary between externals and internals but is specifically for HTTP. This means that it not only checks the packet internet protocol (IP) addresses (open systems interconnection (OSI) Layer 3) and the ports it is directed to (in this case port 80, or layer 4), but also checks every HTTP command (OSI Layers 5 and 7). Therefore, it works in a more detailed (granularity) way than the other choices.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Circuit gateway
choices.justification: A circuit gateway is based on a proxy or program that acts as an intermediary between external and internal accesses. This means that, during an external access, instead of opening a single connection to the internal server, two connections are established—one from the external server to the proxy (which conforms the circuit-gateway) and one from the proxy to the internal server. OSI Layers 3 and 4 (IP and Transmission Control Protocol) and some general features from higher protocols are used to perform these tasks.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor finds that conference rooms have active network ports. Which of the following prevents this from being of any concern for the enterprise?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: The enterprise network is using an intrusion prevention system (IPS).
choices.justification: An intrusion prevention system (IPS) may stop an attack, but it is far better to restrict the ability of machines in the conference rooms from being able to access the enterprise network altogether.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The conference rooms part of the network is isolated from the enterprise network.
choices.justification: If the conference rooms have access to the enterprise network, unauthorized users may be able to connect to the enterprise network; therefore, both networks should be isolated either via a firewall or by being physically separated.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A single sign-on (SSO) has been implemented in the enterprise network.
choices.justification: A single sign-on (SSO) solution is used for access control but does not mitigate the risk of unauthorized people having physical access to the enterprise network.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antivirus software is in place to protect the enterprise network.
choices.justification: Antivirus software reduces the impact of possible viruses; however, unauthorized users would still be able to access the enterprise network, which is the biggest risk.

questions.text: An organization has experienced a large amount of traffic being re-routed from its Voice-over Internet Protocol packet network. The organization believes it is a victim of eavesdropping. Which of the following can result in eavesdropping of Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) traffic?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Corruption of the Address Resolution Protocol cache in Ethernet switches
choices.justification: On an Ethernet switch, there is a data table known as the address resolution protocol (ARP) cache that stores mappings between media access control and internet protocol (IP) addresses. During normal operations, Ethernet switches only allow directed traffic to flow between the ports involved in the conversation and no other ports can see that traffic. However, if the ARP cache is intentionally corrupted with an ARP poisoning attack, some Ethernet switches simply flood the directed traffic to all ports of the switch, which can allow an attacker to monitor traffic not normally visible to the port where the attacker was connected, and thereby eavesdrop on Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) traffic.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use of a default administrator password on the analog phone switch
choices.justification: VoIP systems do not use analog switches and inadequate administrator security controls are not an issue.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deploying virtual local area networks VLANs without enabling encryption
choices.justification: VoIP data are not normally encrypted in a local area network (LAN) environment because the controls regarding virtual local area network (VLAN) security are adequate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: End users having access to software tools such as packet sniffer applications
choices.justification: Most software tools, such as packet sniffers, cannot make changes to LAN devices, such as the VLAN configuration of an Ethernet switch used for VoIP. Therefore, the use of software utilities of this type is not a risk.

questions.text: Which of the following is a passive attack to a network?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Message modification
choices.justification: Message modification involves the capturing of a message and making unauthorized changes or deletions, changing the sequence or delaying transmission of captured messages. An attack that modifies the data is an active attack.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Masquerading
choices.justification: Masquerading is an active attack in which the intruder presents an identity other than the original identity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Denial-of-service
choices.justification: Denial-of-service occurs when a computer connected to the Internet is flooded with data and/or requests that must be processed. This is an active attack.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Traffic analysis
choices.justification: Traffic analysis allows a watching threat actor to determine the nature of the flow of traffic between defined hosts, which may allow the threat actor to guess the type of communication taking place without taking an active role.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of the defense in-depth security principle?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Using two firewalls to consecutively check the incoming network traffic
choices.justification: Use of two firewalls does not represent an effective defense in-depth strategy because the same attack can circumvent both devices. By using two different products, the probability of both products having the same vulnerabilities is diminished.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Using a firewall as well as logical access controls on the hosts to control incoming network traffic
choices.justification: Defense in-depth means using different security mechanisms that back up each other. When network traffic passes the firewall unintentionally, the logical access controls form a second line of defense.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lack of physical signs on the outside of a computer center building
choices.justification: Having no physical signs on the outside of a computer center building is a single security measure known as security by obscurity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Using two firewalls in parallel to check different types of incoming traffic
choices.justification: Using two firewalls in parallel to check different types of incoming traffic provides redundancy but is only a single security mechanism and, therefore, no different than having a single firewall checking all traffic.

questions.text: Which of the following preventive controls BEST helps secure a web application?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Password masking
choices.justification: Password masking is a necessary preventive control but is not the best way to secure an application.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Developer training
choices.justification: Of the given choices, teaching developers to write secure code is the best way to secure a web application.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use of encryption
choices.justification: Encryption will protect data but is not sufficient to secure an application because other flaws in coding can compromise the application and data. Ensuring that applications are designed in a secure way is the best way to secure an application. This is accomplished by ensuring that developers are adequately educated on secure coding practices.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vulnerability testing
choices.justification: Vulnerability testing can help to ensure the security of web applications; however, the best preventive control is developer education because building secure applications from the start is more effective.

questions.text: An enterprise has established a guest network for visitor access. Which of the following should be of GREATEST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: A login screen is not displayed for guest users.
choices.justification: Using a web captive portal, which displays a login screen in the user’s web browser, is a good practice to authenticate guests. However, if the guest network is not segregated from the production network, users can introduce malware and potentially gain inappropriate access to systems and information.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The guest network is not segregated from the production network.
choices.justification: The implication of this is that guests have access to the enterprise network. Allowing untrusted users to connect to the enterprise network can introduce malware and potentially allow these individuals inappropriate access to systems and information.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Guest users who are logged in are not isolated from each other.
choices.justification: There are certain platforms in which it is allowable for guests to interact with one another. Also, guests can be warned to use only secured systems, and a policy covering interaction among guests can be created.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A single-factor authentication technique is used to grant access.
choices.justification: Although a multifactor authentication (MFA) technique is preferred, a single-factor authentication method should be adequate if properly implemented.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST control to prevent the deletion of audit logs by unauthorized individuals in an enterprise?
questions.taskStatement: Plan an audit to determine whether information systems are protected, controlled, and provide value to the organization

choices.label: A
choices.text: Actions performed on log files should be tracked in a separate log.
choices.justification: Having additional copies of log file activity does not prevent the original log files from being deleted.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Write access to audit logs should be disabled.
choices.justification: For servers and applications to operate correctly, write access cannot be disabled.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only select personnel should have rights to view or delete audit logs.
choices.justification: Granting audit-log access to only system administrators and security administrators reduces the possibility of these files being deleted.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Backups of audit logs should be performed periodically.
choices.justification: Frequent backups of audit logs does not prevent the logs from being deleted.

questions.text: An enterprise uses a biometric control system for managing access. Which of the following indicates the MOST effective biometric control system?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: The highest equal error rate (EER)
choices.justification: The biometric that has the highest equal error rate (EER) is the most ineffective.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The lowest EER
choices.justification: The EER of a biometric system denotes the percent at which the false-acceptance rate (FAR) is equal to the false-rejection rate (FRR). The biometric that has the lowest EER is the most effective.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A false-rejection rate (FRR) equal to the false-acceptance rate (FAR)
choices.justification: For any biometric, there is a measure at which the FRR will be equal to the FAR. This is the EER.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A FRR equal to the failure-to-enroll rate (FER)
choices.justification: Failure-to-enroll rate (FER) is an aggregate measure of FRR.

questions.text: An organization with extremely high security requirements is evaluating the effectiveness of biometric systems. Which of the following performance indicators is MOST important?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: False-acceptance rate (FAR)
choices.justification: False-acceptance rate (FAR) is the frequency of accepting an unauthorized person as authorized, thereby granting access when it should be denied. In an enterprise with high security requirements, limiting the number of false acceptances is more important than the impact on the false reject rate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Equal-error rate (EER)
choices.justification: Equal-error rate EER (also called the crossover error rate) is the point where the FAR equals the false-rejection rate (FRR). This is the criteria used to measure the optimal accuracy of the biometric system, but, in a highly secure environment, the FAR is more important than the EER.

choices.label: C
choices.text: False-rejection rate (FRR)
choices.justification: FRR denies an authorized person access, but this is less important than the FAR because it is better to deny access to an authorized individual than to grant access to an unauthorized individual.

choices.label: D
choices.text: False-identification rate (FIR)
choices.justification: False-identification rate (FIR) is the probability that an authorized person is identified but is assigned a false ID.

questions.text: The responsibility for authorizing access to a business application system belongs to the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate data classification practices for alignment with the organization's data governance program, privacy program, and applicable external requirements

choices.label: A
choices.text: data owner.
choices.justification: When a business application is developed, a good practice is to assign an information or data owner to the application. The information owner should be responsible for authorizing access to the application itself or to back-end databases for queries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: security administrator.
choices.justification: The security administrator normally does not have responsibility for authorizing access to business applications.

choices.label: C
choices.text: IT security manager.
choices.justification: The IT security manager normally does not have responsibility for authorizing access to business applications.

choices.label: D
choices.text: requestor’s immediate supervisor.
choices.justification: The requestor’s immediate supervisor may share the responsibility for approving user access to a business application system; however, the final responsibility should go to the information owner.

questions.text: There is a concern that the risk of unauthorized access may increase after implementing a single sign-on (SSO) process. To prevent unauthorized access, the MOST important action is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: monitor failed authentication attempts.
choices.justification: Ensuring that all failed authentication attempts are monitored is a good practice but is not a preventive control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: review log files regularly.
choices.justification: the log files can increase the probability of detecting unauthorized access but does not prevent unauthorized access.

choices.label: C
choices.text: deactivate unused accounts promptly.
choices.justification: Ensuring that all unused accounts are deactivated is important; however, unauthorized access may occur via a regularly used account.

choices.label: D
choices.text: mandate a strong password policy.
choices.justification: Strong passwords are important in any environment but take on special importance in an single sign-on (SSO) environment, where a user enters a password only one time and thereafter has general access throughout the environment. Of the options given, only a strong password policy offers broad preventative effects.

questions.text: Which of the following is a form of two-factor user authentication?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: A smart card and personal identification number
choices.justification: A smart card is something that a user has, while a personal identification number paired with the card is something the user knows. This is an example of two-factor authentication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A unique User ID and complex, nondictionary password
choices.justification: Both an ID and a password are something the user knows, so this pairing provides single-factor user authentication regardless of complexity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An iris scan and a fingerprint scan
choices.justification: Both an iris scan and a fingerprint scan are something the user is, so this pairing is not a basis for two-factor user authentication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A magnetic-strip card and a proximity badge
choices.justification: Both a magnetic card and a proximity badge are examples of something a user has, so these are not adequate for two-factor authentication.

questions.text: Which stage of a biometrics system operation should the information systems (IS) auditor review FIRST?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enrollment
choices.justification: The users must first be enrolled to use a biometric device; therefore, the information systems (IS) auditor should review this stage first.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identification
choices.justification: The device captures a physical or behavioral image of the human, identifies the unique features and uses an algorithm to convert them into a string of numbers stored as a template to be used in the matching processes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Verification
choices.justification: A user applying for access will be verified against the stored enrolled value.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Storage
choices.justification: The biometric stores sensitive personal information, so the storage must be secure.

questions.text: Which of the following groups would create the MOST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor if the group has full access to the production database?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Application developers
choices.justification: Application developers having access to the production environment bear the highest risk. Due to their focus on delivery of changes, they tend to bypass quality assurance controls installing deficient changes into the production environment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System administrators
choices.justification: System administrators may require full production access to conduct their administration duties; however, they should be monitored for unauthorized activity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Business users
choices.justification: Business users might not need full access to a database. Such a setup might result in negative scenarios (fraud); however, developers having direct access to production environment is a higher concern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Information security team
choices.justification: The data recovery team needs full access to make sure the complete database is recoverable.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing access controls for a client-server environment should FIRST:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: evaluate the encryption technique.
choices.justification: Evaluating encryption techniques would be performed at a later stage of the review.

choices.label: B
choices.text: identify the network access points.
choices.justification: A client-server environment typically contains several access points and uses distributed techniques, increasing the risk of unauthorized access to data and processing. To evaluate the security of the client server environment, all network access points should be identified.

choices.label: C
choices.text: review the identity management system.
choices.justification: the identity management system would be performed at a later stage of the review.

choices.label: D
choices.text: review the application-level access controls.
choices.justification: the application-level access controls would be performed at a later stage of the review.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is assessing a biometric system used to protect physical access to a data center containing regulated data. Which of the following observations is the GREATEST concern to the auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administrative access to the biometric scanners or the access control system is permitted over a virtual private network.
choices.justification: Generally, virtual private network software provides a secure tunnel so that remote administration functions can be performed. This is not a concern.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Biometric scanners are not installed in restricted areas.
choices.justification: Biometric scanners are best located in restricted areas to prevent tampering, but video surveillance is an acceptable mitigating control. The greatest concern is the lack of a securely encrypted tunnel between the scanners and the access control system.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Data transmitted between the biometric scanners and the access control system do not use a securely encrypted tunnel.
choices.justification: Data transmitted between the biometric scanners and the access control system should use a securely encrypted tunnel to protect the confidentiality of the biometric data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Biometric system risk analysis was last conducted three years ago.
choices.justification: The biometric risk analysis should be reperformed periodically, but an analysis performed three years ago is not necessarily a cause for concern.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST access control procedure?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: The data owner formally authorizes access and an administrator implements the user authorization tables.
choices.justification: The data owner holds the privilege and responsibility for formally establishing the access rights. An information systems (IS) administrator should then implement or update user authorization tables at the direction of the owner.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Authorized staff implements the user authorization tables and the data owner approves them.
choices.justification: The owner sets the rules and conditions for access. It is best to obtain approval before implementing the tables.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The data owner and an information systems (IS) manager jointly create and update the user authorization tables.
choices.justification: The data owner may consult with the IS manager to set out access control rules, but the responsibility for appropriate access remains with the data owner. The IT department should set up the access control tables at the direction of the owner.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The data owner creates and updates the user authorization tables.
choices.justification: The data owner does not usually manage updates to the authorization tables.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor evaluating logical access controls should FIRST:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: document the controls applied to the potential access paths to the system.
choices.justification: Documentation and evaluation is the second step in assessing the adequacy, efficiency and effectiveness of the controls, and is based on the risk to the system that necessitates the controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: test controls over the access paths to determine if they are functional.
choices.justification: The third step is to test the access paths—to determine if the controls are functioning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: evaluate the security environment in relation to written policies and practices.
choices.justification: It is only after the risk is determined and the controls documented that the information systems (IS) auditor can evaluate the security environment to assess its adequacy through review of the written policies, observation of practices and comparison of them to appropriate security good practices.

choices.label: D
choices.text: obtain an understanding of the security risk to information processing.
choices.justification: When evaluating logical access controls, an IS auditor should first obtain an understanding of the security risk facing information processing by reviewing relevant documentation, inquiries, and conducting a risk assessment. This is necessary so that the IS auditor can ensure that the controls are adequate to address risk.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing system access and discovers an excessive number of users with privileged access. The IS auditor discusses the situation with the system administrator, who states that some personnel in other departments need privileged access and management has approved the access. Which of the following is the BEST course of action for the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Determine whether compensating controls are in place.
choices.justification: An excessive number of users with privileged access is not necessarily an issue if compensating controls are in place.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the issue in the audit report.
choices.justification: An information systems (IS) auditor should gather additional information before presenting the situation in the report.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recommend an update to the procedures.
choices.justification: An update to procedures does not address a potential weakness in logical security and may not be feasible if individuals are required to have this access to perform their jobs.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discuss the issue with senior management.
choices.justification: The IS auditor should gather additional information before reporting the item to senior management.

questions.text: The information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the implementation of a storage area network (SAN). The SAN administrator indicates that logging and monitoring is active, hard zoning is used to isolate data belonging to different business units and all unused SAN ports are disabled. The administrator implemented the system, performed and documented security testing during implementation and is the only user with administrative rights to the system. What should the IS auditor’s initial determination be?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: There is no significant potential risk.
choices.justification: Although the storage area network (SAN) may have been implemented with good controls, there is risk created by the combination of roles held by the SAN administrator.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Soft zoning presents a potential risk.
choices.justification: Hard zoning is more secure than soft zoning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Disabling unused ports presents a potential risk.
choices.justification: Unused ports should generally be disabled to increase security.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The SAN administrator presents a potential risk.
choices.justification: The potential risk in this scenario is posed by the SAN administrator. One concern is having a single point of failure. Because only one administrator has the knowledge and access required to administer the system, the enterprise is susceptible to risk. For example, if the SAN administrator decided to quit unexpectedly or was otherwise unavailable, the enterprise may not be able to adequately administer the SAN. In addition, having a single administrator for a large, complex system, such as a SAN, also presents a separation of duties risk. The enterprise currently relies entirely on the SAN administrator to implement, maintain and validate all security controls; this means that the SAN administrator can modify or remove those controls without detection.

questions.text: A data center has a badge-entry system. Which of the following is MOST important to protect the computing assets in the center?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Badge readers are installed in locations where tampering would be noticed.
choices.justification: Tampering with a badge reader cannot open the door, so this is irrelevant.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The computer that controls the badge system is backed up frequently.
choices.justification: The configuration of the system does not change frequently; therefore, frequent backup is not necessary.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A process for promptly deactivating lost or stolen badges is followed.
choices.justification: The biggest risk is from unauthorized individuals who can enter the data center, whether they are employees or not. Thus, having and following a process of deactivating lost or stolen badges is important.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All badge entry attempts are logged, whether or not they succeed.
choices.justification: Logging the entry attempts is important, but not as important as ensuring that a lost or stolen badge is disabled as quickly as possible.

questions.text: The BEST overall quantitative measure of the performance of biometric control devices is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: false-rejection rate (FRR).
choices.justification: The false-rejection rate (FRR) only measures the number of times an authorized person is denied entry.

choices.label: B
choices.text: false-acceptance rate (FAR) .
choices.justification: The false-acceptance rate (FAR) only measures the number of times an unauthorized person may be accepted as authorized.

choices.label: C
choices.text: equal error rate (EER).
choices.justification: A low equal error rate (EER) is a combination of a low FRR and a low FAR. EER, expressed as a percentage, is a measure of the number of times that the FRR and FAR are equal. A low EER is the measure of the more effective biometrics control device.

choices.label: D
choices.text: estimated-error rate.
choices.justification: The estimated-error rate is not a valid biometric term.

questions.text: A new business application was designed in a large, complex enterprise, and the business owner requested that the various reports be viewed on a need-to-know basis. Which of the following access control methods is the BEST method to achieve this requirement?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mandatory
choices.justification: An access control system based on mandatory access control is expensive and difficult to implement and maintain in a large complex enterprise.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Role-based
choices.justification: Role-based access control limits access according to job roles and responsibilities and is the best method to allow only authorized users to view reports on a need-to-know basis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discretionary
choices.justification: With discretionary access control (DAC), the owner of the resources decides who should have access to those resources. Most access control systems are an implementation of DAC. This control is not specific enough for this scenario.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Single sign-on (SSO)
choices.justification: Single sign-on (SSO) is an access control technology that is used to manage access to multiple systems, networks and applications. This control is not specific enough for this question.

questions.text: During an access control review for a mainframe application, an information systems (IS) auditor discovers user security groups without designated owners. The PRIMARY reason that this is a concern to the IS auditor is that, without ownership, there is no one with clear responsibility for:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: updating group metadata.
choices.justification: Updating data about the group is not a great concern when compared to unauthorized access.

choices.label: B
choices.text: reviewing existing user access.
choices.justification: Although the periodic review of user accounts is a good practice, this is a detective control and not as robust as preventing unauthorized access to the group in the first place.

choices.label: C
choices.text: approval of user access.
choices.justification: Without an owner to provide approval for user access to the group, unauthorized individuals can potentially gain access to any sensitive data within the rights of the group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: removing terminated users.
choices.justification: Revoking access to terminated users is a compensating control for the normal termination process and is also a detective control.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing access controls for a manufacturing organization. During the review, the IS auditor discovers that data owners can change access controls for a low-risk application. The BEST course of action for the IS auditor is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: recommend that mandatory access control be implemented.
choices.justification: Recommending mandatory access control is not correct because it is more appropriate for data owners to have discretionary access control (DAC) in a low-risk application.

choices.label: B
choices.text: report this as a finding to upper management
choices.justification: The use of DAC may not be an exception and, until confirmed, should not be reported as an issue.

choices.label: C
choices.text: report this to the data owners to determine whether it is an exception.
choices.justification: Although an information systems (IS) auditor may consult with data owners regarding whether this access is allowed normally, the IS auditor should not rely on the auditee to determine whether this is an issue.

choices.label: D
choices.text: not report this issue because discretionary access controls are in place.
choices.justification: DAC allows data owners to modify access, which is a normal procedure and is a characteristic of DAC.

questions.text: While auditing an internally developed web application, an information systems (IS) auditor determines that all business users share a common access profile. Which of the following is the MOST relevant recommendation to prevent the risk of unauthorized data modification?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enable detailed logging of user actions.
choices.justification: Logging is a detective control and often a secondary recommendation in the event that technical issues or costs prohibit implementation of preventive controls.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Customize user access profiles per job responsibility.
choices.justification: The strongest control is a preventive control that is automated through the system. Developing additional access profiles ensures that the system restricts users to privileges defined by their job responsibilities and that an audit trail exists for those user actions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enforce a strong password policy for all accounts.
choices.justification: Although an enforcing password policy is a type of preventive control, it is not as effective as removing excessive access rights from users who do not need it to perform their job duties.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement regular access rights review.
choices.justification: Access right review will not help in this scenario, because all profiles have similar set of access rights.

questions.text: Which of the following findings is of GREATEST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor during a review of logical access to an application?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Some developers have update access to production data.
choices.justification: Developers might need limited update access to production data to perform their jobs, and this access, when approved and reviewed by management, is acceptable even though it does pose a risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Developers can run a debugging tool in the production environment.
choices.justification: A debugging tool displays the execution of a program step by step and allows the user to modify data during execution. Using such a tool in production may result in unauthorized modification of production data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The change control team has knowledge of the application ID password.
choices.justification: Knowledge of the application ID password by the change control team does not pose a great concern if adequate separation of duties exists between change control and development activities. There may be occasions when the application ID needs to be used by change control in the production environment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The application does not enforce the use of strong passwords.
choices.justification: Although the lack of a strong password policy and configuration can result in compromised accounts, the risk is lower than if the application ID password is compromised because the application ID password does not allow for traceability.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor discovers that the configuration settings for password controls are more stringent for business users than for IT developers. Which of the following is the BEST action for the IS auditor to take?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Determine whether this is a policy violation and document it.
choices.justification: If the policy documents the purpose and approval for different procedures, then an information systems (IS) auditor only needs to document observations and tests about whether the procedures are followed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Document the observation as an exception.
choices.justification: This condition is not considered an exception if procedures are followed according to approved policies.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recommend that all password configuration settings be identical.
choices.justification: There may be valid reasons for these settings to be different; therefore, the auditor does not normally recommend changes before researching enterprise policies and procedures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recommend that logs of IT developer access are reviewed periodically.
choices.justification: Although reviewing logs may be a good compensating control, the more important course of action is to determine if policies are being followed.

questions.text: Which of the following should an information systems (IS) auditor be MOST concerned about in a financial application?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Programmers have access to source code in the user acceptance testing environment.
choices.justification: Programmers who have access to application source code are not of concern to the information systems (IS) auditor because programmers need access to source code to do their jobs. The user acceptance testing (UAT) environment is separate from the production environment, and changes cannot be moved into the production environment without prior authorization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Secondary controls are documented for identified role conflicts.
choices.justification: When separation of duties conflicts are identified, secondary controls should be in place to mitigate risk. Although the IS auditor reviews secondary controls, in this case, the greater concern is programmers having access to the production database.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The information security officer does not authorize all application changes.
choices.justification: The information security officer is not likely to authorize all application changes; therefore, this is not a concern for an IS auditor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Programmers have access to the production database.
choices.justification: Programmers having access to the production database is considered a separation of duties conflict.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST effective control when granting temporary access to vendors?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vendor access corresponds to the service level agreement (SLA).
choices.justification: The service level agreement (SLA) may have a provision for providing access, but this is not a control; it would merely define the need for access.

choices.label: B
choices.text: User accounts are created with expiration dates and are based on services provided.
choices.justification: The most effective control is to ensure that the granting of temporary access is based on services to be provided and that there is an expiration date (automated is best) associated with each unique ID. The use of an identity management system enforces temporary and permanent access for users, at the same time ensuring proper accounting of their activities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administrator access is provided for a limited period.
choices.justification: Vendors may require administrator access for a limited period during the time of service. However, it is important to ensure that the level of access granted is set according to least privilege and that access during this period is monitored.

choices.label: D
choices.text: User IDs are deleted when the work is completed.
choices.justification: Deleting these user IDs after the work is completed is necessary, but if not automated, the deletion could be overlooked. The access should only be granted at the level of work required.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing the authentication controls of an enterprise should be MOST concerned if:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: user accounts are not locked out after five failed attempts.
choices.justification: If user accounts are not locked after multiple failed attempts, a brute force attack can be used to gain access to the system. Although this is a risk, a typical user has limited system access compared to an administrator.

choices.label: B
choices.text: passwords can be reused by employees within a defined time frame.
choices.justification: The reuse of passwords is a risk. However, the use of shared login credentials by administrators is a more severe risk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: system administrators use shared login credentials.
choices.justification: The use of shared login credentials makes accountability impossible. This is especially a risk with privileged accounts.

choices.label: D
choices.text: password expiration is not automated.
choices.justification: If password expiration is not automated, it is most likely that employees will not change their passwords regularly. However, this is not as serious as passwords being shared, and the use of shared login credentials by administrators is a more severe risk.

questions.text: A business application system accesses an enterprise database using a single ID and password embedded in a program. Which of the following would provide efficient access control over the enterprise data?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Introduce a secondary authentication method such as card swipe.
choices.justification: The issue is user permissions, not authentication; therefore, adding stronger authentication does not improve the situation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply role-based permissions within the application system.
choices.justification: This is a normal process to allow the application to communicate with the database. Therefore, the best control is to control access to the application and procedures to ensure that access to data is granted based on a user’s role.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Have users input the ID and password for each database transaction.
choices.justification: Having a user input the ID and password for access provides better control because a database log identifies the initiator of the activity. However, this may not be efficient because each transaction would require a separate authentication process.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Set an expiration period for the database password embedded in the program.
choices.justification: It is good practice to set an expiration date for a password. However, this might not be practical for an ID automatically logged in from the program. Often, this type of password is set not to expire.

questions.text: Which of the following is an effective preventive control to ensure that a database administrator (DBA) complies with the custodianship of the enterprise’s data?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exception reports
choices.justification: Exception reports are detective controls used to indicate when the activities of the database administrator (DBA) were performed without authorization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Separation of duties (SoD)
choices.justification: Adequate segregation/separation of duties (SoD) is a preventative control that can restrict the activities of the DBA to those that have been authorized by the data owners. SoD can restrict what a DBA can do by requiring more than one person to participate to complete a task.

choices.label: C
choices.text: of access logs and activities
choices.justification: s of access logs are used to detect the activities performed by the DBA.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Management supervision
choices.justification: Management supervision of DBA activities is used to detect which DBA activities were not authorized.

questions.text: Due to resource constraints, a developer requires full access to production data to support certain problems reported by production users. Which of the following choices is a good compensating control for controlling unauthorized changes in production?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provide and monitor separate developer login IDs for programming and for production support.
choices.justification: Providing separate login IDs that only allow a developer privileged access when required is a good compensating control, but it must also be backed up with monitoring and supervision of the activity of the developer.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capture activities of the developer in the production environment by enabling detailed audit trails.
choices.justification: Although capturing activities of the developer via audit trails or logs is a good practice, the control is not effective unless these audit trails are reviewed on a periodic basis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Back up all affected records before allowing the developer to make production changes.
choices.justification: Creating a backup of affected records before making the change allows for rollback in case of an error but does not prevent or detect unauthorized changes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure that all changes are approved by the change manager prior to implementation.
choices.justification: Even though changes are approved by the change manager, a developer with full access can easily circumvent this control.

questions.text: During a logical access controls review, an information systems (IS) auditor observes that user accounts are shared. The GREATEST risk resulting from this situation is that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: an unauthorized user may use a shared ID to gain access.
choices.justification: The risk of an unauthorized user accessing the system with a shared ID is no greater than an unauthorized user accessing the system with a unique user ID.

choices.label: B
choices.text: user access management is time consuming.
choices.justification: Access management would not be any different with shared IDs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: user accountability is not established.
choices.justification: The use of a single user ID by more than one individual precludes knowing who, in fact, used that ID to access a system; therefore, it is more difficult to hold anyone accountable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: passwords are easily guessed.
choices.justification: Shared user IDs do not necessarily have easily guessed passwords.

questions.text: During a logical access controls review, an information systems (IS) auditor observes that user accounts are shared. The GREATEST risk resulting from this situation is that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: an unauthorized user may use the ID to gain access.
choices.justification: The ability of unauthorized users to use a shared ID is more likely than to use an individual ID—but the misuse of another person’s ID is always a risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: user access management is time consuming.
choices.justification: Using shared IDs does not pose an increased risk due to the work required for managing access.

choices.label: C
choices.text: passwords are easily guessed.
choices.justification: Shared user IDs do not necessarily have easily guessed passwords.

choices.label: D
choices.text: user accountability may not be established.
choices.justification: The use of a user ID by more than one individual precludes knowing who used that ID to access a system; therefore, it is impossible to hold anyone accountable.

questions.text: When reviewing an enterprise’s logical access security to its remote systems, which of the following would be of GREATEST concern to an information systems (IS) auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Passwords are shared.
choices.justification: The passwords should not be shared, but this is less important than ensuring that the password files are encrypted.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Unencrypted passwords are used.
choices.justification: When evaluating the technical aspects of logical security, unencrypted passwords represent the greatest risk because it is assumed that remote access is over an untrusted network where passwords can be discovered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Redundant logon IDs exist.
choices.justification: Checking for the redundancy of logon IDs is essential but is less important than ensuring that the passwords are encrypted.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Third-party users possess administrator access.
choices.justification: There may be business requirements, such as the use of contractors that requires them to have system access, so this may not be a concern.

questions.text: In an online banking application, which of the following would BEST protect against identity theft?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Encryption of personal password
choices.justification: A password alone is only single-factor authentication and can be guessed or broken.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Restricting the user to a specific terminal
choices.justification: Restricting the user to a specific terminal is not a practical alternative for an online application, because the users may need to log in from multiple devices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Multifactor authentication (MFA)
choices.justification: Multifactor authentication (MFA) requires two or more independent methods for establishing identity and privileges. Factors include something the user knows, such as a password; something the user has, such as a token; and something the user is, which is biometric. Requiring two or more of these factors makes identity theft more difficult.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Periodic review of access logs
choices.justification: Periodic review of access logs is a detective control and does not protect against identity theft.

questions.text: Authorizing access to application data is the responsibility of the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's data governance program

choices.label: A
choices.text: data custodian.
choices.justification: Data custodians are responsible only for storing and safeguarding the data according to the direction provided by the data owner.

choices.label: B
choices.text: application administrator.
choices.justification: An application administrator is responsible for managing the application itself, not determining who is authorized to access the data that it contains.

choices.label: C
choices.text: data owner.
choices.justification: Data owners have authority to grant or withhold access to the data and applications for which they are responsible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: security administrator.
choices.justification: The security administrator may lead investigations and is responsible for implementing and maintaining information security policy, but not for authorizing data access.

questions.text: Which is the MOST important step an auditor should consider while developing an audit plan based on a risk-based approach?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inventory the information systems in use in the enterprise and categorize them.
choices.justification: An inventory alone will not provide sufficient information to assess risk related to critical functions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assess what risk affect systems and the severity of the impact on the business.
choices.justification: Audit risk is dependent on the auditor’s assessed levels of inherent risk (the susceptibility of an audit area to error, which can be material, assuming there are no related internal controls), control risk (the risk a material weakness will not be prevented or detected by internal controls) and detection risk (the risk substantive tests will not detect an error which can be material).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determine which systems impact critical enterprise functions and how close to real time they operate.
choices.justification: While planning the audit, the auditor decides what level of audit risk they are willing to accept. The more effective and extensive the audit work is, the less risk of a weakness going undetected. Understanding the critical systems of an enterprise and how close to real time they operate will help ensure that the auditor has the information needed to make a risk decision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rank the systems based on risk assessment and decide the audit priority, resources, schedule and frequency.
choices.justification: Not all systems require a place in the audit plan; some noncritical systems may rank higher than critical systems. Critical systems impacting the business should get priority in the audit plan when a risk-based approach is taken.

questions.text: Web and email filtering tools are valuable to an enterprise PRIMARILY because they:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: protect the enterprise from viruses and nonbusiness materials.
choices.justification: The main reason for investing in web and email filtering tools is that they significantly reduce risk related to viruses, spam, mail chains, recreational surfing and recreational email.

choices.label: B
choices.text: maximize employee performance.
choices.justification: Maximizing employee performance can be true in some circumstances (i.e., it needs to be implemented along with an awareness program so that employee performance can be significantly improved). However, the primary benefit is protecting the enterprise from viruses and nonbusiness activity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: safeguard the enterprise’s image.
choices.justification: Safeguarding the enterprise’s image is a secondary benefit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: assist the enterprise in preventing legal issues.
