choices.justification: Preventing legal issues is important, but not the primary reason for filtering.

questions.text: The review of router access control lists should be conducted during:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: an environmental review.
choices.justification: Environmental reviews examine physical security such as power and physical access. They do not require a review of the router access control lists.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a network security review.
choices.justification: Network security reviews include reviewing router access control lists, port scanning, internal and external connections to the system, etc.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a business continuity review.
choices.justification: Business continuity reviews ensure that the business continuity plan is up-to-date, adequate to protect the organization and tested, and do not require a review of the router access control lists.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a data integrity review.
choices.justification: Data integrity reviews validate data accuracy and protect from improper alterations, but do not require a review of the router access control lists.

questions.text: The implementation of which of the following would MOST effectively prevent unauthorized access to a system administration account on a web server?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's database management practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Host intrusion detection software installed on the server
choices.justification: Host intrusion detection software assists in the detection of unauthorized system access but does not prevent such access.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Password expiration and lockout policy
choices.justification: Although controls regarding password expiration and lockout from failed login attempts are important, two-factor authentication methods or techniques most effectively reduce the risk of stolen or compromised credentials. Password-only-based authentication may not provide adequate security.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Password complexity rules
choices.justification: Although controls regarding password complexity are important, two-factor authentication methods or techniques most effectively reduce the risk of stolen or compromised credentials.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Two-factor authentication
choices.justification: Two-factor authentication requires a user to use a password in combination with another identification factor that is not easily stolen or guessed by an attacker. Types of two-factor authentication include electronic access tokens that show one-time passwords on their display panels and biometric authentication systems.

questions.text: An accuracy measure for a biometric system is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: system response time.
choices.justification: An important consideration in the implementation of biometrics is the time required to process a user. If the system is too slow, then it will impact productivity and lead to frustration. However, this is not an accuracy measure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: registration time.
choices.justification: The registration time is a measure of the effort taken to enroll a user in the system. This is not an accuracy measure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: input file size.
choices.justification: The file size to retain biometric information varies depending on the type of biometric solution selected. This is not an accuracy measure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: false-acceptance rate (FAR).
choices.justification: Three main accuracy measures are used for a biometric solution: false-rejection rate (FRR), cross-error rate (CER) and false-acceptance rate (FAR). FRR is a measure of how often valid individuals are rejected. FAR is a measure of how often invalid individuals are accepted. CER is a measure of when the false-rejection rate equals the false-acceptance rate.

questions.text: A key IT systems developer has suddenly resigned from an enterprise. Which of the following will be the MOST important action?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Set up an exit interview with human resources.
choices.justification: The interview with HR is also an important process if it is conducted by the last date of employment, but it is of secondary importance compared to removing the developer’s access to systems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate the handover process to ensure continuity of the project.
choices.justification: If the handover process to a designated employee is conducted by the last date of employment, there should be no problems.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Terminate the developer’s logical access to IT resources.
choices.justification: To protect IT assets, terminating logical access to IT resources is the first and most important action to take after management has confirmed the employee’s clear intention to leave the enterprise.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ensure that management signs off on the termination paperwork.
choices.justification: Ensuring that management signs off on termination paperwork is important, but not as critical as terminating access to the IT systems.

questions.text: With the help of a security officer, granting access to data is the responsibility of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: data owners.
choices.justification: Data owners are responsible for the access to and use of data. Written authorization for users to gain access to computerized information should be provided by the data owners. Security administration with the owners’ approval sets up access rules stipulating which users or group of users are authorized to access data or files and the level of authorized access (e.g., read or update).

choices.label: B
choices.text: programmers.
choices.justification: Programmers develop the access control software that regulates the ways that users can access the data (update, read, delete, etc.), but the programmers do not have responsibility for determining who gets access to data.

choices.label: C
choices.text: system analysts.
choices.justification: Systems analysts work with the owners and programmers to design access controls according to the rules set by the owners.

choices.label: D
choices.text: librarians.
choices.justification: The librarians enforce the access control procedures they have been given but do not determine who gets access.

questions.text: During the collection of forensic evidence, which of the following actions would MOST likely result in the destruction or corruption of evidence on a compromised system?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dumping the memory content to a file
choices.justification: Copying the memory contents is a normal forensics procedure where possible. Done carefully, it will not corrupt the evidence.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Generating disk images of the compromised system
choices.justification: Proper forensics procedures require creating two copies of the images of the system for analysis. Hash values ensure that the copies are accurate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rebooting the system
choices.justification: Rebooting the system may result in a change in the system state and the loss of files and important evidence stored in memory.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Removing the system from the network
choices.justification: When investigating a system, it is recommended to disconnect it from the network to minimize external infection or access.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing an organization to ensure that evidence related to a data breach case is preserved. Which of the following choices would be of MOST concern to the IS auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's policies and practices related to asset life cycle management

choices.label: A
choices.text: End users are not aware of incident reporting procedures.
choices.justification: End users should be made aware of incident reporting procedures, but this is not likely to affect data integrity related to the breach. The information systems (IS) auditor would be more concerned that the organization’s policy exists and provides for proper evidence handling.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Log servers are not on a separate network.
choices.justification: Having log servers segregated on a separate network might be a good idea because ensuring the integrity of log server data is important. However, it is more critical to ensure that the chain of custody policy is in place.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Backups are not performed consistently.
choices.justification: Although not having valid backups is a concern, the more important concern is a lack of a chain of custody policy. Data breach evidence is not normally retrieved from backups.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no chain of custody policy.
choices.justification: Organizations should have a policy in place that directs employees to follow certain procedures when collecting evidence that may be used in a court of law. Chain of custody involves documentation of how digital evidence is acquired, processed, handled, stored and protected, and who handled the evidence and why. If there is no policy in place, it is unlikely that employees will ensure that the chain of custody is maintained during any data breach investigation.

questions.text: Which of the following criteria are MOST needed to ensure that log information is admissible in court? Ensure that data have been:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's log management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: independently time stamped.
choices.justification: Independent time stamps are a key requirement in logging. This is one method of ensuring log integrity; however, this does not prevent information from being modified.

choices.label: B
choices.text: recorded by multiple logging systems.
choices.justification: Having multiple logging resources may work to ensure redundancy; however, increased redundancy may not effectively add value to the credibility of log information.

choices.label: C
choices.text: encrypted by the most secure algorithm.
choices.justification: The strength of the encryption algorithm may improve data confidentiality; however, this does not necessarily prevent data from being modified.

choices.label: D
choices.text: verified to ensure log integrity.
choices.justification: It is important to ensure that log information existed at a certain point of time and it has not been altered. Therefore, evidential credibility of log information is enhanced when there is proof that no one has tampered with this information, something typically accomplished by maintaining a documented chain of custody.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor has been asked by management to review a potentially fraudulent transaction. The PRIMARY focus of the IS auditor while evaluating the transaction should be to:
questions.taskStatement: Conduct audits in accordance with IS audit standards and a risk based IS audit strategy

choices.label: A
choices.text: maintain impartiality while evaluating the transaction.
choices.justification: Although it is important for an information systems (IS) auditor to be impartial, in this case it is more critical that the evidence be preserved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: ensure that the independence of the IS auditor is maintained.
choices.justification: Although it is important for an IS auditor to maintain independence, in this case, it is more critical that the evidence be preserved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: ensure that the integrity of the evidence is maintained.
choices.justification: The IS auditor has been requested to perform an investigation to capture evidence that may be used for legal purposes, and, therefore, maintaining the integrity of the evidence should be the foremost goal. Improperly handled computer evidence is subject to being ruled inadmissible in a court of law.

choices.label: D
choices.text: assess all relevant evidence for the transaction.
choices.justification: Although it is also important to assess all relevant evidence, it is more important to maintain the chain of custody, which ensures the integrity of evidence.

questions.text: When performing a computer forensic investigation, in regard to the evidence gathered, an information systems (IS) auditor should be MOST concerned with:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: analysis.
choices.justification: Analysis is important but not the primary concern related to evidence in a forensic investigation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: evaluation.
choices.justification: Evaluation is important but not the primary concern related to evidence in a forensic investigation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: preservation.
choices.justification: Preservation and documentation of evidence for review by law enforcement and judicial authorities are of primary concern when investigating. Failure to properly preserve the evidence can jeopardize the admissibility of the evidence in legal proceedings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: disclosure.
choices.justification: Disclosure is important but not of primary concern to the information systems (IS) auditor in a forensic investigation.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing security incident management procedures for the enterprise. Which of the following choices is the MOST important consideration?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chain of custody of electronic evidence
choices.justification: The preservation of evidence is the most important consideration in regard to security incident management. If data and evidence are not collected properly, valuable information can be lost and would not be admissible in a court of law should the enterprise decide to pursue litigation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System breach notification procedures
choices.justification: System breach notification is an important aspect and, in many cases, may even be required by laws and regulations; however, the security incident may not be a breach and the notification procedure might not apply.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Escalation procedures to external agencies
choices.justification: Escalation procedures to external agencies such as the local police or special agencies dealing in cybercrime are important. However, without proper chain of custody procedures, vital evidence may be lost and would not be admissible in a court of law should the enterprise decide to pursue litigation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Procedures to recover lost data
choices.justification: Although having procedures in place to recover lost data is important, it is critical to ensure that evidence is protected to ensure follow-up and investigation.

questions.text: When reviewing a digital certificate verification process, which of the following findings represents the MOST significant risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: There is no registration authority for reporting key compromises.
choices.justification: The certificate (certification) authority (CA) can assume the responsibility if there is no registration authority.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The certificate revocation list is not current.
choices.justification: If the certificate revocation list is not current, there can be a digital certificate that is not revoked that can be used for unauthorized or fraudulent activities.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Digital certificates contain a public key that is used to encrypt messages and verify digital signatures.
choices.justification: Digital certificates contain a public key that is used to encrypt messages and verify digital signatures; therefore, this is not a risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Subscribers report key compromises to the certificate authority.
choices.justification: Subscribers reporting key compromises to the CA is not a risk because reporting this to the CA enables the CA to take appropriate action.

questions.text: During an audit of a telecommunications system, an information systems (IS) auditor finds that the risk of intercepting data transmitted to and from remote sites is very high. The MOST effective control for reducing this exposure is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: encryption.
choices.justification: Encryption of data is the most secure method of protecting confidential data from exposure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: callback modems.
choices.justification: A callback system is used to ensure that a user is only logging in from a known location. It is not effective to protect the transmitted data from interception.

choices.label: C
choices.text: message authentication.
choices.justification: Message authentication is used to prove message integrity and source but not confidentiality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: dedicated leased lines.
choices.justification: It is more difficult to intercept traffic traversing a dedicated leased line than it is to intercept data on a shared network, but the only way to really protect the confidentiality of data is to encrypt it.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing Transport Layer Security enabled websites for the enterprise. Which of the following choices would be the HIGHEST risk?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expired digital certificates
choices.justification: An expired certificate leads to blocked access to the website, leading to unwanted downtime. However, there is no loss of data. Therefore, the comparative risk is lower.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Self-signed digital certificates
choices.justification: Self-signed digital certificates are not signed by a certificate (certification) authority (CA) and can be created by anyone. Thus, they can be used by attackers to impersonate a website, which may lead to data theft or perpetrate a man-in-the-middle attack.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Using the same digital certificate for multiple websites
choices.justification: Using the same digital certificate is not a significant risk. Wildcard digital certificates may be used for multiple subdomain websites.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Using 56-bit digital certificates
choices.justification: 56-bit digital certificates may be needed to connect with older versions of operating system (OS) or browsers. Although they have a lower strength than 128-bit or 256-bit digital certificates, the comparative risk of a self-signed certificate is higher.

questions.text: During an audit of an enterprise that is dedicated to ecommerce, the information systems (IS) manager states that digital signatures are used when receiving communications from customers. To substantiate this, an IS auditor must prove that which of the following is used?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: A biometric, digitalized and encrypted parameter with the customer’s public key.
choices.justification: Biometrics are not used in digital signatures or public key encryption.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A hash of the data that is transmitted and encrypted with the customer’s private key.
choices.justification: The calculation of a hash, or digest, of the data that are transmitted, and its encryption require the private key of the client (sender) and is called a signature of the message, or digital signature. The receiver hashes the received message and compares the hash that they compute with the received hash, after the digital signature has been decrypted with the sender’s public key. If the hash values are the same, the conclusion is that there is integrity in the data that have arrived, and the origin is authenticated. The concept of encrypting the hash with the private key of the originator provides nonrepudiation because it can only be decrypted with their public key, and the private key would not be known to the recipient. Simply put, in a key-pair situation, anything that can be decrypted by a sender’s public key must have been encrypted with their private key, so they must have been the sender (i.e., nonrepudiation).

choices.label: C
choices.text: A hash of the data that is transmitted and encrypted with the customer’s public key.
choices.justification: It is not correct to encrypt the hash with the customer’s public key because the recipient would need access to the customer’s private key to decrypt the digital signature.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The customer’s scanned signature encrypted with the customer’s public key.
choices.justification: A scan of the customer’s signature is known as a digitized signature, not a digital signature, and is of little or no value in this scenario.

questions.text: Which of the following provides the GREATEST assurance for database password encryption?
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: Secure hash algorithm-256
choices.justification: Hashing functions are often used to protect passwords, but hashing is not encryption.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Advanced encryption standard (AES)
choices.justification: The use of advanced encryption standard (AES) is a secure encryption algorithm that is appropriate for encrypting passwords.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Secure Shell (SSH)
choices.justification: Secure Shell (SSH) may encrypt passwords that are being transmitted but does not encrypt data at rest.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Triple DES (3DES)
choices.justification: Triple DES (3DES) is a valid encryption method; however, AES is a stronger and more recent encryption algorithm.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing an organization’s information security policy, which requires encryption of all data placed on USB drives. The policy also requires that a specific encryption algorithm be used. Which of the following algorithms would provide the greatest assurance that data placed on USB drives is protected from unauthorized disclosure?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Data Encryption Standard (DES)
choices.justification: Data Encryption Standard (DES) is susceptible to brute force attacks and has been broken publicly; therefore, it does not provide assurance that data encrypted using DES will be protected from unauthorized disclosure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Message digest 5
choices.justification: Message digest 5 (MD5) is an algorithm used to generate a one-way hash of data (a fixed-length value) to test and verify data integrity. MD5 does not encrypt data but puts data through a mathematical process that cannot be reversed. As a result, MD5 cannot be used to encrypt data on a USB drive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)
choices.justification: Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) provides the strongest encryption of all the choices listed and provides the greatest assurance that data are protected. Recovering data encrypted with AES is considered computationally infeasible; therefore, AES is the best choice for encrypting sensitive data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Secure Shell (SSH)
choices.justification: secure shell (SSH) is a protocol that is used to establish a secure, encrypted, command-line shell session, typically for remote logon. Although SSH encrypts data transmitted during a session, SSH cannot encrypt data at rest, including data on USB drives. As a result, SSH is not appropriate for this scenario.

questions.text: The MOST important difference between hashing and encryption is that hashing:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: is irreversible.
choices.justification: Hashing works one way—by applying a hashing algorithm to a message, a message hash/digest is created. If the same hashing algorithm is applied to the message digest, it will not result in the original message. Therefore, hashing is irreversible, while encryption is reversible. This is the basic difference between hashing and encryption.

choices.label: B
choices.text: output is the same length as the original message.
choices.justification: Hashing creates a fixed-length output that is usually smaller than the original message, and encryption creates an output that is usually the same length as the original message.

choices.label: C
choices.text: is concerned with integrity and security.
choices.justification: Hashing is used to verify the integrity of the message and does not address security. The same hashing algorithm is used at the sending and receiving ends to generate and verify the message hash/digest.

choices.label: D
choices.text: is the same at the sending and receiving end.
choices.justification: Encryption may use different keys or a reverse process at the sending and receiving ends to encrypt and decrypt.

questions.text: An enterprise has decided to implement an electronic signature scheme based on a public key infrastructure. The user’s private key will be stored on the computer’s hard drive and protected by a password. The MOST significant risk of this approach is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: use of the user’s electronic signature by another person if the password is compromised.
choices.justification: The user’s digital signature is only protected by a password. Compromise of the password would enable access to the signature. This is the most significant risk.

choices.label: B
choices.text: forgery by using another user’s private key to sign a message with an electronic signature.
choices.justification: Creating a digital signature with another user’s private key would indicate that the message came from a different person, and therefore, the true user’s credentials would not be forged.

choices.label: C
choices.text: impersonation of a user by substitution of the user’s public key with another person’s public key.
choices.justification: Impersonation of a public key would require the modification of the certificate issued by the certificate authority. This is very difficult and least likely.

choices.label: D
choices.text: forgery by substitution of another person’s private key on the computer.
choices.justification: The substitution of another person’s private key would not work because the digital signature would be validated with the original user’s public key.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST reliable method to ensure identity of the sender for messages transferred across the Internet?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Digital signatures
choices.justification: Digital signatures are used for both authentication and integrity, but the identity of the sender is confirmed by the digital certificate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Asymmetric cryptography
choices.justification: Asymmetric cryptography, such as public key infrastructure, appears to authenticate the sender but is vulnerable to a man-in-the-middle attack.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Digital certificates
choices.justification: Digital certificates are issued by a trusted third party. The message sender attaches the certificate and the recipient can verify authenticity with the certificate repository.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Message authentication code
choices.justification: Message authentication code is used for message integrity verification.

questions.text: The information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing findings from a prior IS audit of a hospital. One finding indicates that the organization was using email to communicate sensitive patient issues. The IT manager indicates that to address this finding, the organization has implemented digital signatures for all email users. What should the IS auditor’s response be?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Digital signatures are not adequate to protect confidentiality.
choices.justification: Digital signatures are designed to provide authentication and nonrepudiation for email and other transmissions but are not adequate for confidentiality. This implementation is not adequate to address the prior year’s finding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Digital signatures are adequate to protect confidentiality.
choices.justification: Digital signatures do not encrypt message contents; therefore, an attacker who intercepts a message can read the message.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The IS auditor should gather more information about the specific implementation.
choices.justification: Although gathering additional information is always a good step before drawing a conclusion on a finding, in this case, the implemented solution does not provide confidentiality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The IS auditor should recommend implementation of digital watermarking for secure email.
choices.justification: Digital watermarking is used to protect intellectual property rights for documents rather than to protect the confidentiality of email.

questions.text: Which of the following is a machine learning technique used for identifying patterns and relationships in unlabeled data?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate potential opportunities and risks associated with emerging technologies, regulations, and industry practices

choices.label: A
choices.text: Regression
choices.justification: Regression is a machine learning technique used for predicting continuous numerical values based on input variables. It is not specifically designed for identifying patterns in unlabeled data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clustering
choices.justification: Clustering is a machine learning technique used for identifying patterns and relationships in unlabeled data. Clustering algorithms group similar data points together based on their inherent similarities or distance metrics.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decision tree
choices.justification: Decision tree is a machine learning technique used for classification and regression tasks where labeled data is available. It is not specifically focused on identifying patterns in unlabeled data.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Naive Bayes
choices.justification: Naive Bayes is a machine learning technique commonly used for classification tasks based on probabilistic principles. It is not primarily used for identifying patterns in unlabeled data.

questions.text: Which of the following effectively verifies the originator of a transaction?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Using a secret password between the originator and the receiver
choices.justification: Because they are a shared secret between the user and the system itself, passwords are considered a weaker means of authentication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Encrypting the transaction with the receiver’s public key
choices.justification: Encrypting the transaction with the recipient’s public key provides confidentiality for the information but does not verify the source.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Using a portable document format to encapsulate transaction content
choices.justification: Using a portable document format protects the integrity of the content but not necessarily authorship.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Digitally signing the transaction with the source’s private key
choices.justification: A digital signature is an electronic identification of a person, created using a public key algorithm, to verify the identity of the source of a transaction and the integrity of its content to a recipient.

questions.text: The role of the certificate (certification) authority (CA) as a third party is to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: provide secure communication and networking services based on certificates.
choices.justification: Providing a communication infrastructure is not a certificate (certification) authority (CA) activity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: host a repository of certificates with the corresponding public and secret keys issued by that CA.
choices.justification: The secret keys belonging to the certificates are not archived at the CA.

choices.label: C
choices.text: act as a trusted intermediary between two communication partners.
choices.justification: The CA can contribute to authenticating the communicating partners to each other, but the CA is not involved in the communication stream itself.

choices.label: D
choices.text: confirm the identity of the entity owning a certificate issued by that CA.
choices.justification: The primary activity of a CA is to issue certificates. The primary role of the CA is to check the identity of the entity owning a certificate and to confirm the integrity of any certificate it issued.

questions.text: Which of the following is BEST suited for secure communications within a small group?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Key distribution center
choices.justification: A key distribution center is a part of a Kerberos implementation suitable for internal communication for a large group within an institution, and it will distribute symmetric keys for each session.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Certificate authority
choices.justification: Certificate authority is a trusted third party that ensures the authenticity of the owner of the certificate. This is necessary for large groups and formal communication.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Web of trust
choices.justification: Web of trust is a key distribution method suitable for communication in a small group. It is used by tools such as pretty good privacy and distributes the public keys of users within a group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Kerberos Authentication System
choices.justification: A Kerberos Authentication System extends the function of a key distribution center by generating tickets to define the facilities on networked machines, which are accessible to each user.

questions.text: Applying a digital signature to data traveling in a network provides:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: confidentiality and integrity.
choices.justification: A digital signature does not encrypt the message, so it cannot provide confidentiality.

choices.label: B
choices.text: security and nonrepudiation.
choices.justification: A digital signature does not encrypt the message, so it cannot provide security.

choices.label: C
choices.text: integrity and nonrepudiation.
choices.justification: A digital signature is created by signing a hash of a message with the private key of the sender. This provides for the integrity (through the hash) and the proof of origin (nonrepudiation) of the message.

choices.label: D
choices.text: confidentiality and nonrepudiation.
choices.justification: A digital signature does not provide confidentiality.

questions.text: An online stock trading firm is in the process of implementing a system to provide secure email exchange with its customers. What is the BEST option to ensure confidentiality, integrity and nonrepudiation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Symmetric key encryption
choices.justification: Symmetric key encryption uses a single pass phrase to encrypt and decrypt the message. Although this type of encryption is strong, it suffers from the inherent problem of needing to share the pass phrase in a secure manner and does not address integrity and nonrepudiation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Digital signatures
choices.justification: Digital signatures provide message integrity and nonrepudiation; however, confidentiality is not provided.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Message digest algorithms
choices.justification: Message digest algorithms are a way to design hashing functions to verify the integrity of the message/data. Message digest algorithms do not provide confidentiality or nonrepudiation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Digital certificates
choices.justification: A digital certificate contains the public key and identifying information about the owner of the public key. The associated private key pair is kept secret with the owner. These certificates are generally verified by a trusted authority, with the purpose of associating a person’s identity with the public key. Email confidentiality and integrity are obtained by following the public key-private key encryption. With the digital certificate verified by the trusted third party, nonrepudiation of the sender is obtained.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor reviewing digital rights management applications should expect to find an extensive use for which of the following technologies?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Digitalized signatures
choices.justification: Digitalized signatures are the scans of a signature (not the same as a digital signature) and not related to digital rights management.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hashing
choices.justification: Hashing creates a message hash or digest, which is used to ensure the integrity of the message; it is usually considered a part of cryptography.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Parsing
choices.justification: Parsing is the process of splitting up a continuous stream of characters for analytical purposes and is widely applied in the design of programming languages or in data entry editing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Steganography
choices.justification: Steganography is a technique for concealing the existence of messages or information within another message. An increasingly important steganographic technique is digital watermarking, which hides data within data (e.g., by encoding rights information in a picture or music file without altering the picture or music’s perceivable aesthetic qualities).

questions.text: The Transport Layer Security (TLS) protocol ensures the confidentiality of data and message by using:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: symmetric encryption.
choices.justification: Transport Layer Security (TLS) uses a symmetric key for data and message encryption and asymmetric key for establishing session.

choices.label: B
choices.text: message authentication codes.
choices.justification: A message authentication code is used for ensuring data integrity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: hash function.
choices.justification: Hash function is used for generating a message digest which can provide message integrity; it is not used for message encryption.

choices.label: D
choices.text: digital signature certificates.
choices.justification: Digital signature certificates are used by TLS for server authentication and establishing the session.

questions.text: Confidentiality of transmitted data can best be delivered by encrypting the:
questions.taskStatement: Provide guidance to the organization in order to improve the quality and control of information systems

choices.label: A
choices.text: message digest with the sender’s private key.
choices.justification: Encrypting the message digest with the sender’s private key ensures authentication and nonrepudiation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: session key with the sender’s public key.
choices.justification: Encrypting the session key with the sender’s public key makes the message accessible to only the sender.

choices.label: C
choices.text: messages with the receiver’s private key.
choices.justification: A message encrypted with a receiver’s private key can be decrypted by anyone using the receiver’s public key.

choices.label: D
choices.text: session key with the receiver’s public key.
choices.justification: Encrypting the session key with the receiver’s public key ensures that the session key can only be obtained using the receiver’s private key, retained by the receiver.

questions.text: In what capacity would an information systems (IS) auditor MOST likely see a hash function applied?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Authentication
choices.justification: The purpose of a hash function is to produce a fingerprint of data that can be used to ensure integrity and authentication. A hash of a password also provides for authentication of a user or process attempting to access resources.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identification
choices.justification: Hash functions are not used for identification. They are used to validate the authenticity of the identity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Authorization
choices.justification: Hash functions are not typically used to provide authorization. Authorization is provided after the authentication has been established.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Encryption
choices.justification: Hash functions do not encrypt data.

questions.text: A Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol (TCP/IP)-based environment is exposed to the Internet. Which of the following BEST ensures that complete encryption and authentication protocols exist for protecting information while transmitted?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Work is completed in tunnel mode with internet protocol (IP) security.
choices.justification: Tunnel mode with internet protocol (IP) security provides encryption and authentication of the complete IP package. To accomplish this, the authentication header and encapsulating security payload services can be nested. This is known as IP Security.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A digital signature with RSA has been implemented.
choices.justification: A digital signature with RSA provides authentication and integrity but not confidentiality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Digital certificates with RSA are being used.
choices.justification: Digital certificates with RSA provide authentication and integrity but do not provide encryption.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Work is being completed in transmission control protocol (TCP) services.
choices.justification: Transmission Control Protocol services do not provide encryption and authentication.

questions.text: A digital signature contains a message digest to:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: show if the message has been altered after transmission.
choices.justification: The message digest is calculated and included in a digital signature to prove that the message has not been altered. The message digest sent with the message should have the same value as the recalculation of the digest of the received message.

choices.label: B
choices.text: define the encryption algorithm.
choices.justification: The message digest does not define the algorithm; it is there to ensure integrity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: confirm the identity of the originator.
choices.justification: The message digest does not confirm the identity of the user; it is there to ensure integrity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: enable message transmission in a digital format.
choices.justification: The message digest does not enable the transmission in digital format; it is there to ensure integrity.

questions.text: Which of the following cryptography options increases overhead/cost?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: The encryption is symmetric rather than asymmetric.
choices.justification: An asymmetric algorithm requires more processing time than symmetric algorithms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A long asymmetric encryption key is used.
choices.justification: Computer processing time is increased for longer asymmetric encryption keys, and the increase may be disproportionate. For example, one benchmark showed that doubling the length of an RSA key from 512 bits to 1,024 bits caused the decrypt time to increase nearly six-fold.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The hash is encrypted rather than the message.
choices.justification: A hash is usually shorter than the original message; therefore, a smaller overhead is required if the hash is encrypted rather than the message.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A secret key is used.
choices.justification: A secret key, used as a symmetric encryption key, is generally small and used for the purpose of encrypting user data.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing an enterprise’s controls related to email encryption. The enterprise’s policy states that all sent email must be encrypted to protect the confidentiality of the message because the enterprise shares nonpublic information through email. In a public-key infrastructure implementation properly configured to provide confidentiality, email is encrypted with:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: the sender’s private key and decrypted with the sender’s public key.
choices.justification: Encrypting a message with the sender’s private key and decrypting it with the sender’s public key ensures that the message came from the sender; however, it does not guarantee message confidentiality. With public key infrastructure, a message encrypted with a private key must be decrypted with the responding public key, and vice versa.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the recipient’s private key and decrypted with the sender’s private key.
choices.justification: The sender does not have access to the receiver’s private key.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the sender’s private key and decrypted with the recipient’s private key.
choices.justification: A message encrypted with the sender’s private key cannot be decrypted using the recipient’s private key.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the recipient’s public key and decrypted with the recipient’s private key.
choices.justification: Encrypting a message with the recipient’s public key and decrypting it with the recipient’s private key ensures message confidentiality, because only the intended recipient has the correct private key to decrypt the message.

questions.text: The PRIMARY reason for using digital signatures is to ensure data:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: confidentiality.
choices.justification: A digital signature does not address message confidentiality.

choices.label: B
choices.text: integrity.
choices.justification: Digital signatures provide integrity because the digital signature of a signed message (file, mail, document, etc.) changes every time a single bit of the document changes; thus, a signed document cannot be altered. A digital signature provides for message integrity, nonrepudiation and proof of origin.

choices.label: C
choices.text: availability.
choices.justification: Availability is not related to digital signatures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: correctness.
choices.justification: In general, correctness is not related to digital signatures. A digital signature guarantee data integrity, however, it cannot ensure correctness of signed data.

questions.text: Which of the following is an advantage of elliptic curve encryption over RSA encryption?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: Computation speed
choices.justification: The main advantage of elliptical curve cryptography (ECC) over RSA encryption is its computation speed. This is due in part to the use of much smaller keys in the ECC algorithm than in RSA.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ability to support digital signatures
choices.justification: Both encryption methods support digital signatures.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Simpler key distribution
choices.justification: Both encryption methods are used for public key encryption and distribution.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Message integrity controls
choices.justification: Both ECC and RSA offer message integrity controls.

questions.text: When using a digital signature, the message digest is computed by the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: sender only.
choices.justification: The message digest must be computed by the sender and the receiver to ensure message integrity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: receiver only.
choices.justification: The receiver will compute a digest of the received message to verify the integrity of the received message.

choices.label: C
choices.text: sender and receiver both.
choices.justification: A digital signature is an electronic identification of a person or entity. It is created using asymmetric encryption. To verify integrity of data, the sender uses a cryptographic hashing algorithm against the entire message to create a message digest to be sent along with the message. Upon receipt of the message, the receiver will recompute the hash using the same algorithm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Certificate authority (CA).
choices.justification: The certificate (certification) authority (CA) issues certificates that link the public key with its owner. The CA does not compute digests of the messages to be communicated between the sender and receiver.

questions.text: Email message authenticity and confidentiality is BEST achieved by signing the message using the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: sender’s private key and encrypting the message using the receiver’s public key.
choices.justification: By signing the message with the sender’s private key, the receiver can verify its authenticity using the sender’s public key. Encrypting with the receiver’s public key provides confidentiality.

choices.label: B
choices.text: sender’s public key and encrypting the message using the receiver’s private key.
choices.justification: Signing can only occur using the sender’s private key.

choices.label: C
choices.text: receiver’s private key and encrypting the message using the sender’s public key.
choices.justification: The sender does not have access to the receiver’s private key.

choices.label: D
choices.text: receiver’s public key and encrypting the message using the sender’s private key.
choices.justification: By encrypting the message with the receiver’s public key, only the receiver can decrypt the message using their own private key. The receiver’s private key is confidential and, therefore, unknown to the sender. Messages encrypted using the sender’s private key can be read by anyone with the sender’s public key.

questions.text: The feature of a digital signature that ensures that the sender cannot later deny generating and sending the message is called:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's threat and vulnerability management program

choices.label: A
choices.text: data integrity.
choices.justification: Data integrity refers to changes in the plaintext message that would result in the recipient failing to compute the same message hash.

choices.label: B
choices.text: authentication.
choices.justification: Because only the claimed sender has the private key used to create the digital signature, authentication ensures that the message has been sent by the claimed sender.

choices.label: C
choices.text: nonrepudiation.
choices.justification: Integrity, authentication, nonrepudiation and replay protection are all features of a digital signature. Nonrepudiation ensures that the claimed sender cannot later deny generating and sending the message.

choices.label: D
choices.text: replay protection.
choices.justification: Replay protection is a method that a recipient can use to check that the message was not intercepted and re-sent (replayed).

questions.text: Confidentiality of the data transmitted in a wireless local area network (WLAN) is BEST protected if the session is:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: restricted to predefined media access control addresses.
choices.justification: Limiting the number of devices that can access the network via media access control address filtering is an inefficient control and does not address the issue of encrypting the session.

choices.label: B
choices.text: encrypted using static keys.
choices.justification: Encryption with static keys—using the same key for a long period of time—carries a risk that the key would be compromised.

choices.label: C
choices.text: encrypted using dynamic keys.
choices.justification: When using dynamic keys, the encryption key is changed frequently, thus reducing the risk of the key being compromised and the message being decrypted.

choices.label: D
choices.text: initiated from devices that have encrypted storage.
choices.justification: Encryption of the data on the connected device (laptop, smart phone, etc.) addresses the confidentiality of the data on the device, not the wireless session.

questions.text: The cryptographic hash sum of a message is recalculated by the receiver. This is to ensure:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's information security program

choices.label: A
choices.text: the confidentiality of the message.
choices.justification: A hash function ensures integrity of a message; encrypting with a secret key provides confidentiality.

choices.label: B
choices.text: nonrepudiation by the sender.
choices.justification: Signing the message with the private key of the sender ensures nonrepudiation and authenticity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the authenticity of the message.
choices.justification: Authenticity of the message is provided by the digital signature.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the integrity of data transmitted by the sender.
choices.justification: If the hash sum is different from what is expected, it implies that the message has been altered. This is an integrity test.

questions.text: The use of digital signatures:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: requires the use of a one-time password generator.
choices.justification: A one-time password generator is not a requirement for using digital signatures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: provides encryption to a message.
choices.justification: A digital signature provides integrity and proof of origin for a message but does not address confidentiality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: validates the source of a message.
choices.justification: The use of a digital signature verifies the identity of the sender.

choices.label: D
choices.text: ensures message confidentiality.
choices.justification: A digital signature does not ensure message confidentiality.

questions.text: Which of the following is BEST prevented by a raised floor in the computer machine room?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Damage of wires around computers and servers
choices.justification: The primary reason for having a raised floor is to enable ventilation systems, power cables and data cables to be installed underneath the floor. This eliminates the safety and damage risk posed when cables are scattered on an open floor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A power failure from static electricity
choices.justification: Static electricity should be avoided in the machine room; therefore, measures such as specially manufactured carpet or shoes are more appropriate for static prevention than a raised floor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shocks from earthquakes
choices.justification: Raised floors do not address shocks from earthquakes. To address earthquakes, antiseismic architecture is required to establish a quake-resistant structural framework.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Water flood damage
choices.justification: Computer equipment needs to be protected against water. However, a raised floor does not prevent damage to the machines in the event of overhead water pipe leakage.

questions.text: When auditing security for a data center, an information systems (IS) auditor should look for the presence of a voltage regulator to ensure that the:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: hardware is protected against power surges.
choices.justification: A voltage regulator protects against short-term power fluctuations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: integrity is maintained if the main power is interrupted.
choices.justification: A voltage regulator does not maintain the integrity if power is interrupted or lost.

choices.label: C
choices.text: immediate power will be available if the main power is lost.
choices.justification: An uninterruptible power supply (UPS) is used to provide constant power even if main power is lost.

choices.label: D
choices.text: hardware is protected against long-term power fluctuations.
choices.justification: A voltage regulator protects against short-term power fluctuations.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor inspected a windowless room containing phone switching and networking equipment and documentation binders. The room was equipped with two handheld fire extinguishers—one filled with carbon dioxide (CO2) and the other filled with halon gas. Which of the following should be given the HIGHEST priority in the IS auditor’s report?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: The halon extinguisher should be removed because halon has a negative impact on the atmospheric ozone layer.
choices.justification: The Montreal Protocol allows existing halon installations to remain, although some countries may have laws that require its removal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both fire suppression systems present a risk of suffocation when used in a closed room.
choices.justification: Protecting people’s lives should always be of the highest priority in fire suppression activities. Carbon dioxide (CO2) and halon both reduce the oxygen ratio in the atmosphere, which can induce serious personal hazards. In many countries, installing or refilling halon fire suppression systems is not allowed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The CO2 extinguisher should be removed because CO2 is ineffective for suppressing fires involving solid combustibles (paper).
choices.justification: CO2 extinguishers can be used on most types of fires, and their use in a server room would be appropriate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The documentation binders should be removed from the equipment room to reduce potential risk.
choices.justification: Although not of the highest priority, removal of the documentation would probably reduce some of the risk.

questions.text: Electromagnetic emissions from a terminal represent a risk because they:
questions.taskStatement: Utilize technical security testing to identify potential vulnerabilities

choices.label: A
choices.text: can damage or erase nearby storage media.
choices.justification: Although a strong magnetic field can erase certain storage media, normally terminals are designed to limit these emissions; therefore, this is not normally a concern.

choices.label: B
choices.text: can disrupt processor functions.
choices.justification: Electromagnetic emissions should not cause disruption of central processing units.

choices.label: C
choices.text: can have adverse health effects on personnel.
choices.justification: Most electromagnetic emissions are low level and do not pose a significant health risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: can be detected and displayed.
choices.justification: Emissions can be detected by sophisticated equipment and displayed, thus giving unauthorized persons access to data. TEMPEST is a term referring to the investigation and study of compromising emanations of unintentional intelligence-bearing signals that, if intercepted and analyzed, may reveal their contents.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the network infrastructure of a call center and determines that the internal telephone system is based on Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) technology. Which of the following is the GREATEST concern?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Voice communication uses the same equipment that is used for data communication.
choices.justification: Voice-over Internet Protocol (VoIP) telephone systems use the local area network (LAN) infrastructure of an enterprise for communication, which can save on wiring cost and simplify the installation and support of the telephone system. This use of shared infrastructure is a benefit of VoIP and therefore is not a concern.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ethernet switches are not protected by uninterrupted power supply units.
choices.justification: VoIP telephone systems use the LAN infrastructure of an enterprise for communication, typically using Ethernet connectivity to connect individual phones to the system. Most enterprises have a backup power supply for the main servers and systems, but typically do not have uninterrupted power supply units for the LAN switches. In the case of even a brief power outage, not having backup power on all network devices makes it impossible to send or receive phone calls, which is a concern, particularly in a call center.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Voice communication is not encrypted on the local network.
choices.justification: VoIP devices do not normally encrypt the voice traffic on the local network, so this is not a concern. Typically, a VoIP phone system connects to a telephone enterprise voice circuit, which is not normally encrypted. If the system uses the Internet for connectivity, then encryption is required.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The team that supports the data network also is responsible for the telephone system.
choices.justification: VoIP telephone systems use the LAN infrastructure of an enterprise for communication, so the personnel who support and maintain that infrastructure are now responsible for the data and voice network by default. Therefore, this is not a concern.

questions.text: Which of the following is the MOST effective control over visitor access to a data center?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Visitors are escorted.
choices.justification: Escorting visitors provides the best assurance that visitors have permission to access defined areas within the data processing facility.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Visitor badges are required.
choices.justification: Requiring visitors to wear badges is a good practice, but not a reliable control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Visitors sign in.
choices.justification: Requiring that visitors sign in is good practice, but not a reliable control. After visitors are in the building, the sign-in process will not prevent them from accessing unauthorized areas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Visitors are spot-checked by operators.
choices.justification: Visitors should be accompanied at all times while they are on the premises, not only when they are in the data processing facility.

questions.text: When reviewing the procedures for the disposal of computers, which of the following should be the GREATEST concern for the information systems (IS) auditor?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hard disks are overwritten several times at the sector level but are not reformatted before leaving the enterprise.
choices.justification: Overwriting a hard disk at the sector level completely erases data, directories, indices and master file tables. Reformatting is not necessary, because all contents are destroyed. Overwriting several times makes useless some forensic measures, which are able to reconstruct former contents of newly overwritten sectors by analyzing special magnetic features of the platter’s surface.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All files and folders on hard disks are separately deleted, and the hard disks are formatted before leaving the enterprise.
choices.justification: Deleting and formatting only marks the sectors that contained files as being free. Publicly available tools are sufficient for someone to reconstruct data from hard drives prepared this way.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hard disks are rendered unreadable by hole-punching through the platters at specific positions before leaving the enterprise.
choices.justification: Although hole-punching does not delete file contents, the hard disk cannot be used anymore, especially when head parking zones and track zero information are impacted. Reconstructing data would be extremely expensive because all analysis must be performed under a clean room atmosphere and is only possible within a short time frame or until the surface is corroded.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The transport of hard disks is escorted by internal security staff to a nearby metal recycling enterprise, where the hard disks are registered and then shredded.
choices.justification: Data reconstruction from shredded hard disks is impossible, especially when the scrap is mixed with other metal parts. If the transport can be secured and the destruction can be proved as described in the option, this is a valid method of disposal.

questions.text: An enterprise is proposing to establish a wireless local area network (WLAN). Management asks the information systems (IS) auditor to recommend security controls for the WLAN. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate recommendation?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Physically secure wireless access points to prevent tampering.
choices.justification: Physically securing access points, such as wireless routers, and preventing theft address the risk of malicious parties tampering with device settings. If access points can be physically reached, it is often a simple matter to restore weak default passwords and encryption keys, or to totally remove authentication and encryption from the network.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use service set identifiers that clearly identify the enterprise.
choices.justification: Service set identifiers should not be used to identify the enterprise, because hackers can associate the wireless local area network (WLAN) with a known enterprise, and this increases their motivation to attack and, potentially, the information available to do so.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Encrypt traffic using the wired equivalent privacy (WEP) mechanism.
choices.justification: The original wired equivalent privacy (WEP) security mechanism has been demonstrated to have a number of exploitable weaknesses. The more recently developed Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA) and WPA 2 standards represent considerably more secure means of authentication and encryption.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement the Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) to allow active monitoring.
choices.justification: Installing Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) on wireless access points can open up security vulnerabilities. If SNMP is required at all, then SNMP v3, which has stronger authentication mechanisms than earlier versions, should be deployed.

questions.text: Which of the following exposures associated with the spooling of sensitive reports for offline printing should an information systems (IS) auditor consider to be the MOST serious?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sensitive data might be read by operators.
choices.justification: Operators often have high-level access as a necessity to perform their job duties. To the extent that this is a risk, it exists for any form of nonlocal printing and is not specifically tied to spooled reports.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Data might be amended without authorization.
choices.justification: Data on spool files are no easier to amend without authority than any other file.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Unauthorized report copies might be printed.
choices.justification: Spooling for offline printing may enable additional copies to be printed unless adequate safeguards exist as compensating controls.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Output might be lost in the event of system failure.
choices.justification: Loss of data at the spooler level only requires reprinting.

questions.text: Which of the following environmental controls is appropriate to protect computer equipment against short-term reductions in electrical power?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: Power-line conditioners
choices.justification: Power-line conditioners are used to compensate for peaks and valleys in the power supply and reduce peaks in the power flow to what is needed by the machine. Any valleys are removed by power stored in the equipment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surge-protection devices
choices.justification: Surge-protection devices protect against high-voltage bursts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alternative power supplies
choices.justification: Alternative power supplies are intended for power failures that last for longer periods and are normally coupled with other devices, such as an uninterruptible power supply, to compensate for the power loss until the alternate power supply becomes available.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Interruptible power supplies
choices.justification: An interruptible power supply would cause the equipment to come down whenever there was a power failure.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the physical security measures of an enterprise. Regarding the access card system, the IS auditor should be MOST concerned that:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: nonpersonalized access cards are given to the cleaning staff, who use a sign-in sheet but show no proof of identity.
choices.justification: Physical security is meant to control who is entering a secured area, so identification of all individuals is of utmost importance. It is not adequate to trust unknown external people by allowing them to write down their alleged name without proof (e.g., identity card or driver’s license).

choices.label: B
choices.text: access cards are not labeled with the enterprise’s name and address to facilitate easy return of a lost card.
choices.justification: Having the name and address of the enterprise on the card may be a concern because a malicious finder can use a lost or stolen card to enter the enterprise premises.

choices.label: C
choices.text: card issuance and rights administration for the cards are done by different departments, causing unnecessary lead time for new cards.
choices.justification: Separating card issuance from technical rights management is a method to ensure the proper separation of duties so that no single person can produce a functioning card for a restricted area within the enterprise premises. The long lead time is an inconvenience but not a serious audit risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the computer system used for programming the cards can only be replaced after three weeks in the event of a system failure.
choices.justification: System failure of the card programming device would normally not mean that the readers do not function anymore. It means that no new cards can be issued, so this option is minor compared to the threat of improper identification.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing the physical security controls of a data center and notices several areas for concern. Which of the following areas is the MOST important?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: The emergency power off button cover is missing.
choices.justification: The emergency power-off button issue is a significant concern, but life safety is the highest priority.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Scheduled maintenance of the fire suppression system was not performed.
choices.justification: The primary purpose of the fire suppression system is to protect the equipment and building. The lack of scheduled maintenance is a concern; however, this does not indicate that the system would not function as required. The more critical issue is the emergency exit because life safety is the highest priority.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There are no security cameras inside the data center.
choices.justification: The lack of security cameras inside the data center may be a significant concern; however, the more significant issue is the emergency exit door being blocked.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The emergency exit door is blocked.
choices.justification: Life safety is always the highest priority; therefore, the blocking of the emergency exit is the most serious problem.

questions.text: An information systems (IS) auditor is reviewing an enterprise’s network operations center (NOC). Which of the following choices is of the GREATEST concern? The use of:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate logical, physical, and environmental controls to verify the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets

choices.label: A
choices.text: a wet pipe-based fire suppression system.
choices.justification: Wet pipe systems may damage computer equipment, but they are safe for humans and not as damaging as carbon dioxide (CO2) systems.

choices.label: B
choices.text: a rented rack space in the NOC.
choices.justification: Rented rack space is not a concern if security controls are maintained. Most organizations rent server rack space.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a carbon-dioxide-based fire suppression system.
choices.justification: CO2 systems should not be used in areas where people are present, because their function will cause suffocation in the event of a fire. Controls should consider personnel safety first.

choices.label: D
choices.text: an uninterrupted power supply with 10 minutes of backup power.
choices.justification: Depending on the system, a few minutes might be all that is needed for a graceful shutdown. However, a CO2 system is dangerous for personnel.

questions.text: The risk of dumpster diving is BEST mitigated by:
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's security awareness training program

choices.label: A
choices.text: implementing security awareness training.
choices.justification: Dumpster diving is used to steal documents or computer media that were not properly discarded. Users should be educated to know the risk of carelessly discarding sensitive documents and other items.

choices.label: B
choices.text: placing shred bins in copy rooms.
choices.justification: The shred bins may not be properly used if users are not aware of proper security techniques.

choices.label: C
choices.text: developing a media disposal policy.
choices.justification: A media disposal policy is a good idea; however, if users are not aware of the policy it may not be effective.

choices.label: D
choices.text: placing shredders in individual offices.
choices.justification: The shredders may not be properly used if users are not aware of proper security techniques.

questions.text: Which of the following is the BEST criterion for evaluating the adequacy of an enterprise’s security awareness program?
questions.taskStatement: Evaluate the organization's security awareness training program

choices.label: A
